title: A Cake without Butter
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- five eggs
- six ounces of flour
- beaten almonds
- thin slices of citron

## Directions

1. Beat well five eggs.
2. Add six ounces of flour.
3. Flavour with beaten almonds, and add, if liked, thin slices of citron.
4. Bake in a mould in a moderate oven.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A Fish Sauce without Butter
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a cup of water
- vinegar
- an onion
- a little rasped horseradish
- pepper
- two or three cloves
- a couple of anchovies
- the beaten yolks of two eggs

## Directions

1. Put on in a small saucepan a cup of water well flavoured with vinegar, an onion chopped fine, a little rasped horseradish, pepper, and two or three cloves, and a couple of anchovies cut small
2. When it has boiled, stir carefully in the beaten yolks of two eggs, and let it thicken until of the consistency of melted butter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A Juditha
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- gooseberries
- a little water
- well-beaten yolks of eggs
- white sugar
- biscuit ice or other cream ice

## Directions

1. Put some gooseberries into a saucepan with a little water.
2. When they are soft, pulp them through a sieve.
3. Add several well-beaten yolks of eggs, and sweeten with white sugar.
4. Have ready a shape of biscuit ice, or any other cream ice that may be preferred.
5. Take off a thick slice of the ice from the top carefully, and without breaking, so that it may be replaced on the ice.
6. Scoop out a large portion of the ice, which may be mixed with the gooseberry cream.
7. Fill up the hollow with it.
8. Cover the shape with the piece that was removed.
9. Serve.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A la Mode Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ten pounds of lean beef
- two ounces of beef dripping
- a couple of onions
- flour
- boiling water
- one drachm of black pepper
- two of allspice
- four bay leaves

## Directions

1. Take ten pounds of lean beef of any kind, cut it into small pieces
2. put two ounces of beef dripping and a couple of onions into a stewpan
3. As soon as it is hot, flour the meat and put it into the pan
4. keep stirring it about
5. When it has been some few minutes in the pan, dredge it with flour, and cover it with boiling water
6. Skim it when it boils, and put in one drachm of black pepper, two of allspice, and four bay leaves
7. Set the pan by the side of the fire, and let it simmer for three hours
8. When you find the meat sufficiently tender serve it in a tureen
9. when cold it ought to jelly
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A Luction, or a Rachael
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- thin nouilles paste
- beaten eggs
- white sugar
- a little fresh butter
- grated lemon-peel
- preserved cherries
- custard or cream
- brandy

## Directions

1. Make thin nouilles paste, cut into strips of about two inches wide
2. Leave it to dry
3. Boil the strips in a little water, and drain through a colander
4. When the water is strained off, mix it with beaten eggs, white sugar, a little fresh butter, and grated lemon-peel
5. Bake or boil in a shape lined with preserved cherries
6. When turned out, pour over a fine custard, or cream, flavoured with brandy, and sweetened to taste
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A Plain Bola
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three-quarters of a pound of white sugar
- three-quarters of a pound of fresh butter
- two eggs
- one pound and a half of flour
- three spoonfuls of yeast
- a little milk
- two ounces of citron
- powdered sugar
- cinnamon

## Directions

1. Take three-quarters of a pound of white sugar, three-quarters of a pound of fresh butter, two eggs, one pound and a half of flour, three spoonfuls of yeast, a little milk, and two ounces of citron, cut thin.
2. Mix into a light paste.
3. Bake in a tin.
4. Strew powdered sugar and cinnamon over it before baking.
5. The above ingredients are often baked in small tins or cups.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A Rich Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Two pounds and a half of Jones's patent flour
- A pint and a half of cold milk
- A pound of butter, oiled
- A pound of sifted sugar
- A pound of currants
- A quarter of a pound of lemon and orange-peel
- A little grated nutmeg, cinnamon, and allspice

## Directions

1. Put these ingredients in a round tin and bake an hour
2. This cake may be iced or ornamented
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A Scotch Haggis
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- haggis bag
- drought
- liver
- beef
- suet
- onions
- dried meal
- salt
- mixed spices
- water

## Directions

1. Make the haggis bag perfectly clean
2. Parboil the drought, boil the liver very well, so as it will grate, dry the meat before the fire
3. Mince the drought and a pretty large piece of beef very small
4. Grate about half the liver
5. Mince plenty of the suet and some onions small
6. Mix all these ingredients very well together, with a handful or two of the dried meal
7. Spread them on the table, and season them properly with salt and mixed spices
8. Take any of the scraps of beef that is left from mincing, and some of the water that boiled the drought, and make about a chopin (i.e., a quart) of good stock of it
9. Put all the haggis meat into the bag, and that broth in it
10. Sew the bag, but be sure to put out all the wind, before you sew it quite close
11. If you think the bag is thin, you may put it in a cloth
12. If it is a large haggis, it will take at least two hours boiling
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A White Fricandeau of Veal
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four or five pounds of breast of veal, or fillet from the shoulder
- veal stuffing
- water
- calf's foot
- one onion
- a blade of mace
- white pepper
- salt
- a sprig of parsley
- beaten yolks of four eggs
- juice of two lemons
- button mushrooms or pieces of celery

## Directions

1. Stuff the veal with a fine-flavoured veal stuffing
2. Put it into a stewpan with water sufficient to cover it; a calf's foot cut in pieces may be added
3. Season with one onion, a blade of mace, white pepper and salt, and a sprig of parsley
4. Stew the whole gently until the meat is quite tender
5. Skim and strain the gravy
6. Stir in the beaten yolks of four eggs and the juice of two lemons, previously mixed smoothly with a portion of the gravy
7. Button mushrooms or pieces of celery, stewed with the veal, may be added by way of varying the flavour
8. Garnish the dish with egg and forcemeat balls
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A White Fricandeau of Veal
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four or five pounds of breast of veal or fillet from shoulder
- veal stuffing
- water
- calf's foot
- onion
- blade of mace
- white pepper
- salt
- sprig of parsley

## Directions

1. Take four or five pounds of breast of veal or fillet from the shoulder
2. Stuff it with a fine-flavoured veal stuffing
3. Put it into a stewpan with water sufficient to cover it
4. A calf's foot cut in pieces is sometimes added
5. Season with one onion, a blade of mace, white pepper and salt, and a sprig of parsley
6. Stew the meat
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: A-la-mode Beef, or Sour Meat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- piece of ribs of beef, boned and filleted, or piece of round
- vinegar diluted with water
- onions
- pepper
- salt
- whole allspice
- three or four bay-leaves
- cupful of raspings

## Directions

1. Cover meat with vinegar diluted with water
2. Season with onions, pepper, salt, whole allspice, and three or four bay-leaves
3. Add a cupful of raspings
4. Let the whole stew gently for three or four hours, according to the weight of the meat
5. This dish is excellent when cold
6. A rump-steak stewed in the same way will be found exceedingly fine
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Absinthe
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four pounds of the tops of wormwood
- one ounce root of angelica
- one ounce calamus aromaticus
- one ounce aniseed
- one ounce leaves of dittany
- four gallons alcohol
- water
- oil of aniseed

## Directions

1. Take of the tops of wormwood, four pounds; root of angelica, calamus aromaticus, aniseed, leaves of dittany, of each one ounce
2. Macerate these substances during eight days
3. Add a little water
4. Distil by a gentle fire until two gallons are obtained
5. Reduce to a proof spirit
6. Add a few drops of the oil of aniseed
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Acidulated Drops
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pounded loaf sugar
- rasped orange-peel
- orange-juice

## Directions

1. Put a quantity of pounded loaf sugar, with some rasped orange-peel, into a saucepan
2. Add a quantity of orange-juice, to give it flavour
3. Dry the whole over the fire, until it is of a proper consistence
4. Drop the confection from the point of a knife on clean paper
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Aiguillettes de Ris de Veau
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three throat sweet-breads
- water
- one onion
- one carrot
- one turnip
- two bay-leaves
- one pint of stock
- salt
- pepper
- one ounce of butter
- white sauce
- yolks of eggs
- juice of a lemon
- egg
- bread crumbs
- hot lard

## Directions

1. Boil sweet-breads in water ten minutes
2. Pour off the liquor
3. Add one onion, one carrot, one turnip, two bay-leaves, and a pint of stock
4. Simmer twenty minutes
5. Cut them into round pieces a little larger than a shilling
6. Season with salt and pepper
7. Put them into a stewpan with an ounce of butter
8. Dress them white
9. Dip each piece with a fork into a mixture of white sauce, yolks of eggs, and the juice of a lemon
10. When cold, run the skewer through the pieces, putting several on each skewer
11. Dip them into egg well beaten, then into bread crumbs
12. Fry in hot lard
13. Serve on a napkin
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ale Posset
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of new milk
- a slice of toasted bread
- a quart of ale
- nutmeg
- ginger

## Directions

1. Boil a pint of new milk with a slice of toasted bread
2. Sweeten a quart of ale and pour it into a basin with nutmeg and ginger
3. Add the boiling milk to it
4. When the head rises, serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Aleberry
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ale
- spice
- sugar
- toasted bread

## Directions

1. Boil ale with spice, sugar, and toasted bread
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Alma Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of butter
- half a pound of powdered sugar
- half a pound of flour
- a quarter of a pound of currants
- four eggs

## Directions

1. Beat half a pound of butter to a cream
2. Strew in by degrees half a pound of powdered sugar and mix both together
3. Dredge in half a pound of flour and add a quarter of a pound of currants
4. Moisten with four eggs well beaten
5. When all the ingredients are well stirred and mixed, butter a mould that will hold the mixture
6. Tie it down with a cloth and put the pudding into boiling water
7. Boil for a couple of hours
8. When turned out, put on it some powdered sugar and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Almond Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ounces of sweet almonds
- two ounces of bitter almonds
- one pound of loaf sugar
- white of an egg

## Directions

1. Blanch and beat almonds till smooth
2. Add one pound of loaf sugar
3. Mix all together
4. Beat the white of an egg to a froth and mix it with the rest
5. Bake in lumps on wafer-paper to prevent their sticking
6. A quick oven is necessary
7. They should be done of a light brown
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Almond Puffs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of sweet almonds
- four ounces of bitter almonds
- two ounces of butter
- two table-spoonfuls of flour
- two ounces of sugar

## Directions

1. Blanch and pound two ounces of sweet and four ounces of bitter almonds
2. Melt two ounces of butter
3. Dredge in two table-spoonfuls of flour and add two ounces of sugar and the pounded almonds
4. Beat the mixture well and bake in a moderate oven
5. Turn them out on a dish with the bottom of the puff uppermost and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Almond Sponge Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of blanched almonds
- whites of four eggs
- one pound of sifted white sugar
- grated lemon-peel
- yolks of twelve eggs
- whites of twelve eggs
- a quarter of a pound of flour

## Directions

1. Pound in a mortar one pound of blanched almonds, with the whites of four eggs
2. Add one pound of sifted white sugar, some grated lemon-peel, and the yolks of twelve eggs
3. Work them well together
4. Beat to a froth the whites of the dozen eggs
5. Stir them into the other ingredients, with a quarter of a pound of flour
6. Put the mixture in a mould
7. Bake an hour
8. Take the cake out of the mould, and set it on a sieve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Almondigos Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a knuckle of veal
- a calf's foot
- two quarts of water
- a blade of mace
- a bunch of sweet herbs
- a turnip
- white pepper
- salt
- balls of forced meat and egg-balls
- the yolks of four eggs
- a dessert-spoonful of lemon-juice
- three ounces of sweet almonds
- a spoonful of powdered white sugar

## Directions

1. Put a knuckle of veal and a calf's foot into two quarts of water, with a blade of mace and a bunch of sweet herbs, a turnip, a little white pepper, and salt
2. When sufficiently done, strain and skim it, and add balls of forced meat and egg-balls
3. A quarter of an hour before serving beat up the yolks of four eggs, with a dessert-spoonful of lemon-juice, and three ounces of sweet almonds, blanched, and with a spoonful of powdered white sugar
4. Beat this mixture into the soup until it thickens, taking care to prevent its curdling
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: American Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Flathead
- 1/2 pint Brown Sauce
- 3 oz. Fish Forcemeat
- 1 oz. Dripping

## Directions

1. Make a forcemeat and sew it into the fish
2. Rub some dripping over a baking sheet, truss the fish into shape, and lay it on
3. Rub the rest of the dripping on to a piece of paper, cover the fish carefully, bake in rather a hot oven for half an hour or longer, according to size
4. Take off the paper, dish it, and pour round a nice brown sauce
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: American Mincemeat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pieces of vegetables and cold meat
- gravy or dripping
- a few apples
- a little sage
- slices of toasted bread

## Directions

1. Mash the vegetables and chop the meat fine
2. Warm it with gravy or dripping
3. Fry and slice a few apples and mix with it, with a little sage
4. After it has been sufficiently warmed, lay it on slices of toasted bread and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: American Oysters
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bottle of Oysters
- 1 pint of Milk
- 6 Soda Biscuits
- 1 oz. Butter
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Put the milk and butter into a saucepan; when it boils put in the oysters and simmer for five minutes.
2. Season with pepper and salt; break up the biscuits and throw them in.
3. Boil up and pour into a deep dish, and it is ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: American Puffets for Tea or Breakfast
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of cold milk, that has been boiled
- a quarter of a pint of yeast
- half a pound of butter
- half a pound of sugar
- five eggs

## Directions

1. Make into a stiff paste
2. Put on the baking-tin to rise
3. Bake in the usual manner without taking off
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Amnastich
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of rice
- one quart of strong gravy
- onion
- cloves
- bunch of sweet herbs
- chicken
- forcemeat
- yolks of four eggs
- juice of a lemon
- saffron

## Directions

1. Stew gently one pint of rice in one quart of strong gravy till it begins to swell
2. Add an onion stuck with cloves, a bunch of sweet herbs, and a chicken stuffed with forcemeat
3. Let it stew with the rice till thoroughly done
4. Take it up and stir in the rice, the yolks of four eggs, and the juice of a lemon
5. Serve the fowl in the same dish with the rice, which should be coloured to a fine yellow with saffron
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: An Aitch or H Bone of Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- aitch bone of beef
- carrots or parsnips

## Directions

1. Boil like the round
2. The slower it boils the better it will look, and the more tender it will be
3. Serve with carrots or parsnips
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: An Excellent Recipe for a Fruit Charlotte
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fine picked strawberries
- liquid jelly
- a pottle of strawberries
- half a pound of white sugar
- a pint of whipped cream
- one ounce and a half of dissolved isinglass

## Directions

1. Line a jelly-mould with fine picked strawberries, which must be first dipped into some liquid jelly, to make them adhere closely.
2. Take a pottle of strawberries, mix them with half a pound of white sugar.
3. Rub this through a sieve, and add to it a pint of whipped cream and one ounce and a half of dissolved isinglass.
4. Pour it into the mould, which must be immersed in ice until ready to serve.
5. Carefully turn out on the dish and garnish according to fancy.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: An Irish Stew
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of mutton
- four pounds of potatoes
- six onions
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Take two pounds of mutton cut into fair pieces, and four pounds of potatoes
2. Peel the potatoes and cut them in half
3. Peel and slice six onions
4. First put in the bottom of the saucepan a layer of potatoes, and then some of the meat and onions, a small spoonful of pepper, and two of salt
5. This dish must simmer, but not boil, for a couple of hours
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: An Unexceptionable Devil
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cold turkey, fowl, or ducks
- butter
- salad oil
- mushroom ketchup
- Harvey or Worcester sauce
- superlative and Chili vinegar
- mustard
- pepper
- salt
- chutney
- cayenne
- pickles

## Directions

1. Cut into joints cold turkey, fowl, or ducks
2. Put the joints in a pan, with a moderate quantity of butter, and the best salad oil, with the following sauces: mushroom ketchup, Harvey or Worcester, superlative and Chili vinegar, along with mustard, pepper, salt, and chutney, a moderate amount of cayenne, a few pickles cut in small pieces
3. Warm thoroughly, and serve hot, both from the fire and the sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Anchovies
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- anchovies
- parsley or other minced garnish
- hard egg
- oil

## Directions

1. In the middle of a plate place the anchovies, cut in fillets, and lay them transversely
2. Around them place a wreath of parsley or other minced garnish
3. Upon them place quarters of hard egg
4. Serve with oil
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Appert's Mode of Preserving Fruit or Vegetables
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Wide-mouthed glass bottles
- Finest corks
- Cold water
- Resin

## Directions

1. Put the article into wide-mouthed glass bottles
2. Use the finest corks and ensure the bottles are dry and when corked, air-tight
3. Put the corked bottles into a saucepan, fill it with cold water, and set it on the fire
4. Boil the bottled articles in the bain marie
5. Remove the saucepan from the fire and let it get almost cold before taking out the bottles
6. Dip the cork in resin
7. Put the bottles in the cellar
8. Fill the bottles to within two inches of the necks only, and not even so high if the bottle only contains liquid
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Apple and Onion Compound
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- equal quantities of apples and onions
- cayenne
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Mince fine equal quantities of apples and onions, after peeling both and coring the former
2. Add a little cayenne, and sufficient vinegar to cover the mixture
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Apple Bread (French)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- apples mashed
- yeast

## Directions

1. Take apples and flour, in the proportion of two parts of flour to one of apples mashed well together
2. Add a quantity of yeast, and allow it to rise a sufficient time
3. Use very little water if the apples are juicy
4. Bake in long loaves
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Apple Fritters
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Apples
- Frying Batter
- Hot Fat
- Sugar
- Lemon

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up the apples into rounds, take out the core with a small round cutter.
2. Make frying batter by directions given elsewhere, and flavour with lemon juice.
3. Dip in the pieces of apple, plunge into plenty of hot fat, and fry till a good colour.
4. Drain on kitchen paper, pile high on a dish, and sprinkle well with sugar.
5. Serve very hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Apple Jelly
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pound and a half of apples
- quart of water
- half an ounce of isinglass per pint of juice
- juice and peel of a lemon
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Pare and core a pound and a half of apples
2. Put them in a quart of water
3. Boil them till the apples are in pulp
4. Put them into a hair sieve to drain
5. To every pint of juice add half an ounce of isinglass, the juice and peel of a lemon, sugar to taste
6. Boil ten minutes
7. Pass it through a flannel bag into a mould
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Apple Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six baking apples
- a table-spoonful of cold water
- butter
- sugar

## Directions

1. Pare and core six baking apples
2. put them into a saucepan, with a table-spoonful of cold water
3. let them simmer until done
4. add a little butter and sugar to sweeten them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Apple Trifle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- apple pulp
- sugar
- lemon
- half a pint of milk
- half a pint of cream
- yolk of an egg

## Directions

1. Place apple pulp flavoured with sugar and lemon at the bottom of the trifle-dish
2. Mix half a pint of milk, half a pint of cream, and the yolk of an egg
3. Give it a scald over the fire, with a little sugar in it
4. Let it cool
5. Cover the apples with it
6. Put a whip on the top
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Apple Trifle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pulp of apples
- sugar
- lemon
- half a pint of milk
- half a pint of cream
- the yolk of an egg

## Directions

1. Place a pulp of apples at the bottom of the trifle-dish, flavoured with sugar and lemon
2. Mix half a pint of milk, half a pint of cream, and the yolk of an egg
3. Give it a scald over the fire, with a little sugar in it
4. Let it cool, then cover the apples with it, and put a whip on the top
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Appleade
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- apples
- boiling water
- sugar
- ice

## Directions

1. Cut apples in slices — the more juicy the better — and pour on them boiling water
2. Strain and sweeten
3. Cool with ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Apples a la Cremona
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- apples
- sugar (half their weight)
- lemon-peel
- three or four table-spoonfuls of cider or perry

## Directions

1. Cut the apples into small squares, and strewed over with half their weight in sugar and a few pieces of lemon-peel
2. After remaining covered up until the next day, lightly drop into a small stewpan, with three or four table-spoonfuls of cider or perry
3. Simmer gently until they become clear
4. Take them out, and when cold build in a wall-like form round a small china or glass dish
5. Pour the syrup into the middle, and place the strips of lemon on the top
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Apples and Rice
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 large Apples
- 2 oz. Rice
- 2 oz. Sugar
- 1 tablespoonful Jam
- 1 Egg
- ½ pint Milk

## Directions

1. Peel the apples and scoop out the core and fill in with jam ; put into a pie-dish and bake till the apples are soft.
2. While they are baking, boil the rice and milk together till the rice is soft and the milk absorbed.
3. Beat in the egg and sugar, pour over the apples ; brush over with milk, and bake till a nice colour.
4. Serve either hot or cold.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Apples and Tapioca
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 apples
- 1.5 oz. tapioca
- 1 lemon
- 2 oz. sugar
- 1.5 pints water
- A few drops of cochineal

## Directions

1. Peel and quarter the apples and remove the core, put them into a saucepan with the lemon juice, sugar, and a spoonful of water, and stew till soft but not broken.
2. Place them in a glass dish.
3. Wash the tapioca in cold water, put it in a saucepan, pour over it 1.5 pints of water, and stir till it boils.
4. Cook it till quite clear, sweeten and flavour with a few drops of lemon juice, and colour with cochineal.
5. Pour over the apples and put away till cold; it is then ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Apricot Drink
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of apricot juice
- half a pound of sugar
- one ounce of tartaric acid

## Directions

1. Take one pint of apricot juice and add half a pound of sugar
2. Add one ounce of tartaric acid
3. Bottle and cork for use
4. Use it with water and a small quantity of carbonate of soda
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Arrowroot Blanc-mange
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of new milk
- a quarter of a pound of arrowroot
- a little cinnamon water or lemon

## Directions

1. To a quart of new milk add a quarter of a pound of arrowroot, sweetened to taste
2. Add a little cinnamon water or lemon for flavour
3. Stir it well on the fire, and boil for about fifteen minutes
4. Pour it into a mould to cool
5. When served add milk, cream, jam, or a custard over it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Artichoke Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Artichokes
- 2 Onions
- 1½ pints Milk
- 2 quarts Bone Stock (White)
- 1 tablespoonful Vinegar
- 1 tablespoonful Lemon Juice
- 1 doz. White Peppercorns

## Directions

1. Peel the artichokes and lay them in vinegar and water for an hour; this will make them a good colour.
2. Mix up half a pint of the milk with the stock, and boil the artichokes, onions, and peppercorns in this for an hour.
3. Rub through a hair sieve with a wooden spoon.
4. Stir in the milk and some salt, pour back into the saucepan and stir until it boils.
5. If the artichokes do not thicken the soup sufficiently, sprinkle in a little sago or semolina when it is returned to the saucepan.
6. Serve with fried bread.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Artichokes, to Boil
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Artichokes
- 1/2 pint White Sauce
- Vinegar
- Milk
- Salt

## Directions

1. Wash and peel the artichokes and put them into some water; add a tablespoonful of vinegar and leave them for half an hour.
2. Drain the water all away and put them into a saucepan, cover with cold water; add one gill of milk and some salt.
3. Bring to the boil and cook slowly for about an hour.
4. Take half a pint of the liquor in which the artichokes were boiled, and make a sauce; dish them and pour this over.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Artificial Yeast
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two parts of fine flour of pale barley malt
- one part of wheat flour
- fifty pounds of the mixture
- one hundred quarts of cold water
- one quart of good, fresh beer-yeast

## Directions

1. Mix two parts of fine flour of pale barley malt with one part of wheat flour
2. Stir fifty pounds of this mixture gradually into one hundred quarts of cold water, with a wooden spatula, till it forms a smooth pap
3. Put this pap into a copper over a slow fire
4. Stir it well till the temperature rise to fully 150° to 160°, when a partial formation of sugar will take place, but this sweetening must not be pushed too far
5. Turn out the paste into a flat cooler, and stir it from time to time
6. As soon as the wort has fallen to 59° Fahr., transfer it to a tub, and add for every fifty quarts of it one quart of good, fresh beer-yeast, which will throw the wort into brisk fermentation in the course of twelve hours
7. Occasionally stir the preparation
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ash Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- meal
- water

## Directions

1. Scald the meal, and put it in little heaps to cool
2. Mix it with more water (warm) into dough, and mould it into flat cakes rather larger than a breakfast saucer
3. Open a place in the side of a wood fire on the hearth, and put in the cakes, each between two cabbage leaves
4. Lay them on the hot hearth, sprinkle some ashes lightly over first
5. Put hot coals on the top, and if these appear to cool fast, remove them from time to time, and replace them with hotter coals from the fire
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Asparagus
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- asparagus
- salt
- toast

## Directions

1. Tie asparagus in bundles of the same size and length
2. Put into boiling water with a little salt
3. When done, take it out of the pan
4. Serve on toast
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Asparagus a la Francaise
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- asparagus
- boiled onion
- salt
- pepper
- egg yolk
- toasted bread
- melted butter

## Directions

1. Boil some asparagus
2. Chop the heads and tender parts of the stalks together with a boiled onion into small pieces
3. Add a little salt and pepper and the beaten yolk of an egg
4. Beat up and serve on sippets of toasted bread with melted butter poured over
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Athol Brose
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two wine-glassfuls of Scotch whisky
- one wine-glassful of heather-honey
- one well-beaten egg

## Directions

1. Add two wine-glassfuls of Scotch whisky to a wine-glassful of heather-honey
2. Mix well, and stir in a well-beaten egg
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Aunt Nelly's Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of suet
- half a pound of flour
- half a pound of treacle
- a few shreds of candied lemon-peel
- three table-spoonfuls of cream
- two eggs

## Directions

1. Chop half a pound of suet fine
2. Mix with it half a pound of flour, half a pound of treacle, a few shreds of candied lemon-peel, three table-spoonfuls of cream, and two eggs well beaten
3. Beat the ingredients well
4. Put it into a buttered basin
5. Tie it down with a cloth and boil for a couple of hours
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Baba
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- brioche paste
- the size of a nut of saffron in powder
- a quarter of a pound of the best raisins stoned
- double the quantity of currants
- sugar
- some slices of candied citron cut in small pieces

## Directions

1. Make with the brioche paste
2. Add thereto the size of a nut of saffron in powder, a quarter of a pound of the best raisins stoned, and double the quantity of currants, sugar, and some slices of candied citron cut in small pieces
3. Mix and knead all together
4. Let the paste be soft and well mixed
5. Put it into a buttered pan, and let it stand eight hours in winter and four in summer
6. Without touching it, let it bake in a slow oven for an hour
7. It will be done enough when it has taken a good colour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bachelor's Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ounces of apples
- four ounces of currants
- four ounces of grated bread
- two ounces of sugar
- three eggs

## Directions

1. Pare, core, and mince four ounces of apples
2. Add to these four ounces of currants well cleaned, four ounces of grated bread, and two ounces of sugar
3. Whisk three eggs and beat them up with the rest of the ingredients
4. When all are thoroughly mixed put the pudding in a buttered basin
5. Tie it down with a cloth and boil for a couple of hours
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bachelors' Buttons
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 oz. Flour
- 2 oz. Sugar
- 1/4 teaspoonful Carbonate of Soda
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 teaspoonful Cream of Tartar
- 6 drops Essence of Almonds
- 1 Egg

## Directions

1. Rub the butter into the flour
2. Stir in the sugar, carbonate of soda, and cream of tartar
3. Mix into a stiff dough with the egg and flavouring
4. Roll into small balls about the size of a marble
5. Toss in coarse sugar
6. Put on to a greased baking sheet, and bake from five to eight minutes
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Bacon with Green Peas or Beans
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pint of well-boiled peas or beans
- six spoonfuls of brown sauce
- three spoonfuls of brown gravy
- two onions
- parsley
- quarter of a pound of lean bacon
- ounce of butter

## Directions

1. Put a pint of well-boiled peas or beans into a stewpan, with six spoonfuls of brown sauce, three of brown gravy, two onions, and a little parsley
2. Let it boil ten minutes
3. Have ready about a quarter of a pound of lean bacon cut into pieces, add it to the peas or beans
4. Season with an ounce of butter, and stew fifteen minutes, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Badminton
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a moderate-sized cucumber
- four ounces of powdered sugar
- a little nutmeg
- a bottle of claret
- a bottle of soda-water

## Directions

1. Peel half a moderate-sized cucumber, and put it into a mug, silver or crockery
2. with four ounces of powdered sugar, a little nutmeg, and a bottle of claret
3. When the sugar is dissolved, add a bottle of soda-water, and it is then fit for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Baked Apples
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- apples
- fresh butter
- powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Scoop out the inside of the apple by the stalk, leaving the eye impierced
2. Fill the hole with fresh butter
3. Place the apple on a plate (not tin), crown downwards
4. Sprinkle it thickly with powdered sugar
5. Set it to bake very slowly in an oven
6. Eat it before it cools
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Baked Bream And Egg Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Bream
- 1/2 pint White Sauce
- 1 Egg
- Parsley
- Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. Wash the bream, rub some dripping on to a baking sheet, lay on it the fish, squeeze over a few drops of lemon juice; cover with a piece of paper well rubbed with dripping, and bake in a moderate oven for about twenty minutes or longer, if the fish is large.
2. Remove the skin and fins, and put them on the dish; pour over the white sauce, which should be just thick enough to coat the fish.
3. Chop the parsley finely, and boil the egg hard, cut it in half, and either chop the yolk or rub it through a sieve, and chop the white.
4. Arrange these in alternate rows all over the fish, and garnish with a few lemon slices.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Baked Chop
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Chop
- Pepper and Salt
- Butter

## Directions

1. Choose a nice loin chop with an undercut.
2. Rub a little butter in a soup plate, lay in the chop, cover with another plate, and stand in a cool oven for an hour.
3. Put on a very hot plate and pour over the gravy which has run from it.
4. Serve very hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Baked Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 Mullet or Jew-fish
- 2 oz. Bread Crumbs
- 1 oz. Butter
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- 1 teaspoonful Sweet Herbs
- 1/2 Lemon
- 2 oz. Suet

## Directions

1. Split open the fish and remove the head and backbone, wash well in cold water and dry in a cloth.
2. Chop the parsley, herbs, and suet, and mix these together; add half the crumbs, the rind of half a lemon, and pepper and salt.
3. Butter a baking tin, lay on a fish skin downwards.
4. On this place a layer of seasoning, a little lemon juice, and a few pieces of butter; on this another fish with the cut part next the seasoning.
5. Do the rest in the same way, piling one on top of another; over all put the rest of the crumbs and butter, bake in a moderate oven for half an hour.
6. Slip into a hot dish, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Baked Lemon Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four eggs
- four ounces of sugar
- a quarter of a pound of butter
- a lemon
- paste

## Directions

1. Beat four eggs to a froth
2. Mix them with four ounces of sugar and a quarter of a pound of warmed butter
3. Mix well together, putting in the strained juice and grated rind of a lemon
4. Line a shallow dish with paste and bake in a moderate oven for half an hour
5. Turn the pudding out and strew over it sifted sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Baked Onions
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Onion
- 1 pint Thick Gravy
- 1/2 pint Water
- Pepper and Salt
- Butter

## Directions

1. Peel the onions, put them on in cold water, and bring to the boil.
2. Strain the water off, butter a baking dish, put in the onions, pour in the water, cover with a plate, and stand in a moderate oven.
3. Stew until soft, place in a hot dish and pour over them, either a nice gravy thickened with a little butter and flour, or some plain melted butter.
4. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Baked Rice Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of rice
- a well-made custard

## Directions

1. Boil six ounces of rice.
2. When done, put it in a dish and pour on it a well-made custard.
3. Bake quickly in an oven.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Baked Tomatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- tomatoes
- bread crumbs
- butter
- mustard
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Slice up the tomatoes, spread with a very little made mustard
2. Season some brown bread crumbs with pepper and salt, and sprinkle the slices well
3. Put into a buttered dish and bake till soft
4. Serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Baked Vegetable Marrow
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Vegetable Marrow
- 1/2 pint Gravy
- 1 oz. Dripping
- Flour
- Salt
- Pepper

## Directions

1. Peel the marrow and cut into pieces, remove the seeds, put on to a baking sheet with some beef dripping, and bake till soft and rather brown.
2. Thicken a little gravy with some flour, and season and flavour it nicely; dish the marrow and pour this sauce over.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Banana and Orange Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- ripe bananas
- oranges
- sugar

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up some ripe bananas and oranges, removing the pips from the oranges, but saving the juice
2. Take a deep glass dish, lay at the bottom some bananas, then a layer of oranges
3. Sprinkle well with sugar, then some more bananas and oranges and sugar, until all the materials are used up
4. Cover and let it stand for an hour, then serve as a sweet
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Banana Souffle
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Bananas
- 2 oz. Sugar
- 1/2 pint Milk
- 2 Eggs

## Directions

1. Choose ripe bananas, peel and slice them up, and lay them in a glass dish
2. Sprinkle with sugar
3. Make a custard with the milk and yolks of the eggs by directions for boiled custard
4. Flavour with a pinch of ginger, and pour it over the bananas
5. Let it stand till quite cold
6. Whip the whites to a very stiff froth and heap them on top
7. Sprinkle with sugar, and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Bananas Stewed
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 doz. Green Bananas
- Lemon Juice
- 1/2 pint Brown Sauce
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Peel the bananas and put them in boiling water to which a few drops of lemon juice have been added; boil them for half an hour, or until soft.
2. Make sauce by directions already given, flavour with lemon juice, pepper, and salt.
3. Strain all the water from the bananas, dish, and pour over the sauce.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Banbury Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a gill of warm milk
- a pound of flour
- three spoonfuls of yeast
- half a pound of currants
- a quarter of a pound of orange and lemon-peel
- an ounce of mixed spice
- half a pound of honey

## Directions

1. Put a gill of warm milk to a pound of flour, and three spoonfuls of yeast
2. When it has worked a little, mix with it half a pound of currants, washed and cleaned
3. Add a quarter of a pound of orange and lemon-peel, cut small, and an ounce of mixed spice
4. Mix the whole together with half a pound of honey
5. Roll out some paste thin
6. Make it in the form of an oval
7. Put into each the quantity of the mixture
8. Close it round and soft
9. Sugar over
10. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Banbury Cakes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Pastry
- 1 oz. Currants
- 1 oz. Raisins
- Half a Lemon
- Half an Orange
- 1 oz. Cake or Bread Crumbs
- 1 oz. Sugar

## Directions

1. Stone the raisins and chop them lightly, put them into a basin with the currants cleaned, the sugar, and the cake or bread crumbs.
2. Mix together, grate over the rind of half a lemon, and half an orange.
3. Strain in the juice, and let it stand for an hour.
4. Roll out the pastry and cut into rounds about three inches long.
5. Lay a little of the mixture in the centre, close over the pastry, turn the cake over, flatten it out in the middle.
6. Brush over with sugar, and bake in rather a quick oven.
7. Serve warm.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Bang
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of cider
- one pint of warm ale
- sugar
- nutmeg
- ginger
- one wine-glass of whisky

## Directions

1. Take a pint of cider, and add to a pint of warm ale
2. Sweeten with sugar
3. Grate in some nutmeg and ginger, and add a wine-glass of whisky
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Barberry Tart
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- barberries
- sugar
- a pound of sugar to a pound of fruit
- paste

## Directions

1. Pull the barberries from the stalks and put the fruit into a stone jar in boiling water
2. Let it simmer until the fruit is soft
3. Put it into a preserving-pan with the sugar (a pound of sugar to a pound of fruit) and boil gently for fifteen minutes
4. Line a tartlet-pan with paste and bake it
5. When the paste is cold fill in with fruit
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Barley Bannocks
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- barley-meal
- water
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Mix barley-meal with water and a little salt
2. Roll them out into a paste, three quarters of an inch thick
3. Divide it into cakes
4. Bake in an oven or on the girdle of a light brown colour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Barley Sugar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- clarified syrup
- rasped lemon-peel
- butter

## Directions

1. Put clarified syrup, containing rasped lemon-peel, into a saucepan with a lip as for melting butter
2. Boil it to caramel height, carefully skimming as it boils
3. Have ready a marble slab, well buttered
4. Pour the syrup on it of the thickness required for the sticks of barley sugar
5. Twist every stick at each end when hot, and it will assume the form required
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bath Buns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of butter
- a pound of flour
- six eggs
- two table-spoonfuls of yeast
- half a cupful of lukewarm milk
- six ounces of white sugar
- a few carraway-seeds
- six or eight carraway comfits

## Directions

1. Rub half a pound of butter into a pound of flour
2. Beat up six eggs, and add them to the flour, with two table-spoonfuls of yeast
3. Mix them together with half a cupful of lukewarm milk
4. Set it to rise
5. Add six ounces of white sugar, and a few carraway-seeds
6. Mould into buns
7. Put on the top of each six or eight carraway comfits
8. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Batter Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three eggs
- a salt-spoon of salt
- about four ounces of flour
- half a pint of milk
- a little powdered ginger

## Directions

1. Break three eggs into a basin, with a salt-spoon of salt, beat them well
2. add about four ounces of flour, and, by degrees, half a pint of milk and a little powdered ginger
3. Boil in a cloth or mould well floured, or bake
4. Serve with sweet sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beans
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beans
- bacon
- parsley
- butter

## Directions

1. Beans are sometimes boiled with a small piece of bacon
2. If by themselves, they need the addition of parsley and butter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bechamel Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a piece of butter
- onions
- a carrot
- some parsley and mushrooms
- three spoonfuls of flour
- half a pint of cream
- salt
- white pepper
- nutmeg
- a small piece of fresh butter

## Directions

1. Put into a saucepan a piece of butter, onions, and a carrot shred, some parsley and mushrooms
2. Set it on the fire
3. Put in three spoonfuls of flour, half a pint of cream, salt, white pepper, and nutmeg
4. Keep it stirred until it boils
5. Let it simmer for half an hour
6. Strain it
7. When ready to serve add a small piece of fresh butter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bedford Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Dry crusts of bread
- 1 pint of milk
- 1 tablespoonful jam
- 2 eggs
- 15 oz. sugar
- Grated lemon peel

## Directions

1. Soak the bread in cold water till quite soft, put it into a cloth and squeeze all the water out of it; turn into a basin and beat it smooth with a spoon.
2. Beat in the yolks of the eggs, sugar, milk, and a little grated lemon peel.
3. Pour into a pie dish and bake till quite firm, then take from the oven and spread the jam on the top.
4. Whip the whites to a stiff froth and spread over the jam; put back in the oven for a few minutes till brown, then sprinkle with sugar and serve either hot or cold.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beef and Beans
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- piece of brisket of beef
- water
- one quartern of fresh beans
- two onions
- pepper
- salt
- dessert-spoonful of flour
- coarse brown sugar
- large tea-cupful of vinegar

## Directions

1. Cover beef with water
2. When boiling, skim off the fat
3. Add one quartern of fresh beans cut small and two onions cut in quarters
4. Season with pepper and salt
5. When nearly done, take a dessert-spoonful of flour, one of coarse brown sugar, and a large tea-cupful of vinegar
6. Mix them together and stir in with the beans
7. Continue stirring for about half an hour longer
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef Broth
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- part of a leg of beef
- half a gallon of water
- a few blades of mace
- a bunch of parsley
- a crust of bread

## Directions

1. Take part of a leg of beef and crack the bone in two or three places
2. Put it into half a gallon of water and let it simmer
3. Skim well and add a few blades of mace, a bunch of parsley, and a crust of bread
4. Let it simmer for two hours and serve with toasted bread
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef en Miroton
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- onions
- butter
- flour
- broth
- white wine
- boiled beef
- mustard
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Slice a few onions, fry them with a piece of butter and a little flour, until they are of a fine brown colour
2. Add a little broth, a glass of white wine, and stew the whole until the onions are well done
3. Add boiled beef cut in small thin slices, called miroton
4. Stew them until they are well flavoured with onion
5. Flavour them with mustard or a dash of vinegar, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef Essence
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pound gravy beef
- Salt

## Directions

1. Mince the meat very small
2. Put it into a brown baking jar and cover down with a closely-fitting lid or with brown paper
3. Stand in a saucepan of boiling water for one hour
4. Pour off the essence and add a little salt
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beef Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- slice of bacon or ham
- four or five pounds of gravy beef
- carrot
- onion
- couple of cloves
- head of celery
- quart of water

## Directions

1. Put the bacon or ham, gravy beef cut into pieces, carrot, onion, cloves, and celery into a stewpan
2. Put a quart of water to it, or a little more
3. Let it simmer for three hours
4. Strain through a tamis
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef Hams
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a leg of beef
- saltpetre
- salt
- an ounce of bay-salt
- an ounce of saltpetre
- a pound of coarse brown sugar
- a pound of common salt
- bran or sawdust

## Directions

1. Select a leg of beef and rub it well with saltpetre and salt
2. Make a pickle of an ounce of bay-salt, an ounce of saltpetre, a pound of coarse brown sugar, and a pound of common salt
3. Rub this well in every day for a week
4. Rub it every other day for about three weeks
5. Roll it in bran or sawdust and smoke
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef Olives
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- slices of beef
- forcemeat of crumbs of bread
- shalot
- suet or dripping
- pepper
- salt
- gravy
- lemon

## Directions

1. Cut slices of beef of about a quarter of an inch thick
2. Lay on each piece a forcemeat of crumbs of bread, shalot, a little suet or dripping, pepper, and salt
3. Roll each piece separate, and fasten with a skewer
4. Put them into a stew-pan, with some gravy, and stew till tender
5. Serve with slices of lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef Olives
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Rump Steak
- 1 lb. Veal Seasoning
- 12 Peppercorns
- 1 pint Stock
- 1 Onion
- 1 Carrot

## Directions

1. Cut up the steak into thin slices about three inches long and two broad.
2. Shape the seasoning into small corks, roll a piece up in each slice of steak, thread them on a skewer and lay them in a saucepan.
3. Pour in the stock, add the peppercorns and vegetables, bring to the boil, simmer very gently for one hour and a half.
4. Place the olives on a hot dish and draw out the skewers, remove the fat, boil up the gravy, season and flavour to taste, and pour round.
5. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beef or Veal en Roulade
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef, veal, or mutton
- anchovies
- parsley
- basil
- salt
- pepper
- grated nutmeg
- bacon
- melted butter
- flour
- bouillon
- lemon

## Directions

1. Take a piece of beef, veal, or mutton, and cut it into thin slices
2. Beat them, and spread one of the sides of each slice with anchovies, parsley, basil, the whole chopped very fine
3. Add salt, pepper, and grated nutmeg
4. Cut as many pieces of bacon as you have slices of meat, and of the same length
5. Place them on the edge of the meat, and roll up the whole, so that the slice of bacon may be in the centre
6. Tie them up with a string, and put them into a saucepan with some melted butter
7. When they are well browned take them out, put into the fat a spoonful of flour, let it brown, and then add some bouillon, pepper, and salt
8. Put the roulades (from which you have taken the string) into this sauce to finish
9. Add a few slices of lemon, and serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef Stock
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Leg of beef
- Vegetables
- Cold water
- Dessertspoonful of salt
- 2 dozen peppercorns
- One onion
- Two carrots

## Directions

1. Have the butcher chop the bone small
2. Remove the marrow from the bones and cut the meat into small pieces
3. Put all together into a stock pot or digester
4. Cover well with cold water and bring it to the boil
5. Add a dessertspoonful of salt
6. Remove the scum carefully when it is thrown up
7. Put in 2 dozen peppercorns, an onion, and two carrots
8. Draw away from the fire and let it boil steadily for five or six hours or longer
9. Strain off through a colander and stand away in a cool place
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beef Tea No. 1
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pound gravy beef
- 1 pint water
- Salt to taste

## Directions

1. Remove all fat and skin from the meat
2. Put it twice through a sausage machine or scrape it into a pulp with a sharp knife
3. Pour over the cold water and let it stand for an hour
4. Pour it into a brown baking jar and put it into a cool oven
5. Keep it below boiling point for an hour or longer according to the heat of the oven
6. It should look brown, thick, and rich when sufficiently cooked
7. Strain through a colander and add salt to taste
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beef Tea, or Brown Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of lean beef
- cold water
- salt
- roasted onions or burnt sugar

## Directions

1. Take one pound of lean beef, free from fat, and separated from the bones, in the finely-chopped state in which it is used for sausages or mincemeat
2. Uniformly mix it with its own weight of cold water
3. Slowly heat to boiling
4. Boil briskly for a minute or two
5. Strain through a sieve, from the coagulated albumen and the fibrin
6. Mix with salt, and other additions by which soup is usually seasoned
7. Tinge somewhat darker by means of roasted onions, or burnt sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef Triples
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Roast Beef
- 4 oz. Bread Crumbs
- 1 teaspoonful Onion
- 1 Egg
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley or Horse-radish
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Mince the beef and onion very finely, and mix it with the bread crumbs, pepper, and salt.
2. Add either some chopped parsley or finely scraped horse-radish; mix thoroughly.
3. Moisten with an egg well beaten, and if very dry a spoonful of gravy or milk.
4. Butter some small cups or moulds, fill them with this mixture, and bake for about half an hour.
5. Garnish with sprigs of parsley, and serve with them some horse-radish sauce or brown gravy.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beef à la Mode
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 lbs. Leg of Beef or Silverside
- 1 Calf's Foot
- 2 Onions
- 2 Carrots
- 1 Turnip
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 40 Peppercorns
- 1 blade of Mace
- 6 Cloves
- Salt

## Directions

1. Have the foot well chopped up, put it on in cold water, bring it to the boil.
2. Let it boil for five minutes, then take it up and scrape and wash it well.
3. Lay it in a stewpan with the beef cut into pieces.
4. Cover with cold water and bring to the boil.
5. Put in the spices tied in a piece of muslin, and let it simmer very gently for three hours.
6. Slice up the vegetables and put them in, and continue simmering altogether for about five hours.
7. Take up the foot, take out all the bones, and cut into pieces.
8. Put back the meat of the foot into the saucepan.
9. Take out the spices, season with salt, remove the fat, boil up, and serve.
10. This dish is always better for being made the day before it is wanted, as the fat can be more easily removed.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beef-steak Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of rump-steak
- two or three onions
- good crust
- pepper
- salt
- a dozen oysters
- mushroom ketchup
- flour
- water
- mustard

## Directions

1. Cut the rump-steak into seasonable pieces
2. Cut into shreds two or three onions
3. Paste the pudding-basin with good crust, not too rich nor too poor
4. Put the meat into the basin, with some pepper and salt, and a dozen oysters
5. Add a little thickening, composed of mushroom ketchup, flour and water, and mustard
6. Simmer for an hour and a half
7. Serve in the basin or turn it out, if the gravy in the pudding can be retained
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beef-steaks and Onions
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef-steaks
- clarified dripping
- onions
- brown sauce

## Directions

1. Have the steaks cut rather thinner than for broiling
2. Put some clarified dripping into the pan, and fry the steaks until done
3. Put in the onions, having been previously sliced, and fry them of a light brown
4. Serve with brown sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Beefsteak Rolls
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. of Beefsteak
- Bread and Butter
- 2 Cloves
- 1 Onion
- Stalk of Celery
- 1/2 pint Gravy
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1/2 oz. Flour
- Salt
- Pepper

## Directions

1. Take a thick steak and split it open, cut it into strips five inches wide by three long.
2. Cut some very thin bread and butter the same size seasoned with pepper and salt.
3. Lay it on the steak and roll it up.
4. Thread on a skewer and dust with flour.
5. Put the butter into a frying-pan, and when it is hot put in the rolls and fry them quickly.
6. Take out and lay in a saucepan.
7. Cut up the onion and fry in the same butter as the rolls were fried in.
8. Shake in a teaspoonful of flour and pour in the gravy.
9. Stir until it boils, then pour over the rolls.
10. Put in the celery and cloves, and simmer very gently for two hours.
11. Take up the rolls on a hot dish and slip off the skewers.
12. Boil up and flavour the gravy.
13. Remove the fat and pour round the meat.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beefsteak Stuffed
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 1/2 lbs. of Beefsteak
- 1/2 lb. Potatoes
- 2 oz. Dripping
- Salt and Pepper
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 tablespoonful Milk
- 1 Onion
- 1/2 teaspoonful Sage

## Directions

1. Boil and mash the potatoes with the butter, milk, and salt.
2. Lay the steak flat and spread the potatoes over it.
3. Chop the onion very fine and powder the sage, and sprinkle over the potatoes.
4. Roll up and tie with a tape or string.
5. Rub some dripping over a baking sheet, put in the steak, and plenty of dripping on the top.
6. Put into a moderate oven and bake for an hour, basting frequently.
7. Put on to a hot dish, take off the tapes, and pour round it some nice gravy.
8. Send mashed potatoes to table with it.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beetroot and Macaroni Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 oz. Macaroni
- 2 tablespoonsful Oil
- 1 bunch Beetroot
- Pepper and Salt
- 2 tablespoonsful Vinegar

## Directions

1. Boil both the macaroni and the beetroot by directions given elsewhere.
2. When quite cold, peel and slice up the beetroot and cut the macaroni into pieces about two inches long; arrange them in alternate layers on a dish.
3. Blend the oil and vinegar with the salt and pepper and pour it over; let it stand for an hour, basting continually with the oil and vinegar.
4. By that time it should be of a bright red colour.
5. It is then ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beetroot And Onion Stew
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- beetroots
- onions
- butter
- sugar
- salt
- milk
- vinegar
- flour
- mashed potatoes
- parsley

## Directions

1. Boil the beetroots by directions given and slice them up
2. Peel and slice up the onions and fry in the butter, but do not let them brown
3. Stir in the flour and the milk and bring to the boil
4. When it has boiled a few minutes stir in gradually the vinegar, salt, and sugar, then the beetroot
5. Simmer slowly for one hour
6. Make a border of the potatoes on a hot dish, garnish with sprigs of parsley
7. Put the beetroot and onion in the centre, and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Beetroot In Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- beetroots
- white sauce

## Directions

1. Wash the beetroots, but do not cut them
2. Put them in cold water, and boil till they feel soft if pressed
3. Take them up, peel and slice them
4. Make the sauce by directions given elsewhere
5. Put in the beetroot and simmer for about half an hour
6. Dish the beets and pour the sauce over
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Belvidere Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one quart of flour
- four eggs
- a piece of butter the size of an egg
- cold milk
- one teaspoonful of salt

## Directions

1. Mix the butter with the flour
2. Beat the eggs together with cold milk
3. Pour gradually into the flour
4. Add one teaspoonful of salt
5. Work it for a few minutes
6. Cut the dough the size of a breakfast-plate
7. Bake in a quick oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Berlin, White, or Pale Beer (Wess-bier)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- One part barley malt
- Five parts wheat malt
- Water at 95°F., thirty quarts per scheffel of malt
- Seventy quarts of boiling water
- Half a pound of Altmark hops per bushel of malt

## Directions

1. Mingle the barley malt and wheat malt together, then moisten and crush between rollers
2. Work up the mixture with water at 95°F., in the proportion of thirty quarts per scheffel of malt
3. Add seventy quarts of boiling water to the pasty mixture
4. Mash the whole in the tun
5. Leave it a little to settle
6. Draw off a portion of the thin liquor by the tap and transfer to the copper
7. Add to it a decoction of half a pound of Altmark hops, separately prepared, for each bushel of malt
8. After half an hour's boiling, turn the hopped wort back with the hops into the mash-tun, of which the temperature should now be 162°F., but not more
9. In half an hour draw the wort from off the grains and pump into the cooler
10. Afterwards mash the grains with from forty to fifty quarts of boiling water per scheffel of malt
11. Draw off this infusion and add to the former malt
12. Set the whole mixture at 66°F. with a due proportion of top yeast or ordinary barm
13. Ferment very moderately
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Berry Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pottle of strawberries
- half a pound of white sugar
- a pint of whipped cream
- one ounce and a half of dissolved isinglass

## Directions

1. Mix strawberries with half a pound of white sugar
2. Rub this through a sieve
3. Add to it a pint of whipped cream and one ounce and a half of dissolved isinglass
4. Pour it into the mould, which must be immersed in ice until ready to serve
5. Carefully turn out on the dish and garnish according to fancy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bindon aux Legumes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- turkey
- slices of bacon
- puree of chicory, celery, or turnips

## Directions

1. Wrap round the turkey prepared for roasting some slices of bacon, and dress it of a pale colour
2. Prepare a puree of chicory, celery, or turnips; celery is preferable
3. Put the roast turkey into it, to give the puree a flavour, and serve
4. Or stew the vegetables, and surround the turkey in the dish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Binoles
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- short paste
- hashed meat or forcemeat
- very hot fat

## Directions

1. Take short paste and roll very thin
2. Cut it into small round pieces
3. Put into them some hashed meat or forcemeat
4. Gather up the paste over it and moisten the edges
5. Press them securely together
6. Fry them in a piece of very hot fat
7. Drain them and serve them hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Biscuit Drops
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the whites and yolks of six eggs
- a spoonful of rose-water
- six ounces of powdered loaf sugar
- one ounce of bruised carraway seeds
- six ounces of flour

## Directions

1. Beat up the whites and yolks of six eggs separately with a spoonful of rose-water
2. Add six ounces of powdered loaf sugar
3. Beat the whole well
4. Add one ounce of bruised carraway seeds and six ounces of flour
5. Drop them on wafer-paper
6. Bake in a moderate oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Biscuits a la Francaise
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the yolks of four eggs
- one pound of sugar
- the whites of four eggs
- half a pound of flour

## Directions

1. Beat up the yolks of four eggs with one pound of sugar
2. Whip the whites separately
3. When they are well frothed, mix them with the yolks and sugar
4. Stir in half a pound of flour lightly
5. Have ready some tin moulds, buttered
6. Put in the biscuit paste, filling the moulds only half
7. Throw some sugar over them
8. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bishop
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three best oranges
- half a pint of claret
- a pound and a quarter of loaf sugar

## Directions

1. Grill the oranges to a pale brown colour over a clear fire
2. Place them in a small punch-bowl and pour over them half a pint of claret in which a pound and a quarter of loaf sugar is dissolved, and cover it
3. When served, cut the oranges into pieces and place in a jug containing the remainder of the bottle of claret, made hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bissoles
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Meat
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 1/2 oz. Flour
- 1 Egg
- Bread Crumbs
- 1/2 pint Stock
- Quarter of an Onion
- Parsley
- Pepper and Salt
- Hot Fat

## Directions

1. Mince up the meat and mix in some chopped parsley, pepper, and salt.
2. Put the butter into a stewpan, and when it is dissolved mince up the pieces of onion very finely and fry that for two minutes.
3. Stir in the flour.
4. Pour in the gravy and stir until it boils.
5. Mix in the meat and let it get thoroughly hot.
6. Turn it out on to a plate, spread it over, and leave until quite cold.
7. Make up into balls, cover with egg and bread crumbs, and fry in hot fat.
8. Arrange in a circle and garnish with fried parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Bitter Oakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of butter
- half a pound of sugar
- grated rind of a lemon
- a little cinnamon
- flour
- spice
- eggs
- candied orange or lemon peel

## Directions

1. Take equal quantities of butter and sugar, say half a pound of each.
2. Grate the rind of a lemon, add a little cinnamon, and as much flour as will form it into a paste, with spice and eggs.
3. Roll it out.
4. Cut it into small cakes.
5. Bake.
6. A piece of candied orange or lemon peel may be put on the top of each cake.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bitters
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- One ounce and a half of gentian-root
- One ounce and a half of lemon-peel
- One ounce and a half of orange-peel
- One quart of sherry

## Directions

1. Steep these ingredients for about a month in a quart of sherry
2. Strain and bottle for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Black Cap Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 2 Eggs
- 1 oz. Currants
- 1/4 lb. Flour

## Directions

1. Put the eggs into a basin
2. Beat in the flour and then the milk
3. Pour into a buttered basin
4. Clean the currants and drop them in
5. Steam for one hour
6. Turn out of the basin
7. Sprinkle with sugar and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Black Puddings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- blood
- salt
- grits or boiled rice
- bread
- milk
- winter savory
- thyme
- pennyroyal
- pepper
- ground spice
- beef suet or pig fat
- eggs

## Directions

1. Stir the blood with salt until cold
2. Put a quart of it to a quart of grits or boiled rice, and soak some bread in milk
3. Clean the grits well with salt and water
4. Chop winter savory, thyme, pennyroyal, pepper, and salt, and a little ground spice
5. Mix these about with three pounds of beef suet, or the fat of the pig
6. Add six eggs well beaten up
7. Add the rice, or grits, and bread, and fill your skins
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Blanc-mange
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one quart of milk
- an ounce of isinglass
- a quarter of a pound of the best sugar
- a quarter of an ounce of cinnamon
- a little grated nutmeg
- half the peel of a lemon
- a bay leaf

## Directions

1. Simmer over a slow fire till the isinglass is dissolved
2. Strain it through a napkin into a mould for the table
3. It can be coloured or flavoured any way, as long as no ingredient is used that does not curdle the milk
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Blow My Skull
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pints of boiling water
- loaf sugar
- lime or lemon-juice
- one pint of ale or porter
- one pint of rum
- half a pint of brandy

## Directions

1. Combine two pints of boiling water with quantum sufficit of loaf sugar and lime or lemon-juice, one pint of ale or porter, one pint of rum, and half a pint of brandy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Boiled Cod
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cod
- salt

## Directions

1. Wash and clean the fish, and rub some salt on the inside
2. Put the fish in the kettle, so that it may be covered
3. Put in a handful of salt, and then put in the fish
4. A small fish will be done in about fifteen minutes, and a larger one will take longer time
5. Garnish the dish with the roe and liver
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Boiled Custard
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of milk
- the peel of a lemon
- a little grated nutmeg
- a bay or laurel leaf
- a stick of cinnamon
- eight egg yolks
- four egg whites
- a little brandy or white wine

## Directions

1. Put a quart of milk into a stewpan, with the peel of a lemon cut thin, a little grated nutmeg, a bay or laurel leaf, a stick of cinnamon
2. set it over a quick fire, and mind it does not boil over
3. when it boils set it beside the fire, and simmer ten minutes
4. break the yolks of eight and the whites of four eggs into a basin, beat them well
5. pour in the milk a little at a time, set it on the fire again, and stir it with a wooden spoon
6. let it have one boil, pass it through a tamis
7. when cold, add a little brandy or white wine, and serve in cups
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Boiled Custard
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 3 Eggs
- 1 1/2 oz. Sugar
- Flavouring

## Directions

1. Put the yolks of the eggs into a basin and whisk them
2. Put the milk into a saucepan, and when it is boiling pour it over the eggs, stirring all the time
3. Strain back into the saucepan and whisk well till it comes to boiling point
4. Draw away from the fire, but continue whisking for a few minutes
5. Pour into a basin, sweeten and flavour to taste
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Boiled Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- fish
- salt
- vinegar or lemon juice

## Directions

1. The water must boil well before the fish is put in, and be seasoned with salt and a teaspoonful of vinegar or lemon juice; lay the fish carefully in, and bring the water to the boil again.
2. Then draw it away from the fire, cover down closely, and keep it just below the boil.
3. About ten minutes to every lb. will be sufficient.
4. It is always done when it begins to leave the bone.
5. Take it out of the water directly it is cooked, and if it is not wanted just at the time, cover it with a cloth and keep it hot.
6. Any kind of fish sauce can be served with it, such as plain melted butter, parsley, or egg sauce.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Boiled Flounders
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flounders
- cold water
- salt
- plain melted butter

## Directions

1. Wash and clean them well, and cut the back side of them, the same as you do turbot
2. Put them into a kettle, with cold water and salt
3. When it comes to a boil, skim them
4. Let them simmer for five minutes, and they are ready
5. Serve eaten with plain melted butter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Boiled Leg of Mutton
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- leg of mutton
- mashed turnips

## Directions

1. Simply simmer until done
2. Take off the scum occasionally
3. Ensure the dish is done but not over-done
4. Serve with mashed turnips which must be neither stringy nor bitter
5. If turnips are bitter, a little sugar put in the water will absorb that unpleasant taste
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Boiled Turkey
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- turkey
- liver
- gizzard
- flour
- napkin
- crumbs of bread
- parsley
- pepper
- salt
- nutmeg
- lemon-peel
- anchovy
- oysters
- shredded mushrooms
- suet
- butter
- egg
- lemon juice
- salt
- celery
- oyster sauce
- brown sauce

## Directions

1. Truss the turkey, with the liver and gizzard in the wings
2. Well dredge with flour
3. Fasten in a napkin
4. Fill the crop with forcemeat made of crumbs of bread, parsley, pepper, salt, nutmeg, lemon-peel, anchovy, oysters chopped, shredded mushrooms, a little suet, butter, and bound together with an egg
5. In the water in which the turkey is boiled put the juice of three lemons, two ounces of butter, and a handful of salt
6. Let it simmer
7. Serve with celery, oyster, or brown sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bola d'Amor
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of white sugar
- yolks of twenty eggs
- one pound of sweet almonds
- orange rose-water
- citrons
- myrtle
- gold and silver leaf

## Directions

1. Clarify two pounds of white sugar
2. To ascertain when it is of proper consistency, drop a spoonful in cold water, form it into a ball, and try if it sounds when struck against a glass
3. Take the yolks of twenty eggs, mix them up gently, and pass them through a sieve
4. Have ready a funnel, the hole of which must be about the size of a vermicelli
5. Hold the funnel over the sugar while it is boiling over a charcoal fire
6. Pour the egg through, stirring the sugar all the time, and taking care to hold the funnel at such a distance from the sugar as to admit of the egg dripping into it
7. When the egg has been a few minutes in the sugar, it will be hard enough to take out with a silver fork, and place on a drainer
8. Continue adding egg to the boiling sugar till enough is obtained
9. Prepare one pound of sweet almonds finely pounded and boiled in sugar, clarified with orange rose-water only
10. Place in a dish a layer of this paste, over which place a layer of citrons cut in thin slices, and then a thick layer of the egg prepared as above
11. Continue working thus in alternate layers till high enough to look handsome
12. Pile in the form of a cone, with the egg forming the last layer
13. Place in a gentle oven till it becomes a little set, and the last layer slightly crisp
14. Serve in the dish in which it is baked
15. Ornament with myrtle and gold and silver leaf
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bola de Toledo
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of butter
- a little milk
- a pound of flour
- six beaten eggs
- a quarter of a pound of beaten sweet almonds
- two table-spoonfuls of yeast
- egg prepared as for bola d'amor
- slices of citron
- egg marmalade
- cinnamon
- a rich puff paste
- clarified sugar with orange-flower water

## Directions

1. Warm a pound of butter over the fire with a little milk
2. Put it into a pan with a pound of flour, six beaten eggs, a quarter of a pound of beaten sweet almonds, and two table-spoonfuls of yeast
3. Make these ingredients into a light paste, and set it before the fire to rise
4. Grease a deep dish, and place in a layer of the paste
5. Then some egg prepared as for bola d'amor
6. Then slices of citron, and a layer of egg marmalade
7. Sprinkle each layer with cinnamon, and fill the dish with alternate layers
8. Line the dish with a rich puff paste
9. Bake in a brisk oven
10. Pour clarified sugar with orange-flower water over till the syrup has thoroughly penetrated the bola
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bonne Bouche
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a dessert-spoonful of mustard
- a salt-spoonful of salt
- a little cayenne
- a large glassful of port wine or claret
- a table-spoonful of ketchup

## Directions

1. Mix a dessert-spoonful of mustard, a salt-spoonful of salt, and a little cayenne in a large glassful of port wine or claret
2. Pour it on the meat just before serving
3. A table-spoonful of ketchup may be added
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bonne Bouche
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- dessert-spoonful of mustard
- salt-spoonful of salt
- little cayenne
- large glassful of port wine or claret
- tablespoonful of ketchup

## Directions

1. Mix the mustard, salt, and cayenne in the large glassful of port wine or claret
2. Pour it on the meat just before serving
3. A tablespoonful of ketchup may be added
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bordeaux Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- paste
- butter
- flour
- choice preserve

## Directions

1. Roll paste out less than a quarter of an inch thick
2. Cut it into six or seven portions of equal size
