3. Lay them on buttered tins floured
4. Bake them in an oven until they are firm and crisp and of a pale brown
5. When cold, spread upon each a different kind of choice preserve
6. Pile the whole evenly in the form of a cake
7. The top may be iced and decorated
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bottling Cherries
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cherries
- six ounces of loaf sugar to every pound of fruit

## Directions

1. To every pound of fruit for tarts through the winter, add six ounces of loaf sugar
2. Fill the jars with fruit
3. Shake the sugar over, and tie down with two bladders, as there is danger of one bursting through the boiling
4. Place the jars in a boiler of cold water
5. After the water has boiled, let them remain three hours
6. Take them out, and, when cool, put them in a dry place, where they will keep over a year
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bouillon aux Herbes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- leaves of sorrel
- leeks
- lettuce
- purslain
- chervil
- about two handfuls of each
- salt
- some fresh butter
- three pints of water
- the yolks of two eggs
- a spoonful of cream

## Directions

1. Take leaves of sorrel, leeks, lettuce, purslain, and chervil, about two handfuls of each.
2. Pick and wash them.
3. Add salt, some fresh butter.
4. Boil together in three pints of water till it is reduced to half.
5. Take it off and strain it.
6. If you like you can add the yolks of two eggs, beaten with a spoonful of cream.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Braised Leg of Mutton
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Leg of Mutton
- 1 Rasher of Ham
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 20 Peppercorns
- 1 1/2 oz. Butter
- 2 Carrots
- 1 Turnip
- 1 Onion
- 1 quart Stock

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is dissolved put in the mutton and brown it all over
2. Then lay the ham and vegetables round it, pour in the stock, and bring it to the boil
3. Cover down closely, and stand the saucepan in a moderate oven where it will cook slowly
4. If the braising is being done by a coal fire the lid of the stewpan may be reversed and some hot coals placed in it; these will want renewing from time to time
5. In any case cook very slowly, then dish the meat, strain the gravy, remove the fat carefully, and boil to a sort of half glaze
6. Pour round the dish, serve with Julienne or plain vegetables
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Brandy Smash
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a nip of brandy
- a table-spoonful of port wine
- a table-spoonful of ice
- sugar and water to taste
- a sprig of mint

## Directions

1. Combine a nip of brandy, a table-spoonful of port wine, and a table-spoonful of ice
2. Add sugar and water to taste
3. Add a sprig of mint
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brandy Smash
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a nip of brandy
- a tablespoonful of port wine
- a tablespoonful of ice
- sugar and water to taste
- a sprig of mint

## Directions

1. Mix brandy, port wine, ice, sugar and water to taste with a sprig of mint
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Branzy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lean beef
- salt
- boiled meat
- sausage meat
- parsley
- lemon-peel
- eggs
- bread crumbs
- milk
- curdled milk
- bay leaf
- onion
- cloves
- peppercorns
- ginger
- bouillon
- vinegar
- wine

## Directions

1. Cut lean beef into thin slices
2. sprinkle them with salt, and beat them to flatten them
3. Make a forcemeat of boiled meat, sausage meat, parsley and lemon-peel chopped fine, three eggs, some bread crumbs soaked in milk and drained, and a little curdled milk
4. Spread some of this forcemeat on each slice of beef
5. roll it up and tie and put it into a stewpan, with a bay leaf, lemon-peel, onion stuck with cloves, peppercorns, ginger, bouillon, vinegar, and wine
6. cover it up and let it stew
7. when half done, add a roux
8. when quite done, strain the sauce, and serve with rolled slices untied
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brazilian Stew
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- shin or leg of beef
- good vinegar
- onions (optional)
- other flavouring or vegetable substances (optional)

## Directions

1. Take shin or leg of beef and cut it into slices of two or three ounces each
2. Dip it in good vinegar
3. With or without onions, or any other flavouring or vegetable substances, put it into a stewpan without water
4. Let it stand on the hot hearth, or by a slow fire, for two, three, or four hours
5. The meat will be thoroughly done, will have yielded plenty of gravy, and be as tender as a chicken
6. Care must be taken that the heat is moderate
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brazilian Stew
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Leg of Beef
- 1 Onion
- 1 Carrot
- 1 tablespoonful Vinegar
- 1 doz. Peppercorns

## Directions

1. Cut the beef up in small pieces, dip them in the vinegar, and lay in a jar.
2. Slice in the vegetables, add the peppercorns, and tie a paper over the top of the jar.
3. Stand in a saucepan of boiling water for three hours.
4. Dish up the meat, garnish with the carrot, strain the gravy, season and flavour, boil up and pour over.
5. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Bread and Butter Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of currants
- four or five layers of bread and butter
- a custard

## Directions

1. Wash and clean two ounces of currants, get ready four or five layers of bread and butter
2. put the latter in the dish, and between the layers put the currants
3. over all put in a custard
4. Bake about three-quarters of an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bread Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 slices Stale Bread
- 1 gill Oil
- 3 Pickled Onions
- 1 piece Pickled Cauliflower
- 2 Eggs
- 1 Beetroot
- 2 slices Cold Mutton
- 1 tablespoonful Vinegar
- Mustard and Cress
- Pepper and salt

## Directions

1. Trim off the crust and cut the bread into dice.
2. Put into a bowl and pour over the oil.
3. Let it stand till all the oil is absorbed.
4. Mince up the onion, cauliflower, eggs, and meat, and strew them over.
5. Season with pepper and salt.
6. Well wash the mustard and cress and arrange on the top.
7. Cut the beetroot into neat shapes and arrange as a garnish.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Bread Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two onions
- milk
- stale bread
- butter
- flour
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Boil two onions, cut in parts, in milk until reduced to pulp
2. Pour the milk on stale bread, and cover it close
3. In half an hour put it into a saucepan, with a piece of butter, and add a very small quantity of flour
4. add pepper and salt, and let it simmer for ten minutes
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Breakfast Bolls
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of flour
- a little salt
- two ounces of butter
- one ounce of sugar
- two eggs, well beaten up
- half a pint of milk, lukewarm
- two table-spoonfuls of yeast

## Directions

1. Mix flour, salt, butter, sugar, eggs, milk, and yeast
2. Let the dough rise
3. Make it into six rolls
4. Set them to rise again
5. Bake in tins
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Breakfast Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of flour
- one ounce of butter
- a very little salt
- two eggs
- a table-spoonful of milk

## Directions

1. Make a paste of half a pound of flour, one ounce of butter, a very little salt, two eggs, and a table-spoonful of milk
2. Set it to rise before the fire
3. Roll it out
4. Cut it into cakes the size of a small cheese-plate
5. Sprinkle with flour
6. Bake in a tin in a brisk oven, or on a girdle
7. Butter hot and serve quickly
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Breakfast Dish of Beef
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Slices of Cold Roast Beef (underdone)
- 1/2 gill Melted Butter Sauce
- 1/2 gill Gravy or Water
- Salt and Pepper
- 1 tablespoonful Walnut Ketchup or Vinegar
- 1 tablespoonful Red Currant Jelly

## Directions

1. Cut some thin slices of beef and lay them in a saucepan or basin.
2. Mix the melted butter sauce, gravy, jelly, and ketchup together, and pour over them.
3. Cover down closely and stand the saucepan in a larger one, half full of boiling water, and steam for half an hour.
4. Put the meat into a dish and pour the sauce over it.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Breakfast Meat
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Meat
- 2 oz. Macaroni
- 3 Tomatoes
- 1/2 gill Stock
- Pepper and Salt
- Bread Crumbs

## Directions

1. Mince up the meat, or any remains of cold hash or mince will do.
2. If there is any cold macaroni it can be used; if not, boil some by directions given, and slice up the tomatoes.
3. Butter a dish in which it can be cooked and served.
4. Place at the bottom a layer of meat, then one of macaroni, then one of the tomatoes, season with pepper and salt, and continue this in layers until all the materials are used up.
5. Sprinkle a few bread crumbs on the top, put into the oven, and bake for half an hour.
6. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Breakfast Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Tomatoes
- 1 Cucumber
- 1 tablespoonful Oil
- 1 Spring Onion
- Half a Lettuce
- 2 tablespoonsful Vinegar
- Pepper and salt

## Directions

1. Scald the tomatoes and take off the skin.
2. Put them into cold water or on to the ice until quite cold.
3. Cut them up the same as an orange.
4. Peel and cut up the cucumber into very thin slices.
5. Mince up the onion.
6. Sprinkle these with pepper and salt.
7. Pour over the oil and vinegar.
8. Shred up the lettuce and lay on the top.
9. It is then ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Bream Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Bream
- 1 gill Melted Butter
- 1/2 lb. Suet
- 1/2 teaspoonful Parsley
- Pepper
- Salt
- 1/2 lb. Flour

## Directions

1. Skin and fillet the fish and cut into small pieces.
2. Make a dry crust of the suet and flour and line a pudding basin with it.
3. Lay the fish in lightly, and season with the parsley, pepper, and salt.
4. Pour over the melted butter; this should be made with 1/2 oz. butter, 1/2 oz. flour, and 1 gill of water.
5. Cover the top of the pudding with crust, tie down securely with a cloth and string, and plunge into plenty of boiling water.
6. Boil for one hour and a half, turn out of the basin, and sprinkle with chopped parsley.
7. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Breast of Mutton and Peas
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Breasts of Mutton
- 2 Onions
- 1 Carrot
- 1 Egg
- Bread Crumbs
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 1 pint Peas
- Salt and Pepper
- Hot Fat
- 12 Peppercorns

## Directions

1. Wipe the meat with a warm damp cloth, and put it into a saucepan with the vegetables.
2. Bring to the boil and stew very gently for two hours.
3. Take it up and remove all the bones, put it between two boards and stand some heavy weights on it till quite cold.
4. Cut into neat-shaped pieces, egg and bread crumb them.
5. Fry a good colour.
6. Boil the peas by recipe given elsewhere.
7. Pile the mutton on a dish and put the peas round.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Breast of Veal with Green Peas
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- breast of veal
- onion
- stock
- green peas
- pepper
- salt
- lemon juice or vinegar

## Directions

1. Cut the breast into pieces, and fry of a light brown, with a shredded onion
2. When browned put the pieces into a stewpan, with a little stock, and allow it to simmer
3. When nearly done add a quantity of green peas, with pepper, salt, and the juice of half a lemon, or a little vinegar
4. Keep the cover of the stewpan off, and serve as soon as the peas are done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brighton Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of flour
- one drachm of carbonate of ammonia in fine powder
- four ounces of white sugar
- one ounce of arrowroot
- four ounces of butter
- one egg
- new milk

## Directions

1. Mix together flour, carbonate of ammonia, sugar, arrowroot, butter, and egg
2. Add new milk so as to form a stiff paste
3. Beat the mass with a rolling-pin
4. Roll it out
5. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brighton Hunting Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef
- spring water
- powdered saltpetre
- common salt
- a quarter of a pound of allspice
- two ounces of pepper

## Directions

1. Put the beef into spring water and allow it to remain there a few hours
2. Take it out and drain well
3. Rub a few ounces of powdered saltpetre well into the meat
4. Repeat this two or three times
5. Rub in common salt several times for the next twenty-four hours
6. Rub in a mixture of a quarter of a pound of allspice and two ounces of pepper for another twenty-four hours
7. Let the beef remain ten days in the pickle, turned every day
8. Bake it in a pan for four hours, covered with crust, and round the bottom of the baking-pan
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brioches
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of flour
- a little hot water
- a spoonful of yeast
- a pound of flour
- three-quarters of a pound of butter
- five eggs
- a little water
- a few grains of salt

## Directions

1. Make a paste with half a pound of flour and a little hot water, with a spoonful of yeast
2. Wrap up this paste in a cloth, and put it in a warm place, for twenty minutes in summer and an hour in winter
3. Put a pound of flour on the board, mix the paste that you have leavened with it, and three-quarters of a pound of butter, five eggs, a little water, and a few grains of salt
4. Knead all together three times
5. Wrap up warmly, and let it remain nine or ten hours
6. Cut the paste into pieces, the size you wish to make your brioches
7. Mould them into the form you wish, egg them over, and bake small ones half an hour; those of a larger size an hour and a half
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Broiled Chops and Steaks
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- chops or steaks
- pepper
- salt
- butter (optional)
- tomato sauce (optional)
- minced shalot (optional)
- dried mint (optional, for pork-chops)
- tarragon (optional)

## Directions

1. Cut the chops a little over half an inch thick
2. broil them over a clear fire until done, turning them with the beef-steak tongs about three times
3. do not pierce them with a fork
4. sprinkle pepper and salt over them in moderation
5. Serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Broiled Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Mullet
- 2 teaspoonsful Oil
- Pepper
- Salt
- suet
- butter

## Directions

1. Split the mullet open and wash away the black substance from the bones, dry on a cloth, rub with oil and sprinkle them with pepper and salt, and leave them in a cool place for an hour.
2. Rub a gridiron with a piece of suet, and when it is quite hot put on the fish and broil it carefully, turning it two or three times whilst cooking.
3. Lay on a hot dish and rub over with a little butter.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Broth (A la Minute)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of water
- half a pint of the gravy from roast meat
- salt

## Directions

1. Boil a quart of water.
2. Put to it half a pint of the gravy from roast meat.
3. Add salt, and it will serve for all purposes.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Broth Bouillon
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of beef or veal
- an onion
- a carrot
- a bit of bacon
- a glass of water
- another glass of water
- some salt
- about a quart of boiling water

## Directions

1. Take a pound of beef or veal, cut into dice, chop them, and put them into a stewpan, with an onion, a carrot, a bit of bacon, and a glass of water.
2. Just as it comes to the boil throw in another glass of water, add some salt, boil it a little longer, and add about a quart of boiling water.
3. Take it up, strain, and serve.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brown and Poison's Souffle Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of Brown and Poison's patent corn-flour
- six ounces of best white sugar
- a quart of milk
- four ounces of fresh butter
- a pinch of salt
- a few drops of essence of vanilla or lemon
- six eggs

## Directions

1. Put six ounces of corn-flour into a stewpan with six ounces of white sugar
2. Mix well with a quart of milk, four ounces of fresh butter, a pinch of salt, and a few drops of essence of vanilla or lemon
3. Stir well until it boils
4. Work in the yolks of six eggs
5. Whisk the whites into a froth and lightly incorporate with the butter
6. Pour into a buttered pie-dish
7. Bake in a moderately heated oven for half an hour
8. Sugar the top and serve when risen
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brown Bread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three pounds of flour
- one pound of rye flour
- yeast
- salt

## Directions

1. Mix flour and rye flour together
2. Add the same quantity of yeast and salt as previously given for regular bread
3. Bake longer than white bread
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brown Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six or eight onions
- ounce of butter
- half an ounce of flour
- stock
- pepper
- salt
- tablespoonful of ketchup

## Directions

1. Cut onions into a stewpan with an ounce of butter
2. Set it on a slow fire and turn the onions until lightly browned
3. Stir in gradually half an ounce of flour with a little stock, pepper and salt
4. Simmer for a short time
5. Add a tablespoonful of ketchup
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Brown Macaroni Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1½ oz. Macaroni
- 1 oz. Butter
- Vegetables
- Cornflour
- 2 quarts Bone Stock
- 1 doz. peppercorns
- onions or leeks
- 1 carrot
- a fagot of herbs
- sugar
- salt
- pepper

## Directions

1. Slice up the onions or leeks, one carrot, and make a fagot of herbs
2. Fry them in the butter with 1 doz. peppercorns till they are quite brown, but not burnt
3. Sprinkle over a tablespoonful of cornflour, and when brown pour over the boiling stock and stir till it boils up
4. Let it simmer for an hour
5. If it is not brown enough, burn a little sugar in a spoon and stir it in
6. When the vegetables are soft rub the soup through a wire sieve and return to the saucepan
7. Boil the macaroni in salt and water for twenty minutes, strain off, and cut into pieces one inch long
8. Put these into the soup and simmer for a quarter of an hour
9. Flavour with a little salt and pepper if necessary, and pour into a hot tureen
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Brown Mince
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Roast Beef
- 1 lb. Bread Crumbs
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 Egg
- 1 pint Gravy
- Nutmeg

## Directions

1. Mince up the beef finely and mix it with the bread crumbs.
2. Season with pepper, salt, nutmeg, or parsley.
3. Beat up the egg, mix it with the gravy, and pour over the meat and crumbs.
4. Butter a basin, sprinkle well with brown bread crumbs, put in the mince.
5. Cover over with a plate and bake for an hour.
6. Turn on to a hot dish and pour a little nice gravy round it.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Brown Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Stock
- 1 oz. Butter
- 0.5 oz. Flour
- 1 Stalk of Celery
- 1 Carrot
- 1 Onion
- 0.5 Turnip
- 1 doz. Peppercorns
- Salt
- Lemon juice

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is quite hot, slice up the vegetables and put them in with the peppercorns, and fry a good colour
2. Stir in the flour and brown that too
3. Pour in the stock and stir till it boils
4. Cover down and let it simmer slowly for an hour
5. Rub through a sieve, return to the saucepan
6. Season with salt and lemon juice, boil up, and it is ready to serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Bubble and Squeak
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cold salt meat
- cold cabbage
- salt
- pepper

## Directions

1. Slightly fry cold salt meat in a pan
2. Cut cold cabbage into pieces
3. Put a little salt and pepper on it
4. Warm the cabbage
5. Dish up the fried cabbage, and garnish with the beef
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Burnt Butter
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- butter
- vinegar
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Put butter into a pan
2. when it becomes brown add a little vinegar and pepper and salt
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Bush Cookery
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- kangaroo
- fat bacon

## Directions

1. Divide a piece of kangaroo nicely into cutlets, two or three inches broad and a third of an inch thick.
2. Take a straight clean stick, about four feet long, sharpened at both ends.
3. On the narrow part of this, for the space of a foot or more, spit the cutlets at intervals, and on the end place a piece of delicately rosy fat bacon.
4. Stick the strong end of the stick-spit fast and erect in the ground, close by the fire, to leeward, taking care that it does not burn.
5. Allow the bacon on the summit of the spit to soften in the blaze, dropping a lubricating shower of rich and savoury fat upon the leaner kangaroo cutlets below.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Butter Crust, for Boiled Puddings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of butter or clarified dripping
- one pound of flour
- half a pint of water
- flour for dredging

## Directions

1. Work the flour to a paste with half a pint of water
2. Roll out the crust rather thin
3. Place the butter over it in small pieces
4. Dredge over it some flour
5. Fold the paste over
6. Repeat the rolling
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Buttered Toast
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- bread
- butter

## Directions

1. Cut the bread not thicker than about three-quarters of an inch
2. Toast with attention, browning the crust
3. Butter on both sides at once while warm
4. Keep warm
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Buttered Toddy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a glass of rum-grog
- honey
- nutmeg
- lemon-juice
- a piece of fresh butter about the size of half a walnut

## Directions

1. Mix a glass of rum-grog pretty strong and hot
2. Sweeten to taste with honey
3. Flavour with nutmeg and lemon-juice
4. Add a piece of fresh butter about the size of half a walnut
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Buttered Whiting
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Whiting
- Pepper and Salt
- 1.5 oz. Butter
- 1 Lemon
- flour

## Directions

1. Wash the whiting, dry them in a cloth, mix a little flour, pepper, and salt together, cover the fish thoroughly with this.
2. Butter a tin dish, lay the whiting in and put the rest of the butter over them in small pieces, and put them into a hot oven; baste constantly with the butter.
3. This must not be allowed to get black; it should be brown.
4. When the whiting are done, which will be in from fifteen to twenty minutes, according to the thickness of the fish, place them in a hot dish and pour the butter in which they have been cooked over them.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Buttermilk Breakfast Oakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of buttermilk
- flour
- a tea-spoon half full of bi-carbonate of soda

## Directions

1. Take half a pint of buttermilk, add to it sufficient flour to make it into dough
2. Mix in a tea-spoon half full of bi-carbonate of soda well
3. Put them in the oven or on the girdle as soon as the soda and buttermilk have come in contact
4. Bake quickly, and butter hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cabbage and Bacon Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 cabbage
- 1 pound bacon
- 1 dozen peppercorns
- 2 turnips
- 1 carrot
- 1 onion
- 2 quarts water
- pieces of stale bread

## Directions

1. Put two quarts of water into a saucepan
2. When it boils put in a pound of bacon neither too lean nor too fat
3. Let it boil slowly for one hour
4. The bacon must be well washed and scraped before cooking, and when it boils skim the pot thoroughly
5. Well wash the cabbage and soak it in hot water for half an hour
6. Take all the water away and put the cabbage into the saucepan with the bacon and vegetables cut up, and the peppercorns tied in a piece of muslin
7. Let them simmer together for two and a half hours
8. Take up the cabbage, and cut it into quarters
9. Take one quarter and cut it into small pieces and put it into a soup tureen
10. Cut some stale pieces of bread into thin slices and lay on the top
11. Pour over the boiling liquor, and serve
12. Dish the bacon, pull off the rind, and put the rest of the cabbage round the dish
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cabbage Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Cabbage
- 2 oz. Butter
- 1 pint Milk
- Pepper
- Salt
- Bread
- 2 quarts boiling water

## Directions

1. Wash and strain the cabbage well, and cut it up into slices; throw it into boiling salt and water, and cook for five minutes; strain all the water off and put it into a saucepan with the salt, pepper, and two quarts of boiling water, and boil for one hour.
2. Add the milk and let it boil up again.
3. Toast the slice of bread and cut it up into dice.
4. Put it into a warm soup tureen and pour the boiling soup over it.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cafe au Lait
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 10 or 12 teaspoonsful of coffee
- a sixth part of chicory
- 1 pint of water
- hot milk

## Directions

1. Pour enough boiling water into the upper part of the cafetiere
2. Add the specified proportions of coffee and chicory
3. Allow to brew
4. Half fill the cup with hot milk
5. Pour the coffee into the cup
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cafe Noir
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 teaspoonsful of coffee, heaped up
- a very small teaspoonful of chicory, or none at all
- 1 pint of water

## Directions

1. Pour enough boiling water into the upper part of the cafetiere
2. Add the specified proportions of coffee and chicory
3. Allow to brew
4. Pour the coffee into a cup
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Caledonian Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Two tea-spoonfuls of white sugar
- one tea-spoonful of raspberry jam
- the whites of two eggs
- the juice of one lemon
- fancy biscuits

## Directions

1. Beat well for half an hour
2. serve up, sprinkled with fancy biscuits
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Calf's Foot Broth
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a calf's foot
- water
- a little lemon-peel
- butter
- sugar
- nutmeg
- the yolk of an egg

## Directions

1. Simmer the foot in water, with a little lemon-peel, taking off the scum.
2. Put it into a basin, and, when cold, take off the fat.
3. When wanted, warm it up again with a little of the broth, adding butter, sugar, and nutmeg.
4. Add the yolk of an egg and serve.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Calf's Head Boiled
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head
- brains
- parsley
- sage leaf
- butter
- pepper
- salt
- tongue
- bacon
- ham
- pickled pork
- lemon

## Directions

1. Tie the head in a cloth, and boil for two hours in plenty of water
2. Tie the brains up in a separate cloth, with a little parsley and a leaf of sage
3. Boil the brains one hour
4. Chop the brains small, warm them up in a saucepan, with a little butter, pepper, and salt
5. Lay the tongue, which has been boiled at the same time and peeled, in the middle of a small dish
6. Place the brains round it
7. Or mince the brains and put them into parsley and butter, and pour over the head
8. Serve with bacon, ham, or pickled pork in another dish
9. Garnish with lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Calf's Head Boiled
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head
- parsley
- sage leaf
- butter
- pepper
- salt
- tongue
- bacon, ham, or pickled pork
- lemon

## Directions

1. Tie the head in a cloth, and boil for two hours in plenty of water.
2. Tie the brains up in a separate cloth, with a little parsley and a leaf of sage; boil them one hour.
3. Chop the brains small, warm them up in a saucepan, with a little butter, pepper, and salt.
4. Lay the tongue, which has been boiled at the same time and peeled, in the middle of a small dish.
5. Place the brains round it.
6. Alternatively, mince the brains and put them into parsley and butter, and pour over the head, and serve with bacon, ham, or pickled pork in another dish.
7. Garnish with lemon.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Calf's Head Hashed
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head
- flour
- liquor the head was boiled in or stock
- blades of mace
- salt
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Cut the head up into slices
2. Flour them, and put them into a stewpan, with a little of the liquor the head was boiled in, or some stock, with two blades of mace, a salt-spoon of salt, and flour to thicken
3. Let it simmer, and before serving add a dessert-spoonful of lemon juice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Calfs Foot Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Calves' feet
- Salad dressing or oil and vinegar
- White lettuce
- Sliced beetroot

## Directions

1. Boil calves' feet very thoroughly for at least eight hours.
2. Strain off the stock and remove the bones.
3. Put the meat on one side till quite cold.
4. Cut up into neat pieces and put into a salad bowl.
5. Pour over a salad dressing or just oil and vinegar.
6. Shred over it a nice white lettuce.
7. Garnish with sliced beetroot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Calves' Feet Jelly
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four calves' feet
- water
- pound of lump sugar
- peel of two lemons
- juice of six lemons
- six whites of eggs
- bottle of sherry

## Directions

1. Slit the calves' feet in two and wash them in lukewarm water
2. Put them into simmer for six hours in sufficient water to cover them
3. When reduced to half the quantity, after skimming it, strain it through a sieve
4. Skim off all the oily part on the top of the liquor
5. Put the liquor in a stewpan to melt, with a pound of lump sugar, the peel of two lemons, the juice of six, six whites of eggs and shells beat well together, and a bottle of sherry
6. Whisk the whole together until it simmers for half an hour
7. Strain through a jelly-bag
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cambridge Drink
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- equal quantities of ale and soda-water
- one sprig of borage

## Directions

1. Mix equal quantities of ale and soda-water
2. Add a sprig of borage
3. Optional: some parties use lemon and a little sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Camp Ketchup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Old beer, two quarts
- White wine, one quart
- Anchovies, four ounces
- Peeled shalot, three ounces
- Mace, half an ounce
- Nutmegs
- Ginger
- Black pepper

## Directions

1. Take old beer two quarts and white wine one quart with anchovies four ounces
2. Mix and heat to the boiling point
3. Remove from the fire
4. Add three ounces of peeled shalot, half an ounce of mace, nutmegs, ginger, and black pepper
5. Macerate for fourteen days
6. Strain
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Canards a la Braise
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pair of ducks
- couple of slices of bacon or ham
- two onions
- salt
- pepper
- glass of claret or port
- morels, capers, or artichokes
- consomme or stock

## Directions

1. Lard a pair of ducks
2. Place them in a stewpan breasts downwards
3. Lay a couple of slices of bacon or ham in the stewpan with two onions sliced, salt, pepper, and a glass of claret or port
4. Let it stew gently
5. When done serve with sauce; morels, capers, or artichokes may be sliced and added with a small quantity of consomme or stock
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Captain's Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three pounds and a half of flour
- a quarter of a pound of butter
- a pint of milk

## Directions

1. Mix them together till they make a hard, even dough
2. Cut it into pieces
3. Roll it into paste half an inch thick
4. Mould them into shape
5. Bake in a crisp oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Caramel
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of the finest powdered loaf sugar
- half a pint of water
- the white of an egg

## Directions

1. Whisk the mixture till it boils, taking off the scum as it rises
2. Let it boil five minutes, then run it through a tamis
3. Put it again on the fire and boil it up
4. When it has reached caramel height, it must be taken off
5. To ascertain the latter, when the syrup has become thick, dip into it the handle of a spoon, and immediately put it into cold water: if the sugar on it becomes crisp, it is done
6. To spin a basket or any other ornament, when the caramel begins to cool take some up with a spoon, and run it cleverly in thin threads over the mould, which must have been oiled
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Caramel
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Half a pound of Sugar
- Half a pint of Water

## Directions

1. Put half a pound of sugar into a frying-pan and let it get very brown
2. Pour over half a pint of water and stir till it boils
3. Strain into a bottle
4. It will keep good a long time, and is very useful for colouring soup and gravies
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Carraway Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three pounds of flour
- two ounces of butter
- half a pound of sugar
- one ounce of carraway seeds
- half an ounce of ground coriander seed
- half a tea-spoonful of carbonate of soda
- a table-spoonful of arrowroot
- warm milk

## Directions

1. Mix the whole together
2. Make a stiff paste with warm milk
3. Cut into thin cakes
4. Prick with a fork
5. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Carrot Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 Cold Boiled Carrots
- 1/4 lb. Cold Boiled Mutton
- 1 stalk Celery
- 6 Capers
- Half a teaspoonful Parsley
- Salad Dressing

## Directions

1. Cut up some cold boiled mutton into small pieces and lay them in a salad bowl.
2. Mince up the celery and capers and strew over it.
3. Pour over the dressing.
4. Slice up the cold carrots and lay them on top.
5. Garnish with the chopped parsley, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Carrot Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Carrots
- 1 oz. Butter
- Sugar, Salt, and Pepper
- 3 quarts Bone Stock

## Directions

1. Scrape and slice up the carrots and put them into a saucepan with the butter.
2. Sprinkle over a teaspoonful each of salt and sugar and a quarter of a teaspoonful of pepper.
3. Turn them about in the butter for five minutes, pour over the boiling stock and boil for an hour.
4. Rub through a sieve, return to the saucepan and boil up, season to taste, and serve very hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Carrots in Butter
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 Carrots
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- Pinch of Salt and Sugar

## Directions

1. Scrape the carrots and slice them up, put them into boiling water seasoned with salt and sugar, and boil for ten minutes.
2. Strain off the water.
3. Put the butter into a small saucepan, and when it is hot stir in the parsley and a few drops of lemon juice.
4. Toss the carrots in this until they are thoroughly hot, then cover down and cook slowly till soft.
5. Dish and pour over the butter in which they were cooked.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cassolettes of Vegetables
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Turnips
- Salt
- Water
- Cold vegetables such as beans, peas, carrots, and parsnips
- Butter

## Directions

1. Peel some turnips and scoop out the centre; boil them in salt and water till soft, but quite whole.
2. If there are any cold vegetables in the larder, such as beans, peas, carrots, and parsnips, make them hot; if not, cut some into small pieces and boil separately.
3. Stir them into any cold sauce that may be left, or toss them in a little butter.
4. Fill the turnip cups with these, arranging them on a dish, alternately red and green.
5. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cauliflower Fritters
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cold Cauliflower
- Frying Batter
- Hot Fat

## Directions

1. Take any cold cauliflower that may be left, divide it into branches.
2. Make the frying batter by directions given.
3. Dip the pieces of cauliflower into it, and put into very hot fat.
4. Fry a good colour and pile high on a dish.
5. Garnish with fried parsley and serve very hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cauliflower Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Cauliflower
- Half a Lettuce
- 2 Eggs
- 1/4 gill Oil and Vinegar

## Directions

1. Boil the cauliflower and branch it carefully.
2. Boil the eggs hard.
3. Separate the whites from the yolks.
4. Chop the whites small and cut the yolks in slices.
5. Shred up the lettuce in a bowl.
6. Put the branches of cauliflower all round it.
7. Put the slices of yolk of egg outside as a border.
8. Pour on the salad dressing.
9. Put the white of egg in little heaps on the lettuce.
10. It is then ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cauliflowers and Tomatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Cauliflowers
- ½ pint Tomato Sauce

## Directions

1. Boil the cauliflowers and make the sauce by directions given elsewhere.
2. Dish the cauliflowers carefully and pour over them the sauce, leaving just the centre of the flowers clear.
3. Put into the oven for five minutes, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cauliflowers au Gratin
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Cauliflower
- 1/2 pint White Sauce
- 2 oz. Dry Grated Cheese
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Boil the cauliflower and make the sauce by directions already given.
2. Put the cauliflower into a dish in which it can be served, put half the cheese into the white sauce, season with pepper and salt, make it hot and pour over.
3. Sprinkle the rest of the cheese on the top, and put into the oven till quite brown; it is then ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Celery Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Head of Celery
- 1 Lettuce
- Salad Dressing

## Directions

1. Pull the celery to pieces, wash it, and cut into small pieces; shred up some lettuce and lay it at the bottom of the dish.
2. Stir the celery into the dressing and lay it on the top of the lettuce.
3. Cover with more lettuce, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Celery Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 heads of Celery
- 2 quarts Pot Boilings
- 1 pint of Milk
- 1 oz. Sago

## Directions

1. If vegetables have been boiled with the meat the stock will be sufficiently flavoured; if not, boil an onion and carrot in it and strain out
2. Wash the celery thoroughly and cut it into pieces one inch long
3. Put it into the boiling stock and boil for half an hour
4. Sprinkle in 1 oz. of sago and stir until it is transparent
5. Pour in the milk and bring to boiling point
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Champagne Cider
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Good pale vinous cider, one hogshead
- Pale proof spirit, three gallons
- Honey or sugar, fourteen pounds
- Orange-flower water, three pints
- Skimmed milk, half a gallon

## Directions

1. Mix the cider, spirit, and honey or sugar well
2. Let them remain together in a temperate situation for a month
3. Add three pints of orange-flower water
4. In a few days fine it down with half a gallon of skimmed milk
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Chantilly Trifle Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a savoury cake
- half a pint of sherry wine
- whipped cream
- vanilla or lemon
- a little brandy and wine
- preserved fruits or blanched sweet almonds
- custard

## Directions

1. Take a savoury cake of the size required
2. Cut off the top and scoop out the inside to about three-quarters of an inch from the edge, having about the same thickness at the bottom
3. Put it on the dish on which it is to be served
4. Pour about half a pint of sherry wine over the sides and bottom of the cake
5. As it soaks in, pour it over until it has absorbed
6. Just before serving fill the cake with whipped cream flavoured with vanilla or lemon, or a little brandy and wine
7. Garnish the edge of the cake with preserved fruits or blanched sweet almonds cut in thin slices lengthways and stuck round
8. Custard may be substituted for the cream in filling the cake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cheese Cakes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Sour Milk
- 2 Eggs
- 1/4 lb. Flaky Pastry
- 2 oz. Sugar
- Flavouring

## Directions

1. Pour the milk through a sieve and use only the thick curd which does not run through into the basin
2. Beat in the sugar, yolks of the eggs, and flavouring to taste
3. Roll out some flaky pastry and line some patty pans with it
4. Fill them with rice or crusts of bread, and bake for about ten minutes
5. Take out the rice or crusts and fill with the cheese cake mixture
6. Finish baking, and stand on a sieve till cool
7. Sprinkle well with sugar, and serve cold
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cheese Savoury
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Dry, hard cheese
- Dry crusts of bread
- Boiling milk
- 1 egg
- Pepper and salt
- Butter

## Directions

1. Pour a little boiling milk over the bread.
2. Cover it down till quite soft.
3. Beat it with a fork.
4. Grate up the cheese and beat it in with the yolk of an egg and some pepper and salt.
5. Beat the white of the egg to a stiff froth and stir it lightly in.
6. Pour into a buttered pie-dish.
7. Bake in a quick oven for twenty minutes.
8. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cheesecakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two quarts of new milk
- two table-spoonfuls of rennet or less quantity of patent rennet
- four ounces of sugar
- three ounces of butter
- four eggs
- a little nutmeg grated
- lemon-peel
- a glass of brandy
- paste

## Directions

1. Put two quarts of new milk into a stewpan and set it near the fire.
2. Stir in two table-spoonfuls of rennet or less quantity of patent rennet and let it stand until set, about an hour.
3. Break it well when set and pour off the whey.
4. Put the curd to drain.
5. When dry, add four ounces of sugar and three ounces of butter to be oiled and stir well together.
6. Beat the yolks of four eggs in a basin with a little nutmeg grated, lemon-peel, and a glass of brandy.
7. Add this to the curd and stir well together.
8. Line tins with paste and bake.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Chejados
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of sugar
- half a pint of water
- a moderately-sized cocoa-nut
- rose-water
- the beaten yolks of six eggs
- thin nouilles pastry

## Directions

1. Clarify a pound of sugar in half a pint of water
2. Peel and grate a moderately-sized cocoa-nut, add it to the syrup
3. Let it simmer till perfectly soft, putting rose-water occasionally to prevent its becoming too dry
4. Stir it to prevent burning
5. Let it cool, and mix it with the beaten yolks of six eggs
6. Make a thin nouilles pastry, cut it into rounds the size of a tea-cup
7. Pinch up the edges deep enough to form a shape
8. Fill them with the sweetmeat, and bake of a light brown
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cherry Bounce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six pounds of morella cherries
- three pounds of loaf sugar
- two ounces of powdered bitter almonds
- two quarts of the best brandy

## Directions

1. To six pounds of morella cherries add three pounds of loaf sugar and two ounces of powdered bitter almonds
2. Pour over them two quarts of the best brandy
3. Let it remain steeped, and afterwards bottle according to taste
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cherry Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- morella cherries
- loaf sugar
- brandy

## Directions

1. Pound morella cherries in a mortar, and let them be in a cool place for twenty-four hours
2. Press the pulp through a fine tin strainer, and to every quart of juice add a pound of loaf sugar
3. Allow it to ferment the proper time, and add a little brandy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Chicken Broth
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a chicken
- about a quart of water
- salt
- a table-spoonful of rice

## Directions

1. Put half a chicken, cut into pieces, into a stewpan, with about a quart of water.
2. Let it simmer for an hour, skim well, add salt and a table-spoonful of rice.
3. Put it on the fire again for half an hour, and it will be found to be excellent broth.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Chicken Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef-suet paste
- chicken
- chorissa or sliced smoked veal
- beef gravy
- mushroom essence or powder
- pepper
- salt
- nutmeg
- mace

## Directions

1. Line a basin with a good beef-suet paste
2. Cut up a chicken and lightly fry the pieces
3. Place them in a stewpan with thin slices of chorissa or, if at hand, slices of smoked veal
4. Add enough good beef gravy to cover them
5. Season with mushroom essence or powder, pepper, salt, and a very small quantity of nutmeg and mace
6. Simmer gently for a quarter of an hour and fill the pudding
7. Pour over part of the gravy and keep the rest to be poured over the pudding when served in the dish
8. Cover the pudding closely with the paste, the end of which should be wetted with a paste-brush to make it adhere closely
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Chilli Vinegar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- an ounce of cayenne pepper or green birds'-eye chillies
- a pint of vinegar

## Directions

1. Put an ounce of cayenne pepper, or the same quantity of the green birds'-eye chillies, to a pint of vinegar, and allow the infusion to remain a month
2. You may filter it if you please, or not
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Chocolate
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- chocolate
- boiling water
- cream or milk

## Directions

1. Do not grate the chocolate, but cut the quantity required
2. Boil it in water
3. To an ounce of chocolate put an ounce and a half of boiling water
4. Add cream or milk
5. Do not allow it to boil over if you desire the flavour to be preserved
6. It must not be boiled a second time
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Chorissa Omelette
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- half a tea-cupful of finely-minced chorissa

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs well
2. Add half a tea-cupful of finely-minced chorissa to the eggs
3. Lightly fry on both sides, or hold the salamander over long enough to dress the chorissa
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Choux Pastry
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of fresh butter
- two ounces of sugar
- a little salt
- the peel of a lemon
- very dry flour
- five or six eggs

## Directions

1. Let the butter, sugar, salt, and lemon peel boil until the whole is properly melted
2. Take some very dry flour and pass through a sieve
3. Take the lemon-peel out with a ladle, and throw a handful of flour into the preparation while boiling, taking care not to put more flour than the liquor can soak up
4. Stir with a wooden spoon until the paste can be easily detached from the stewpan, and then take it off the fire
5. Break an egg into this paste, and mix it well; then break a second, which also mix
6. Do not put more eggs than the paste can absorb, but be careful not to make this preparation too liquid
7. Form them en choux, in the shape of a ball, an inch in circumference
8. Dress the choux on a baking-sheet, at an inch distance from each other
9. Brush them over with egg-wash to which has been added a little milk
10. Put them into an oven moderately hot, but do not open the oven till they are quite baked
11. Dry them, and sometimes you may glaze them, or not
12. To detach them from the baking-sheet, apply the sharp end of a knife, and take them off gently
13. Make a small opening in the side of the choux, into which put with a tea-spoon such sweetmeats as you think proper, and serve them dished en buisson
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Christmas Yule Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of fresh butter
- one pound of sugar
- one pound and a half of flour
- two pounds of currants
- a glass of brandy
- one pound of sweetmeats
- two ounces of sweet almonds
- ten eggs
- a quarter of an ounce of cinnamon
- allspice

## Directions

1. Melt the butter to a cream, and put in the sugar, stir it light, adding the allspice and cinnamon
2. Take the yolks of the eggs and work them and the whites separate
3. Work in the whites gradually, then add the orange-peel, lemon, and citron; cut in strips, add currants and almonds, which must be well mixed in
4. Add the flour and brandy
5. Bake in a tin hoop, in a hot oven for three hours, and paper the bottom to prevent its being burnt
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cider Cup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two quarts of best bottled cider
- sugar
- half a nutmeg
- a glass of brandy
- a glass of noyeau
- a lemon

## Directions

1. Mix together two quarts of best bottled cider
2. Sweeten to taste, taking care that the sugar is perfectly melted
3. Add half a nutmeg grated and a glass of brandy
4. Add a glass of noyeau
5. Cut a lemon in moderately thin slices and let them remain there
6. Make it two hours before wanted and stand it in some ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Claret Cup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sherry, six table-spoonfuls
- brandy, two table-spoonfuls
- sugar, an ounce and a half
- two or three shreds of lemon-peel
- one bottle of claret
- a bottle of soda-water
- a lump of ice
- a little borage

## Directions

1. Add sherry, brandy, sugar, and lemon-peel to one bottle of claret and a bottle of soda-water
2. Keep in a cool place or refrigerator and only open before drinking
3. Add a lump of ice and a little borage as improvements
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Claret Cup No. 1
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of ordinary claret
- a bottle of soda-water
- a glass of sherry or curacoa
- the peel of a lemon
- powdered sugar
- lumps of clear ice

## Directions

1. To each bottle of ordinary claret add a bottle of soda-water
2. Add a glass of sherry or curacoa and the peel of a lemon cut very thin
3. Add powdered sugar according to taste
4. Let the whole remain an hour or two before serving
5. Then add some lumps of clear ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Claret Cup No. 2
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of ordinary claret
- a bottle of soda-water
- a glass of sherry or curacoa
- the peel of a lemon
- powdered sugar
- a few slices of cucumber or some sprigs of borage
- lumps of clear ice

## Directions

1. To each bottle of ordinary claret add a bottle of soda-water
2. Add a glass of sherry or curacoa and the peel of a lemon cut very thin
3. Add powdered sugar according to taste
4. Add a few slices of cucumber, or some sprigs of borage instead of the cucumber
5. Let the whole remain an hour or two before serving
6. Then add some lumps of clear ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Claret Cup No. 3
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of ordinary claret
- a bottle of soda-water
- a glass of sherry or curacoa
- a pint of ripe raspberries or four or five peaches or nectarines
- powdered sugar
- lumps of clear ice

## Directions

1. To each bottle of ordinary claret add a bottle of soda-water
2. Add a glass of sherry or curacoa
3. Substitute a pint of ripe raspberries or four or five peaches or nectarines cut in slices for the lemon-peel
4. Add powdered sugar according to taste
5. Let the whole remain an hour or two before serving
6. Then add some lumps of clear ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Coagulated Milk
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- new milk
- patent rennet
- wine
- sugar
- nutmeg

## Directions

1. Coagulate new milk with patent rennet
2. Serve with a little wine, sugar, and nutmeg
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cobbett's Yeast Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- seven pounds of Indian corn meal
- three and a half pounds of rye flour
- three ounces of hops
- one gallon of boiling water

## Directions

1. Separate the hops by the hand, strew and boil them in the water for half an hour
2. Strain the liquor into an earthen vessel
3. Put in the rye while hot, stirring quickly as the fermentation commences
4. Next day, when it is working, put in the Indian meal, stirring it well
5. Knead it well, and roll it out as you would a pie-crust, to the thickness of the third of an inch
6. Cut it into cakes with a tumbler
7. Place these cakes in the sun to dry
8. Turn them every day until they are hard
9. Keep them in a dry place
10. When you bake, take a couple of these cakes, break them and put them into warm water over-night
11. Let the vessel containing them stand near the fire; they will dissolve
12. Use the liquid for setting your sponge, just as you would the yeast of beer
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Coburg Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of flour
- a pint of new milk
- six ounces of sugar
- six ounces of butter
- six ounces of currants
- six eggs
- a little brandy
- grated nutmeg

## Directions

1. Mix six ounces of flour to a smooth batter with a pint of new milk
2. Add six ounces of sugar, six ounces of butter, six ounces of currants, six eggs, a little brandy and grated nutmeg
3. When well mixed put it into moulds
4. Bake for an hour and turn the puddings out in a dish
5. Serve with sweet sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cock a Leekie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four pounds of lean beef
- a fowl
- leeks
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Boil four pounds of lean beef until the liquor is good
2. Take out the meat, and put into the soup a fowl cut into pieces, with half the quantity of leeks intended to be used
3. Let it simmer
4. Half an hour before serving, add the remainder of the leeks, and the seasoning of pepper and salt
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cock-A-Leekie Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 9 leeks
- 1 set giblets
- 2 ounces beef dripping
- 3 quarts water or pot boilings
- salt and peppercorns
- bacon rind

## Directions

1. Wash and slice up the leeks into pieces about one inch long
2. Put them into a saucepan with the butter or dripping made thoroughly hot
3. Cover over and let them cook for half an hour, stirring occasionally
4. While they are cooking clean the giblets thoroughly, washing them first in hot and then in cold water
5. Cut open the gizzard, remove the stones, and cleanse well
6. Cut them all up into small pieces and put them into the saucepan with the leeks
7. Pour over the boiling water or liquor
8. Put in the peppercorns tied in a piece of muslin, and a piece of bacon rind if there is any in the larder
9. Let it simmer slowly for three hours
10. If not brown enough add a few drops of caramel
11. Take out the peppercorns and bacon rind
12. Season to taste
13. Pour into a hot tureen and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Codfish and Potatoes-Bouillabaisse of Cod
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Murray Cod
- 1 lb. Potatoes
- Slices of Roll
- 1 quart Water
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 2 Leeks or 1 Onion
- Pinch of Saffron
- 1.5 oz. Butter

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is hot add the leeks or onion chopped small, and let them get a good colour without burning; then add a quart of water, the fagot of herbs, the saffron tied in a piece of muslin, and the potatoes peeled.
2. Bring up to the boil, and when they are nearly cooked cut the cod into slices and lay it in.
3. Cook slowly for twenty minutes, take up the fish, and put it in a hot dish and lay the potatoes round.
4. Season and flavour the liquor, and boil up.
5. Cut the bread into slices, put it into a hot dish, and strain the liquor over; serve with the fish.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cods' Roes In Tomato Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Roes
- 1 gill Tomato Sauce
- Cayenne
- 3 slices Toast
- 1 Egg
- Nutmeg and Salt

## Directions

1. Wash the roes well, cover with cold water seasoned with salt, and boil for half an hour, or longer if the roes are large
2. Take them up and stand away till cold, then cut into slices about half an inch thick
3. Make some tomato sauce by recipe given elsewhere
4. When it is boiling, season with cayenne, nutmeg, and salt
5. Stir in the yolk of an egg, lay in the slices of roe, cover down until hot through
6. Cut the toast into as many pieces as there are slices of roe, stand them in a dish, and put on each some roe
7. Make the sauce very hot, pour it over, and serve at once
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Colcannon
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cold boiled cabbage
- Cold boiled potatoes
- Pepper
- Salt
- Butter

## Directions

1. Take any remains of cold boiled cabbage and potatoes, and cut them into small pieces, season with pepper and salt.
2. Put a small piece of butter into a frying pan; put in the vegetables and fry them until they are thoroughly hot through and well mixed.
3. Turn them on to a hot dish, make into a neat pile, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cold Fish Au Gratin
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- scraps of cold fish
- 1 gill of milk or water
- 1 oz. of butter
- 1 oz. of flour
- brown bread crumbs

## Directions

1. If any fish sauce is left, warm it in; if not, make a little with 1 gill of milk or water, 1 oz. of butter, and 1 oz. of flour.
2. Flake the fish up, butter a plate, put the fish in and pour the sauce over.
3. Sprinkle with brown bread crumbs, and bake in the oven for a quarter of an hour.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cold Tea Summer Drink
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cold tea
- sliced lemon
- a wine-glass of good brandy
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Properly make the tea by pouring boiling water on the leaves
2. Allow to stand five minutes
3. Pour into a jug with slices of lemon at the bottom
4. Add a wine-glass of good brandy when cool
5. Add sugar to taste
6. Do not add milk
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cold Turkey, Goose, Fowl, &c.
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- egg
- grated nutmeg
- pepper
- salt
- parsley
- sweet herbs
- crumbs of bread
- flour
- ketchup
- lemon

## Directions

1. Cut them in quarters
2. Beat up an egg, with some grated nutmeg, pepper, salt, and parsley fine, a few sweet herbs, and crumbs of bread
3. Mix all together, and cover the fowl, &c.
4. Fry in a pan until well warmed
5. Thicken a little gravy with flour and ketchup
6. Lay the poultry fry on the dish, and garnish with slices of lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Collared Eels
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Eels
- 1 Egg
- 1 oz. Gelatine
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 1 Onion
- 1 Carrot
- 1 spoonful Vinegar
- Pepper and Salt
- Salt
- 2 dozen peppercorns

## Directions

1. Clean the eels, cut them into pieces 2 inches long
2. Put them in cold water well seasoned with salt, 2 dozen peppercorns and the vegetables, and a spoonful of vinegar
3. Bring to the boil, and skim well
4. Boil steadily for an hour, or longer if the eels are large
5. Take out the fish, slip out the bones, and cut the meat into small pieces
6. Put back the bones and boil the liquor quickly without the lid for half an hour, then strain off
7. Dissolve the gelatine in a little cold water or gravy and stir in
8. Rinse a mould in cold water, arrange in it the pieces of eel and a hard boiled egg cut into slices with a few sprigs of parsley
9. Strain the liquor over and stand away till cold
10. Turn out and serve with a salad
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: College Puddings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four eggs
- two ounces of flour
- a little nutmeg and ginger
- three ounces of white sugar
- six ounces of suet, chopped fine
- six ounces of currants, well washed and cleaned
- a glass of white wine or brandy

## Directions

1. Beat up four eggs with two ounces of flour, a little nutmeg and ginger, and three ounces of white sugar.
2. Beat to a batter.
3. Add six ounces of suet, chopped fine, six ounces of currants, well washed and cleaned, with a glass of white wine or brandy.
4. Bake in an oven or patty-pan, or boil and serve with wine sauce.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Colonial Jam and Molasses
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mangold
- grated carrot, about one-third of the bulk of mangold juice
- essence of lemon or vanilla

## Directions

1. Wash and pare the mangold
2. Grate on a coarse grater
3. Express the juice through a cheese cloth
4. Add to the juice obtained about one-third of its bulk of grated carrot
5. Simmer gently for about forty-eight hours
6. Add a drop or two of essence of lemon or vanilla to improve it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Comfit Buns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound and a half of fresh butter
- warm water
- three-quarters of a pint of good yeast
- a wine-glassful of rose-water
- two pounds of flour
- three-quarters of a pound of sifted sugar
- half a pound of carraway comfits

## Directions

1. Melt the butter in a little warm water
2. Add three-quarters of a pint of good yeast and a wine-glassful of rose-water
3. Pour this mixture on two pounds of flour mixed with three-quarters of a pound of sifted sugar
4. Knead the whole into a light paste with half a pound of carraway comfits
5. Make it into buns and put a few comfits on the top of each
6. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Common Nectar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of loaf sugar
- one pint of cold water
- a bottle of hock
- one bottle of Madeira
- half a nutmeg
- a drop or two of essence of lemon

## Directions

1. Put half a pound of loaf sugar into a large jug
2. add one pint of cold water
3. stir the sugar till dissolved
4. pour over it a bottle of hock and one bottle of Madeira
5. Mix them well together, and grate in half a nutmeg, with a drop or two of essence of lemon
6. Set the jug in a bucket of ice for an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Common Seed Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Two pounds and a half of flour
- Half a pound of white sugar
- Half a pint of lukewarm milk
- Two tablespoonfuls of yeast
- Half a pound of fresh butter
- An ounce of carraway seeds
- Warm milk to make it a middling stiffness

## Directions

1. Take two pounds and a half of flour, and half a pound of white sugar; put it into a bowl, and make a cavity in the centre, and pour in half a pint of lukewarm milk, and two tablespoonfuls of yeast
2. Set it in a warm place for an hour
3. Melt to an oil half a pound of fresh butter, and add to the other ingredients, with an ounce of carraway seeds, and warm milk to make it a middling stiffness
4. Put it into a round tin, and allow it to rise again a short time
5. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Compote of Apples
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- apples
- water
- sugar
- a glass of wine
- a little lemon acid

## Directions

1. Cut apples in halves or quarters, according to their size
2. Put them in to stew with sufficient water and sugar to taste
3. Add a glass of wine, and a little lemon acid
4. Simmer, and when sufficiently done, serve in their own syrup
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Conger Soup in the Jersey Mode
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- five pounds of conger
- three pints of water
- new milk
- green peas

## Directions

1. Take five pounds of conger to three pints of water
2. Cut the conger into small pieces and place them in a boiler with the water until the liquid is reduced to two-thirds of its original quantity
3. Strain it
4. Add to it the same quantity of new milk as there is of soup
5. Warm this up again
6. Throw in some green peas, which should be boiled up in the soup until thoroughly done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cool Tankard
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of mild ale
- a wine-glassful of white wine
- a wine-glassful of brandy
- a wine-glassful of capillaire
- the juice of a lemon
- some of the rind
- a sprig of balm or borage
- some toasted bread
- nutmeg

## Directions

1. Put to a quart of mild ale a wine-glassful of white wine, the same of brandy and capillaire, the juice of a lemon, and some of the rind
2. Add a sprig of balm or borage, some toasted bread, and nutmeg grated on the top
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Corn Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two cups of Indian corn meal
- one cup of flour
- two eggs
- large spoonful of melted butter
- two small tea-spoons of cream of tartar
- one tea-spoon of soda
- one tea-spoon of sugar

## Directions

1. Mix
2. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cornflour Custard
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pint Milk
- 1 Egg
- 1 dessertspoonful Cornflour
- Sugar
- Flavouring

## Directions

1. Put the milk into a saucepan to boil
2. Mix the cornflour with a spoonful of cold milk
3. When the milk in the saucepan is nearly boiling, stir it in and continue stirring till it boils
4. Let it boil two or three minutes
5. Draw the saucepan away from the fire, beat in the yolk of the egg and flavouring
6. Put back on the fire and bring to boiling point
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Corstorphine Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the milk of three or four days together
- cream
- sugar
- strawberries

## Directions

1. Putting the milk of three or four days together, with the cream, into a vessel, and allowing it to remain there until it becomes sour and coagulated
2. The whey is then drawn off and fresh cream added
3. when it is brought to table it is eaten with sugar, and, in the strawberry season, with that fruit
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Corstorphine Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- milk of three or four days
- cream
- sugar
- strawberry fruit (in season)

## Directions

1. Put the milk of three or four days together, with the cream, into a vessel
2. Allow it to remain there until it becomes sour and coagulated
3. Draw off the whey
4. Add fresh cream
5. When brought to table, eat with sugar, and in the strawberry season with that fruit
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cosmopolitan Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- fruits in season such as oranges, mandarins, passion fruit, apricots, nectarines, pine-apples, bananas
- sugar

## Directions

1. Take any fruits in season, such as oranges, mandarins, passion fruit, apricots, nectarines, pine-apples, bananas, &c.
2. Peel and slice them up, and put them into a glass dish in layers, with plenty of sugar between each layer
3. Stand in a cool place for an hour covered over, and it is ready to serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Cotelettes à la Soubise
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ten or more cutlets
- larding
- good braise
- small stewed onions
- puree of onions

## Directions

1. Take ten cutlets, or more, according to your party; do not pare or shape them, and take care that each cutlet has a bone
2. Lard them, and season your larding before you use it
3. Stew them in good braise for three or four hours
4. When done put them on a tin plate, with another cover on it, on which you place a weight to make the cutlets a good shape
5. When they are cold trim them neatly and fry them
6. When you serve them, place them either in a crown, or one bone over the other in a sort of pyramid, with small stewed onions round, and in the middle a puree of onions
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cottage Bread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fourteen pounds of flour
- one quart of warm water
- one quarter of a pint of good yeast
- three teaspoonfuls of salt

## Directions

1. Put fourteen pounds of flour into a pan
2. Add to one quart of warm water a quarter of a pint of good yeast
3. Make a hole in the flour, pour in the water and the yeast
4. Stir it well with a wooden spoon until it forms a thickish paste
5. Throw a little flour over, and leave it in a warm place
6. In about an hour when it has risen and burst through the covering of flour, add more warm water and three teaspoonfuls of salt
7. Knead until it forms a rather stiff dough, working it as much as possible
8. Let it remain, covered with a cloth, for an hour
9. Divide it into loaves
10. Bake for an hour and a quarter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cottage Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six quarts of water
- one pound of bacon
- a proportionate quantity of carrots
- turnips
- cabbages
- leeks
- onions
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Fill an earthen pot or saucepan with six quarts of water
2. Add one pound of bacon with a proportionate quantity of carrots, turnips, cabbages, leeks, and onions
3. Season with pepper and salt
4. Simmer for five or six hours
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cow-heel Boiled
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cow-heel
- onions
- milk
- salt
- parsley
- butter
- ham, bacon, or corned pork

## Directions

1. Let it be perfectly clean.
2. Boil it well for five or six hours, together with five or six onions and a quart of milk.
3. Add a little salt.
4. Serve with parsley and butter, and a little ham, bacon, or corned pork.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cracknels (American)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one quart of flour
- half a nutmeg
- yolks of four eggs
- two spoonfuls of rose-water
- cold water
- one pound of butter

## Directions

1. Mix flour with grated nutmeg
2. Beat the yolks of eggs with two spoonfuls of rose-water
3. Make into a stiff paste with sufficient cold water
4. Roll in a pound of butter
5. Make them into cracknel shapes
6. Put them into a kettle of boiling water
7. Boil them until they can swim
8. Take them out and place them in cold water
9. When hardened, lay them out to dry
10. Bake on tin plates
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cramhamball
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two bottles of porter or ale
- half a pint of rum
- from half a pound to a pound of white sugar
- the whites and yolks of six eggs

## Directions

1. Boil two bottles of porter or ale
2. Add half a pint of rum and from half a pound to a pound of white sugar
3. After this has boiled remove it from the fire and put into the mixture the whites and yolks of six eggs previously well whisked
4. Stir it for a minute and serve in a punch-bowl
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Crayfish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- crayfish
- milk
- mace
- onion
- cayenne
- salt
- lemon
- butter
- flour
- cream

## Directions

1. Boil, in a pint of milk, two blades of mace, one onion sliced, cayenne, and salt, with lemon, a little butter and flour, and a gill of cream
2. Serve with crayfish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cream Toast
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 slices Toast
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 pint White Sauce

## Directions

1. Make the toast and lay it in a dish.
2. Make the sauce by directions given for white sauce.
3. Season with pepper and salt, and pour over it; serve hot.
4. If a richer dish is desired, a little butter may be put on the toast.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Crecy Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Carrots
- 2 oz. Butter
- 1 Onion
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar
- 1 teaspoonful Salt
- 1 Turnip
- 1 stalk of Celery
- 3 pints of Boiling Water

## Directions

1. Slice up the carrots and vegetables, put them into boiling water, and cook for half-an-hour.
2. Strain them out of the water, which must be saved, and put them into a saucepan with the butter and a few scraps of bacon, if any are in the larder.
3. Sprinkle over the sugar, make very hot, and cover down closely until the vegetables are very soft.
4. Rub them through a sieve and pour on by degrees the water in which the vegetables were boiled; mix well together, return to a saucepan, and boil slowly for an hour.
5. Stir in a small piece of butter and it is ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Creme a l'Eau
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- rind of a lemon
- sugar
- a pint of water
- yolks of eight eggs

## Directions

1. Grate a rind of a lemon, and add to it, with sugar, a pint of water
2. boil, and then add the yolks of eight eggs, well beaten
3. when cool, put in a bowl in a saucepan of hot water, till done enough
4. pour into the dish on which you serve it, garnished with slices of lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Crimping Salmon
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- salmon
- cold spring water
- salt and water

## Directions

1. Stun the fish by a blow on the head
2. Cut cross-wise just below the gills
3. Crimp by cutting to the bone on each side, so as almost to divide into slices
4. Put into cold spring water for ten minutes
5. Put slice by slice into a pot of salt and water, boiling furiously, allowing time for the water to recover its heat after the throwing in of each slice
6. Leave the head out, and throw in the thickest pieces first
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Crisp Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of flour
- the yolk of an egg
- milk

## Directions

1. Mix into a stiff paste
2. Beat it well
3. Knead it smooth
4. Roll the paste thin
5. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Croquettes de Veau, on de Volaille
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of veal, chicken, or turkey
- ten ounces of butter
- a table-spoonful of fine flour
- yolks of two eggs
- a little milk
- the juice of a lemon
- a tea-cupful of stock
- pepper
- salt
- a little nutmeg grated
- a little lemon-peel
- bread crumbs
- lard
- parsley

## Directions

1. Mince two pounds of veal, chicken, or turkey
2. Melt ten ounces of butter in a stewpan
3. When hot add a table-spoonful of fine flour and mix
4. Beat the yolks of two eggs with a little milk and the juice of a lemon
5. Mix it with the flour and butter by degrees
6. Add the minced meat with a tea-cupful of stock
7. Season with pepper, salt, a little nutmeg grated, and a little lemon-peel
8. Stir it together and let it boil till of proper thickness
9. Pour it into a basin till cold
10. Grate some bread on a pasteboard
11. Cut the mince into pieces of the same size and roll them lightly in the bread crumbs
12. Beat the yolk of an egg or two and dip the croquettes into this
13. Roll them again in the bread crumbs, making them a neat shape like a pear
14. Have some lard melted and put the croquettes in
15. Fry them a light brown
16. Drain them on a colander and keep them hot
17. Serve with garnished parsley
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cross Buns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- butter
- yeast
- spice
- an ounce of spice

## Directions

1. Make similar to plain buns
2. Cross when baked
3. Add an ounce of spice to the mixture
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cross Buns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- half a pound of moist sugar
- half a gill of good yeast
- half a pint of lukewarm milk
- half a pound of butter
- an ounce of spice

## Directions

1. Make similar to plain buns
2. Add an ounce of spice to the mixture
3. Cross when baked
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Crowdie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of oatmeal
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Put half a pint of oatmeal into a porringer, with a little salt, and thicken the crowdie sufficiently with meal.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Crumpets
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound and a half of flour
- three pints of milk
- two table-spoonfuls of yeast
- two eggs

## Directions

1. Add three pints of milk to a pound and a half of flour
2. Add two table-spoonfuls of yeast, and two eggs
3. Mix the milk lukewarm
4. Beat it into a batter
5. Let it stand until it rises into bladders
6. Bake on polished iron in the tin rims
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Curaçoa
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of thin-cut Seville oranges
- a pint of genuine whisky
- clarified syrup
- a drachm of powdered alum
- a drachm of carbonate of potash

## Directions

1. Put six ounces of thin-cut Seville oranges into a quart bottle, with a pint of genuine whisky
2. Cork it tightly, and let the rind infuse for ten or twelve days
3. Take out the peel, and fill up the bottle with clarified syrup
4. Shake it well, and let it remain for three days
