5. Pour a tea-spoonful of the liqueur into a mortar, and mix with it a drachm of powdered alum, and the same quantity of carbonate of potash
6. Pour this into the bottle, shake it well, and the curaçoa will be bright and well-flavoured
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Currant and Raspberry Jelly
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of ripe currants
- a pound of raspberries
- a spoonful of water
- sugar
- a little isinglass

## Directions

1. Take two pounds of ripe currants and a pound of raspberries, put them on a stone in a preserving-pan, with a spoonful of water
2. slightly mash them with a wooden spoon, and strain them through a napkin
3. Mix this juice with sugar and a little isinglass
4. boil and skim, and put it in a mould to set
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Currant Dumplings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three table-spoonfuls of flour
- two table-spoonfuls of finely-minced beef suet
- two table-spoonfuls of currants
- a little salt
- milk or water

## Directions

1. Mix flour, suet, and currants with salt and as much milk or water as will make a batter of the ingredients.
2. Tie in a well-floured cloth and boil for an hour.
3. Serve plain or with sweet sauce.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Curried Chops
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Chops
- 1/2 oz. Curry Powder
- 1 oz. Butter
- Lemon Juice
- 1/4 lb. Rice
- 1/2 pint Gravy or Water
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 Apple
- 1 Onion
- Salt

## Directions

1. Trim some of the fat away from the chops
2. Put the butter into a stewpan, put in the chops and brown them quickly
3. Take out, chop up the apple and onion, and fry that too
4. Sprinkle with the curry powder and flour, pour in the stock or water and stir until it boils, then put back the chops
5. Bring to the boil, and simmer very gently for three hours
6. Dish carefully, boil up the gravy, and if it is not thick enough boil quickly without the lid for some minutes
7. Season with salt and lemon juice and pour over the chops
8. Boil the rice by directions given elsewhere
9. Rinse out a small mould or cup in cold water, press the rice into it, and turn out
10. Serve this in a separate dish, but send it to table with the curry
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Curried Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Bream
- 0.5 pint Curry Sauce
- 1 lb. Rice

## Directions

1. Make the curry sauce by recipe given elsewhere.
2. Fillet the fish and cut each fillet in two pieces, butter a saucepan and lay in the fish.
3. Pour over the sauce, bring it up to the boil, and cook on the stove very slowly for an hour.
4. Just before serving, season with salt and lemon juice to taste.
5. Boil the rice and dry thoroughly; press into little cups or moulds.
6. Dish the fish carefully and pour the sauce over it; garnish with the moulds of rice.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Curried Tomatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- tomatoes
- butter
- milk
- flour
- rice
- apple
- onion
- curry powder
- salt

## Directions

1. Mince the onion and apple finely, and fry in the butter till a good colour
2. Sprinkle over it the curry powder and flour, and mix well
3. Pour in the milk and stir until it boils
4. Slice the tomatoes and put them in and simmer very gently for half an hour
5. Season with salt, dish carefully and serve either in a border of rice, or with rice moulds on a separate dish
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Curried Vegetables
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- potatoes
- peas
- carrots
- beans
- cauliflowers
- salt
- sugar
- butter
- flour
- curry powder
- milk
- boiled rice

## Directions

1. Take any vegetables in season, such as potatoes, peas, carrots, beans, and cauliflowers
2. Slice up the potatoes and branch the cauliflowers
3. If they have not been boiled before, boil them in water seasoned with a little salt and sugar, for ten minutes, and strain off the water
4. Put one ounce or more of butter into a saucepan according to the quantity of vegetables, and when hot stir in half an ounce of flour, and the same of curry powder
5. Pour in half a pint of milk and stir till it boils
6. Put in the vegetables and simmer very gently for about half an hour
7. They should not be broken, but quite soft, and all the liquor absorbed
8. Pile in a hot dish and serve with boiled rice
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Curry
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- meats, fowls, or rabbits
- onion
- curry stuff
- flour
- lemon
- butter
- ketchup

## Directions

1. Cut meats, fowls, or rabbits into small joints, and fry them brown in a pan with an onion chopped up
2. When half done take them out and put them into a stewpan, with two table-spoonfuls of curry stuff and a dredge of flour, and let it simmer with the juice of half a lemon, a table-spoonful of butter, and a little ketchup
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Curry Powder
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Mustard seed, one ounce and a half
- Coriander, four ounces
- Turmeric, four and a quarter ounces
- Black pepper, two and a half ounces
- Cayenne, one ounce and a quarter
- Ginger, half an ounce
- Cinnamon, quarter of an ounce
- Cloves, quarter of an ounce
- Mace, quarter of an ounce

## Directions

1. Well pound all ingredients
2. Mix together
3. Keep in a stoppered bottle in a dry place
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Curry Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Onion
- 1 Apple
- 1 oz. Flour
- Lemon Juice
- Salt
- 1 oz. Curry Powder
- 1 oz. Butter or Dripping
- 1 pint Gravy

## Directions

1. Peel and chop up the apple and onion.
2. Put the butter or dripping into a saucepan, and when it is melted put in the apple and onion, and fry for a few minutes; sprinkle over the curry powder and the flour.
3. Pour over the gravy and stir until it boils.
4. Simmer for half an hour, then strain, flavour with lemon juice and salt, boil up, and it is ready.
5. If this sauce is for fish, use milk or fish stock instead of gravy.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Custards
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint and a half of milk
- a bit of lemon-peel
- a couple of peach-leaves
- a salt-spoonful of salt
- two ounces and a half of powdered white sugar
- the yolks of eight eggs

## Directions

1. Put a pint and a half of milk on the fire in a clean saucepan
2. When it boils add a bit of lemon-peel, a couple of peach-leaves, a salt-spoonful of salt, and two ounces and a half of powdered white sugar
3. Let the milk simmer until it has acquired the flavour of the lemon-peel and peach-leaves, but keep stirring it all the time, or it will burn
4. Use a wooden spoon for this purpose
5. Have the yolks of eight eggs ready beaten up, put them into the milk, and stir the whole over the fire until the custard is thick
6. If it boils or becomes too hot it will curdle
7. When cold put into glasses
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cutlets a la Maintenon
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal cutlets or mutton or lamb-chops
- egg yolk
- bread crumbs
- sweet herbs

## Directions

1. Cut veal cutlets, mutton, or lamb-chops, about half an inch thick, and flatten them
2. Brush them over with the yolk of an egg
3. Put over them crumbs of bread and sweet herbs, quant. suff.
4. Fold each cutlet in a separate piece of paper
5. Fry them until nearly done
6. Take them out of the pan, and put them on the gridiron to finish and dry
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Cyprus Wine (Imitation)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ten quarts of syrup of elderberry
- eighty pints of water
- two ounces of ginger
- two ounces of cloves
- a few bruised grapes

## Directions

1. To ten quarts of syrup of elderberry add eighty pints of water, two ounces of ginger, and two ounces of cloves, and boil together
2. Add a few bruised grapes and strain
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: De Mestre Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 1 lb. raisins
- 1 lb. sugar
- 1 oz. dripping
- 1 teaspoonful carbonate of soda
- 1 gill boiling water

## Directions

1. Put the flour into a basin; stone the raisins and cut them in half, mix in the sugar and carbonate of soda.
2. Dissolve the dripping in the water, pour in and make into a dough, leave it to stand all night.
3. Dip a cloth in boiling water and tie the pudding up tightly.
4. Plunge into plenty of boiling water, and keep it boiling steadily for three hours; turn into a hot dish.
5. A little custard sauce served with this pudding is a great improvement.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Delhi Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 1 oz. Almonds
- 2 oz. Sugar
- 1 1/2 oz. Arrowroot

## Directions

1. Blanch and chop the almonds very small
2. Mix them with the sugar and arrowroot
3. Put the milk on to boil
4. When it boils pour it on to the arrowroot and stir
5. If it does not get thick enough, pour back into the saucepan and boil for a minute
6. Turn into a wet mould and stand away till firm
7. Turn out and serve with jam or custard sauce, or it may be served plainly
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Derby Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of butter
- a pound of flour
- half a pound of currants
- half a pound of sugar
- one egg
- a quarter of a pint of milk

## Directions

1. Rub half a pound of butter into a pound of flour
2. Put in half a pound of currants, half a pound of sugar, and one egg
3. Mix all together with a quarter of a pint of milk
4. Cut them into round cakes
5. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Descaides
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- livers of chickens or any other poultry
- good gravy
- a little onion
- mushroom essence
- pepper
- salt
- yolks of one or two eggs
- a round of toast

## Directions

1. Stew the livers gently in a little good gravy, seasoned with a little onion, mushroom essence, pepper and salt
2. When tender remove the livers, place them on a pasteboard, and mince them
3. Return them to the saucepan, and stir in the yolks of one or two eggs, according to the quantity of liver, until the gravy becomes thick
4. Have a round of toast ready on a hot plate, and serve it on the toast
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Devilled Ale
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a slice of bread about an inch thick
- butter
- cayenne pepper
- ginger
- a pint of warm ale
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Cut a slice of bread about an inch thick, toast and butter it
2. Sprinkle it with cayenne pepper and ginger and place in the bottom of a jug
3. Add a pint of warm ale and sugar to taste
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Devilled Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- common ship's biscuit or captain's biscuit
- lukewarm water
- butter
- cayenne
- anchovy or herring paste

## Directions

1. Soak the biscuit for a quarter of an hour in lukewarm water
2. Toast them and butter well
3. Add cayenne and anchovy or herring paste
4. Hold before the fire in a cheese-toaster for a few minutes
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Devilled Meat
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 teaspoonful Mustard
- 1 teaspoonful Worcester Sauce
- 2 teaspoonsful Vinegar
- 1/2 oz. Butter
- 1 teaspoonful Oil
- 1 teaspoonful Lemon Juice
- 1/2 teaspoonful Curry Powder
- Mashed Potatoes
- 1/2 gill Gravy
- Slices of Cold Meat

## Directions

1. Put the mustard, made with vinegar instead of water, into a basin.
2. Add gradually the oil and butter, curry powder, sauce, vinegar, and lemon juice, and mix very thoroughly, then pour in the gravy.
3. Cut some slices of underdone meat and lay them in a pie dish, pour over the mixture, cover with a plate, and stand in a hot oven for ten minutes.
4. Stir frequently.
5. Serve with mashed potatoes.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Devilled Sardines
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 tin sardines
- 1 oz. mustard
- vinegar
- cayenne
- salt
- dripping
- buttered toast

## Directions

1. Make the mustard with vinegar instead of water
2. Stir into it some cayenne and salt
3. Rub the sardines over with this mixture
4. Either fry them in a little dripping or grill them
5. Cut the toast into fingers
6. Lay a sardine on each piece and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Devonshire Junket
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lukewarm milk
- rennet
- clouted cream
- sugar
- cinnamon
- wine or brandy
- nutmeg

## Directions

1. Put lukewarm milk into a bowl, and turn it with rennet
2. then add some clouted cream, sugar, and cinnamon on the top, without breaking the curd
3. then drain the milk, and add a little wine or brandy
4. When turned, put sugar and nutmeg on the top, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Devonshire Junket
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart Milk
- 1 tablespoonful Rennet
- 1 oz. Sugar
- Nutmeg

## Directions

1. Make the milk tepid, stir in the sugar and a spoonful of rennet or a rennet tablet ; pour into a dish and stand on the stove till solid.
2. Grate a little nutmeg on top and serve cold.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Devonshire Squab Pie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mutton-chops or mutton
- onions
- pepper
- salt
- sugar
- water
- apples
- potatoes
- crust

## Directions

1. Lay mutton-chops or mutton at the bottom of the dish
2. On the meat strew some onions, with pepper, salt, a little sugar, and half a tea-cupful of water
3. Place on the top apples and potatoes, in layers, cut thin
4. Cover the sides and top of the dish with crust
5. Bake well
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Digestive Biscuits
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. Brown Meal
- 1/4 lb. Flour
- 1 1/2 gills Water
- 1 oz. Butter or Lard
- 1 oz. Sugar

## Directions

1. Mix the meal and flour together
2. Rub in the butter or lard and the sugar
3. Mix into a dry paste with water
4. Knead till smooth
5. Roll out very thin
6. Cut into rounds
7. Bake in rather a slow oven
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Dog's Rose
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of ale
- a wine-glassful of gin
- half a pint of cold ale
- toast

## Directions

1. Warm half a pint of ale, and add a wine-glassful of gin to it
2. Add half a pint of cold ale, and serve with toast
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Dr. Kitchener's Barley Broth (Scotch)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three-quarters of a pound of Scotch barley
- a shin or leg of beef of about eight pounds' weight, or the same weight of mutton
- cold water
- two onions
- two heads of celery
- a large turnip
- pepper and salt
- a glass of port wine

## Directions

1. Wash three-quarters of a pound of Scotch barley in a little cold water.
2. Put it into a soup-pot, or digester, with a shin or leg of beef of about eight pounds' weight, or the same weight of mutton, sawn in pieces.
3. Cover it well with cold water.
4. Set it on the fire; when it boils, skim it, and put in two onions.
5. Let it simmer for about a couple of hours.
6. Put in two heads of celery, and a large turnip cut into pieces.
7. Let it simmer a short time longer, and take out the meat to serve separate with the broth.
8. When the meat is taken out, season with pepper and salt, and a glass of port wine may be added or not.
9. The meat can be served with caper sauce or minced gherkins or walnuts.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Dr. Kitchener's Sauce Superlative
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of claret
- one pint of port wine
- one pint of mushroom ketchup
- half a pint of walnut liquor
- four ounces of pounded anchovies
- one ounce of fresh lemon-peel finely pared
- one ounce of sliced shalots
- one ounce of scraped horseradish
- half an ounce of allspice and black pepper
- one drachm of cayenne
- three drachms of curry-powder
- one drachm of celery seed bruised

## Directions

1. Put into a wide-mouthed bottle
2. Shake every day for a fortnight
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Drink Divine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of cider
- half a bottle of perry
- half a bottle of sherry
- half a gill of brandy
- one sliced lemon with rind pared thin
- one toasted biscuit

## Directions

1. Mix cider, perry, and sherry with half a gill of brandy
2. Add a sliced lemon with rind pared thin
3. Add a toasted biscuit
4. Drink iced or cooled
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Drink Superb
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one wine-glass of brandy
- more than half lemonade
- green lime shrub

## Directions

1. Put a wine-glass of brandy into a tumbler
2. Add more than half lemonade
3. Fill up with green lime shrub
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Dry Salting for Early Use
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Two pounds of salt
- One ounce and a half of saltpetre
- One ounce good moist sugar

## Directions

1. Rub in and turn occasionally
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Dutch Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef
- brown sugar
- a pound of salt
- a quarter of a pound of saltpetre
- an ounce of juniper berries

## Directions

1. Rub the beef well with brown sugar
2. Mix a pound of salt with a quarter of a pound of saltpetre and an ounce of juniper berries
3. Rub the beef well in with this mixture in the pan for ten days
4. Press the beef
5. Dry and smoke it
6. Boil it in a cloth and do not remove it until cold
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Dutch Horseradish Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- some horseradish
- some stock
- some flour
- butter
- a little vinegar

## Directions

1. Scrape some horseradish fine
2. Put it in a stewpan with some stock
3. Let it stew
4. Add some flour, butter, and a little vinegar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: East Indian Salad Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Eggs
- 1 teaspoonful Curry Powder
- 1/4 gill Oil
- 1/4 gill Vinegar

## Directions

1. Boil the eggs hard.
2. Put the yolks into a bowl and work them till they are quite smooth.
3. Work in gradually the curry powder, oil, and vinegar.
4. Blend well, and it is ready.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Easter Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- one pound of butter melted
- half a pound of currants
- one pound of moist sugar
- four yolks of eggs
- a table-spoonful of brandy
- a little cinnamon

## Directions

1. Mix two pounds of flour, one pound of butter melted, half a pound of currants, one pound of moist sugar, four yolks of eggs, and a table-spoonful of brandy
2. Add a little cinnamon
3. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Eel And Tomato Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Eel
- 6 Tomatoes
- 2 oz. Veal Seasoning
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Flour
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Stuff and cook the eel as in the preceding recipe, and strain off the liquor
2. Rub the tomatoes through a sieve
3. Mix with half a pint of the liquor in which the fish was cooked
4. Make a sauce of this with the butter and flour, season with pepper and salt, and pour it over the fish
5. Garnish with parsley, and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Eels
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eels
- beef gravy or stock, about a quart
- flour
- two table-spoonfuls of port wine
- one table-spoonful of ketchup

## Directions

1. Clean them well, wipe them dry, and fry them until brown
2. Have ready some beef gravy, or stock, about a quart; put the eels into it when cold, and let it simmer for about fifteen minutes
3. Take them out of the stewpan, and place them in a deep dish
4. Thicken the sauce with flour, and put into it two table-spoonfuls of port wine, and one of ketchup
5. Let it simmer for about ten minutes
6. Pour on the fish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Egg Balls
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four eggs, boiled till hard
- a small quantity of salad oil
- a tea-spoon of flour
- some chopped parsley
- a little salt and cayenne

## Directions

1. When the eggs are cold put the yolks into a mortar, with a small quantity of salad oil, a tea-spoon of flour, some chopped parsley, a little salt and cayenne
2. Rub them well together
3. Roll into small balls
4. Boil two or three minutes
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Egg Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Eggs
- 1 Lettuce
- 1 bunch Watercress
- Mayonnaise or Salad Dressing
- 1 Beetroot

## Directions

1. Put the eggs into boiling water and boil fifteen minutes.
2. Plunge into cold water till quite cold, peel and cut into quarters.
3. Wash and cleanse the watercress and lettuce and cut into pieces.
4. Put a layer of this at the bottom of the bowl, then one of eggs dipped in the dressing, then another of lettuce and egg until all are used up, leaving plenty of lettuce for the top.
5. Garnish with sprigs of watercress and slices of beetroot alternately.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Egg Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the yolks of four eggs
- the juice of a lemon
- a tea-spoonful of flour
- a little cold water

## Directions

1. Beat up the yolks of four eggs with the juice of a lemon, a tea-spoonful of flour, and a little cold water
2. Mix well together, and set it on the fire to thicken, stirring it to prevent curdling
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Egg Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 quart White Stock
- 1 pint of Milk
- 3 Yolks of Eggs
- 1 oz. Sago
- 1 Onion
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Boil the sago, stock, and onion together till the sago is clear; then take out the onion and season the soup with salt and pepper.
2. Beat the yolks of the eggs in a basin, pour over the boiling milk, strain into the stock.
3. Put over the fire and whisk till it comes to boiling point, but do not let it boil, or it may curdle.
4. Pour into a tureen, sprinkle with chopped parsley, and send some fried bread to table with it.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Egg Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- an egg
- a tablespoonful of water
- a wine-glass of wine
- half a glass of water
- sugar and nutmeg to taste

## Directions

1. Beat up an egg and mix it with a tablespoonful of water
2. Put a wine-glass of wine, half a glass of water, and sugar and nutmeg to taste into a small saucepan and place over a slow fire
3. When it boils add it gradually to the egg, stirring well
4. Return the whole to the saucepan and place over the fire again and stir for a minute
5. Remove and serve with toast. If it boils a second time, it will curdle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Eggs au Miroir
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- butter
- eggs
- salt
- pepper
- milk

## Directions

1. Spread a piece of butter upon a dish that can be set on the fire.
2. Break the eggs over it, adding salt, pepper, and two spoonfuls of milk.
3. Place it on a slow fire, with a red-hot shovel over it, and serve when the eggs are set.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Eggs Dressed with Oil
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- salad oil
- eggs

## Directions

1. Pour into a Dutch oven or cheese-toaster two table-spoonfuls of salad oil.
2. Break the eggs into the tin, which ought to take out, and when the yolk is set send up the tin, with the eggs in it, hot.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Eggs à l'Ardennaise
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- butter

## Directions

1. Beat to a froth the whites of six eggs which have been seasoned as for an omelette.
2. Pour this froth into a buttered baking-tin.
3. Pour across it four spoonfuls of cream, dropped into it at equal distances, the yolks whole.
4. Bake, but not too briskly, and serve.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Elder Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- twenty-four pounds of raisins
- three gallons of boiling water
- three pints of elderberry juice
- one pound of sugar

## Directions

1. Pick and shred twenty-four pounds of raisins, and cover them with three gallons of boiling water
2. Stir the liquor every day, and add three pints of elderberry juice and one pound of sugar
3. Let it ferment its usual time
4. Close the cask, and bottle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Elderberry Wine (Another Mode)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six gallons of berries
- seven gallons of water
- a quarter of a pound of allspice
- two ounces of ginger
- a few cloves
- three pounds and a half of sugar per gallon
- thick yeast

## Directions

1. To six gallons of berries add seven of water, and a quarter of a pound of allspice, two ounces of ginger, and a few cloves
2. Boil for half an hour
3. Squeeze the berries through a sieve, adding to every gallon three pounds and a half of sugar
4. Boil until the liquor becomes pretty clear, taking off the scum
5. Put it into the cask, and remove it to a cool place
6. When it becomes lukewarm, ferment it by toasted bread being dipped in thick yeast
7. When the fermentation has subsided, bung up the cask
8. It will be fit to drink in three months, and will keep for years
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: English Chutney
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of apples
- a quarter of a pound of green mint
- juice of two lemons
- half a pound of bird's-eye chillies
- half a pound of salt
- a quarter of a pound of onions
- a quarter of a pound of garlic
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Peel and core the apples
2. Pound the apples
3. Chop the mint fine
4. Pound all ingredients together
5. Add a small quantity of vinegar
6. If you cannot procure the green chillies, use cayenne
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Escobeche
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cold fried fish
- vinegar
- water
- grated ginger
- allspice
- cayenne pepper
- bay-leaves
- salt
- lemon-juice
- sliced onions

## Directions

1. Take some cold fried fish, and place it in a deep pan
2. Boil half a pint of vinegar with two table-spoonfuls of water, a little grated ginger, allspice, cayenne pepper, two bay-leaves, a little salt, and a table-spoonful of lemon-juice with sliced onions
3. When boiling, pour it over the fish
4. Cover the pan, and let it stand twenty-four hours before serving
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Everton Toffy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of butter
- half a tea-cupful of water
- three pounds of good moist sugar
- grated peel of one lemon

## Directions

1. Put half a pound of butter and half a tea-cupful of water into a pan
2. Add three pounds of good moist sugar, the grated peel of one lemon
3. Boil in water till it becomes brittle
4. Pour on a tin
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fillets a la Orly
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 bream
- lemon juice
- parsley
- half an onion
- 1 pint tomato sauce
- pepper
- salt
- batter or flour

## Directions

1. Fillet the fish and lay them in a dish
2. Sprinkle them well with lemon juice, pepper, salt, and parsley
3. Lay over them some slices of onion
4. Leave them for an hour
5. Fry them either in batter or flour
6. Drain them for a few moments on kitchen paper
7. Serve on a dish very hot with some good thick tomato sauce in a sauce-boat
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fillets of Fish and Cheese Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Mullet or Bream
- 1 pint Cheese Sauce
- 1 oz. Dry Cheese
- 1 oz. Butter
- Lemon Juice
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Fillet the fish, wash and dry them
2. Put them on to a baking sheet, sprinkle with lemon juice
3. Put a few little pieces of butter over them
4. Cover with buttered paper and bake from 20 minutes to half an hour, according to the thickness of the fillets
5. Place them carefully on the dish in which they are to be served
6. Pour over them the cheese sauce nicely flavoured with pepper, salt, and parsley
7. Sprinkle over them some dry cheese, brown in front of the fire, or under the grill if using a gas stove, and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish a L'Aurore
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 jew-fish
- 1 small onion
- 1 teaspoonful parsley
- 1 egg
- 1 pint white sauce
- pepper
- salt
- dripping
- buttered paper
- melted butter

## Directions

1. Put some dripping on a tin dish and lay the fish in it
2. Cover with a buttered paper and bake in the oven for twenty minutes
3. Take it out, split open and take out the centre bone
4. Sprinkle the inside of the fish with finely chopped onion and parsley, pepper, and salt
5. Put back the upper fillet and trim away some of the bones
6. Pour over the melted butter or white sauce
7. Put back into the oven for ten minutes
8. Boil the egg hard, remove the shell, take out the yolk and either chop it or rub it through a sieve
9. Cut the white into shapes
10. Take the fish from the oven and decorate the top with the yolk and white of egg
11. Serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish A La Maitee D'Hotel
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Bream
- 1/2 pint White Sauce
- Lemon
- Parsley
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Fillet the fish, wash and trim them, roll them lightly up with the skin inside.
2. Rub a baking sheet with some butter or dripping.
3. Put on the rolls of fish close together.
4. Squeeze over them some lemon juice, cover with a piece of buttered paper, and bake in the oven for twenty minutes or until they look milk white.
5. Dish them carefully, make the white sauce by recipe given, season it with pepper, salt, and half a teaspoonful of lemon juice.
6. Chop half a teaspoonful of parsley very finely and stir it in, pour over the fish, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish and Butter Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Whiting or Bream
- 1.5 oz. Butter
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley, Pepper, and Salt

## Directions

1. Fillet the fish and cut them into strips, wash them well in cold water and dry in a cloth; twist them round, and lay in a buttered soup plate.
2. Sprinkle with white pepper and salt, and chopped parsley.
3. Put in the rest of the butter, cover with another soup plate, and stand over a saucepan of boiling water for three-quarters of an hour; reverse the plates once while it is cooking.
4. Place in a hot dish, and pour over it the butter and parsley in which it was cooked.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish And Tomato Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Bream
- 1/2 pint of Tomato Sauce
- Salt
- Pepper
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Fillet the bream; cut each fillet into two pieces, wash and trim them.
2. Make some tomato sauce by recipe given.
3. Butter a pie dish, lay in the fillets, and season them; pour over the sauce, and bake in a moderate oven for twenty minutes.
4. Garnish with a little chopped parsley, and serve in the dish in which they were cooked.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Au Gratin
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Sole
- 1 teaspoonful of Parsley
- 4 teaspoonsful Bread Crumbs
- 1 Small Onion
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 gill Good Gravy
- 1 oz. Fat Bacon

## Directions

1. Mince the onion, parsley, and bacon very finely, and put them into a basin with the seasoning and crumbs, and mix thoroughly
2. Butter a dish in which the fish can be both cooked and served
3. Spread half the seasoning on it, wash and dry the fish and lay it on this bed of seasoning
4. Spread the rest of the seasoning on the top, pour over gently the gravy
5. Cover with a few brown raspings and put the butter on in tiny pieces
6. Put it into a quick oven and bake from 15 to 20 minutes, according to the thickness of the fish
7. Pin a paper collar round the dish, and serve at once
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Baked In Vinegar
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Mullet
- 1/2 pint Vinegar
- 1 gill Water
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 1 doz. Peppercorns
- Salt

## Directions

1. Wash the fish, dry them on a cloth, and rub them with a little salt
2. Lay them in a deep dish, put in the herbs and peppercorns, pour over the vinegar and water
3. Cover with a tin, and stand in a cool oven, and bake very slowly for an hour
4. Take them out and let them get quite cold in the vinegar, then lay them in a dish, and strain the sauce over
5. Garnish with sprigs of parsley
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Balls
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Fish
- 1 gill Thick Sauce
- 1 teaspoonful Anchovy
- 1 pint Melted Butter
- 2 oz. Fat Bacon
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- 1 Egg
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 oz. Butter

## Directions

1. Chop the fish, bacon, and parsley finely, and mix them together with the seasoning
2. Make a thick sauce with 1 gill water, 1 oz. flour, and 1 oz. butter
3. Flavour with anchovy and stir the fish in
4. Simmer for a few minutes, stir in the yolk of the egg, and turn on to a plate to cool
5. Make up into small balls
6. Fill a frying pan with boiling water, put in the balls
7. Cover over and simmer gently for ten minutes
8. Dish the balls in a circle and pour over the melted butter, which has been nicely flavoured with anchovy
9. Garnish with parsley, and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Cakes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Boiled Fish
- 1/2 lb. Cold Boiled Potatoes
- Pepper and Salt
- Frying Fat
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 Egg
- 1 tablespoonful of Milk
- Bread Crumbs

## Directions

1. Free the fish from skin and bone and flake it up; mash the potatoes smoothly, mix together and season with pepper and salt.
2. Put the milk and butter into a saucepan, and when it is quite hot put in the fish and the potatoes.
3. Beat up the egg, and put half in, and mix together till hot through; spread on to a plate and stand away to cool.
4. Add a teaspoonful each of water and oil to the egg.
5. Make some bread crumbs on a sieve, and put them on to a piece of paper.
6. Shape the fish mixture into cakes about one inch high and two inches across; brush them over with the egg, and toss them into the crumbs.
7. Shape again and fry in very hot fat, arrange in the form of a wheel on a dish paper, garnish with fresh or fried parsley, and serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Fried in Oil
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- soles, plaice, or salmon
- beaten eggs
- flour
- frying-oil
- parsley

## Directions

1. When prepared by salting or drying, have a dish ready with beaten eggs
2. Turn the fish well over in them, and sprinkle it freely with flour, so that the fish may be covered entirely with it
3. Place it in a pan with a quantity of the best frying-oil, at boiling heat
4. Fry the fish in it gently till of a fine, equal, brown colour
5. When done it should be placed before the fire for the oil to drain off
6. Take care that the oil should have ceased to bubble when the fish is put in, otherwise it will be greasy
7. The oil will serve again if strained off and poured into a jar
8. Fish preserved in this way is usually served cold, garnished with parsley
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fish Hash
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Boiled Fish
- 1 lb. Cold Boiled Potatoes
- 1/2 of an Onion
- 2 oz. Fat Bacon
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 gill Milk or Gravy
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Flake up the fish free from skin and bone, mash the potatoes and mix them together
2. Season with half the parsley, pepper and salt
3. Mince the bacon and onion very finely
4. Put them into a frying pan with the butter and fry for a few minutes
5. Stir in the fish and potatoes and turn about until thoroughly hot through
6. Pour over the gravy or milk and again make thoroughly hot
7. Heap on to a dish, and garnish with the rest of the parsley
8. Serve very hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish in Batter
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Mullet
- Frying Batter
- Hot Fat
- Flour
- Salt
- Pepper
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Fillet the mullet and cut into small pieces
2. Dip in flour seasoned with salt and pepper
3. Cover with French frying batter
4. Plunge into plenty of hot fat and fry until a good colour
5. Drain for a few minutes on kitchen paper
6. Pile high on a dish, garnish with parsley, and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish in Batter
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Mullet
- Frying Batter
- Hot Fat
- Flour
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Fillet the mullet and cut into small pieces; dip in flour seasoned with salt and pepper.
2. Cover with French frying batter, the recipe for which is given elsewhere.
3. Plunge into hot fat.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Patties
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Small Bream
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 teaspoonful Anchovy Sauce
- 1 gill Milk
- Pepper, Salt, and Lemon Juice
- Flaky Pastry

## Directions

1. Bake the fish in the oven, unless there is cold fish in the larder, which will do just as well; take away the skin and bone, and flake it up.
2. Make a sauce of the butter, flour, and milk; season with anchovy, pepper, salt, and lemon juice; stir in the fish and mix well.
3. Line some small patty pans with flaky pastry, put a spoonful of the mixture in the centre, cover with a round of pastry, press the edges together, and trim into a neat shape.
4. Make a small hole in the centre with a skewer, brush over with egg or milk, put into a quick oven, and bake for about twenty minutes.
5. Dish on a fancy paper, and garnish each patty with a tiny sprig of parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Pie
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 Bream
- 1 gill Milk or Melted Butter
- Short Pastry
- Pepper and Salt
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Cold fish will do very well for this dish. If fresh is used, fillet it and cut into small pieces; if cooked, flake up into small pieces.
2. Lay in a buttered pie-dish, season with pepper, salt, and chopped parsley.
3. Pour over the sauce and cover with a short pastry made with 0.5 lb. flour and 0.5 lb. dripping.
4. Brush over with egg or milk, and bake for three-quarters of an hour; garnish with parsley, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 0.5 lb. Blue Cod
- 1 lb. Potatoes
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 Egg
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Use cold fish and potatoes, if there are any in the larder; if not, boil a piece of blue smoked cod in some water for five minutes.
2. Flake it up free from skin and bone and put it into a basin; mash up the potatoes and mix them in with the pepper and salt.
3. Bind into a paste with an egg; rub some dripping on a baking sheet, turn the mixture on to it and shape into the letter S.
4. Brush over with egg or milk, and bake till brown.
5. Slip it off on to a hot dish, and garnish with parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Rissoles
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. Cold Fish
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 gill Milk
- Bread Crumbs
- Hot Fat
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 teaspoonful Anchovy
- 1/2 teaspoonful Parsley
- Pepper
- Salt

## Directions

1. Pick the fish free from skin and bone, and chop it up.
2. Make a smooth thick sauce with the flour, butter, and water, by directions given elsewhere.
3. Flavour it with anchovy, parsley, pepper, and salt; stir in the fish, and mix well.
4. Turn on to a plate till cold.
5. Make up into small balls, cover with egg and bread crumbs, and fry in hot fat.
6. Drain for a few minutes on kitchen paper, arrange carefully on a dish, and garnish with parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cold Boiled Fish
- 1 Lettuce
- 1 Egg
- Salad Dressing, or Remoulade Sauce

## Directions

1. Make a salad dressing the same as that given for lettuce salad
2. Flake up the fish free from skin and bone
3. Wash and dry the lettuce and shred it up, mix the fish with the dressing
4. Put a layer of lettuce at the bottom of the bowl, then one of fish and dressing
5. Do this alternately, leaving plenty of lettuce for the top
6. Garnish with hard boiled eggs cut into slices
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sufficient quantity of fish
- two quarts of veal, beef, or mutton broth
- a few slices of lean ham
- two or three carrots
- onions
- heads of celery
- some sweet herbs
- salt
- cayenne
- a piece of butter
- some flour
- two glassfuls of white wine
- a tablespoonful of garlic vinegar

## Directions

1. Cut fish into pieces and put into a stewpan with two quarts of veal, beef, or mutton broth
2. Add a few slices of lean ham, two or three carrots, onions, and heads of celery, some sweet herbs, salt, and cayenne
3. Stew the fish until it will pass through a coarse sieve
4. Return it to the stewpan with a piece of butter and some flour to thicken
5. Add two glassfuls of white wine and a tablespoonful of garlic vinegar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fish Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cod's-head
- water
- pepper
- salt
- mace
- celery
- parsley
- sweet herbs
- two or three onions
- cutlets of fish (sole or brill)
- wine
- butter
- juice of a lemon
- cream
- flour
- forcemeat balls of cod's liver

## Directions

1. Make a good stock by simmering a cod's-head in water, enough to cover the fish
2. Season it with pepper and salt, mace, celery, parsley, and a few sweet herbs, with two or three onions
3. When sufficiently done strain it
4. Cut very small, well trimmed cutlets from any fish (sole or brill are perhaps best)
5. Stew them in equal quantities of water and wine, but not more than will cover them, with a large lump of butter, and the juice of a lemon
6. When they have stewed gently for about fifteen or twenty minutes, add them to the soup
7. Thicken the soup with cream and flour
8. Serve the soup with the cutlets in a tureen
9. Forcemeat balls of cod's liver are sometimes added
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fish Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 pints Fish Stock
- 1 pint Milk
- Cornflour
- Six white peppercorns
- 1 onion
- 1 slice of turnip
- 1 fagot of herbs
- Carrot
- Fish
- Lemon Juice
- Salt
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Remove all the fat from the fish stock and put it into a saucepan with six white peppercorns, an onion, one slice of turnip, a fagot of herbs, and some carrot.
2. Boil this together for twenty minutes, then strain out the vegetables and pour back into the saucepan.
3. Mix a tablespoonful of cornflour smoothly with the milk and stir it in; continue stirring till it boils.
4. Skin and fillet the fish and cut it into dice, put these pieces of fish into the soup, and simmer for ten minutes.
5. Just before serving add a few drops of lemon juice, and salt to taste.
6. Pour into a tureen and sprinkle a little chopped parsley on top.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish Soup (Another Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- five pounds of fish, assorted kinds
- water or weak broth, about a quart
- cayenne
- common pepper
- salt
- a bundle of sweet herbs
- parsley
- a couple of onions
- flour
- butter
- ketchup
- soy
- sauce
- macaroni or vermicelli

## Directions

1. Cut the fish into pieces, and fry until half done
2. Place them in a boiler, in about a quart of water or weak broth, having first seasoned the pieces of fish with cayenne, common pepper, and salt, a bundle of sweet herbs, and parsley
3. Let the whole stew for about three hours, so that all the goodness may be extracted from the fish
4. Whilst stewing, fry a couple of onions, well coated with flour, in the fat the fish was previously fried in, then put them into the soup
5. Thicken it with a little flour and butter, and ketchup, soy, and sauce
6. Strain the contents through a hair sieve, and, when cold, take off the fat
7. When wanted for use, warm it up, and add macaroni or vermicelli
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fish Stewed White
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- an onion
- a little oil
- half a pint of water
- fish
- pepper
- salt
- mace
- ground allspice
- nutmeg
- ginger
- the beaten yolks of four eggs
- the juice of two lemons
- a tea-spoonful of flour
- a table-spoonful of cold water
- a little saffron
- the liver and the roe
- chopped parsley
- warm butter
- crumbs of bread
- eggs

## Directions

1. Put an onion, finely chopped, into a stewpan, with a little oil, till the onion becomes brown
2. Add half a pint of water, and place the fish in the stewpan, seasoning with pepper, salt, and mace, ground allspice, nutmeg, and ginger
3. Let it stew gently until the fish is done
4. Prepare the beaten yolks of four eggs, with the juice of two lemons, a tea-spoonful of flour, a table-spoonful of cold water, and a little saffron
5. Mix well in a cup, and pour it into the stewpan, stirring it carefully one way until it thickens
6. Balls should be thrown in about twenty minutes before serving
7. For the balls: Take a little of the fish, the liver and the roe, if there is any, beat it up finely with chopped parsley, and spread warm butter, crumbs of bread, and seasoning according to taste
8. Form this into a paste with eggs, and make it into balls of a moderate size
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fish Stock
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Fish bones
- Vegetables
- Peppercorns
- Teaspoonful of salt
- 1 dozen white peppercorns
- A fagot of herbs
- One onion
- One carrot

## Directions

1. Wash the bones well in cold water and cut away any black substance adhering to them
2. Break them up and put into a saucepan with a teaspoonful of salt
3. When it boils remove the scum and put in 1 dozen white peppercorns, a fagot of herbs, one onion, and one carrot
4. Boil steadily for two hours or longer
5. Strain through a sieve into a basin
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish à la Crème
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 Whiting or Schnapper
- 1 gill Milk
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Flour
- Lemon Juice
- Pepper and Salt
- 1/2 pint white stock

## Directions

1. Fillet the fish, wash the bones, and put them into half a pint of white stock, and boil them for half an hour
2. Strain out and mix with 1 gill of milk
3. Wash the fillets and roll them up, stand them in a stewpan and cook them in this liquor, covering them with a piece of buttered paper
4. They will take about 20 minutes
5. Dish them carefully, strain the liquor, and make a sauce of it with the butter and flour by directions given
6. Season and flavour this and pour it over the fillets
7. Garnish with chopped parsley and red bread crumbs, and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish à la Saumarez
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Bream
- 2 Tomatoes
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 1 Carrot
- 1 oz. Flour
- Pepper
- Salt
- 1 Onion
- 1 doz. Peppercorns
- Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. Fillet the fish, put the bones in a saucepan, and just cover them with water.
2. When they boil, skim well, and add the tomatoes sliced up, the peppercorns and vegetables; boil quickly without the lid for half an hour, then strain, rubbing the pulp of the tomatoes through with the liquor.
3. Make a smooth sauce with half a pint of this liquor, the butter, and the flour; if the colour is not good add a few drops of cochineal.
4. Fold the fillets of fish neatly, and bake in the oven with a little lemon juice, and covered with a buttered paper.
5. Arrange them on a dish and pour the sauce over.
6. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fish, To Fry
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Fish
- 1 spoonful of Flour
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 Egg
- Water
- Oil
- Dry fine crumbs
- Hot Fat
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Wash fish well in cold water, but not soaked; dry in a cloth.
2. Mix on a plate a spoonful of flour, pepper, and salt.
3. Beat on another plate an egg, with a spoonful each of water and oil, and have plenty of dry fine crumbs on a sheet of paper.
4. Dip the fish in the flour and dust off again; put at once into the egg and cover well.
5. Drop into the crumbs, shake them all over it; toss in the hands to shake all the loose crumbs off.
6. Lay on a plate separately, and either fry at once or leave in a cool place for an hour or two.
7. Plunge into plenty of hot fat and fry till crisp and brown; drain for a few minutes on kitchen paper.
8. Pile on a dish, and garnish with either fresh or fried parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Flaky Crust
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 4 oz. butter
- 0.5 oz. lard
- 1 pint water
- Juice of half a lemon

## Directions

1. Sift the flour into a basin, cut about one ounce of the lard into it with a knife, then mix into a paste with the water; it should be about the same consistency as the butter.
2. Roll it out evenly, and lay on it small pieces of the butter and lard, sprinkle with flour and roll into three; roll out again and proceed as before.
3. It is ready for use at once if required, but it is much improved by standing in a cool place for an hour.
4. This kind of pastry requires a very quick oven; and if used for meat pies, a piece of buttered paper should be laid over the top as soon as it has risen, to prevent it getting too brown.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Flan
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a table-spoonful of flour
- a table-spoonful of brandy or orange-flower water
- eight yolks of eggs
- a little salt
- a quarter of a pound of sugar
- a pint of milk

## Directions

1. Mix a table-spoonful of flour with a table-spoonful of brandy or orange-flower water, eight yolks of eggs, and a little salt
2. When well mixed, add a quarter of a pound of sugar to a pint of milk, which pour over the eggs, stirring all the time
3. Put the mixture into a buttered tart-pan, and bake in a moderate oven
4. Powder it with sugar and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Flank of Beef Roasted
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flank of beef
- gravy
- horseradish

## Directions

1. Take a flank of beef and roll up very tight
2. Roast until well done
3. Serve with gravy
4. Grate some horseradish over the top before coming on the table
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fondu
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Four eggs well beaten
- Three ounces of butter
- Three ounces of cheese
- A gill of cream

## Directions

1. Mix well
2. Bake for twenty minutes in small shapes or in a dish
3. Serve hot, for cold fondu is unbearable
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fondu
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four eggs
- three ounces of butter
- three ounces of cheese
- a gill of cream

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs well
2. Mix well with the butter, cheese, and cream
3. Bake for twenty minutes in small shapes or in a dish
4. Serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fondu (Another Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Yolks of eight eggs
- A quarter of a pound of melted butter
- Half a pound of grated cheese of good flavour
- Whites of the eggs whisked to a froth

## Directions

1. Beat up the yolks of eight eggs together with a quarter of a pound of melted butter, and half a pound of grated cheese of good flavour
2. Mix the ingredients with the whites of the eggs whisked to a froth
3. Bake in small dishes
4. Serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fondu (Another Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the yolks of eight eggs
- a quarter of a pound of melted butter
- half a pound of grated cheese

## Directions

1. Beat up the yolks of eight eggs together with a quarter of a pound of melted butter, and half a pound of grated cheese
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Forcemeat Balls for Fish, Soups, &c.
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flesh and soft parts of a medium-sized lobster or crayfish
- half an anchovy
- a large piece of boiled celery
- the yolk of an egg, hard-boiled
- a little cayenne
- mace
- salt
- pepper
- two table-spoonfuls of bread crumbs
- two ounces of butter
- two eggs, well beaten up

## Directions

1. Beat the flesh and soft parts of a medium-sized lobster or crayfish, half an anchovy, a large piece of boiled celery, the yolk of an egg, hard-boiled, a little cayenne, mace, salt, and pepper, with two table-spoonfuls of bread crumbs, two ounces of butter, and two eggs, well beaten up
2. Make into balls
3. Fry of a light brown
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Forcemeat Balls for Fish, Soups, &c.
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a medium-sized lobster or crayfish
- half an anchovy
- a large piece of boiled celery
- the yolk of an egg, hard-boiled
- a little cayenne
- mace
- salt
- pepper
- two table-spoonfuls of bread crumbs
- two ounces of butter
- two eggs

## Directions

1. Beat the flesh and soft parts of a medium-sized lobster or crayfish, half an anchovy, a large piece of boiled celery, the yolk of an egg, hard-boiled, a little cayenne, mace, salt, and pepper, with two table-spoonfuls of bread crumbs, two ounces of butter, and two eggs, well beaten up
2. Make into balls
3. Fry of a light brown
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Forcemeat Balls for Turtle or Made Dishes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef or veal
- a third of its weight in butter
- the same weight of bread crumbs
- milk
- chopped parsley
- shalot
- the yolks of three eggs, boiled hard
- salt
- pepper
- curry-powder
- the yolks of two eggs

## Directions

1. Pound some beef or veal, and a third of its weight in butter
2. Put into a stewpan the same weight of bread crumbs, moistened with milk
3. Add chopped parsley and shalot
4. Rub them in a mortar until they form a smooth paste
5. Put it through a sieve
6. When cold, pound and mix all together, with the yolks of three eggs, boiled hard
7. Season with salt, pepper, and curry-powder
8. Add the yolks of two eggs
9. Rub together and make small balls
10. Put into the soup a few minutes before it is served
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fowl in Batter
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fowl
- handful of mushrooms
- rasped bacon
- bleached artichoke bottoms
- truffles
- beef suet
- anchovy
- parsley
- bread crumbs
- pepper
- salt
- mixed spice
- butter
- glass of white wine
- yolks of two eggs
- nutmeg

## Directions

1. Truss the fowl
2. Make forcemeat by chopping together mushrooms, rasped bacon, artichoke bottoms, truffles, beef suet, anchovy, and parsley
3. Mix these together with bread crumbs, pepper, salt, and mixed spice
4. Put a lump of butter into a stewpan; when warm add the mixture and let it fry a little
5. Moisten it with a glass of white wine and stew for a quarter of an hour
6. Take it off the fire and add the yolks of two eggs
7. Put this forcemeat into the body of the fowl and cover it with paper
8. Roast the fowl; when half done remove the paper
9. Make a light batter with a little salt and grated nutmeg
10. Baste the fowl with the batter to give it a thin coating
11. When the first coating is dry, add a second and third coating until a delicate coat of light-brown colour is formed
12. Serve with sauce of any kind, thickened with cream
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fowl à la Marengo
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fowl
- salt
- Lucca oil
- fine herbs
- pepper
- cayenne
- slice of ham or bacon

## Directions

1. Cut up a fowl as for fricassee, and put it in a stewpan
2. First put the legs, as they take longer doing, and then the other parts, with a little salt, and a quantity of Lucca oil
3. Let the joints brown, and, when half done, add some fine herbs, with pepper and cayenne
4. Serve with sauce, and the dish garnished, but if the fowl is tender it will require no addition, except a slice of ham or bacon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fowls Stewed
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fowls
- Bechamel sauce
- cream
- white pepper
- salt
- onion

## Directions

1. Divide fowls in joints and stew in Bechamel sauce, or done in cream, with white pepper, salt, and an onion
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fowls Stewed
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fowls
- Bechamel sauce
- cream
- white pepper
- salt
- onion
- ham or bacon
- lemon or bread sippets

## Directions

1. Divide fowls in joints
2. Stew in Bechamel sauce or done in cream with white pepper, salt, and an onion cut in shreds
3. Serve the dish garnished with slices of ham or bacon, and lemon or bread sippets
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fowls Stewed with Rice and Chorissa
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fowl
- water or gravy
- half a pound of rice
- one pound of chorissa
- white pepper
- salt
- saffron

## Directions

1. Boil a fowl in sufficient water or gravy to cover it
2. When boiling for ten minutes, skim off the fat
3. Add half a pound of rice and one pound of chorissa cut in about four pieces
4. Season with a little white pepper, salt, and a pinch of saffron to colour it
5. Stew till the rice is thoroughly tender
6. The rice should be perfectly moist when served
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fowls with Ragout of Oysters
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- forcemeat
- one dozen oysters
- fowls
- thin slices of bacon
- writing paper
- gravy
- oysters
- ketchup
- lemon juice
- flour
- butter

## Directions

1. Make a forcemeat, and add to it one dozen oysters
2. Fill the craw of the fowls with it
3. Truss them
4. Put on their breasts thin slices of bacon with writing paper over it
5. Roast them
6. Make some gravy, and put into it some oysters, a little ketchup, and the juice of a lemon
7. Thicken with flour and butter
8. Take away the bacon and paper
9. Serve the fowls in the gravy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: French Bread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four pounds of fine flour
- one quart of lukewarm milk
- salt
- one quarter of a pound of melted butter
- three beaten eggs

## Directions

1. Mix the milk and butter together
2. Add the beaten eggs
3. Handle as little as possible
4. Let the dough rise
5. Mould it into shape
6. Bake in a quick oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: French Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a dessert-spoonful of arrowroot or potato starch
- a small quantity of cold water
- a pint and a half of milk
- a little salt
- three ounces of pounded white sugar
- the yolks of eight eggs
- a table-spoonful of orange-flower water

## Directions

1. Dilute a dessert-spoonful of arrowroot or potato starch in a small quantity of cold water
2. Put a pint and a half of milk to boil in a saucepan
3. When it boils, add a little salt, three ounces of pounded white sugar, and the diluted arrowroot or starch
4. When the milk has sufficiently thickened, put to it the yolks of eight eggs, well beaten, and a table-spoonful of orange-flower water
5. Stir the whole sufficiently, and pour into a cream-dish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: French Ragout of Mutton
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter of a pound of dripping
- a dozen turnips
- a quarter of a pound of flour
- three pounds of mutton
- water
- a tea-spoonful of salt
- one tea-spoonful of pepper
- one tea-spoonful of brown sugar
- a little garlic

## Directions

1. Put in a saucepan a quarter of a pound of dripping
2. When hot, cut into small pieces a dozen turnips, put them into the dripping, and brown
3. Take them out, and put into the fat a quarter of a pound of flour; stir it until brown
4. Cut up three pounds of mutton into small pieces, and put in the saucepan, and add sufficient water to cover the meat
5. When the mutton is nearly done, add the turnips, season with a tea-spoonful of salt, one of pepper, and one of brown sugar, and a little garlic
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: French Salad
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of cold beef
- a couple of lettuce
- half a tea-spoonful of salt
- one spoonful of vinegar
- three spoonfuls of good salad oil
- a tea-spoonful of black pepper
- garlic or a shalot

## Directions

1. Cut half a pound of cold beef into thin slices
2. Put the slices into a salad bowl with a couple of lettuce cut into pieces
3. Season with half a tea-spoonful of salt, one spoonful of vinegar, three of good salad oil, and a tea-spoonful of black pepper, with a little garlic or a shalot cut up fine
4. Mix well together
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: French Sausages
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- meat
- garlic
- brown sugar
- salt
- saltpetre
- allspice
- mace
- white pepper
- preserved bay-leaf
- lard
- peppercorns
- pickled chitterlings

## Directions

1. Cut the meat into pieces and rub with garlic
2. Put in a mortar and pound until it becomes a paste
3. Sprinkle with brown sugar, and work up again
4. Work up with the following ingredients: two parts of salt, and one of saltpetre, well pounded with allspice, mace, and white pepper, and a small quantity of preserved bay-leaf
5. Lay the meat on the table, and roll out
6. Lay strips of lard on it with peppercorns
7. Roll the sausages, place in pickled chitterlings, tie very tight at each end
8. Hang up to dry
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: French Shalot Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- some shalots
- vinegar
- pepper
- salt
- good stock

## Directions

1. Chop fine some shalots
2. Boil them in vinegar with pepper and salt to taste
3. Use about a wine-glassful of vinegar
4. Add some good stock, thickened lightly
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: French Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Potatoes
- 3 Carrots
- 2 Turnips
- 2 quarts Bone Stock
- Pepper
- 2 Onions
- 1/2 stalk Celery
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar
- Salt

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up the vegetables and sprinkle them with the sugar and salt
2. Put them into a saucepan with the butter, and sweat for five minutes
3. Pour over the boiling stock and stir until it boils
4. Boil slowly for an hour
5. Rub through a sieve
6. If it is too thick, reduce it with a little more stock or milk
7. Return to a saucepan, and bring to the boil
8. When tomatoes are in season slice up two with the other vegetables; these will make the soup a good colour and improve the flavour
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: French Vol-au-vent
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- puff paste
- short paste
- yolk of an egg
- ragout of fowl, fish, or veal

## Directions

1. Prepare puff paste
2. Make an under-crust of short paste as thick as a penny piece
3. Moisten it a little
4. Over this crust put some puff paste, well rolled
5. Put these two crusts at the bottom of a pie-dish
6. Cover it with a lid the size intended for the vol-au-vent
7. Cut the puff paste all round the lid
8. Brush the surface with the yolk of an egg
9. With a knife cut the vol-au-vent within an inch and a half of the edge, striking the knife into the paste three-quarters of its thickness, to form the cover
10. Bake it in a moderate hot oven
11. When the crust is well browned take it out
12. Fill it with whatever you like; ragout of fowl, fish, veal, or any morsel
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fricassee de Poulet
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- chickens
- a piece of butter
- a bunch of sweet herbs
- some mushrooms
- chives
- two shalots
- a little flour
- a glass of white wine
- a glass of pale stock
- salt
- pepper
- yolks of three eggs
- a spoonful of vinegar
- a spoonful of stock

## Directions

1. Cut the chickens into small pieces after they are semi-boiled
2. Put them into a stewpan with a piece of butter, a bunch of sweet herbs, some mushrooms, chives, two shalots
3. Add a little flour, a glass of white wine, and the same of pale stock
4. Season with salt and pepper
5. Stew for half an hour slowly
6. Take out the herbs
7. Add to the sauce the yolks of three eggs, to which add a spoonful of vinegar and the same of stock
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fricassee of Liver
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Half a Calf's Liver
- 1 1/2 oz. Butter
- 1 Carrot
- Lemon Juice
- 1 Onion
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 pint of Gravy
- Parsley
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Wash and slice up the liver, and dip in the flour.
2. Fry very lightly and quickly in the butter and lay in a saucepan.
3. Slice up the carrot and fry in the same butter.
4. Stir in the gravy, boil up, and pour over the liver.
5. Simmer very gently for one hour, then dish carefully.
6. Season the gravy with salt, pepper, and lemon juice.
7. Boil up and pour over it.
8. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fricondelle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cold veal or poultry
- French roll
- white gravy
- sweet herbs
- pepper
- salt
- parsley
- essence of lemon
- two or three eggs
- crumbs of bread

## Directions

1. Prepare cold veal or poultry
2. Add a French roll soaked in white gravy with sweet herbs, pepper, salt, parsley, and essence of lemon
3. Mix all well with two or three eggs
4. Form it into shapes
5. Sprinkle with crumbs of bread
6. Place in a frying-pan as deep as a shallow saucepan
7. When they have fried enough to become set, pour enough white gravy in the pan to cover the fricondelles
8. Let them stew in it gently
9. Place them both in the same dish and pour over any well-thickened sauce that may be selected
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fried Bread Crumbs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- stale bread
- butter

## Directions

1. Rub stale bread through a colander
2. Put them in a pan, with two ounces of butter
3. Place them over a moderate fire
4. Stir them with a wooden spoon, until they are slightly brown
5. Spread them on a sieve to drain
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fried Crayfish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Crayfish
- French Frying Batter
- 1 teaspoonful Anchovy
- Frying Fat
- Lemon Juice
- Pepper
- Salt

## Directions

1. Pick all the white meat from a crayfish, and cut it into pieces about two inches long and one inch broad.
2. Make a frying batter by recipe given elsewhere, and season with anchovy, lemon juice, pepper, and salt.
3. Dip the pieces of crayfish into this and plunge into plenty of very hot fat.
4. Fry a good colour, drain on kitchen paper for a few minutes, pile high on a dish, and garnish with fried parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fried Eggs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- fat

## Directions

1. Break the eggs into a pan with boiling fat, and spoon some fat over them.
2. Mind the yolks are not done hard.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fried Matsos
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- thickest matsos
- milk
- fresh butter

## Directions

1. Soak some of the thickest matsos in milk, taking care they do not break.
2. Fry in boiling fresh butter.
3. This is a very nice method of preparing them for breakfast or tea.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Fried Roes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Roes
- Frying Batter
- Hot Fat
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Put the roes on in cold water and boil for about half an hour
2. Take them up and let them get quite cold, then cut into slices
3. Make some frying batter by recipe given elsewhere
4. Season it with salt and pepper, dip in the slices, and fry a good colour
5. Pile high on a dish and garnish with fried parsley
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fried Tomatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 doz. Tomatoes
- 1 gill Milk
- 1 oz. Butter
- Pepper and Salt
- Flour

## Directions

1. Slice up the tomatoes, mix a spoonful of flour with some pepper and salt; dip in the slices and fry quickly, pile on a dish.
2. Pour the milk into the pan in which they were fried, stir until it boils, and pour over.
3. Garnish with fried bread and serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Fruit Pies or Tarts
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- gooseberries, damsons, morello cherries, currants mixed with raspberries, plums of all kinds, cranberries, and mulberries
- half a pound of moist sugar to a quart of fruit
- yolks of eggs
- paste
- whites of two eggs
- pounded sugar

## Directions

1. Lay fresh picked and washed fruit in the dish, with the centre highest, and half a pound of moist sugar to a quart of fruit
2. rub the edges of the dish with yolks of eggs, cover with paste in the usual way, and bake in a quick oven
3. For icing, beat up the whites of two eggs to a froth, lay it over the pie or tart, sift over it pounded sugar, and return it to the oven to set
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Frying Batter
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Flour
- 1 gill Tepid Water
- White of Egg
- 1 dessertspoonful Oil

## Directions

1. Sift the flour into a basin, pour over it the oil, then the water, and beat into a smooth batter
2. Stand away for an hour, if possible in a cool place
3. Whip the white of the egg to a stiff froth, and stir it in
4. It is ready to use
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Galette
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three-quarters of a pound of butter
- a pound of flour
- a salt-spoonful of salt
- yolks of two eggs
- a tea-cupful of cream or water

## Directions

1. Work lightly three-quarters of a pound of butter into a pound of flour, add a salt-spoonful of salt
2. Make it into a paste with the yolks of two eggs, mixed with a tea-cupful of cream or water
3. Roll round, three-quarters of an inch thick, score it in small diamonds
4. Brush yolks of eggs over the top, and bake it for about half an hour in a quick oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Game Pie for Christmas
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pheasant
- a hare
- a capon
- two pigeons
- two rabbits
- paste
- livers and hearts
- two mutton kidneys
- forcemeat
- egg balls
- seasoning
- spice
- ketchup
- mushrooms
- gravy made from the different bones

## Directions

1. Bone the pheasant, hare, capon, pigeons, and rabbits
2. Put them into the paste in the shape of a bird
3. Add the livers and hearts, two mutton kidneys, forcemeat and egg balls, seasoning, spice, ketchup, and mushrooms
4. Fill up with gravy made from the different bones
5. Bake sufficiently
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Garlic Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pound of beef
- pepper
- salt
- couple of carrots
- four cloves of garlic
- quarter of a pound of ham
- little water
- flour
- quart of stock
- bunch of sweet herbs
- little spice
- slice of a lemon

## Directions

1. Cut a pound of beef into small pieces and pepper and salt it
2. Put it into a stewpan with carrots and sliced garlic cloves, a quarter pound of ham, and a little water
3. Put it over a slow fire and turn the meat when it sticks
4. When browned, dredge it with flour
5. Add a quart of stock, a bunch of sweet herbs, a little spice, and the slice of a lemon
6. Let it simmer for an hour
7. Take off the fat and strain
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gateaux d'Epice
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of treacle
- half a pound of butter
- an ounce of powdered ginger
- an ounce of powdered cinnamon
- a quarter of an ounce of allspice
- two ounces of candied lemon-peel chopped fine
- six drops of tincture of vanilla
- flour as much as necessary

## Directions

1. Place the treacle over the fire
2. add the butter, and successively all the other things but the flour
3. when well mixed, take a single boil, stirring them, and then let them cool
4. When cold, mix in, with a wooden spoon, enough flour to make it into a pretty stiff paste
5. butter a tin baking-dish, and lay on it with the spoon the paste, in the size necessary to form the nuts
6. set the tin in the oven, and bake
7. When cold they are hard, if done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: German Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pint of wine
- sugar to taste
- little cinnamon
- yolks of eight eggs
- slices of lemon

## Directions

1. Take a pint of wine, put in sugar to taste and a little cinnamon
2. Boil it half an hour
3. Mix it lukewarm with the yolks of eight eggs, strained after being well beaten
4. When cool, put it in a bowl, in a saucepan of hot water till done enough
5. Pour it into the dish on which you serve it, cold, garnished with slices of lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: German Sour-Kraut
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- full-grown cabbages
- juniper-berries
- salt

## Directions

1. Take some full-grown cabbages, cut them in very thick slices, and put them in layers, of about two fingers' thickness, in a tub, and sprinkle each layer with juniper-berries
2. For twenty cabbages use two pounds of salt
3. When the tub is full, put on a cover which exactly fills the tub; put on the cover a weight of forty or fifty pounds, and put the tub in a moderate heat
4. The cabbage sinks when fermentation begins, and the liquor rises to the surface over the cover
5. When it smells sour, the fermentation has begun; then put the tub into the cellar, keep it covered, and let the pickle cover the sour-kraut
6. Cover it close each time any is taken out
7. When you use it, wash it in warm water, and stew it in butter or fat
8. Serve with ham, pickled pork, or sausages
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: German Sour-Krout
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- full-grown cabbages
- juniper-berries
- salt

## Directions

1. Take some full-grown cabbages, cut them in very thick slices, and put them in layers, of about two fingers' thickness, in a tub, and sprinkle each layer with juniper-berries; for twenty cabbages use two pounds of salt
2. When the tub is full, put on a cover which exactly fills the tub; put on the cover a weight of forty or fifty pounds, and put the tub in a moderate heat
3. The cabbage sinks when fermentation begins, and the liquor rises to the surface over the cover
4. When it smells sour, the fermentation has
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Giblet Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- giblets
- onion
- pepper
- salt
- little flour
- lemon

## Directions

1. Let giblets simmer for about three hours with an onion, pepper and salt
2. Add a thickening of a little flour
3. Squeeze of a lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gin Punch
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of gin
- outer peel of a lemon
- lemon-juice
- sugar
- a glass of maraschino
- about a pint and a quarter of water
- two bottles of iced soda-water

## Directions

1. Pour half a pint of gin on the outer peel of a lemon
2. Add a little lemon-juice, sugar, a glass of maraschino, about a pint and a quarter of water, and two bottles of iced soda-water
3. The result will be three pints of punch
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gin Sling
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- gin
- mint
- sugar
- ice

## Directions

1. prepare in the same way as mint julep
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ginger and Lemon Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- a pound of good sugar
- one ounce of best ginger
- three-quarters of a pound of lard or butter
- four eggs

## Directions

1. Add a pound of good sugar to two pounds of flour
2. Mix one ounce of best ginger well with the flour
3. Melt three-quarters of a pound of lard or butter
4. Beat four eggs well
5. Mix the lard and eggs together
6. Stir in the flour, which will form a paste
7. Roll out in thin cakes
8. Bake in a moderately-heated oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ginger Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of bruised ginger
- fourteen pounds of loaf sugar
- six gallons of water
- rind of a dozen lemons
- lemon-juice
- half a cup of yeast
- a pint of brandy

## Directions

1. Boil half a pound of bruised ginger and fourteen pounds of loaf sugar in six gallons of water
2. Pour the liquor on the rind of a dozen lemons
3. Let it stand until cool, and then add the lemon-juice and half a cup of yeast
4. Let the fermentation proceed
5. Before you bottle the wine, add a pint of brandy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ginger, Currant, and Cinnamon Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of butter
- four pounds of flour
- one pound of sugar
- an ounce and a half of volatile salts
- four eggs well beaten
- as much butter as will make into dough
- an ounce of ground ginger
- two ounces of currants
- half an ounce of ground cinnamon
- sugar for sprinkling

## Directions

1. Rub six ounces of butter into four pounds of flour
2. make a paste, and add one pound of sugar, an ounce and a half of volatile salts, four eggs well beaten, and as much butter as will make into dough
3. divide it into three equal parts
4. add to one half an ounce of ground ginger, to another two ounces of currants, and to the third half an ounce of ground cinnamon
5. Bake on buttered tins, and sprinkle the top with sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ginger-beer
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one gallon of boiling water
- one pound of loaf sugar
- one ounce of best ginger, bruised
