- one ounce of cream of tartar, or a sliced lemon
- a table-spoonful of good yeast

## Directions

1. Stir them until the sugar is dissolved
2. Let it remain until it cools to the heat of milk, then add a table-spoonful of good yeast
3. Cover and let it stand for twenty-four hours
4. Bottle it, not filling the bottles
5. Cork well and tie down, and it will be ready for use in two days
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ginger-beer (Family Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- best white sugar, five pounds
- lemon-juice, a quarter of a pint
- bruised ginger, five ounces
- honey, a quarter of a pound
- water, four gallons and a half
- white of an egg
- essence of lemon, a dozen drops

## Directions

1. Boil the ginger in three quarts of the water for half an hour
2. Add the sugar, lemon-juice, and honey, with the remainder of the water
3. Strain through a cloth or strainer
4. When cold add the white of an egg, and a small quantity of essence of lemon
5. Let the whole stand four days, and then bottle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ginger-beer (Ure's Dictionary)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Barbadoes ginger, twelve ounces
- tartaric acid, three ounces
- white sugar, eight pounds
- gum arabic, eight ounces
- essence of lemon, two drachms
- water, nine gallons
- fresh yeast

## Directions

1. Bruise the ginger-root and boil for an hour
2. Strain the liquor and add the tartaric acid and sugar
3. Boil and renew the same
4. Dissolve the gum arabic in a separate portion of water and add with the essence of lemons
5. When the whole has cooled to about 130° Fahr. add some fresh yeast
6. Carefully ferment the beer
7. Bottle for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gingerbread Nuts
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- three-quarters of a pound of sugar
- two pounds of treacle
- half a pound of candied orange-peel
- an ounce and a half of ginger
- an ounce of ground allspice
- carraways
- corianders
- three-quarters of a pound of butter

## Directions

1. Mix two pounds of flour, three-quarters of a pound of sugar, two pounds of treacle, half a pound of candied orange-peel cut small, an ounce and a half of ginger, an ounce of ground allspice, carraways, and corianders mixed, and three-quarters of a pound of butter, oiled
2. Mix all together, and set it aside for six hours
3. Roll it out in pieces the size of a walnut
4. Press them flat and bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Girambing
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ounces and a half of powdered ginger
- fourteen quarts of water, wine measure
- whites of four eggs
- nine pounds of white sugar
- nine lemons
- a little yeast

## Directions

1. Boil four ounces and a half of powdered ginger in fourteen quarts of water
2. Beat up the whites of four eggs to a froth, and mix them together, with nine pounds of white sugar
3. Add to the preceding water and ginger
4. Take nine lemons, and add the juice and the rind
5. Put the whole in a barrel, with a little yeast
6. In a few days bottle it off, and it will be soon fit for drinking
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Good Broth
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Four ounces of Scotch barley
- Four ounces of sliced onions
- Five quarts of water

## Directions

1. Put four ounces of Scotch barley and four ounces of sliced onions into five quarts of water
2. Boil gently for one hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Good Broth
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ounces of Scotch barley
- four ounces of sliced onions
- five quarts of water
- two ounces of beef or mutton dripping
- three ounces of fat bacon
- four ounces of oatmeal
- black pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Put four ounces of Scotch barley and four ounces of sliced onions into five quarts of water
2. Boil gently for one hour
3. Pour it into a pan
4. Put into the saucepan two ounces of beef or mutton dripping, and three ounces of fat bacon
5. When melted, stir into it four ounces of oatmeal
6. Rub these until you make a paste
7. Add the paste to the barley broth
8. Season with black pepper, salt, &c.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Good Potato Yeast
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- twelve full-sized potatoes
- a handful of hops
- three tablespoonfuls of sugar
- six pints of water
- about half a pint of stale yeast

## Directions

1. Take about twelve full-sized potatoes, and when boiled, mash them
2. Boil a handful of hops and three tablespoonfuls of sugar in six pints of water
3. When nearly cold, mix the whole with about half a pint of stale yeast
4. Set it near the fire to work
5. It is ready for use when done working, and will keep a fortnight
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Good Short Crust
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of butter
- the yolks of two eggs
- two ounces of sugar
- about a quarter of a pint of milk
- flour

## Directions

1. Rub the butter into the flour
2. Add the sugar
3. Mix the whole as lightly as possible to a smooth paste with yolks of eggs well beaten and the milk
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Goose
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- goose
- onion
- sage
- bread-crumbs
- pepper
- salt
- cayenne
- liver
- yolk of an egg
- apple sauce
- lemons

## Directions

1. Roast goose
2. Stuff it well with onion, sage, bread-crumbs, pepper, salt, and a little cayenne; with the liver, and the yolk of an egg
3. Ensure it is well done
4. Serve up with apple sauce
5. Garnish the dish with cut lemons
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Goose and Duck Stuffing
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of onion
- an ounce of green sage
- four ounces of bread crumbs
- the yolk and white of an egg
- a little minced apple
- cayenne

## Directions

1. Chop fine two ounces of onion, an ounce of green sage, four ounces of bread crumbs, the yolk and white of an egg, and a little minced apple and cayenne
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gooseberry Pool
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of gooseberries
- sufficient water
- six eggs
- a pint of cream or milk
- a table-spoonful of orange-flower water
- spice
- sugar

## Directions

1. Blanch a quart of gooseberries, closely covered with sufficient water to pulp them
2. Beat six eggs, and add them to a pint of cream (or milk)
3. Add a table-spoonful of orange-flower water, spice and sugar to sweeten
4. Stir it over a fire till of a proper thickness
5. Dish, and sift powdered white sugar on the top
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gooseberry Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of green gooseberries
- the same quantity of melted butter
- lemon or ginger

## Directions

1. Top and tail and scald half a pint of green gooseberries
2. drain them, and put them to the same quantity of melted butter
3. a little lemon or ginger is an improvement
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gooseberry Trifle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of gooseberries
- sugar
- nutmeg
- half a pint of cream
- half a pint of milk
- the yolk of an egg
- a pint of cream
- two eggs
- lemon-peel
- sugar

## Directions

1. Proceed with a quart of gooseberries and pass them through a sieve
2. Place them at the bottom of a dish
3. Add sugar and a little nutmeg
4. Mix half a pint of cream with the same quantity of milk, and the yolk of an egg
5. Scald it over the fire, and stir it
6. Add a little sugar, and let it cool
7. Pour over the gooseberries a whip made the day before of a pint of cream, two eggs, lemon-peel to flavour a little, and sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Grape Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ripe grapes
- water
- loaf sugar
- white of eggs or isinglass
- brandy
- crude tartar

## Directions

1. Take any given quantity of ripe grapes, macerate and strain them
2. Add two gallons of water to one gallon of juice, and three pounds and a half of loaf sugar
3. Let it ferment for six weeks
4. Clear it with white of eggs or isinglass, and barrel it
5. Add some brandy
6. A little crude tartar is an improvement
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Grape Wine (Colonial)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a gallon of water
- a gallon of grapes (bruised)
- three pounds of loaf sugar per gallon of wine
- a bottle of brandy per ten gallons of wine
- isinglass

## Directions

1. To a gallon of water add a gallon of grapes (bruised), and let them stand a week without stirring
2. Draw it off fine, and to every gallon of wine add three pounds of loaf sugar
3. Put it in a cask; do not bung it until the fermentation has ceased
4. Add a bottle of brandy to every ten gallons of wine
5. Bottle it on a clear day in July or August
6. Try it previously; and if not fine, dissolve an ounce of isinglass and put in the cask, and let it remain for two or three weeks
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gravy Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of ham or lean bacon
- six pounds of lean beef
- two onions
- two turnips
- two carrots
- a head of celery
- a few cloves
- a blade of mace
- hot water
- cold water
- whites of two eggs

## Directions

1. Cut half a pound of ham, or the same quantity of lean bacon, into slices, and lay them at the bottom of a digester, with six pounds of lean beef, cut into pieces
2. Clean two onions, two turnips, and two carrots, and a head of celery, a few cloves, and a blade of mace
3. Cover the digester close, and set it over the fire
4. When the meat adheres to the bottom of the digester, turn it
5. When there is a brown glaze on the meat, cover with hot water
6. When coming to a boil, put in half a pint of cold water, take off the scum
7. Put in some more cold water, and again skim it
8. Set it aside to simmer for full three hours
9. When done, strain it through a tamis, turn it into a pan
10. When cold, take off the fat
11. If thick, it must be clarified with the whites of two eggs
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Gravy without any Meat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of beer
- half a pint of water
- pepper
- salt
- lemon peel
- couple of cloves
- two spoonfuls of walnut or mushroom ketchup
- onion or two
- butter

## Directions

1. Mix half a pint of beer, the same quantity of water, with pepper, salt, lemon peel, a couple of cloves, and two spoonfuls of walnut or mushroom ketchup together
2. Flour and skin an onion or two and fry it in butter until brown
3. Put the mixture into a stewpan and simmer it for a quarter of an hour
4. It is fit for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Green Peas
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- green peas
- butter
- mint

## Directions

1. Boil green peas in plenty of water
2. When done, remove from the fire
3. Drain them on a hair sieve
4. Put them in the dish with the least bit of butter
5. A sprig of mint boiled with them is usually sent up with the peas
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Green Stick
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of biscuit-powder
- a little brown sugar
- grated lemon-peel
- six eggs
- three-quarters of a pound of warmed fresh butter
- four apples
- a quarter of a pound of sweet almonds blanched and chopped
- half a pound of stoned raisins
- a little nutmeg grated
- half a pound of coarse brown sugar
- a glass of white wine or a little brandy
- brown sugar-and-water syrup
- lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Make into a stiff paste one pint of biscuit-powder, a little brown sugar, grated lemon-peel, six eggs, and three-quarters of a pound of warmed fresh butter
2. Prepare four apples chopped finely, a quarter of a pound of sweet almonds blanched and chopped, half a pound of stoned raisins, a little nutmeg grated, half a pound of coarse brown sugar, and a glass of white wine, or a little brandy
3. Mix the above ingredients together
4. Put them on a slow fire, to simmer for half an hour
5. Place in a dish to cool
6. Make the paste into the form of small dumplings
7. Fill them with the fruit
8. Bake them
9. When put in the oven, pour over a syrup of brown sugar-and-water, flavoured with lemon-juice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hainan's Fritters
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two spoonfuls of the best Florence oil
- one pound of flour
- four beaten eggs
- oil for frying

## Directions

1. Take two spoonfuls of the best Florence oil, scald it
2. When hot, mix with it one pound of flour
3. Add four beaten eggs, and make it into a paste
4. Roll it out thin, and cut it into pieces about four inches square
5. Let them dry, and fry them in oil
6. The moment the pieces are put into the frying-pan they must be drawn up with silver skewers into different forms, according to fancy
7. Fry them a few minutes; they should be crisp when done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Half-pay Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter of a pound of suet
- a quarter of a pound of currants
- a quarter of a pound of stoned raisins
- a quarter of a pound of flour
- a quarter of a pound of bread-crumbs
- two table-spoonfuls of treacle
- half a pint of milk

## Directions

1. Chop a quarter of a pound of suet fine
2. Mix it with a quarter of a pound of currants well washed, a quarter of a pound of stoned raisins, a quarter of a pound of flour, the same of bread-crumbs, two table-spoonfuls of treacle, and half a pint of milk
3. Beat up the ingredients until they are well mixed
4. Put them into a buttered basin and boil for a couple of hours
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hams
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- black pepper
- allspice
- saltpetre
- salt
- sugar
- beer

## Directions

1. If the weather will allow it, let them hang for a few days
2. Mix in proportion one ounce of black pepper, one ounce of allspice, one ounce of saltpetre, half a pound of salt, and half a pound of sugar with a quart of beer
3. Boil them together, and pour them hot on the hams
4. Turn them in this pickle for three weeks
5. Let the ham drain, and brush it over with pyroligneous acid, and sprinkle over it some sharps, bran, or pollard
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hams (Alternative Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- brown sugar
- salt
- saltpetre

## Directions

1. One pound and a half of brown sugar, two pounds and a half of salt, and half a pound of saltpetre
2. Rub it dry on the hams, bacon, and cheeks
3. Turn every day for the first ten days, and afterwards every other day
4. Use the acid, and bran it as before
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Handsome Tipsy Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sponge cakes
- jam
- three glasses of sherry
- two glasses of brandy
- rich custard

## Directions

1. Provide some sponge cakes, place four close upon the dish upon which the cake is to be served
2. spread over them a thick layer of jam
3. upon this place three sponge cakes close together, spread over a thick layer of jam
4. complete the pyramid with one
5. Pour over the whole three glasses of sherry, so that the cakes may absorb all the wine
6. when this is done, add two glasses of brandy slowly, so that in absorbing the spirits, the cakes do not become too moist and break
7. Make a rich custard and pour over the whole
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hanover Oakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- yolks of eight eggs
- whites of four eggs
- a pound of finely-powdered white sugar
- rind and juice of two lemons
- a pound of blanched almonds, coarsely bruised
- three-quarters of a pound of flour

## Directions

1. Beat the yolks of eight eggs and the whites of four well together
2. Add a pound of finely-powdered white sugar, the rind and juice of two lemons
3. Beat them well together with a pound of blanched almonds, coarsely bruised, and three-quarters of a pound of flour
4. Shape and put them on a tin to bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hard Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of butter
- milk
- a pound of flour

## Directions

1. Warm the butter in milk
2. Convert the flour into a stiff paste
3. Beat it smooth
4. Roll them out thin
5. Cut them into round biscuits
6. Prick them with a fork
7. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Haricot Bean Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Haricot Beans
- 2 Onions
- 1 pint of Milk
- 2 quarts Bone Stock
- 1 doz. white peppercorns
- salt
- pepper
- 1 ounce of butter

## Directions

1. Soak the haricot beans for an hour or two
2. Put them into a saucepan with the stock or water, the onions, and 1 doz. white peppercorns
3. Boil for four hours and then rub through a sieve
4. Return to the saucepan with the milk and seasoning of pepper and salt
5. Stir until it boils
6. An ounce of butter stirred in just before it is finished is a great improvement
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Haricot Beans
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Haricot beans
- Salt
- Onion
- Parsley butter sauce

## Directions

1. Soak the haricots over night, if possible; if not, at least for two or three hours.
2. Put them on in plenty of cold water seasoned with salt and an onion, and boil them steadily for three hours.
3. Strain the water off, put them into a vegetable dish, and pour over them some parsley butter sauce.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Haricot Beans and Bacon
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Haricot Beans
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- 1/2 lb. Bacon
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Soak the haricot beans and boil them by directions already given.
2. Rub them through a wire sieve.
3. The bacon should be in thin rashers and very fat. Cook it carefully in a small clean frying-pan, and as the fat runs from it, pour it on the beans.
4. Mash them up with this and a little pepper and salt, and put them into a hot dish.
5. Sprinkle over with parsley and lay the bacon rashers on top.
6. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Haricot Mutton
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mutton
- turnips
- carrots
- onions
- stock
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Fry in a pan a sufficient quantity of mutton, cut in slices
2. When nearly done take the meat out, and fry of a brown colour equal quantities of turnips and carrots, cut into small pieces, and a few onions
3. When done put the mutton and vegetables in a stewpan, with a small quantity of stock, and pepper and salt
4. Simmer and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hashed Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef
- an onion
- water or stock
- flour thickening
- ketchup
- other sauce
- bread or toast sippets

## Directions

1. Cut the beef in slices, and put it into a stewpan, with an onion, and a little water or stock
2. Let it simmer, then add a little flour thickening, with ketchup and other sauce
3. Let it simmer for a quarter of an hour, and serve with bread or toast sippets
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hashed Beef and Tomato Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Roast Beef
- 8 Tomatoes
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 1 gill Gravy
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1/2 teaspoonful Sugar
- Salt and Pepper
- Toast

## Directions

1. Slice up the tomatoes and put them into a saucepan with the butter, herbs, pepper, salt, sugar, and gravy.
2. Stir about until it becomes quite a pulp; then rub through a sieve, season to taste, and let it get quite cold.
3. Cut the beef into thin slices and lay it in a saucepan, pour over the cold sauce and let it get hot through, very slowly.
4. Arrange on a hot dish, and garnish with fried sippets of bread or toast.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Hashed Poultry or Game
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pepper
- salt
- mustard
- pint of broth or stock
- couple of shredded onions
- vinegar
- toasted bread sippets

## Directions

1. Cut into joints, and flour them with a little pepper, salt, and mustard
2. Put into a stewpan with a pint of broth, or stock, and a couple of shredded onions
3. Let the joints simmer, and add a little more thickening, if necessary, and a table-spoonful of vinegar
4. Serve, and garnish with toasted bread sippets
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hasty Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 1 oz. Butter
- 3 oz. Flour
- 2 oz. Sugar

## Directions

1. Put the milk on the fire to boil, and when boiling stir in the flour quickly; it should be rather lumpy.
2. Pour it into a dish, melt the butter and sugar, and pour it in the middle of the pudding.
3. A little flavouring of grated lemon peel may be put into the milk, or jam served with the pudding.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Hasty Pudding Souffle
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 2 Eggs
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 oz. Sugar
- Flavouring

## Directions

1. Put the milk on to boil, mix the flour smoothly with a little cold milk
2. When the milk in the saucepan nearly boils stir this in and stir until it boils
3. Take off the fire and beat in the sugar, flavouring, and the yolks of the eggs
4. Whisk the whites to a stiff froth and stir them lightly in
5. Pour into a buttered pie-dish, and bake in a brisk oven for forty minutes
6. Serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Haunch of Venison Roasted
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- haunch of venison
- salt
- water
- clean dripping or butter
- flour
- white paper ruffle
- plain gravy
- currant jelly

## Directions

1. Take a haunch of venison and set it before a slow fire, taking care to paper it to prevent its scorching.
2. Baste it well with salt and water at first, but afterwards use clean dripping or butter.
3. Dredge it with flour.
4. When it is done it will be frothed and browned on both sides.
5. Wrap a white paper ruffle round the knuckle-bone, and send it to table with plain gravy.
6. Serve with currant jelly.
7. If the venison has hung long, the outer skin had better be removed.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Herring and Anchovy Paste
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- herring or anchovies
- warm beer or water
- a small quantity of butter
- good cayenne
- a little vinegar
- a little powdered mace

## Directions

1. Soak herring or anchovies in warm beer or water
2. Take out all the bones and skins
3. Pound them with a small quantity of butter, some good cayenne, and a little vinegar
4. A little powdered mace may be added
5. Pot them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Herring Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- herrings
- sourish apples
- pickled gherkins
- boiled beetroot
- capers
- cold roast veal
- potatoes
- mayonnaise sauce

## Directions

1. Soak herrings in water or milk for three hours
2. Remove herring heads and tails and discard
3. Gut, skin, and wash the herring bodies
4. Remove bones and cut flesh into small dice-like cubical pieces
5. Peel and core two sourish apples and cut them into small cubes like the herrings
6. Add two pickled gherkins, boiled beetroot, and a few capers, dividing them into the same small pieces
7. If desired, add a quarter of a pound of cold roast veal in small pieces
8. Mix all ingredients together
9. Boil two potatoes with their jackets on
10. Peel the potatoes and cut into small pieces while hot
11. Add the warm potato to the preceding ingredients and mix thoroughly
12. Pour milt or roe of the herring over the salad as previously indicated
13. Pour mayonnaise sauce over the herring salad to complete
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Herrings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- herring
- mustard sauce

## Directions

1. Place the herring upon the gridiron, over the clearest of fires
2. When sufficiently embrowned, transfer instantly to the hottest of plates
3. Eat with mustard sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hippocras
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a drachm of mace
- ginger
- cloves
- nutmegs
- three quarts of Lisbon wine
- cinnamon
- twenty ounces of white sugar

## Directions

1. Add half a drachm of mace, ginger, cloves, and nutmegs to three quarts of Lisbon wine; also cinnamon
2. Let it stand for a few days, when strain, and add twenty ounces of white sugar to the liquor
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hodge Podge
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of mutton
- one pound of beef
- half a pound of lean pork
- two quarts of water
- a good-sized carrot
- two turnips
- two onions
- a shalot
- a few lettuces
- half a dozen potatoes
- a pint of green peas
- pepper
- salt
- lemon

## Directions

1. Cut the mutton, beef, and pork into pieces, and put the meat into two quarts of water, and set it on the fire to simmer for an hour
2. Add a good-sized carrot, two turnips, two onions cut in pieces, a shalot, and a few lettuces, and again simmer
3. Add half a dozen potatoes cut up, and a pint of green peas, pepper and salt, and the squeeze of a lemon
4. Simmer till done, and serve in a tureen
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Horseradish Salad for Roast Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- scraped horseradish
- vinegar
- moist sugar
- spoonful of mustard
- yolk of an egg
- dust of cayenne
- cream

## Directions

1. Moisten scraped horseradish with a small quantity of vinegar
2. Add a little moist sugar, a spoonful of mustard, and the yolk of an egg, with a dust of cayenne
3. Soften the whole with cream to the consistence required
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hot Purl
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of mild ale
- a tablespoonful of grated nutmeg
- a little cold ale
- sugar to taste
- two eggs
- a quarter of a pint of whisky

## Directions

1. Put a quart of mild ale into a saucepan and add a tablespoonful of grated nutmeg
2. Place it over a slow fire until it nearly boils
3. Mix a little cold ale with sugar to taste and gradually two eggs well beaten
4. Add the hot ale, stirring one way to prevent curdling, and a quarter of a pint of whisky
5. Warm the whole again and pour it from one vessel into another until it becomes smooth
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: How to Make Your Own Yeast
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of the best hops
- four quarts of water
- a small handful of salt
- half a pound of brown sugar
- one pound of the best flour
- three pounds of potatoes

## Directions

1. Monday morning boil two ounces of the best hops in four quarts of water for half an hour
2. Strain it, and let the liquor cool down to new milk warmth
3. Put in a small handful of salt and half a pound of brown sugar
4. Beat up one pound of the best flour with some of the liquor, and then mix all together
5. On Wednesday add three pounds of potatoes boiled and then mashed, to stand till Thursday
6. Strain it and put it into bottles, and it is ready for use
7. Stir frequently while it is making, and keep near the fire
8. Before using shake the bottle well up
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Hydromel
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eight pints of water
- one pint of good honey
- a glass of brandy

## Directions

1. Boil eight pints of water and one pint of good honey till reduced half
2. Then add a glass of brandy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ice Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cream
- sugar
- lemon-juice or the pulp of a pint of mashed strawberries or raspberries

## Directions

1. Put the cream into a pan, then stir in the sugar by degrees, and, when all is well mixed, strain it through a sieve
2. put it into a tin that has a close cover, and set it in a tub
3. Fill the tub with ice broken in small pieces, and strew round the ice a quantity of salt, taking care that none of the salt gets to the cream
4. Scrape the cream down with a spoon, as it freezes round the edges of the tin
5. while the cream is freezing stir in gradually the lemon-juice or the pulp of a pint of mashed strawberries or raspberries
6. When it has frozen, dip the tin into lukewarm water, take out the cream, and fill the glasses
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Icing for Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of sugar
- the whites of six eggs
- powder blue
- the juice of a lemon

## Directions

1. Take one pound of sugar; put into a pan
2. break in the whites of six eggs, and as much powder blue as will lie on a sixpence
3. beat it well with a wooden spoon for ten minutes, then squeeze in the juice of a lemon, and beat it till it becomes transparent
4. Set the cake you intend to ice in an oven or warm place for five minutes
5. then spread over the mixture as smooth as possible
6. A plain ice, or ornament the cake with fancy articles of any description
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Imitation Omelet
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Egg
- ⅛ gill Milk
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar
- 1 teaspoonful Jam
- 1 teaspoonful Flour

## Directions

1. Beat the yolk and white of egg separately.
2. Beat the flour and milk together, and mix in the sugar and yolk of egg.
3. Stir in the white, butter a saucer, put the jam at the bottom.
4. Pour in the mixture, bake in the oven for five minutes, sprinkle with sugar, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Imitation Soy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of salt
- two pounds of dark brown sugar
- two pints of boiling water
- a quarter of a pint of essence of anchovies
- a few herbs

## Directions

1. Fry and brown the salt and dark brown sugar over a fire
2. Add two pints of boiling water, a quarter of a pint of essence of anchovies, and a few herbs
3. Boil
4. When cold, bottle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Imitation Spinach
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Young green shoots of the pumpkin plant
- Salt
- Carbonate of soda
- 1 ounce Butter
- Pepper
- Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. Wash the very young green shoots of the pumpkin plant well and put them into a large saucepan, with a very little water seasoned with salt and a pinch of carbonate of soda; keep pressing them down into the water and boil till soft.
2. Turn into a colander and squeeze very dry, put into a saucepan with one ounce of butter, pepper, salt, and a few drops of lemon juice.
3. Stir about till thoroughly hot through, dish neatly, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Impanada
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- halibut, plaice, or soles
- potatoes
- short crust paste
- brown sugar
- small pickles
- pepper
- gherkins
- West India pickles
- water or butter

## Directions

1. Cut fish in small pieces
2. Place them in a deep dish in alternate layers with slices of potatoes and dumplings made of short crust paste, sweetened with brown sugar
3. Season well with small pickles, pepper, gherkins, or West India pickles
4. Throw over a little water or butter, warmed
5. Bake it thoroughly
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Imperial Pop
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three ounces of cream of tartar
- one ounce of bruised ginger
- one pound and a half of white sugar
- one ounce of lemon-juice
- one gallon and a half of boiling water
- two table-spoons of yeast

## Directions

1. Take three ounces of cream of tartar, an ounce of bruised ginger, a pound and a half of white sugar, an ounce of lemon-juice
2. Pour a gallon and a half of boiling water on them, and, when cool, add two table-spoons of yeast
3. Strain and bottle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Indian Chutney
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of green tamarinds
- two pounds of unripe mangoes
- two pounds of salt
- two pounds of ginger roots
- one pound of onions
- one pound of garlic
- one pound and a half of raisins
- six parts of good vinegar

## Directions

1. Stone the tamarinds
2. Stone the raisins
3. Well pound all the ingredients together
4. Add to six parts of good vinegar
5. Jar them for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Indian Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four large apples
- grated nutmeg
- two table-spoonfuls of sugar
- a tea-spoonfal of minced lemon-peel
- crust
- six ounces of currants
- three-quarters of a pound of minced suet

## Directions

1. Pare, core, and cut four large apples into slices
2. Put them into a saucepan with some grated nutmeg, two table-spoonfuls of sugar, and a tea-spoonfal of minced lemon-peel
3. Stir them over the fire until soft
4. Have ready some crust and roll it out thin
5. Spread the apples over the paste
6. Sprinkle over the apples six ounces of currants and three-quarters of a pound of minced suet
7. Roll the pudding up, closing the ends
8. Tie it in a floured cloth and boil a couple of hours
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Invalid Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three-quarters of a pound of butter
- one pint of new milk
- three-quarters of a pound of white sugar
- half a dessert-spoonful of yeast
- one whole dessert-spoonful of caraway seeds
- flour

## Directions

1. Melt butter in new milk
2. Pour it on white sugar
3. When cool, put in half a dessert-spoonful of yeast and a whole one of caraway seeds
4. Add flour sufficient to make it into a stiff paste to roll thin
5. Prick and cut into shapes
6. Bake them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Italian Bread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- six table-spoonfuls of sugar
- three eggs, well beaten
- two ounces of fresh butter
- a little grated lemon-peel
- white of an egg

## Directions

1. Make a stiff dough with flour, sugar, eggs, butter, and lemon-peel
2. Mix them in a pan with a wooden spoon
3. If the dough is not firm, add more flour and sugar
4. Turn it out of the pan, and work it slightly
5. Cut it into the shape of round long biscuits
6. Glaze them with the white of an egg
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Italian Cabbage
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Cabbage
- 1 oz. Butter
- 2 oz. Dry Cheese
- 1 spoonful Flour
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Boil the cabbage by directions given, strain away the water and press it very dry.
2. Put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is dissolved, chop up the cabbage and put a layer at the bottom of the saucepan.
3. Sprinkle over some grated cheese, pepper, and salt, then more cabbage and cheese, until all are used up.
4. Simmer gently for fifteen minutes, slip it on to a hot dish, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Italian Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint and a half of milk
- the peel of an orange and lemon
- a little salt
- three ounces of pounded lump sugar
- the yolks of six eggs
- a sufficient quantity of isinglass, dissolved

## Directions

1. Boil a pint and a half of milk, and when it is boiling infuse the peel of an orange and lemon until the milk is flavoured
2. Put in a little salt, three ounces of pounded lump sugar, and the yolks of six eggs
3. Add a sufficient quantity of isinglass, dissolved, to make it tenacious
4. Put it into moulds until cold
5. To take it from the mould, dip a cloth in hot water, and rub round the mould; the cream will then turn out entire
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Italian Macaroons
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of Jordan or Valencia almonds
- whites of four eggs
- two pounds and a half of the best white sugar
- whites of six more eggs
- slips of blanched almonds

## Directions

1. Blanch a pound of Jordan or Valencia almonds
2. Pound them fine with the whites of four eggs
3. Add two pounds and a half of the best white sugar
4. Pound all together
5. Add the whites of six more eggs
6. Add slips of blanched almonds on the top of each macaroon
7. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Italian Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Salt Herring
- Cold slices of Meat
- 1 teaspoonful Mustard
- 1 Beetroot
- 4 tablespoonsful Oil
- 3 tablespoonsful Tarragon Vinegar
- 1 oz. Capers
- 3 Boiled Potatoes

## Directions

1. Wash the herring in cold water and soak it in milk for an hour; cut it open and take out the bone and slice up both the fish and the meat.
2. Arrange in a bowl, chop the capers and put over.
3. Put the mustard into a basin, add gradually the oil and vinegar; pour this, when well mixed, over the fish and meat, and cover with slices of cold potatoes.
4. Garnish with any cold vegetables in the larder or with some green pickles from a bottle of pickles, a little chopped parsley, and some small radishes.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Italian Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 ounces macaroni
- 2 quarts water or pot boilings
- 3 tomatoes
- 1 ounce butter
- 2 ounces cheese rind
- pepper and salt

## Directions

1. Put the water or stock on to boil
2. When it boils put in the macaroni and boil from twenty-five to thirty minutes
3. While it is boiling grate up a dry piece of cheese
4. Put the tomatoes into boiling water and remove the skin
5. Slice them up and put them into a saucepan with the butter and some pepper and salt, and cook them for a few minutes
6. When the macaroni is soft, cut it into pieces one inch long
7. Put a layer of tomatoes at the bottom of the soup tureen, then a layer of grated cheese, then one of macaroni
8. Repeat this until all the materials are used up
9. Pour over it boiling the liquor in which the macaroni has been cooked
10. Cover down for a few minutes, and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Jam Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 tablespoonful Jam
- 1 pint Water
- 1 oz. Sugar
- 1 teaspoonful Cornflour
- 1 Lemon

## Directions

1. Put the water, jam, lemon juice, and sugar into a small saucepan and boil it for five minutes.
2. Mix the cornflour with a little cold water and pour it in; stir till it boils up.
3. Strain the jam out, and it is ready to serve; a few drops of cochineal improve the colour.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Jaune-mange
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ounce of isinglass
- half a pint of boiling water
- yolks of six eggs
- half a pint of sherry
- juice of a lemon
- sugar to taste
- peel of the lemon

## Directions

1. Dissolve an ounce of isinglass in half a pint of boiling water
2. Beat the yolks of six eggs, and mix them with half a pint of sherry, the juice of a lemon, and sugar to taste
3. Rub the peel of the lemon on some of the sugar to extract the flavour
4. Stir all well together, and boil ten minutes
5. Strain, and put in a mould for shape
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Jelly Posset
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eight eggs
- half a pint of white wine
- a little strong ale
- sugar
- a pint of milk
- a little nutmeg
- cinnamon

## Directions

1. Take eight eggs, leave out the whites of four, and beat the remainder well together in a basin
2. Add half a pint of white wine, a little strong ale, and sugar
3. Put it into a saucepan and set it over a slow fire, stirring it all the time
4. Boil a pint of milk with a little nutmeg and cinnamon, just enough to flavour it
5. When the eggs and wine are hot, add the boiling milk to it
6. Remove it from the fire, pour into a punch-bowl, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Jenny Lind Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of flour
- a quarter of a pound of butter
- a quarter of a pound of moist sugar
- a little spice
- two tea-spoonfuls of soda
- three-quarters of a pint of fresh-churned buttermilk
- candied peel

## Directions

1. Mix one pound of flour, a quarter of a pound of butter, and a quarter of a pound of moist sugar with a little spice
2. Mix with two tea-spoonfuls of soda in three-quarters of a pint of fresh-churned buttermilk
3. Add candied peel to taste
4. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Jersey Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 quarts White Stock
- 1 pint Milk
- 1 oz. Sage
- 1 Leek
- 1 Fagot of Herbs
- 1 doz. White Peppercorns
- Salt
- parsley

## Directions

1. Put the stock into a stewpan; slice in the leek and add the fagot of herbs and the peppercorns.
2. Boil them together for half an hour, strain out the vegetables and return to the saucepan.
3. Stir in the sage and continue stirring until it is clear and the soup is thick.
4. Pour in the boiling milk, boil up and pour into a tureen.
5. Sprinkle finely chopped parsley on the top before serving.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Jersey Wonders
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- six ounces of butter
- six ounces of white sugar
- a little nutmeg
- ground ginger
- lemon-peel
- eight eggs
- a taste of brandy
- boiling dripping or lard

## Directions

1. Take two pounds of flour, six ounces of butter, six ounces of white sugar, a little nutmeg, ground ginger, and lemon-peel
2. Beat eight eggs, and knead them well together
3. a taste of brandy is an improvement
4. Roll them out the thickness of your wrist, cut off a small slice, and roll it into an oval, about four inches long and three inches wide, not too thin
5. cut ten slips in it, but not through either end, there will be then three bands
6. Pass the left one through the aperture to the right, and throw it into a skillet of boiling dripping or lard
7. cook three or four at a time
8. In about ten minutes turn them with a fork, and you will find them browned, and swollen or risen in two or three minutes more
9. Remove them from the pan to a dish, when they will dry and cool
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Jingle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a few apples
- nutmeg
- sugar to taste
- some slices of toasted bread
- some ale

## Directions

1. Roast a few apples and grate some nutmeg on them with sugar to taste
2. Place them in a jug with some slices of toasted bread
3. Heat some ale and fill the jug
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Johnny Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- meal
- water

## Directions

1. Make in the same manner as ash cake
2. Bake before the fire, first on one side, and then on the other
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Jugged Hare or Kangaroo
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- hare or kangaroo
- pound of bacon
- flour
- pepper
- allspice
- onions
- winter savory
- pint of stock
- currant jelly
- sharp sauce

## Directions

1. Cut the hare or kangaroo into pieces
2. Cut up a pound of bacon into small squares, and fry it in a pan for five minutes
3. Add the hare or kangaroo, and stir them in a stewpan until a little brown
4. Add some flour, pepper, allspice, onions, and a little winter savory
5. Moisten the stew with a pint of stock, and allow it to simmer for half an hour
6. Serve with currant jelly and sharp sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Jugged Rabbits
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Rabbits
- 1/2 lb. Pickled Pork
- 1 Onion
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 1 pint Gravy
- 1/2 oz. Flour
- 1 tablespoonful Red Currant Jelly

## Directions

1. Wash and joint up the rabbits and cut the pork into slices.
2. Lay some of the pork over the bottom of a baking jar, and on this some joints of rabbit.
3. Continue in layers until all the meat is in, then put in the onion, sliced up, the fagot of herbs, and a few peppercorns.
4. Cover down closely, stand in a moderate oven, and cook for two hours.
5. Take up the meat and arrange nicely on a hot dish, strain the gravy into a saucepan, thicken with the flour, and when it boils stir in the jelly.
6. Flavour to taste, pour it over the rabbits, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Kabobs
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1½ lbs. of Steak
- 1 dessertspoonful Curry Powder
- 1 dessertspoonful Worcester Sauce
- 1 dessertspoonful Vinegar
- ½ pint Stock
- 1 tablespoonful Flour
- 1 tablespoonful Chutney
- ½ lb. Rice
- Salt
- Saffron

## Directions

1. Slice up the steak into pieces about three inches long and two broad.
2. Mix the curry powder, sauce, vinegar, flour, and chutney together and spread this over the steak.
3. Roll up and thread on small wooden skewers.
4. Lay them in a saucepan, pour over the stock, bring to the boil and simmer one hour and a half.
5. While they are cooking, well wash the rice in cold water and let it soak for half an hour.
6. Throw it into boiling water for three minutes and strain off.
7. Put a pinch of saffron in some fresh water, season with salt, and finish cooking in this.
8. Strain off and dry in the saucepan.
9. Pile this on a dish and lay the kabobs over it.
10. Boil up the gravy, season and flavour, and strain round the dish.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Kangaroo Ham (Prize Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter of a pound of fine Liverpool salt
- three ounces of coarse brown sugar
- one ounce of pounded allspice

## Directions

1. Take a quarter of a pound of fine Liverpool salt, add three ounces of coarse brown sugar, and one ounce of pounded allspice
2. Mix them well together
3. Rub the whole well on
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Kangaroo Ham (Prize Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- quarter of a pound of fine Liverpool salt
- three ounces of coarse brown sugar
- one ounce of pounded allspice
- cold water
- mace
- salt
- pepper
- forcemeat balls
- smoked bacon
- boiled pork or ham

## Directions

1. Mix salt, brown sugar, and allspice well together
2. Rub the whole well on and down the leg bone
3. Let them lay in the pickle for a fortnight, rubbing them every other day
4. If not required for immediate use they may be hung up to dry
5. When required for the table, chop off the bone close to the meaty part
6. Cut slightly the sinew over the knee-joint, and bind it round
7. Steep in cold water for twelve hours or more before using
8. Place it in a saucepan of cold water on a slow fire
9. Let it boil only ten minutes, drawing it aside until the water is nearly cool
10. When dished, pour over a rich brown gravy, flavoured with mace, salt, and pepper
11. Garnish with forcemeat balls, adding to the usual ingredients a little smoked bacon very finely chopped
12. A slice of boiled pork or ham is an agreeable addition
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Kangaroo Steamer
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- kangaroo meat
- milk
- onion
- pepper
- salt
- salt pork or bacon
- ketchup
- jelly

## Directions

1. Cut the meat in pieces of about a quarter of an inch square
2. Put it into a pan with a well-covered lid
3. Add a spoonful of milk, an onion shredded into small pieces, and some pepper and salt to taste
4. When it has been on the fire a short time add about a tenth in quantity of salt pork or bacon cut to the same size as the kangaroo
5. Add a spoonful of ketchup
6. Serve hot, with jelly
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Kangaroo Steamer
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- hind-quarters of kangaroo
- bones, skin, tail, or part of fore-quarter
- water
- small onion
- pound of nice fat bacon
- mushroom ketchup
- flour
- salt
- pepper
- sippets of toasted bread
- forcemeat

## Directions

1. Cut the meat off the hind-quarters and take away the skin.
2. Mince, pound, or grind the meat in a sausage mill, not set finely.
3. Set it aside until next day.
4. Prepare the gravy by slowly simmering the bones, skin, tail, or part of the fore-quarter, well bruised and hacked up, with three pints of water and a small onion till reduced to little more than one pint.
5. Do this the day before you want to serve the steamer and strain off the gravy to be cold.
6. Next day take a pound (or more, if liked) of nice fat bacon, free from rust, cut it into dice.
7. Add it to the minced meat and stir all well into the cold gravy.
8. Let the whole warm gradually and simmer slowly until cooked.
9. Season to your palate, being careful not to add much salt, if any, as the bacon salts it.
10. A spoonful or two of mushroom ketchup and a dredge of flour will improve the flavour and consistency.
11. Serve with sippets of toasted bread and if you would have a steamer superlative, garnish round with nicely-fried balls of forcemeat.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Kangaroo Steamer (Prize Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- tender part of kangaroo
- smoked bacon (fat)
- marjoram
- pepper
- salt
- white of egg
- mace
- rich brown gravy
- forcemeat balls

## Directions

1. Take the most tender part of the kangaroo, being careful to remove all the sinews.
2. Chop it very fine, about the same quantity of smoked bacon (fat).
3. Season with finely-powdered marjoram, pepper, and a very little salt.
4. Let it steam or stew for two hours.
5. Pack or press tight in open-mouthed glass bottles.
6. Seal the bung down and wash the outside of the bottles well with white of egg, beaten.
7. Preserved in this way it will keep good for twelve months or more.
8. When needed for use, put the vessel containing the preserve into a saucepan of cold water and allow to boil for fifteen minutes (if a large bottle).
9. When dished, pour a little rich brown gravy over it, flavoured with mace, salt, and pepper.
10. Garnish with forcemeat balls.
11. If required for immediate use, half an hour will cook it sufficiently and no gravy will be necessary.
12. Forcemeat balls without bacon will be found a great improvement.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Kedgeree
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Fish
- 1 lb. Boiled Rice
- 2 Hard Boiled Eggs
- 1 oz. Butter
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Flake up the fish and mix it with the rice
2. Shell the eggs and cut them in half, put the yolks on one side
3. Chop the whites and mix them with the rice and fish
4. Season nicely and put into a saucepan with the butter, and stir until thoroughly hot
5. Pile on a dish, and either chop the yolks and sprinkle them over, or hold a sieve over the kedgeree and rub them lightly through
6. Serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Kelly's Sauce Piquante
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a table-spoonful of parsley leaves
- a table-spoonful of capers
- a table-spoonful of fresh mustard
- three hard yolks of eggs
- six anchovies
- a table-spoonful of vinegar
- two table-spoonfuls of oil
- a finely-chopped shalot
- half a pint of melted butter or strong gravy

## Directions

1. Put a table-spoonful of parsley leaves, and the same of capers, into a mortar, and pound them
2. add a table-spoonful of fresh mustard, and three hard yolks of eggs, and properly mix the whole
3. Then add six anchovies, boned and forced through a sieve, a table-spoonful of vinegar, two of oil, and a finely-chopped shalot, and mix the whole
4. When to be used, stir the sauce into half a pint of melted butter or strong gravy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Kidney Fritters
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Kidneys
- 1 teaspoonful Minced Herbs
- 1 teaspoonful Onion
- Frying Batter
- Pepper and Salt
- Hot Fat

## Directions

1. Skin the kidneys and cut up each one into three or four slices.
2. Make a frying batter by directions given elsewhere.
3. Stir in the minced onions and herbs, and season with pepper and salt.
4. Dip the slices of kidney into this and plunge into very hot fat.
5. Fry a good colour, pile high on a dish, garnish with fried parsley, and serve very hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Kidney Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Ox Kidney
- 2 Onions
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Cornflour
- Salt
- Lemon Juice
- Parsley
- 2 quarts Stock

## Directions

1. Slice up the onions and fry them in the butter, strain them out and return the butter to the saucepan.
2. Stir in the cornflour, and when well mixed pour over the stock and stir until it boils.
3. Slice the kidney up into small pieces, and put it in.
4. Simmer very gently for one hour.
5. Just before serving, season with salt and a little lemon juice.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Kidney Toast
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Kidneys
- 1/2 oz. Butter
- 1/2 Slice of Toast
- Parsley
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Skin and chop the kidneys small, put into a saucepan with the butter, and cook for two or three minutes.
2. Season with pepper and salt.
3. Spread it on the toast, sprinkle over some chopped parsley, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: King Cup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- rind and juice of a lemon
- a lump of sugar
- a small piece of bruised ginger
- about one pint and a half of boiling water
- one wine-glass of sherry

## Directions

1. Take the rind and juice of a lemon, a lump of sugar, a small piece of bruised ginger, and pour on them about a pint and a half of boiling water
2. When cold strain, and add a wine-glass of sherry
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Kingswood Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3/4 lb. Flour
- 1/4 lb. Bread Crumbs
- 1/4 lb. Raisins
- 6 oz. Suet
- 1/4 lb. Sultanas
- 1/4 lb. Sugar
- 2 Apples

## Directions

1. Mix the flour and bread crumbs together
2. Shred the suet finely and mix it in with the sugar
3. Stone the raisins and pull them in half
4. Clean the sultanas and mix these in
5. Peel and core the apples
6. Put in the pips, chop the apples finely, and add them
7. Let it stand for an hour and then mix it into a paste
8. Press into a basin
9. Tie down tightly and boil at least six hours
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Kinsmil Meat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- small piece of rump of beef or under-cut of sirloin
- three pints of vinegar
- two ounces of caraway-seeds
- salt
- pepper
- spices

## Directions

1. Place meat in a deep pan with three pints of vinegar, two ounces of caraway-seeds tied in a muslin bag, salt, pepper, and spices
2. Cover it down tight
3. Bake thoroughly in a slow oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Kottbullar
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Fillet of Beef
- 1/4 lb. Suet
- Salt and Pepper
- Hot Fat
- 1/2 pint Milk
- 2 Eggs
- Nutmeg
- Soda Biscuit

## Directions

1. Mince the lean of the meat very small with about a quarter of a pound of the suet which surrounds it
2. Season with pepper, salt, and nutmeg
3. Make a little boiled custard by recipe given elsewhere, pour it over the biscuit, which must be made into fine crumbs, then stir in the meat and let it all get quite cold
4. Roll into small balls with a little flour
5. Put three ounces of dripping into a frying pan, and when very hot drop in the balls and fry a good colour
6. Drain for a few minutes on kitchen paper, and dish in a circle
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Kromskies
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. Cold Meat
- 2 Rashers Fat Bacon
- 1 oz. Butter
- Frying Batter
- 1/4 gill Stock
- 1 oz. Flour
- Parsley, Pepper, and Salt
- Hot Fat

## Directions

1. Mince the meat finely or put it through the sausage machine, season with parsley, pepper, and salt
2. Put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is melted stir in the flour and the stock
3. Stir until it boils, then add the meat and mix thoroughly
4. Turn on to a plate to cool
5. When cold make up into pieces about the size of a cork
6. Take some very thin rashers of fat bacon and cut into strips about half an inch wide by two inches long
7. Roll the meat in this, dip in frying batter, and fry in very hot fat
8. Drain for a few minutes on kitchen paper, pile high on a dish, garnish with fried parsley, and serve very hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Lady of Arundel's Manchet
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a bushel of white wheat flour
- twenty eggs
- three pounds of fresh butter
- salt
- barm
- new milk

## Directions

1. Take a bushel of white wheat flour, twenty eggs, three pounds of fresh butter
2. Take as much salt and barm as the ordinary manchet
3. Temper it together with new milk pretty hot
4. Let it lie the space of half an hour to rise
5. Work it into bread, and bake it
6. Let not your oven be too hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lait Sucre
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of milk
- white sugar
- lemon

## Directions

1. Boil a pint of milk, sweeten with white sugar, and flavour with lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lamb Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cold roast lamb
- 2 lettuces
- 1 tomato
- 12 capers
- 2 eggs
- Remoulade dressing

## Directions

1. Cut the lamb into small pieces and lay it in a bowl.
2. Cut the tomato into thin slices and lay it over, then the capers chopped small.
3. Pour over the dressing, break up the lettuces and put over, and garnish with the hard boiled eggs cut in slices.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Lamb à la Poulette
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- any part of a quarter of lamb
- a bit of butter
- a spoonful of flour
- two or three glasses of boiling water
- pepper
- salt
- a bunch of mixed herbs
- some small onions
- some mushrooms

## Directions

1. Blanch any part of a quarter of lamb, put it into a saucepan with a bit of butter, and a spoonful of flour
2. when well mixed pour over it by degrees two or three glasses of boiling water, in order that it may mix well with the butter
3. When quite smooth put in the lamb, with pepper, salt, a bunch of mixed herbs, and some small onions
4. Stew the whole gently, and half an hour before serving add some mushrooms
5. take out the meat, skim the fat off the sauce, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lamb's Wool
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- roasted apples
- ale
- sugar
- spice

## Directions

1. Compose the liquor of roasted apples and ale
2. Work the pulp of the apples up with the ale till the mixture formed a smooth beverage
3. Add sugar and spice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lamplich
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of currants
- one pound of raisins
- one pound of sugar
- a little citron
- ground cloves
- cinnamon
- eight apples
- rich paste
- one ounce of fresh butter
- a tea-cupful of clarified sugar
- essence of lemon

## Directions

1. Take a pound of currants, the same quantity of raisins and sugar, a little citron, ground cloves and cinnamon, with eight apples, finely chopped.
2. Mix all together.
3. Have ready a rich paste; cut into small triangles.
4. Fill them with the fruit, like puffs, and lay them in a deep dish.
5. Let the pieces be placed closely.
6. When the dish is full, pour over one ounce of fresh butter, melted in a tea-cupful of clarified sugar, flavoured with essence of lemon.
7. Bake in an oven not too brisk.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lampreys
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lampreys
- strong beef gravy
- allspice
- mace
- cloves
- salt
- cayenne pepper
- port wine
- sherry
- horseradish vinegar
- anchovies
- lemon
- butter
- flour
- sliced lemon
- sippets of toasted bread

## Directions

1. Cleanse the fish and remove the tough membrane from the back
2. Put the lampreys into a stewpan and cover them with strong beef gravy
3. Add a dessert-spoonful of mixed allspice, mace, and cloves in powder, a spoonful of salt, a few grains of cayenne pepper, a gill of port wine, the same of sherry, and a table-spoonful of horseradish vinegar
4. Cover the pan and stew gently till the fish are tender
5. Take them out and add to the gravy two anchovies beaten to paste and the juice of a lemon
6. Boil it up and strain it, and if requisite, thicken it with butter and flour
7. Warm the lampreys in this sauce before serving
8. Garnish with slices of lemon and sippets of toasted bread
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Leg of Lamb with Green Peas
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- leg of lamb
- stock
- pepper
- salt
- ketchup
- pint of green peas
- fried eggs

## Directions

1. Stew a leg of lamb in a simmer of stock until half done
2. Add seasoning of pepper, salt, a little ketchup, and a pint of green peas
3. Put it again on the fire, and simmer till done
4. This dish can be garnished with fried eggs
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lemon and Orange Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ten pounds of loaf sugar
- four gallons of water
- rind of eighteen oranges and lemons
- eighteen oranges and lemons
- two pounds of white sugar
- a little yeast

## Directions

1. Boil ten pounds of loaf sugar in four gallons of water for half an hour
2. Pour the liquor on the rind of eighteen oranges and lemons
3. Squeeze the fruit, and boil it in a syrup with two pounds of white sugar
4. Mix the syrup with the liquor, and let it cool
5. Ferment with a little yeast
6. When the fermentation subsides, bung it down, and bottle in three or four months
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lemon Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of flour
- six ounces of good sugar
- three-quarters of a pound of lard or butter
- two eggs
- about eight drops of essence of lemon

## Directions

1. Add sugar to flour
2. Melt the lard or butter
3. Beat the eggs well
4. Mix the lard and eggs together
5. Stir in the flour to form a paste
6. Add about eight drops of essence of lemon
7. Roll out into thin cakes
8. Bake in a moderately heated oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lemon Biscuits
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- ½ lb. Flour
- 3 oz. Dripping
- 1 teaspoonful Baking Powder
- 3 oz. Sugar
- 1 Lemon
- 1 Egg

## Directions

1. Rub the dripping into the flour, stir in the sugar and baking powder, and grate over the rind of the lemon.
2. Beat up the egg and strain in the lemon juice ; add these to the dry ingredients, mix into a stiff dough, and knead for a few minutes.
3. Roll out, cut into small biscuits, and bake in a quick oven for about ten minutes.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Lemon Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of good lemonade
- the yolks of eight eggs
- a small quantity of dissolved isinglass

## Directions

1. Make a quart of good lemonade sweet
2. Strain it, and put it into a stewpan over the fire
3. Add the yolks of eight eggs, well beaten up, and stir the cream well
4. Put it into cream-glasses, or a cream-dish
5. If to be put in moulds, add a small quantity of dissolved isinglass
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lemon Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. Flour
- 3 oz. Suet
- 1 Egg
- 2 Lemons
- 2 oz. Sugar
- 1/4 gill Water

## Directions

1. Sift the flour
2. Chop the suet finely and mix together
3. Stir in the sugar and the grated rind of the lemons
4. Beat up the egg, add the juice of one lemon, and mix the pudding into a dough with this and a little water if required
5. Dip a cloth in boiling water
6. Tie the pudding in it
7. Plunge into boiling water and boil two hours
8. Take out of the cloth and turn on to a hot dish
9. Pour round it sauce made by squeezing the juice of the other lemon into a small saucepan, stirring in some sugar and a gill of water, and boiling up
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Lemon Sponge
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half an ounce of isinglass
- boiling water
- juice of eight lemons
- sugar to taste

## Directions

1. Take half an ounce of isinglass and dissolve it in a little boiling water
2. Take the juice of eight lemons, and put sugar to your taste
3. Whisk it together until it becomes a sponge
4. Wet the mould and put it in
5. When set, turn it out
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lemon-Juice
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lemons
- olive oil

## Directions

1. Cut off the peel of the lemons, and squeeze them until all the juice is out into a basin
2. strain it through muslin
3. Have ready dry bottles, fill them with the juice until near the top
4. Pour into the bottles after a teaspoon of olive oil
5. Cork them, and place them upright in a cool place
6. Dry the peel, and string it for use
7. When you require to use the juice, open the bottle, and put in a skewer, with some cotton at the end, which will absorb the oil
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lemonade
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sixteen lemons
- one pound and a half of white sugar
- one gallon of boiling water
- one wine-glass of sherry wine

## Directions

1. Take sixteen lemons, pare them, cut in halves, squeeze well, and throw them into a pan
2. Add a pound and a half of white sugar, a gallon of boiling water, and a wine-glass of sherry wine
3. Mix, strain, and cool
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lemonade au Lait
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of lemon-juice
- half a pint of white wine
- three-quarters of a pound of white sugar
- one quart of boiling water
- one pint of boiling milk

## Directions

1. Mix lemon-juice, white wine, sugar, and one quart of boiling water
2. When cold, add one pint of boiling milk
3. Let it stand twelve hours
4. Pour through a jelly-bag
5. This will make two quarts
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lenten Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Onions
- 2 oz. Butter or Beef Dripping
- 2 quarts of Water or Pot Liquor
- Crusts of Bread
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up the onions and put them into a saucepan with the butter or dripping, and brown them.
2. Then let them cook, covered over, for an hour.
3. Break in some brown dry crusts of bread.
4. Pour over the boiling liquor the water in which some vegetables, such as carrots, turnips, or cauliflowers, have been boiled, stir it well and boil for an hour.
5. Rub through a sieve.
6. If it is not thick enough, let it boil again without the lid for ten minutes.
7. Season well with pepper and salt, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Lentil Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Split Lentils
- 1 oz. Butter
- 3 Onions
- 2 doz. Peppercorns
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar
- 3 quarts Water
- Salt

## Directions

1. Wash the lentils well in two or three waters and put them into a saucepan with the onions, peppercorns, sugar, salt, and half the butter, and sweat them for five minutes.
2. Pour over the boiling water and boil steadily for four hours.
3. If the soup gets too thick, pour in a little more water or stock.
4. Rub through a sieve and return to the saucepan; stir in the butter, salt, and pepper to taste.
5. Boil up and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Lentils, to Boil
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Lentils
- Salt
- Water
- Butter

## Directions

1. Wash the lentils well in cold water, cover them with cold water seasoned with salt, and boil for one hour and a half.
2. Strain all the water off, put into a hot dish with about half an ounce of butter, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Lettuce Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Lettuces
- 1 tablespoonful Condensed Milk
- 2 teaspoonsful Mustard
- 2 Eggs
- 1 gill Vinegar
- 1 gill Oil
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Boil the eggs hard; take the yolk of one and put it into a basin and work it quite smooth with a spoon.
2. Add the mustard made with vinegar instead of water, the condensed milk, pepper, and salt, and then the oil slowly; last of all the vinegar.
3. Mix it all very thoroughly.
4. Cut off the outside leaves of the lettuce, and pull it all to pieces, wash in cold water and dry thoroughly in a cloth.
5. Break into small pieces and put into a salad bowl, pour over the dressing.
6. Garnish with the other egg and the white that was not used in the dressing.
7. These should be cut into slices and placed round.
8. A few of the best pieces of lettuce should be laid over the dressing.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Lettuce Stewed
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 Lettuces
- 1 oz. Butter
- Nutmeg
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Wash the lettuces very thoroughly and lay them in salt and water for half an hour.
2. Plunge them into plenty of boiling water seasoned with salt and a quarter of a teaspoonful of carbonate of soda.
3. Boil quickly without the lid from fifteen to twenty minutes, take up and squeeze all the water from them.
4. Chop them up and put into a saucepan with some butter, nutmeg, pepper and salt, and a few drops of lemon juice; stir them about and cook for about five minutes.
5. Turn into a hot dish and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Light Dumplings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Jones's patent flour
- milk or water

## Directions

1. Mix lightly Jones's patent flour with cold milk or water.
2. As soon as mixed, put them into boiling water.
3. When done, take out and serve with melted butter and sweet sauce.
4. They won't take much more than five minutes boiling.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Liqueur de Quatre Fruit
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- strawberries
- raspberries
- currants
- morella cherries
- white sugar
- a gill of brandy
- cherry and apricot kernels

## Directions

1. Extract the juice from strawberries, raspberries, currants, and morella cherries separately
2. Add to each a small proportion of white sugar so as to make it sweet and rich, but not a thick syrup
3. Strain it off as clear as possible
4. Mix the juices of the four fruits together, observing to put in a smaller proportion of currant and raspberry juice than of the cherry and strawberry
5. To a pint of juice add a gill of brandy
6. Bottle it with the addition of some cherry and apricot kernels
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lobster and Crayfish Salad
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lobster or crayfish
- yolk of an egg, boiled hard
- a mealy potato
- a table-spoonful of vinegar
- two spoonfuls of oil
- a dessert-spoonful of mustard
- half a tea-spoonful of salt
- half a tea-spoonful of cayenne
- a couple of medium-sized lettuces
- a table-spoonful of cream

## Directions

1. Cut lobster or crayfish into small pieces
2. Add the yolk of an egg, boiled hard, a mealy potato, a table-spoonful of vinegar, two of oil, a dessert-spoonful of mustard, half a tea-spoonful of salt, the same of cayenne
3. Slice into pieces a couple of medium-sized lettuces
4. A table-spoonful of cream may be added
5. Mix the whole well together
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lobster Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Lobster, Crayfish, or Tin of Lobster
- 2 quarts Fish Stock
- 1 pint of Milk
- 1 oz. Cornflour
- Lemon Juice
- Salt
- Pepper

## Directions

1. The fish stock for this soup should be well flavoured with vegetables.
2. If a crayfish be used, remove all the white meat and boil the shells in the stock for half an hour and strain them out; thicken with the cornflour, pour in the milk, and boil up.
3. Cut the lobster into small pieces and put into the soup; simmer for ten minutes.
4. Flavour with lemon juice and salt, pour into a warm tureen, and serve with fried bread.
5. Wash the shells well in cold water before putting them into the soup.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Loin of Mutton Stuffed
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- loin of mutton
- veal stuffing
- brown gravy
- port wine

## Directions

1. Take the skin off a loin of mutton with the flap on
2. Bone it neatly
3. Fill the inside of the loin, where the bones were removed, with veal stuffing
4. Roll it up tight, skewer the flap, and bind it together
5. Put the outside skin over it till nearly roasted, and then remove it that the mutton may brown
6. Serve with brown gravy, with a little port wine in it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Lyonnaise Potatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Potatoes
- 1/2 pint Onion Sauce
- Lemon juice

## Directions

1. Peel and boil the potatoes in the usual way, slice them up and put them into a hot dish.
2. Make some rather thin onion sauce by directions given elsewhere, season with a few drops of lemon juice, and pour over the potatoes.
3. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Macaroni and Cheese Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. Macaroni
- 1/4 lb. Cheese
- 1 teaspoonful French Mustard
- 3 tablespoonsful Oil
- 3 tablespoonsful Vinegar
- 1 Head of Celery
- 1 Lettuce
- Beetroot

## Directions

1. Boil the macaroni, or use any cold that may be in the larder.
2. Cut it into pieces about three inches long.
3. Cut the cheese into very thin slices.
4. Cut the celery into very small pieces.
5. Lay these alternately in a bowl with some shredded lettuce.
6. Make a dressing of the mustard, oil, and vinegar.
7. Pour it over.
8. Garnish with a little beetroot, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Macaroni Cheese
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. Macaroni
- 1/2 pint White Sauce
- 3 oz. Dry Cheese
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Put the macaroni into boiling salt and water, and boil for half an hour or until soft
2. Strain off the water and cut into pieces about 1 1/2 inch long
3. Make the sauce by directions given elsewhere
4. Mix in half the cheese and some pepper and salt
5. Take a dish in which it can be served, and lay at the bottom some macaroni
6. Then some sauce and a little of the dry cheese
7. Continue in this way till all the materials are used up, leaving plenty of dry cheese for the top
8. Put in the oven for five or ten minutes till a nice colour
9. Serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Macaroni Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 oz. Macaroni
- 1 lb. Cold Meat
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 pint Gravy
- 2 Eggs
- 1 pint Milk
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley

## Directions

1. Boil the macaroni in stock or water.
2. Mince the meat finely, and season with parsley, pepper, and salt.
3. Rub a pudding basin or mould with butter, put the macaroni and meat in in layers, and season nicely.
4. Beat up the eggs and milk and pour them over, cover with buttered paper, and steam for one hour.
5. Turn out of the basin carefully, and pour round it a little nice brown gravy or white sauce.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Macedoine of Vegetables
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- equal quantity of carrots and turnips
- dozen button onions
- butter
- gravy
- mushrooms
- young beans
- French beans
- haricots
- Brussels sprouts
- asparagus plants
- tops of a cauliflower
- teaspoon of sugar
- flour

## Directions

1. cut carrots and turnips of the size of almonds
2. peel a dozen button onions
3. put all in a saucepan with a piece of butter
4. let them brown gently
5. add a little gravy and some mushrooms, young beans, French beans, haricots, and some Brussels sprouts (the last must be parboiled), some asparagus plants, and the tops of a cauliflower, with a teaspoon of sugar
6. let them all boil gently
7. just before serving thicken the sauce with a little flour
8. serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Macrotes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of French roll dough
- six ounces of fresh butter
- two eggs
- flour
- Florence oil
- clarified sugar
- essence of lemon

## Directions

1. Take a pound of French roll dough, six ounces of fresh butter, two eggs, as much flour as will be requisite
2. Knead together and roll it in the form of a long French roll
3. Cut it in thin long slices
4. Set them at a short distance from the fire to rise
5. Fry them in the best Florence oil
6. When nearly cold, dip them in clarified sugar, flavoured with essence of lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Maids of Honour
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the curd of a quart of milk
- four ounces of butter
- eight ounces of ground white sugar
- the yolks of four eggs
- one mealy potato of medium size
- one ounce each of sweet and bitter almonds
- the strained juice of a lemon
- the rind of one lemon grated
- nutmeg
- a wine-glass of brandy
- puff paste

## Directions

1. Take the curd of a quart of milk, and drain dry
2. rub it through a sieve into a pan
3. mix with it four ounces of butter, eight ounces of ground white sugar, the yolks of four eggs, and one mealy potato of medium size
4. when well mixed, add one ounce each of sweet and bitter almonds (using the strained juice of a lemon to prevent their oiling while being pounded), the rind of one lemon grated, some nutmeg, and a wine-glass of brandy, and stir in with the rest
5. Line the pans with puff paste
6. fill them with the mixture, and bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Maigre Fish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fish
- a shredded onion
- a few cloves
- allspice
- pepper
- boiling water
- butter
- flour
- a glass of wine
- a little ketchup and anchovy
- cayenne
- sippets of bread

## Directions

1. Put the fish into a stewpan, with a shredded onion, a few cloves, allspice, and pepper.
2. Just cover it with boiling water.
3. Let it simmer for about twenty minutes.
4. Strain the liquor into another pan, leaving the fish to keep warm, until the sauce is ready.
5. Rub well together as much butter and flour as will thicken.
6. To each pint of sauce add a glass of wine, a little ketchup and anchovy, and cayenne.
7. Let it simmer for a time, and pour over the fish.
8. Garnish with sippets of bread toasted or fried.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Maitre d'Hotel Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 1 oz. Butter
- Lemon Juice
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- Pepper and Salt
