## Directions

1. Put the butter into a small saucepan, and when it is melted stir in the flour, and mix smoothly; pour in the milk and stir until it boils.
2. Take the saucepan from the fire, add a few drops of lemon juice, a pinch of pepper and salt to taste, last of all the parsley.
3. It is then ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Maleskikes (Polish Pancakes)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eight eggs
- a pint and a half of milk or cream
- two ounces of melted fresh butter
- some grated nutmeg
- lemon-peel rasped upon loaf sugar
- a little salt
- ten ounces of flour
- butter or lard for frying
- a few currants

## Directions

1. Beat up eggs and mix them with milk or cream, melted butter, nutmeg, lemon-peel, salt, and flour to make a smooth batter
2. Put butter or lard into a frying-pan and when it boils pour in some of the batter
3. Sprinkle in a few currants
4. Fry nicely and sprinkle the pancake with sugar
5. Roll it with a two-pronged fork and serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Maria Louisa's Consomme
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two quarts of water
- two pounds of lean beef
- a fowl
- two large carrots
- two onions
- a bay leaf
- two cloves

## Directions

1. In about two quarts of water put two pounds of lean beef, cut in slices, a fowl divided, two large carrots, two onions, a bay leaf, and two cloves
2. Let it simmer for six hours
3. Skim off the fat, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Marine Ketchup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Strong beer, one gallon
- Anchovies, a pound and a half
- Peeled shalots pounded, one pound
- Mace, half an ounce
- Mustard-seed, half an ounce
- Cloves, half an ounce
- Bruised pepper, a quarter of an ounce
- Bruised ginger, a quarter of an ounce
- Mushroom ketchup, one quart
- Vinegar, one quart

## Directions

1. Combine strong beer one gallon, anchovies a pound and a half, and peeled shalots pounded one pound
2. Add half an ounce of mace, mustard-seed, and cloves
3. Add bruised pepper and ginger, a quarter of an ounce
4. Add mushroom ketchup and vinegar, one quart
5. Proceed in the same manner as camp ketchup
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Marlboro' Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ounces of butter melted
- four ounces of sugar
- four eggs well beaten
- paste
- a layer of preserve

## Directions

1. Mix the butter, sugar, and eggs well together
2. Line the dish with paste and a layer of preserve
3. Bake half an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Marlboro' Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ounces of butter
- four ounces of sugar
- four eggs
- paste
- preserve

## Directions

1. Melt four ounces of butter
2. Mix with four ounces of sugar and four eggs well beaten
3. Mix all well together
4. Line the dish with paste and a layer of preserve
5. Bake half an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Marlborough Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eight eggs
- one pound of ground sugar
- one pound of flour
- two ounces of carraway seeds

## Directions

1. Beat eight eggs and one pound of ground sugar well
2. by degrees mix in one pound of flour and two ounces of carraway seeds
3. bake in tin pans in a quick oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Marrow Bones
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- bones
- paste

## Directions

1. Let the bones be sawn even
2. Put paste at the ends, and boil them until they are done, which will require nearly two hours
3. Serve on toasted bread, and send up a silver marrow-spoon and napkin for the helper to use
4. The dish must be sent up hot; cold marrow is decidedly objectionable
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Marrow Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a penny loaf
- a pint of boiling cream or milk
- a pound of beef marrow
- four eggs
- a wineglass of brandy or sherry
- sugar and nutmeg to taste
- two ounces of candied citron

## Directions

1. Grate the penny loaf into crumbs and pour the boiling cream or milk on it.
2. Cut the beef marrow very thin.
3. Beat the four eggs well and add the wineglass of brandy or sherry, with sugar and nutmeg to taste.
4. Mix all well together.
5. Either boil or bake for three-quarters of an hour.
6. Cut the candied citron thin and stick the pieces all over it when served up.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Martinique Royeau
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Preserved quarns and their syrup, or jelly of that fruit
- Oil of sweet almonds
- Sweet almonds, beaten fine
- Bitter almonds
- Preserved ginger and its syrup
- Cinnamon and cloves, bruised
- Nutmeg and pimento
- Jamaica ginger
- Candied lemon and citron, of each
- White sugar-candy, powdered
- Proof spirit of wine
- Orange-flower water
- Brandy

## Directions

1. Put into a stone jar the preserved quarns and their syrup, oil of sweet almonds, sweet almonds beaten fine, bitter almonds, preserved ginger and its syrup, cinnamon and cloves bruised, nutmeg and pimento, Jamaica ginger, candied lemon and citron, white sugar-candy powdered, and proof spirit of wine
2. Boil the oil with a little brandy, and mix it with the almonds, when beaten to a paste with orange-flower water
3. Stop up the jar securely, and let it remain in a warm room, or the sun, shaking it often, for a fortnight
4. Keep it in the tun for twelve months
5. Strain it, and filter repeatedly until it is as clear as spring water
6. Rinse phials or half-pint bottles with any white wine, drain them and fill
7. Cork and seal well
8. In six months it will be fit for use, but will improve greatly by age
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Matso Diet Bread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of white sugar
- a quarter of a pint of water
- eight well-beaten eggs
- one pound of matso flour
- a little grated lemon-peel

## Directions

1. Simmer one pound of white sugar in a quarter of a pint of water
2. Pour hot upon eight well-beaten eggs
3. Beat till cold
4. Add one pound of matso flour and a little grated lemon-peel
5. Bake in a prepared tin, or in small tins
6. Remove the cake while hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Matso Oakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- biscuit-powder
- milk and water
- a little butter
- yolk of an egg
- a little white sugar

## Directions

1. Make a stiff paste with biscuit-powder and milk and water.
2. Add a little butter, the yolk of an egg, and a little white sugar.
3. Cut into pieces, and mould with the hand.
4. Bake in a brisk oven.
5. These cakes should not be too thin.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Matso Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a shin of beef
- four pounds of gravy beef
- a calf's foot
- four quarts of water
- celery
- carrots
- turnips
- pepper
- salt
- a bunch of sweet herbs
- half a pound of matso flour
- two ounces of chopped suet
- ginger
- nutmeg
- four beaten eggs
- a small onion
- a dessert-spoonful of oil

## Directions

1. Boil down half a shin of beef, four pounds of gravy beef and a calf's foot may be added if approved in four quarts of water
2. Season with celery, carrots, turnips, pepper and salt, and a bunch of sweet herbs
3. Let the whole stew gently for eight hours, then strain, and let it stand to get cold, when the fat must be removed
4. Return it to the saucepan to warm up
5. Take half a pound of matso flour, two ounces of chopped suet, season with a little pepper, salt, ginger, and nutmeg
6. Mix with this four beaten eggs, and make it into a paste with a small onion shred and browned in a dessert-spoonful of oil
7. Make the paste into rather large balls, taking care to make them very light
8. Ten minutes before serving throw in the balls
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: May Trank
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of light hock
- about a dozen sprigs of woodruffe
- a quarter of an orange
- about two ounces of powdered sugar
- a bottle of sparkling wine added to four or five bottles of still hock
- ice

## Directions

1. Take with each bottle of light hock about a dozen sprigs of woodruffe and a quarter of an orange cut in small slices
2. Add about two ounces of powdered sugar
3. Remove the herbs after having been in the wine half an hour or longer, according to taste
4. Add a bottle of sparkling wine added to four or five bottles of still hock as a great improvement
5. Add a little ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mayonnaise
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Eggs
- 1 gill Oil
- 1/2 gill Vinegar
- Salt

## Directions

1. Put the yolks of the eggs into a basin, sprinkle over the salt, begin to stir them with a wooden spoon, dropping in the oil very slowly
2. The sauce must be kept thick, and the oil added very slowly
3. When it is quite thick and smooth, pour in the vinegar slowly, and it is ready for use
4. If made some time before it is required for table, it must be kept cool
5. It ought to stand in ice, and the vinegar should be added just before serving
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Mayonnaise Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 eggs (yolks only)
- a salt-spoonful of ordinary dry mustard
- about a tablespoonful of good vinegar
- quite a teacupful of olive oil
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Break each egg by knocking its side midway between the two ends against the rim of the bowl.
2. Allow the greater part of the white of the egg to escape into a small vessel, as it is not required for the mayonnaise.
3. With the yolk in one half of the shell, pour away all the white remaining in the other half.
4. Dextrously turn the yolk into the latter emptied shell and then get rid of the white left in the half previously occupied by the yolk.
5. Slip the two yolks into the bowl and break up with a few stirs of the egg-whisk.
6. Place within the bowl about a salt-spoonful of the ordinary dry mustard and beat well up in a second or two.
7. Add about a tablespoonful of good vinegar, the whisk going vigorously to work, and thus blend well together egg yolk, dry mustard, and vinegar.
8. With the left hand, tilt up the salad bowl and hold a bottle of salad oil at the same time.
9. Keep the bottle inverted over the contents of the bowl in such a way as to allow only a drop or so of the oil to escape at a time.
10. As the oil drops, keep unceasingly plying the mixture with the whisk.
11. Continue adding oil, faster and faster, until the amber-looking sauce is ready.
12. After stirring in a sufficient quantity of pepper and salt, the mayonnaise is complete.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Mead, Or Metheglin
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one part honey
- three parts boiling water
- spices
- ground malt
- toast
- yeast

## Directions

1. Dissolve one part of honey in three parts of boiling water
2. Flavour with spices
3. Add a portion of ground malt to a piece of toast dipped in yeast, to allow the malt to ferment
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Meat Cecils
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- meat
- crumbs of bread
- onions
- shalot
- lemon-peel
- salt
- nutmeg
- anchovies
- butter
- egg
- grated bread

## Directions

1. Mince any kind of meat, add crumbs of bread, onions, shalot, lemon-peel, salt, nutmeg, a couple of anchovies boned, and a small piece of butter
2. Mix well, and make into balls, with an egg
3. Sprinkle over them crumbs of grated bread, and fry them brown
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Meat Sanders
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef or mutton
- onion
- shalot
- pepper
- salt
- gravy
- mashed potatoes
- fat

## Directions

1. Mince beef or mutton small with onion, shalot, pepper and salt, adding a little gravy
2. Put into scallop shells or tins, with a little gravy, making them about half full
3. Fill up with mashed potatoes
4. Put a little fat on the top of each, and brown in a Dutch oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Melena Pie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cold veal or chicken
- smoked beef or tongue
- lemon-juice
- nutmeg
- good beef or veal gravy
- yolks of four eggs
- rich pastry

## Directions

1. Mince finely cold veal or chicken, with smoked beef or tongue
2. Season well, add lemon-juice, and a little nutmeg
3. Let it simmer in a small quantity of good beef or veal gravy
4. While on the fire, stir in the yolks of four eggs
5. Put it in a dish to cool
6. Cover it with a rich pastry and bake it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Melted Butter
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of butter
- a dessert-spoonful of flour
- two table-spoonfuls of milk
- six table-spoonfuls water

## Directions

1. Cut about two ounces of butter into pieces
2. Put it into a stewpan with a dessert-spoonful of flour and two table-spoonfuls of milk
3. When well mixed add six table-spoonfuls more water
4. Hold it over the fire and let it simmer till thick, turning it one way
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Melted Butter Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Water
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Flour
- Salt

## Directions

1. Put half the butter into a small saucepan, and when it is dissolved stir in the flour and mix smoothly; pour in the water.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Meringues
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- whites of eight eggs
- half a pound of white sugar
- essence
- fine preserved sugar

## Directions

1. Whisk the whites of eight eggs up to a strong froth
2. Stir in half a pound of white sugar, as lightly as possible
3. Flavour them with any essence you please
4. Lay them on white paper the shape of half an egg
5. Sift fine preserved sugar on them, and blow off all that does not stick
6. Bake them on a board on a slow oven
7. When of a pale brown, they are done
8. Take them off the paper, and beat in the under part with a spoon, to form a hollow, and dry them
9. Fill them with cream, or any preserved fruit, and stick two together, which will form an egg shape
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mild Pickle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Water, four gallons
- Salt, a quart and a half
- Saltpetre, a wine-glassful

## Directions

1. Boil and skim
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Milk Biscuits
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- ½ lb. Flour
- 1 gill Milk
- 1 oz. Butter
- ⅛ saltspoonful Salt

## Directions

1. Rub the butter into the flour, sprinkle in the salt, and make into a dough with the milk.
2. Knead till smooth, roll out very thin.
3. Cut into small rounds, prick well with a fork, put on to a floured tin and bake for about ten minutes.
4. They should not get brown.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Milk Punch
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six oranges and lemons
- sugar
- a bottle of rum or brandy
- two pounds of sugar
- four quarts of water
- one quart of new milk, boiling hot

## Directions

1. Pare six oranges and lemons as thin as you can
2. Grate them over with sugar to get the flavour
3. Steep the peels in a bottle of rum or brandy for twenty-four hours
4. Squeeze the fruit on two pounds of sugar
5. Add to it four quarts of water and one of new milk boiling hot
6. Add the rum
7. Run it through a jelly-bag, bottle and cork
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Milk Quits (American)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour, sifted
- half a pound of butter
- two eggs
- six wine-glasses of milk
- two wine-glasses of yeast

## Directions

1. Cut the butter into the milk, and warm it lightly in the stove
2. Sift the flour into the pan and pour the milk and butter into it
3. Beat the eggs and pour them in also, lastly the yeast
4. Mix all well together, flour your paste-board, put the dough on it and knead it
5. Cut the dough in small pieces and knead them in round balls
6. Stick the tops of them with a fork, lay them in butter-pans, and set them to rise
7. When they are light, put them in a moderate oven and bake them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Milk Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Potatoes
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 Onion
- 1 pint of Milk
- 3 pints of Water
- salt
- pepper
- fried bread

## Directions

1. Peel, wash, and slice up the potatoes and onions and put them into a saucepan with the butter
2. Stir them about till all the butter is dissolved and worked into the potatoes, but they must not get brown
3. Pour over the boiling water and boil until they are of a pulp
4. Rub them through a sieve, return to the saucepan, add the milk and seasoning
5. Stir till it boils
6. Pour into a hot tureen, and serve with fried bread
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Minced Veal
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal
- bechamel sauce
- a little milk
- a small quantity of floured butter
- a small piece of lemon peel
- bread sippets

## Directions

1. Cut the veal in small pieces, and put it in a stewpan with some bechamel sauce, a little milk, and a small quantity of floured butter, a small piece of lemon peel, and warm
2. Serve with bread sippets
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mincemeat a la Soyer
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four pounds and a half of beef suet
- a quarter of a pound of candied lemon and orange-peel
- a quarter of a pound of citron
- a pound and a half of lean cooked beef
- three pounds and a half of apples, cored and peeled
- four pounds and a half of currants, well washed and picked
- two ounces of mixed spice
- two pounds of moist sugar
- juice of eight lemons
- a pint of brandy
- a pound and a half of raisins, well stoned and chopped (optional)

## Directions

1. Skin and chop the beef suet very fine
2. Chop the candied lemon and orange-peel, citron, lean cooked beef, and apples fine
3. Put all ingredients into a large pan with the currants, mixed spice, and moist sugar
4. Mix the whole well together with the juice of eight lemons and a pint of brandy
5. Place it in jars and tie down for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mint Julep
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- about a dozen sprigs of the tender shoots of mint
- a spoonful of white sugar
- equal proportions of peach and common brandy
- rasped or pounded ice

## Directions

1. Put into a tumbler about a dozen sprigs of the tender shoots of mint
2. upon them put a spoonful of white sugar, and equal proportions of peach and common brandy, so as to fill up one-third, or perhaps a little less
3. Take rasped or pounded ice, and fill up the tumbler
4. As the ice melts you drink
5. Epicures may rub the lips of the tumbler with a piece of fresh pineapple, and the tumbler itself is often encrusted outside with stalactites of ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mint Julep
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- about a dozen sprigs of tender shoots of mint
- a spoonful of white sugar
- equal proportions of peach and common brandy
- rasped or pounded ice
- fresh pineapple

## Directions

1. Put into a tumbler about a dozen sprigs of the tender shoots of mint
2. Upon them put a spoonful of white sugar
3. Add equal proportions of peach and common brandy, so as to fill up one-third, or perhaps a little less
4. Take rasped or pounded ice, and fill up the tumbler
5. Rub the lips of the tumbler with a piece of fresh pineapple
6. As the ice melts you drink
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mint Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a small quantity of fresh mint
- parsley
- a tea-spoonful of moist sugar
- a quarter of a pint of vinegar

## Directions

1. Wash a small quantity of fresh mint with a little parsley
2. pick the stalks out, mince them fine
3. put in a sauceboat, with a tea-spoonful of moist sugar, and a quarter of a pint of vinegar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mississippi Punch
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- One glass of Outard brandy
- half a glass of Jamaica rum
- a table-spoonful of arrack
- a quarter of a lemon
- a table-spoonful of white sugar
- water and ice

## Directions

1. Let it be thoroughly mixed, and serve with a straw
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mock Turtle Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one ounce of lean bacon or ham
- a pound and a half of lean gravy beef
- a cow-heel
- the inner rind of a carrot
- a sprig of lemon-thyme
- winter-savory
- three times the quantity of parsley
- a few green leaves of sweet basil
- two shallots
- a large onion
- four cloves
- eighteen corns of allspice
- eighteen corns of black pepper
- a quarter of a pint of cold water
- boiling water
- two or three table-spoonfuls of flour
- the juice of a lemon
- a table-spoonful of mushroom ketchup
- a tea-spoonful of salt
- half a tea-spoonful of ground black pepper
- a little nutmeg
- a glass of sherry or Madeira

## Directions

1. Line the bottom of a stew pan that will hold five pints with an ounce of lean bacon or ham, a pound and a half of lean gravy beef, a cow-heel, the inner rind of a carrot, a sprig of lemon-thyme, winter-savory, three times the quantity of parsley, a few green leaves of sweet basil, and two shallots
2. Put in a large onion with four cloves stuck in it, eighteen corns of allspice, the same of black pepper
3. Pour on these a quarter of a pint of cold water, cover the stewpan, and set it on a slow fire to boil gently for a quarter of an hour
4. Take off the cover, and watch it; and when it has got a good brown colour, fill up the stewpan with boiling water, and let it simmer gently for two hours
5. If you wish to have the full benefit of the meat, only stew it till it is just tender, cut it into mouthfuls, and put it into the soup
6. Pour to two or three table-spoonfuls of flour a ladleful of the gravy, and stir it quickly till it is well mixed
7. Pour it back into the stewpan where the gravy is, and let it simmer gently for half an hour longer
8. Skim it, and then strain it through a tamis into the stewpan
9. Cut the cow-heel into pieces about an inch square
10. Squeeze through a sieve the juice of a lemon, a table-spoonful of mushroom ketchup, a tea-spoonful of salt, half a tea-spoonful of ground black pepper, a little nutmeg, and a glass of sherry or Madeira
11. Let it simmer for five minutes
12. Forcemeat or egg balls may be added
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Moost Aye; Save-all Pudding or Pie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- scraps of bread
- a little milk
- three eggs
- three ounces of sugar
- a little nutmeg
- ginger
- allspice
- a few currants

## Directions

1. Put scraps of bread in a saucepan, with a little milk
2. break in three eggs, three ounces of sugar, a little nutmeg, ginger, and allspice
3. stew well together
4. This pudding may be either boiled or baked
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Moselle Cup No. 1
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of still or sparkling Moselle
- one bottle of soda-water
- a glass of sherry or brandy
- four or five thin slices of pineapple
- the peel of half a lemon
- powdered sugar
- lumps of clear ice

## Directions

1. To each bottle of still or sparkling Moselle add one bottle of soda-water
2. Add a glass of sherry or brandy
3. Add four or five thin slices of pineapple and the peel of half a lemon cut very thin
4. Add powdered sugar according to taste
5. Let the whole stand about an hour
6. Before serving add some lumps of clear ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Moselle Cup No. 2
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of still or sparkling Moselle
- one bottle of soda-water
- a glass of sherry or brandy
- a pint of fresh strawberries or three or four peaches or nectarines
- the peel of half a lemon
- powdered sugar
- lumps of clear ice

## Directions

1. To each bottle of still or sparkling Moselle add one bottle of soda-water
2. Add a glass of sherry or brandy
3. Substitute a pint of fresh strawberries, or three or four peaches or nectarines for the pineapple
4. Add the peel of half a lemon cut very thin and powdered sugar according to taste
5. Let the whole stand about an hour
6. Before serving add some lumps of clear ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Moselle Cup No. 3
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of still or sparkling Moselle
- one bottle of soda-water
- sprigs of woodruffe
- the peel of half a lemon
- powdered sugar
- lumps of clear ice

## Directions

1. To each bottle of still or sparkling Moselle add one bottle of soda-water
2. Add sprigs of woodruffe instead of fruit
3. Add the peel of half a lemon cut very thin and powdered sugar according to taste
4. Let the whole stand about an hour
5. Before serving add some lumps of clear ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Moselle Cup No. 4
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of still or sparkling Moselle
- one bottle of soda-water
- a glass or two of milk punch or essence of punch
- the peel of half a lemon
- powdered sugar
- lumps of clear ice

## Directions

1. To each bottle of still or sparkling Moselle add one bottle of soda-water
2. Add a glass or two of milk punch or essence of punch instead of sherry or brandy
3. Add a little more of the lemon-peel and powdered sugar according to taste
4. Let the whole stand about an hour
5. Before serving add some lumps of clear ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mother Eve's Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pennyworth of eggs
- half a dozen apples, well pared and well chopped
- six ounces of bread crumbs, grated fine
- six ounces of currants
- five ounces of sugar
- some salt
- some nutmeg

## Directions

1. Combine eggs, chopped apples, bread crumbs, currants, sugar, salt, and nutmeg
2. Boil for three hours without hurry
3. Serve without sugar or butter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mrs. 's Pastry
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- butter
- dripping
- one or two eggs
- warm water

## Directions

1. Take flour, and butter, and dripping enough to make the crust—half butter and half dripping, and to one pound of flour put half the weight of butter
2. Rub the flour and butter well together
3. Beat up one or two eggs, with sufficient warm water to mix the flour
4. Roll once
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mrs. Hill's Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of flour
- a pound of sugar
- a quarter of a pound of butter
- the yolks of two eggs
- the peel of two lemons

## Directions

1. Mix a pound of flour, a pound of sugar, a quarter of a pound of butter, the yolks of two eggs, and the peel of two lemons cut very small
2. Roll into thin cakes
3. Bake in the oven after the bread is drawn
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Muffins
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- five eggs
- a quart of milk
- two ounces of butter
- a teaspoonful of salt
- two large table-spoonfuls of yeast
- enough flour to make a stiff batter

## Directions

1. Warm the milk and butter
2. Cover the mixture, and set it to rise in a warm place
3. When it has risen sufficiently grease your baking-iron and muffin rings
4. Bake them a light brown
5. Tear them asunder, and butter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mulled Ale
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of good sound ale
- a little nutmeg
- sugar
- three eggs
- a little cold ale
- a table-spoonful of brandy

## Directions

1. Boil a pint of good sound ale with a little nutmeg and sugar
2. Beat up three eggs and mix them with a little cold ale
3. Add the hot ale to it gradually and pour backwards and forwards from one vessel to another to prevent its curdling
4. Warm and stir till it thickens
5. Add a table-spoonful of brandy and serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mullet And Tomatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Mullet
- 6 Tomatoes
- Bread Crumbs
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- Salt and Pepper
- 1 oz. Butter

## Directions

1. Fillet and slice up the mullet, season each slice with parsley, pepper, and salt
2. Dip the tomatoes in boiling water, skim and slice them up
3. Butter a pie-dish, lay in the slices of fish and tomatoes alternately
4. Cover the top with bread crumbs and little pieces of butter
5. Cover with buttered paper and bake in a moderate oven for half an hour
6. Take off the paper, and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Mulligatawny Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a few onions
- a few slices of bacon
- a fowl
- a little garlic
- about a quart and a half of stock
- two tablespoonfuls of curry
- salt
- lemon pickle or mango juice
- boiled rice

## Directions

1. Fry a few onions of a brown colour
2. Put a few slices of bacon into the stewpan and cut a fowl up in moderate pieces, then brown them
3. Put in the fried onions, a little garlic, and about a quart and a half of stock
4. Simmer till the fowl is tender
5. Skim carefully, and when the fowl is nearly done, rub two tablespoonfuls of curry to the soup
6. Add salt and lemon pickle or mango juice
7. Send up boiled rice with the mulligatawny in a separate dish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mulligatawny Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 quarts Stock
- 1 Apple
- 1 Onion
- 1 Carrot
- 1/2 oz. Curry Powder
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 oz. Butter

## Directions

1. Slice up the apple, onion, and carrot, and fry them in the butter
2. Sprinkle over the curry powder and flour and brown that too
3. Pour over the boiling stock and stir until it boils up
4. Simmer gently for one hour
5. Rub through a sieve and return to the saucepan
6. Bring to the boil, flavour with salt and lemon juice
7. Pour into a warm tureen and serve
8. Send well-boiled rice to the table with this soup
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Mushroom Ketchup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Fresh gathered mushrooms
- Common salt
- Cloves, half an ounce per gallon
- Mustard-seed, half an ounce per gallon
- Allspice, one ounce per gallon
- Black pepper, one ounce per gallon
- Ginger bruised, one ounce per gallon

## Directions

1. Sprinkle mushrooms with common salt for three days
2. Squeeze out the juice
3. To each gallon of juice add cloves and mustard-seed of each half an ounce
4. Add allspice, black pepper, and ginger bruised, one ounce
5. Boil sufficiently
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mutton Broth
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of mutton
- sufficient cold water
- a tea-spoonful of salt
- a small quantity of pepper
- a table-spoonful of the best grits
- an onion
- three turnips

## Directions

1. Take two pounds of mutton, any part; put it into a stewpan, with sufficient cold water, a tea-spoonful of salt, a small quantity of pepper, a table-spoonful of the best grits, and an onion.
2. Let it simmer, and skim it often.
3. Put into it three turnips and an onion, let it simmer for a couple of hours.
4. It can be thickened with oatmeal, rice, or Scotch barley.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mutton Broth
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 or 5 scrags of mutton and shank bones
- Cold water
- Teaspoonful of salt
- 1 dozen peppercorns
- One onion and carrot (if vegetables are allowed)

## Directions

1. Carefully trim the scrags of mutton and remove the pith from the bones
2. Wipe with a damp cloth
3. Break these and the shank bones into very small pieces
4. Put them into an enamelled saucepan well covered with cold water
5. Add a teaspoonful of salt and stand on the stove
6. When it boils up remove the scum very carefully
7. Add 1 dozen peppercorns and an onion and carrot if vegetables are allowed the patient
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Mutton Broth
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 or 5 scrags of mutton and shank bones
- 1 teaspoonful of salt
- 1 dozen peppercorns
- 1 onion
- 1 carrot
- rice or pearl barley

## Directions

1. Carefully trim the scrags of mutton, remove the pith from the bones, and wipe with a damp cloth
2. Break these and the shank bones into very small pieces
3. Put them into an enamelled saucepan, well covered with cold water
4. Add a teaspoonful of salt
5. Stand on the stove, and when it boils up remove the scum very carefully
6. Add 1 dozen peppercorns, and an onion and carrot, if vegetables are allowed the patient
7. Boil steadily for eight or nine hours; the liquor should then be reduced to one quart
8. Strain off, and, if possible, let it stand till quite cold; it should then be in a jelly, and can be made hot as required
9. When serving this to a convalescent a spoonful of rice or pearl barley well washed in cold water and boiled in either stock or milk may be added
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Mutton Chops in Batter
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Eggs
- 2 lbs. Chops
- 1 pint Milk
- 1 lb. Flour
- Salt and Pepper
- Dripping

## Directions

1. Break the eggs into a basin, beat in the flour with a fork, then add gradually the milk, season with a little pepper and salt.
2. Rub some dripping on a baking dish, pour in the batter, lay in the chops.
3. Put into a moderate oven and bake for about one hour and a quarter.
4. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Mutton Hams
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Fat mutton
- Two ounces of sugar
- An ounce of common salt
- Half a spoonful of saltpetre

## Directions

1. Mix sugar with salt and saltpetre
2. Well rub the meat with this mixture
3. Place in a pan
4. Beat and turn once a day for ten days
5. Dry and smoke
6. Cut rashers thin and serve hot, or serve the hams cold after having been previously simmered sufficiently
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mutton or Ham Bone and Potatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Bone of Mutton or Ham
- 1 Onion
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 pint Water or Stock
- 1 lb. Potatoes
- Salt
- 2 Cloves

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is hot put in the flour; mix together smoothly, pour in the water or stock, and stir until it boils.
2. Joint up a mutton or ham bone and lay it in; if it is mutton, add a little salt.
3. Bring it to the boil, put in the onion whole stuck with two cloves, and simmer for half an hour or longer.
4. Peel the potatoes, cut them in half and put them in, and cook until they are soft.
5. Take out the bones and place on a dish, put the potatoes round, and pour the sauce over.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Mutton Sausages
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mutton
- beef suet
- anchovies
- seasoning
- herbs

## Directions

1. To each pound of mutton add a pound of beef suet
2. Add anchovies, for relish, chopped fine
3. Add what seasoning you choose and herbs or not, as you please
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Mutton Stewed with Celery
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- end of neck of mutton or fillet from leg or shoulder
- water
- carrot
- turnip
- salt
- pepper
- forcemeat balls
- celery
- yolks of two eggs
- flour
- juice of half a lemon

## Directions

1. Take the end of a neck of mutton or a fillet taken from the leg or shoulder
2. Place it in a stewpan with enough water to cover it
3. Throw in a carrot and a turnip and season, but not too highly
4. When nearly done, remove the meat and strain off the gravy
5. Return both to the stewpan with forcemeat balls and some fine celery cut in small pieces
6. Let all stew together till perfectly done
7. Stir in the yolks of two eggs, a little flour, and the juice of half a lemon, which must be mixed with a little of the gravy before pouring into the stewpan
8. Take care to prevent its curdling
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Neck of Veal Braised
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a little lean bacon or ham
- an onion
- two carrots
- two heads of celery
- a glass of sherry or Madeira
- neck of veal
- a little water
- a little butter and flour
- a little lemon

## Directions

1. Put a little lean bacon or ham into a stewpan, with an onion, two carrots, two heads of celery, and a glass of sherry or Madeira
2. Lay on them the neck and a little water, and stew until it is tender
3. Strain off the liquor, mix a little butter and flour in a stewpan until brown, stir some of the liquor in, and boil it up
4. Skim it, and use a little lemon, and serve with the meat
5. The bacon must be browned in a salamander
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Nesselrode Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- about forty chestnuts
- a little syrup
- a pint of syrup made from a pound of sugar clarified
- vanilla
- a pint of cream
- the yolks of a dozen eggs
- a glass of maraschino
- an ounce of citron
- two ounces of currants
- two ounces of stewed raisins

## Directions

1. Blanch about forty chestnuts in boiling water, remove the husks and pound them in a mortar, adding a little syrup
2. rub them through a fine sieve, and mix in a basin, with a pint of syrup made from a pound of sugar clarified, and flavoured with vanilla, a pint of cream, and the yolks of a dozen eggs
3. Set this mixture over the fire, and keep stirring it, and just as it begins to boil take it off and pass it through a tamis
4. When it is cold put it into a freezing-pot, adding a glass of maraschino, and let the mixture set
5. then add an ounce of citron, two ounces of currants, and two ounces of stewed raisins
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Nesselrode Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- forty chestnuts
- syrup
- a pound of sugar
- vanilla
- a pint of cream
- yolks of a dozen eggs
- a glass of maraschino
- an ounce of citron
- two ounces of currants
- two ounces of stewed raisins
- whites of three eggs
- a little syrup

## Directions

1. Blanch about forty chestnuts in boiling water and remove the husks
2. Pound them in a mortar, adding a little syrup
3. Rub them through a fine sieve
4. Mix in a basin with a pint of syrup made from a pound of sugar clarified and flavoured with vanilla, a pint of cream, and the yolks of a dozen eggs
5. Set this mixture over the fire and keep stirring it
6. Just as it begins to boil take it off and pass it through a tamis
7. When it is cold put it into a freezing-pot, adding a glass of maraschino, and let the mixture set
8. Add an ounce of citron, two ounces of currants, and two ounces of stewed raisins
9. Add a plateful of whipped cream to the whites of three eggs beaten to a froth with a little syrup
10. When the pudding is frozen, put it into a pine-apple mould and close the lid
11. Place it again in the freezing-pan and cover over with pounded ice and saltpetre
12. Let it remain until required for table, then turn out the pudding and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: New England Chowder
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- haddock, cod, or any other solid fish
- one pound of fat salt pork
- onions
- flour
- water
- black pepper
- salt
- crackers or Philadelphia pilot bread

## Directions

1. Cut a haddock, cod, or any other solid fish into pieces three inches square
2. Pound a pound of fat salt pork in strips into the pot, set it on the fire and fry out the oil
3. Take out the pork and put in a layer of fish, over that a layer of onions in slices, then a layer of fish with slips of salt pork, then another layer of onions, and so on alternately until the fish is consumed
4. Mix some flour with as much water as will fill the pot, season with black pepper and salt to taste
5. Boil for half an hour
6. Have ready some crackers soaked in water until a little softened
7. Throw them into the chowder five minutes before you take it up
8. Serve in a tureen
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: New Potatoes Sauteed
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. New Potatoes
- 1 oz. Butter
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. Wash the potatoes and put them into boiling salt and water, and boil for ten minutes.
2. Take up, peel and cut them in half.
3. Melt the butter in a saucepan, and when quite hot, put in the potatoes and toss over the fire.
4. Sprinkle over the parsley, pepper, salt, and a few drops of lemon juice; cover down and cook gently till the potatoes are soft but not broken.
5. Put into a hot dish and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Norfolk Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three-quarters of a pound of butter
- three pounds and a half of flour
- a quarter of a pint of yeast
- water

## Directions

1. Melt the butter with water
2. Knead it well
3. Use an ounce for each biscuit
4. Bake on buttered paper
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Normandy Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Stale Buns
- 1 Egg
- 1 pint Milk
- Sugar

## Directions

1. Boil the milk and pour it over the beaten egg
2. Sweeten to taste
3. Put the buns into a pie-dish
4. Pour over the custard, cover and leave for an hour
5. Put into a moderate oven and bake for about half an hour
6. Serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Northumbrian Breakfast Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of flour
- four ounces of butter
- a little salt
- a tea-spoonful of sugar
- a proportionate quantity of milk or cream

## Directions

1. Rub butter well into flour
2. Add salt, sugar, and milk or cream
3. Knead it well together
4. Bake on a girdle
5. When done, open the cakes, and butter
6. Send them up hot to the breakfast-table, enveloped in a napkin
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Nottingham Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six pudding apples
- a light batter

## Directions

1. Peel and core six pudding apples, leaving the apples whole
2. place them in a pie-dish, and pour over them a light batter
3. bake about three-quarters of an hour
4. serve with sweet sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Nougat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pound of sweet almonds
- three-quarters of a pound of sugar in powder
- butter

## Directions

1. Blanch the almonds
2. Cut each almond into four or five slices
3. Dry them on a slow fire, taking care not to let them burn
4. Put three-quarters of a pound of sugar in powder into a pan
5. When melted through put in the almonds hot
6. Mix them well together
7. Put a layer round a buttered mould
8. Smooth them, round equally with a lemon
9. When small let it cool
10. If not quite hard when cold, put your shapes into a cool oven to dry
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Noyeau
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of bitter almonds
- a gallon of brandy
- two pounds of white sugar
- a little spice

## Directions

1. Blanch and pound two pounds of bitter almonds, and put to them a gallon of brandy, with two pounds of white sugar and a little spice
2. Stir it, and let the spirit remain in the almonds for a month, when the noyeau may be bottled for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oat Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of oatmeal
- a pint of buttermilk
- a quarter of an ounce of carbonate of soda
- a little salt
- half a pound of flour

## Directions

1. Soak a pound of oatmeal in a pint of buttermilk
2. Rub a quarter of an ounce of carbonate of soda and a little salt in half a pound of flour, and mix with the oatmeal
3. Roll out any thickness, and bake in a moderate oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oatmeal Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of the finest oatmeal
- half a pound of butter
- half a pound of loaf sugar
- two eggs
- cold water

## Directions

1. Pound the sugar
2. Mix all the ingredients into a stiff paste with cold water
3. The eggs are not to be beaten for these biscuits
4. Make the paste into small balls
5. Flatten them
6. Bake on tins
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Odd Meat with Poached Eggs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef or mutton slices
- eggs
- potatoes
- greens

## Directions

1. Cut slices of beef or mutton of equal thickness, and either broil, fry, or brown, in an oven before the fire
2. Lay them in a dish to keep warm, while you poach eggs and mash potatoes; or you may fry potatoes and greens
3. Place the vegetables on the dish, the meat on the top, and garnish with the poached eggs
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oil Twist
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a quartern of dough
- one gill of the best Florence oil
- half a pound of currants
- half a pound of moist sugar
- a little cinnamon

## Directions

1. Take half a quartern of dough, one gill of the best Florence oil, half a pound of currants, the same of moist sugar, and a little cinnamon.
2. Mix all well together.
3. Make it up in the form of a twist.
4. Bake it.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Omelette aux Confitures
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- lemon-peel
- cream
- orange or apple marmalade, apricot or gooseberry jam, or currant jelly

## Directions

1. Beat up the yolks and whites of six eggs separate.
2. Add to the yolks a little grated lemon-peel.
3. Add the whites, and beat the whole well together, adding a little cream, two spoonfuls of orange or apple marmalade, apricot or gooseberry jam, currant jelly, or any preserve.
4. Fry and finish in the same way as a sugar omelette.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Omelette aux Fines Herbes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three eggs, well beaten
- one shalot
- parsley
- a little ham or bacon
- a little cayenne
- salt to taste
- two ounces of butter

## Directions

1. Chop the shalot, parsley, and ham or bacon very fine all together
2. Mix with the well beaten eggs, cayenne, and salt to taste
3. Put two ounces of butter into the frying-pan
4. When it boils pour in the batter and fry
5. Fry for about five minutes
6. Serve with brown gravy, vinegar, and cayenne
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Omelette Souffle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- lemon-peel
- orange-flower water
- powdered sugar
- salt
- butter

## Directions

1. Put the yolks of six eggs into a basin and the whites into another, with a little rasped lemon-peel, a spoonful of orange-flower water, some powdered sugar, and a little salt.
2. Whip the yolks into a froth.
3. Mix the yolks and whites lightly together.
4. Put a lump of fresh butter into an omelette pan, and, when it boils, pour the omelette into the pan.
5. When the bottom of it has become firm, turn it over into the dish to be baked in, the sides of which may be raised with paper.
6. Bake it in an oven or before the fire.
7. The moment it has risen, it must be sent to table, or it will sink.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Omelettes aux Huitres
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- oysters
- butter
- their own liquor
- coulis
- pepper
- eggs
- salt
- parsley
- bread
- cream

## Directions

1. Scald some oysters
2. put two-thirds in a stewpan, with a little butter
3. moisten them with some of their own liquor and some coulis, with a little pepper
4. Take care the oysters are well done
5. break a dozen eggs
6. season them with salt, parsley, chopped, and pepper
7. have some sippets of bread, about the size of half-a-crown
8. give two or three blows of a knife to the oysters that remain
9. put into the beaten eggs, with some cream
10. beat them all together
11. pour your omelette into a pan, with melted butter
12. stir it till well done
13. put the oysters and their sauce in the dish
14. roll your omelette in the fryingpan
15. serve in the middle of the ragout of oysters
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Onion Salad
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mild onions
- pepper
- cayenne
- best salad oil
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Cut mild onions in thin slices
2. Dress with pepper and cayenne, and three parts of the best salad oil to one of vinegar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Onion Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two dozen onions
- salt
- water
- butter
- flour
- half a tea-spoonful of salt
- about half a pint of milk

## Directions

1. Take two dozen onions and peel them
2. put them in salt and water for an hour, and then boil them until tender
3. drain them, and chop them up
4. Put them in a clean saucepan, with some butter and flour, half a tea-spoonful of salt, and about half a pint of milk
5. simmer till done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Onion Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Small Onions
- 1 oz. Butter
- Lemon Juice
- 1 pint Milk
- 1 oz. Bread Crumbs
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Peel the onions, put them into cold water, and let them boil for a minute.
2. Strain away the water, cover again with cold water, boil up and cook till soft; take out of the water, chop small.
3. Put the butter and milk into a saucepan, and when it boils put in the bread crumbs and onions.
4. Cook slowly for five minutes, season with pepper, salt, and a few drops of lemon juice, and it is ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Onion Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 Onions
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1½ oz. Flour
- 1 gill of Milk
- 2 quarts of Stock
- Salt and Pepper
- lemon juice
- fried bread

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up the onions and fry them in the butter till they are a good brown colour
2. Sprinkle over the flour and brown that too
3. Pour on the boiling stock and boil steadily till the onions are very soft, then rub through a sieve
4. If there is any fat on it remove it carefully
5. Pour back into the saucepan, add the milk, pepper, and salt, and boil up
6. Just before serving put in a few drops of lemon juice
7. Send fried bread to table with it
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Orange Brandy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- rind of three or four lemons
- rind of eight Seville oranges
- three pounds of sugar-candy, powdered
- one gallon of brandy

## Directions

1. Peel the rind of lemons and oranges thin
2. Powder the sugar-candy
3. Steep the whole in a gallon of brandy for four or five days, stirring it frequently
4. Run it through a filterer to clear it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Orange Gingerbread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- a pound and three-quarters of treacle
- six ounces of candied orange-peel
- three-quarters of a pound of moist sugar
- one ounce of ground ginger
- one ounce of allspice
- half or three-quarters of a pound of butter
- yolk of eggs
- milk

## Directions

1. Add to two pounds of flour a pound and three-quarters of treacle, six ounces of candied orange-peel cut small, three-quarters of a pound of moist sugar, one ounce of ground ginger, and one ounce of allspice
2. Melt half or three-quarters of a pound of butter
3. Mix the whole well together and lay it by for six hours
4. Roll it out into pieces in the form of chequers
5. Bake an inch apart
6. Rub them over with a brush dipped in yolk of eggs and milk
7. When baked, rub over a second time
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Orange-Flower Water
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of orange-flowers
- two quarts of white wine

## Directions

1. Take two pounds of orange-flowers, as fresh as you can get them
2. Infuse them in two quarts of white wine
3. Distil them
4. It will yield a curious perfuming spirit
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Orangeade
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- juice of an orange
- peel of an orange
- boiling water
- water
- sugar

## Directions

1. Squeeze the juice of an orange
2. Pour boiling water on some of the peel, and cover it close
3. Boil water and sugar to a thin syrup and skim it
4. When all are cold, mix the juice, the infusion, and the syrup with as much water as will make a rich drink
5. Strain through a jelly-bag, and ice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Orgeat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound and a quarter of bitter almonds
- half a pound of sweet almonds, blanched
- nine pounds of loaf sugar
- six pints of water
- rinds of three lemons
- orange-flower water

## Directions

1. Pound the almonds in a mortar with the sugar
2. Add the water a little by degrees
3. Put the mixture on the fire with the lemon-peel
4. After a boil pour off the syrup and press the almonds, to extract the milk
5. Add the milk to the syrup
6. Strain the whole through a sieve
7. When cold add a little orange-flower water
8. Bottle the mixture
9. The orgeat is used as a summer drink, mixed with water, according to taste
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ox Heart
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ox heart
- veal stuffing
- paper
- flour
- hot water
- currant jelly

## Directions

1. Stuff the heart in the centre with veal stuffing
2. Have a few stuffing balls in the dripping-pan
3. Put paper on the heart at first
4. Afterwards take away the paper, and flour it so as to put a froth on
5. Serve hot, with hot water plates and currant jelly
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oxford Punch
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three fresh lemons
- loaf sugar
- two lemons
- two Seville oranges
- ten lemons
- four Seville oranges
- six glasses of calf's foot jelly
- four quarts of boiling water
- a bottle of capillaire
- half a pint of sherry
- a pint of brandy
- a pint of old rum
- a quart of orange shrub

## Directions

1. Rub the rind of three fresh lemons with loaf sugar so as to extract the oil
2. Peel finely two lemons more and two Seville oranges
3. Use the juice of ten lemons and four Seville oranges
4. Add six glasses of calf's foot jelly and put it into a large jug and stir the whole
5. Pour in four quarts of boiling water and set the jug on the hob for twenty minutes
6. Strain the liquor into a large bowl
7. Pour in a bottle of capillaire, half a pint of sherry, a pint of brandy, a pint of old rum, and a quart of orange shrub
8. Stir it well as you pour in the spirits
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oxford Right Cap
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a tumbler of tea
- sugar
- milk
- a slice of lemon
- a wine-glass of new milk
- a wine-glass of rum or brandy
- a new-laid egg

## Directions

1. Make half a tumbler of tea as usual with sugar and milk
2. Add a slice of lemon and a wine-glass of new milk
3. Add a wine-glass of rum or brandy
4. Beat up a new-laid egg and add to the mixture
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oxford Sausages
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pig meat from griskins
- veal
- beef suet
- powdered sage
- pepper
- salt
- eggs

## Directions

1. Take one pound and a half of pig meat, cut from the griskins, without any skin
2. Add half a pound of veal, and a pound and a half of beef suet
3. Mince these meats very finely
4. Mix them with a dessert-spoonful of powdered sage, pepper, and salt, to taste
5. Add the well-beaten yolks and whites of four eggs
6. Beat the whole together well, as much depends on the mixing
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oxfordshire Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 1½ oz. Rice
- 1 oz. Sugar
- Rind of Half a Lemon

## Directions

1. Wash the rice well, strain off the water and put it into a pie-dish.
2. Mix in the sugar and the rind of the lemon.
3. Pour over the milk, and let it stand for half an hour.
4. Put it into a very slow oven, and bake till firm. This is a very delicious pudding if properly made; it should be firm, but not dry.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Oxtail Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- oxtail
- carrots
- turnips
- celery
- herbs

## Directions

1. Make in a similar way to shin of beef soup
2. Serve pieces of the tail in joints up with the soup
3. Remove the vegetables and herbs out of the soup before being served
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oyster Patties
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- paste
- bread
- oysters
- nutmeg
- white pepper
- salt
- lemon
- cream
- oyster liquor

## Directions

1. Put paste into patty-pans, and cover them, with a bit of bread on each
2. bake them
3. Take off the beards of the oysters
4. cut the other parts in small bits
5. put them into a small saucepan, with a little nutmeg, white pepper and salt, lemon, cream and a small quantity of the oyster liquor
6. Simmer for a few minutes
7. fill the patty-pans
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oyster Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bottle Oysters
- 1 Lettuce
- Half a Lemon
- Mayonnaise or Salad Dressing

## Directions

1. Strain away the liquor from a bottle of oysters; put it into a saucepan, and when it boils put in the oysters and cook for five minutes; let them get cold in the liquor.
2. Wash and break up the lettuce and put some at the bottom of a bowl.
3. Strain the liquor from the oysters and mix a little with the dressing, stir in the oysters and spread over the lettuce.
4. Cover with more lettuce and garnish with slices of lemon and red radishes.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Oyster Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three dozen bearded oysters
- half a pint of melted butter
- milk
- mace
- cayenne

## Directions

1. Put three dozen bearded oysters into half a pint of melted butter
2. Let them just come to the boiling point
3. put the whole into a basin
4. Strain the liquor from them
5. add sufficient milk and a little mace and cayenne
6. Set by fire to become hot, but not to boil
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oyster Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bottle Oysters
- 1 pint of Milk
- Cornflour
- Vegetables
- 2 quarts Fish Stock
- 1 onion
- 6 cloves
- 2 doz. white peppercorns
- a fagot of herbs
- lemon juice
- nutmeg
- salt
- fried bread

## Directions

1. Put the stock and the liquor from the bottle of oysters into a saucepan with an onion stuck with six cloves, 2 doz. white peppercorns, and a fagot of herbs, and boil together for half an hour
2. Strain off and return to the saucepan with the milk
3. When nearly boiling thicken with a tablespoonful of cornflour and boil two or three minutes
4. Put in the oysters and simmer for five minutes
5. Flavour with a little lemon juice, nutmeg, and salt
6. Pour into a warm tureen, and send fried bread to table with it
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Oyster Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- liquor of a hundred oysters
- a quart of new milk
- a quart of water
- pepper
- salt
- a quarter of a pound of fresh butter
- flour
- a little nutmeg
- a little mace

## Directions

1. Put the liquor of a hundred oysters into a stewpan, with a quart of new milk, and the same quantity of water
2. Season with pepper and salt, with a quarter of a pound of fresh butter and flour
3. Let this boil for a short time, after which set it to cool
4. Beard the oysters, and add them to the liquid, and let them boil for two minutes
5. A little nutmeg and mace may be added
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oyster Stew
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bottle Oysters
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1/2 pint Milk
- 1/2 oz. Flour
- Pepper
- Salt
- Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. Make a sauce by directions given, using a little of the oyster liquor mixed with the milk; flavour with salt and pepper, and a little nutmeg and lemon juice.
2. Stir in the oysters and simmer for five minutes, it is then ready to serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Oysters and Bacon
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 doz. Large Oysters
- 3 Rashers Bacon
- Pepper
- Salt
- Lime Juice

## Directions

1. Mix some pepper, salt, and lemon juice together, and lay the oysters in this.
2. The bacon should be cut very thin, and then into strips about 1 inch broad and 3 inches long.
3. Roll these up, and thread on a skewer first a roll of bacon and then an oyster, until the skewer is full.
4. Lay on a baking sheet and cook in the oven for about ten minutes.
5. Have ready a hot dish, slip the bacon and oysters off the skewers on to this, and serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Oysters and Macaroni
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. macaroni
- 1 bottle oysters
- 1 gill milk or melted butter sauce
- cayenne
- salt
- lemon juice
- nutmeg
- bread crumbs
- butter

## Directions

1. Boil the macaroni in the oyster liquor or in weak stock till quite soft
2. Rub a little butter on a dish
3. Cut the macaroni into pieces two inches long and lay it at the bottom
4. On this place the oysters
5. Season them with cayenne, salt, and a little lemon juice or nutmeg
6. Pour over the milk or sauce
7. Cover with bread crumbs
8. Brown it in a quick oven
9. A few little pieces of butter laid on top of the crumbs make a richer dish
10. Serve very hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Oysters and Potatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bottle oysters
- 4 cold potatoes
- 1 egg
- 2 oz. flour
- pepper
- salt
- lemon juice
- milk

## Directions

1. Mash the potatoes and make them into a paste with the flour and egg
2. Roll out and cut into small pieces
3. Season the oysters with lemon juice, pepper, and salt
4. Put three or four oysters into each piece of potato crust
5. Roll it up and brush over with milk
6. Bake for twenty minutes
7. Pile high on a dish and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Oysters Broiled
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- oysters
- their shells
- their liquor
- pepper
- butter
- lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Take them from the shells and beard them
2. put them with their liquor into scallop-tins, with a little pepper and butter
3. Put the shells upon a gridiron, over a good fire
4. serve them when plump and hot
5. Squeeze a little lemon-juice on them as they come from the fire
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oysters Fried
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- milk
- flour
- pepper
- salt
- oysters
- nutmeg
- bread-crumbs

## Directions

1. Make a batter of eggs, milk, and flour
2. season it with pepper and salt
3. dip the oysters into the batter, which ought to be rather thick
4. fry them of a light brown
5. add a little nutmeg and bread-crumbs to the seasoning
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oysters Marinaded
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- oysters
- their liquor
- lemon-juice or vinegar
- pepper
- salt
- nutmeg
- batter

## Directions

1. Put the oysters into a saucepan for a few minutes, to blanch with their liquor
2. take them out
3. lay them on a linen cloth to drain
4. place them for an hour in lemon-juice or vinegar, pepper and salt, and a little nutmeg
5. dip them into batter
6. fry them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oysters Roasted
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- oysters
- their shells

## Directions

1. Place the oysters in their shells between the bars of a fire, or over a clear fire
2. In five minutes they will be done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oysters Scalloped
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- oysters
- shells
- butter
- bread-crumbs
- mace

## Directions

1. Open the shells carefully, and let the oysters remain in the scallop-shell
2. put some butter, bread-crumbs, and a little mace into the shells
3. brown in a Dutch oven before the fire
4. Send them to table hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Oysters Stewed
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of oysters
- their own liquor
- a quarter of a pint of cream or milk
- mace
- cayenne
- salt
- an ounce of butter
- flour
- lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Put a pint of oysters into a stewpan, with their own liquor
2. add to the liquor a quarter of a pint of cream or milk, some mace and cayenne, and a little salt
3. when it boils stir in an ounce of butter mixed smoothly with a little flour
4. put in the oysters, bearded, or not, as you choose
5. simmer for a few moments
6. when ready to serve add a little lemon-juice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Palestine Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a knuckle of veal
- a calf's foot
- one pound of chorissa
- a large fowl
- four quarts of water
- a piece of fresh lemon-peel
- six Jerusalem artichokes
- a bunch of sweet herbs
- salt
- white pepper
- a nutmeg
- a blade of mace
- flour or ground rice

## Directions

1. Stew a knuckle of veal, a calf's foot, one pound of chorissa, and a large fowl, in four quarts of water
2. Add a piece of fresh lemon-peel, six Jerusalem artichokes, a bunch of sweet herbs, a little salt and white pepper, a nutmeg, and a blade of mace
3. When the fowl is thoroughly done remove the white parts to preserve for thickening, and let the rest continue stewing till the stock is sufficiently strong
4. Pound the white parts of the fowl and sprinkle with flour or ground rice, and stir in the soup after it has been strained, until it thickens
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pan Jam
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- kangaroo tails
- fat bacon
- mushrooms
- pepper

## Directions

1. Roast them in the ashes with the skin on.
2. When nearly done, scrape them well, and divide at the joints.
3. Put them into a pan, with a few slices of fat bacon, to which add a few mushrooms, pepper, &c.
4. Fry gently, and serve.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pancakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three spoonfuls of flour
- three eggs, well beaten
- a little salt
- small sprinkling of ginger

## Directions

1. Make a light batter of flour, eggs, salt, and ginger
2. Fry in thin layers in a pan, and turn
3. Roll and serve with sugar and lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Paon (Peacock) Revetu
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- peacock
- truffles
- mushrooms
- livers of fowls
- bacon
- salt
- spice
- thyme
- crumbs of bread
- bay-leaf
- butter
- water
- lemon
- flowers

## Directions

1. Take off the skin with the greatest care, so that the feathers do not get detached or broken
2. Stuff it with truffles, mushrooms, livers of fowls, bacon, salt, spice, thyme, crumbs of bread, and a bay-leaf
3. Wrap the claws and head in several folds of cloth
4. Envelope the body in buttered paper
5. Baste the head and claws with water during the cooking, to preserve them, and especially the tuft
6. Before taking it off the spit, brown the bird by removing the paper
7. Garnish with lemon and flowers
8. If to come on the table cold, place the bird in a wooden trencher, in the middle of which is fixed a wooden skewer, which should penetrate the body of the bird, to keep it upright
9. Arrange the claws and feathers in a natural manner, and the tail like a fan, supported with wire
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Parmentier's Salad Vinegar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three ounces shalots
- three ounces sweet savory
- three ounces chives
- three ounces tarragon
- two table-spoonfuls of dried mint leaves
- two table-spoonfuls of balm
- strong white wine vinegar

## Directions

1. Beat shalots, sweet savory, chives, and tarragon, of each three ounces, two table-spoonfuls of dried mint leaves, and the same of balm together in a mortar
2. Put them into a stone gallon bottle
3. Fill up with strong white wine vinegar
4. Cork it securely
5. Let it stand a fortnight exposed to the sun
6. Filter it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Parsnips and Parsley Butter
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 or 5 Parsnips
- 1/2 oz. Flour
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 gill Milk
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Scrape and cut up the parsnips (or cold ones will do).
2. If raw, boil them in water seasoned with salt for three-quarters of an hour.
3. Make the butter, flour, and milk into a sauce by directions given, and season nicely.
4. Stir in the parsley, put in the parsnips, bring to the boil and simmer for ten minutes.
5. Arrange them on a hot dish, pour the sauce over, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Passover Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- biscuit-powder
- milk
- water
- a small piece of butter
- yolk of an egg
- a little white sugar

## Directions

1. Make a stiff paste with biscuit-powder, milk, and water
2. Add a small piece of butter, the yolk of an egg, and a little white sugar
3. Cut it into pieces and mould with the hand
4. Bake in a crisp oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Passover Fritters
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a tea-cupful of biscuit-powder
- beaten eggs
- white sifted sugar
- grated lemon-peel
- a spoonful of orange-flower water

## Directions

1. Mix into a smooth batter a tea-cupful of biscuit-powder, with beaten eggs, and sweeten with white sifted sugar.
2. Add grated lemon-peel, and a spoonful of orange-flower water.
3. Fry of a light brown.
4. The flavour may be varied by substituting a few beaten almonds, with one or two bitter, instead of the orange-flower water.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Passover Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- equal quantities of biscuit-powder and shred suet
- half the quantity of currants and raisins
- a little spice and sugar
- one ounce of candied peels
- five well-beaten eggs

## Directions

1. Mix equal quantities of biscuit-powder and shred suet, half the quantity of currants and raisins, a little spice and sugar, with an ounce of candied peels, and five well-beaten eggs.
2. Make these into a stiff batter.
3. Boil well.
4. Serve with a sweet sauce.
5. This pudding is excellent baked in a pudding-tin; it must be turned out when served.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Patent Yeast
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of hops
- three gallons of water
- half a peck of ground malt
- three pints of patent yeast

## Directions

1. Boil six ounces of hops in three gallons of water for three hours
2. Strain it off, and let it stand ten minutes
3. Add half a peck of ground malt, stir it well up, and cover it over
4. Return the hops, and put the same quantity of water to them again, boiling them the same time as before
5. Strain it off to the first mash
6. Stir it up and let it remain four hours
7. Strain it off, and set it to work at 90° with three pints of patent yeast
8. Let it stand about twenty hours
9. Take the scum off the top, and strain it through a hair sieve
10. It will then be fit for use. One pint is sufficient to make a bushel of bread.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pea Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of peas
- two quarts of water
- mutton bones
- two heads of celery
- a sprig of savory
- a little sweet marjoram
- pepper
- salt
- an onion
- a pound of bacon
- toasted bread
- powdered mint

## Directions

1. Soak the peas over night
2. Put them on to simmer in two quarts of water, with some mutton bones, two heads of celery, a sprig of savory, and a little sweet marjoram, pepper, and salt
3. Let it simmer for an hour
4. Add an onion, cut small
5. When nearly done, take out the mutton bones, and add a pound of bacon, cut into little pieces
6. Serve with toasted bread and powdered mint
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pea Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Split Peas
- 2 Onions
- 1 Head of Celery
- 1 oz. Butter or Dripping
- 2 Carrots
- 2 doz. Peppercorns
- 3 quarts Water
- Salt
- dried mint
- fried bread

## Directions

1. Wash the peas well in cold water, and put them into a saucepan with the vegetables sliced up, the peppercorns and the water.
2. Bring to the boil and boil steadily for four hours, then rub through a sieve and return to the saucepan.
3. Season well with salt, and stir in 1 oz. butter or dripping.
4. Bring to the boil and pour into a warm tureen.
5. Send some dried mint and fried bread to table with it.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Peas Stewed with Oil
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of peas
- half a lettuce
- a little mint
- a small onion
- two tablespoonfuls of oil
- a dessert-spoonful of powdered sugar
- water

## Directions

1. Put half a pint of peas into a stewpan
2. Add half a lettuce chopped small, a little mint, a small onion cut up, two tablespoonfuls of oil, and a dessert-spoonful of powdered sugar, with water sufficient to cover the peas
3. Watch from time to time that they do not become too dry
4. Stew gently, taking care that they do not burn, till they are perfectly soft
5. When done they should look of a yellowish brown
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pease Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- peas
- cloth
- an ounce of butter
- one egg
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Soak the peas for ten or twelve hours
2. Tie them loosely in a cloth, leaving room for them to swell
3. Simmer for a couple of hours
4. When tender, drain them
5. Rub them through a colander with a wooden spoon
6. Add an ounce of butter, one egg beat up, and pepper and salt to taste
7. Beat them well together
8. Tie lightly in a cloth
9. Boil for half an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Petits Puits d'Amour
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- puff paste
- white of an egg
- sugar
- preserves

## Directions

1. Roll out puff paste about half an inch thick
2. Stamp out what will be used, work it up again and roll it the same thickness
3. Stamp out smaller pieces to correspond with the larger ones
4. Stamp out the centre of the smaller rings
5. Brush over the larger pieces with white of an egg
6. Place a small ring on the top of every large circular piece of paste
7. Egg over the tops and bake sharp for less than a quarter of an hour
8. Sift sugar over them and put them back in the oven to colour
9. Fill in the rings with preserves
10. Dish them up and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pheasants, Partridges, and Other Game, En Papillotes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pheasants
- partridges
- game birds
- butter
- mushrooms
- parsley
- herbs
- shalots
- flour
- salt
- spice
- stock
- wine

## Directions

1. Split the birds in halves
2. Stew them in butter, until nearly half done
3. Make a sauce of the butter, with mushrooms, chopped parsley, a few herbs and shalots
4. Add a little flour, salt, and spice; with some stock, and a glass of wine
5. Stew this sauce
6. Pour it over the halves of the birds
7. Wrap them in paper
8. Broil for about fifteen minutes
9. Serve in the paper, with the sauce as an accompaniment
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pheasants, Partridges, and Quails
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pheasants
- partridges
- quails
- bread sauce
- brown gravy

## Directions

1. Let them hang for a short time, not too long
2. Draw, singe, and truss them
3. Put them down to a clear fire, and let them be quickly done
4. Baste them incessantly
5. Serve with bread sauce, or good brown gravy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Piccalilli
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ginger, powdered
- curry-powder
- mustard
- salad oil
- cayenne pepper
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Mix ginger, powdered, curry-powder, and mustard, of each two ounces, well mixed
2. Dilute with three or four table-spoonfuls of salad oil
3. Add a little cayenne pepper, and the requisite quantity of vinegar
4. Pour it warm on the article that you are desirous of pickling
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pigeon Compote
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four pigeons
- bread
- bacon
- thyme
- parsley
- onion
- lemon-peel
- nutmeg
- pepper
- salt
- egg
- stock
- butter
- flour
- forcemeat balls

## Directions

1. Truss four pigeons as for boiling
2. Grate some bread
3. Scrape some bacon, or cut it into small slices
4. Chop some thyme, parsley, and onion, and some lemon-peel
5. Grate some nutmeg, and season with pepper and salt
6. Mix it up with an egg
7. Put this forcemeat into the craw of the pigeons
8. Lard the breasts, and fry them brown
9. Place them in a stewpan, with some stock, and simmer them for three-quarters of an hour
10. Thicken the gravy with a piece of butter rolled into some flour
11. Serve with forcemeat balls, and pour the gravy over the pigeons
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pikelets
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of milk
- half a pint of water
- an ounce of butter
- two lumps of sugar
- a little salt
- the yolks of two eggs and the white of one
- a table-spoonful of yeast
- one pound of flour

## Directions

1. Put half a pint of milk and the same quantity of water into a saucepan, with an ounce of butter, two lumps of sugar, and a little salt
2. stir it over the fire till rather warmer than new milk
3. beat up the yolks of two eggs and the white of one, and add a table-spoonful of yeast
4. Put them into a jug and mix the whole together
5. add one pound of flour, and stir it in free from lumps
6. Let the jug remain by the fire to rise
7. rub a little butter on the baking-iron, then pour on the batter to bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pink Champagne
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- nine pounds of loaf sugar
- three gallons of water
- a gallon of red and white currants
- a tea-cupful of yeast
- isinglass or white of egg

## Directions

1. Dissolve nine pounds of loaf sugar over the fire in three gallons of water, remove the scum
