2. Have ready picked a gallon of red and white currants, and pour over them the boiling liquor
3. Let it stand until cool, then add a tea-cupful of yeast
4. Allow it to ferment for two days, then put it in a cask, and add some isinglass or white of egg to clear
5. Bung up the cask, but don't do this too soon, or there will be an explosion
6. When ready for bottling add a small lump of sugar to each bottle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pink Pancakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a large red beetroot
- the yolks of five eggs
- two table-spoonfuls of flour
- four table-spoonfuls of cream
- powdered sugar
- nutmeg
- a wineglass of brandy
- lard for frying

## Directions

1. Boil a large red beetroot until it is very tender
2. Peel it, cut it into thin slices, and pound it to a pulp in a marble mortar
3. Add the yolks of eggs, flour, cream, powdered sugar, nutmeg, and brandy
4. Rub the whole into a batter
5. Fry the pancakes with lard
6. Serve hot, garnished with sweetmeats
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Plain Buns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- half a pound of moist sugar
- half a gill of good yeast
- half a pint of lukewarm milk
- half a pound of butter

## Directions

1. To two pounds of flour put half a pound of moist sugar
2. Make a cavity in the centre, and stir in half a gill of good yeast, half a pint of lukewarm milk, with enough flour to thicken it
3. Let it rise for half an hour or more
4. Melt half a pound of butter lukewarm
5. Stir it into the other ingredients with enough warm milk to make it a soft paste
6. Throw some flour over it, and let it rise for three-quarters of an hour
7. Have ready a baking-tin, mould the buns into sizes, and bake
8. When drawn from the oven, milk them over
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Plain Forcemeat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- equal quantity of meat and beef suet
- pepper
- salt
- cloves
- pounded lemon-peel
- nutmeg
- parsley
- sweet herbs
- a little shalot
- onion
- bread crumbs
- yolk of egg

## Directions

1. Take an equal quantity of meat and beef suet
2. Mince and chop small
3. Add pepper, salt, cloves, pounded lemon-peel, and nutmeg, grated, parsley and sweet herbs, a little shalot and onion, a few bread crumbs, grated fine
4. Add yolk of egg sufficient to work it light
5. Roll it into balls
6. If for roast meat, put them in the dripping-pan to brown; for white sauce, boil them; for brown sauce, fry them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Plain Pound Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- A pound of butter
- A pound of sugar
- Nine eggs

## Directions

1. Beat separately
2. Bake in the same way as the twelfth cake, with less time
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Plain Sponge Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- One pound of flour
- One pound of sugar
- Eight eggs
- A few drops of essence of lemon

## Directions

1. Beat the yolks and the whites of the eggs separate sufficiently
2. Add the two together, and put in the sugar
3. When mixed, add the flour by degrees
4. Add a few drops of essence of lemon as an improvement
5. Butter a dish and bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Plum Buns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- butter
- yeast
- spice
- a quarter of a pound of currants

## Directions

1. Make the same way as plain buns
2. Add a small quantity of spice
3. Add a quarter of a pound of currants, washed and picked
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Plum Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef suet chopped
- six ounces
- Malaga raisins stoned
- six ounces
- currants washed and picked
- eight ounces
- bread crumbs
- three ounces
- flour
- three ounces
- eggs
- three ounces
- sixth of a nutmeg
- small blade of mace
- cinnamon
- half a tea-spoon of salt
- sugar
- four ounces
- candied lemon
- one ounce
- citron
- one ounce
- half a pint of milk

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs and spice together
2. Mix the milk with them by degrees, then the rest of the ingredients
3. Dip a clean linen cloth into boiling water and put it in a hair sieve
4. Flour it a little and pour in the mixture
5. Tie it up close
6. Put it into a saucepan containing six quarts of boiling water
7. Keep a kettle of boiling water alongside of it to fill up your pot as it wastes
8. Keep it boiling six hours, at least
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Plum Pudding Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a glass of sherry
- half a glass of brandy
- two tea-spoonfuls of white sugar
- a little grated lemon-peel
- a quarter of a pint of thick melted butter
- nutmeg

## Directions

1. Mix sherry, brandy, white sugar, and grated lemon-peel together
2. Add the mixture to a quarter of a pint of thick melted butter
3. Grate nutmeg on the surface
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Poached Eggs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eggs
- water
- salt
- vinegar
- toast or spinach

## Directions

1. Put in a stewpan a pint of water, with half a table-spoonful of salt, and three of vinegar.
2. When simmering break in the eggs, and let them remain until the yolk is set.
3. Serve on toast or spinach.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Poivrade Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a bit of butter as big as an egg
- two or three onions
- carrots
- turnips
- a clove of garlic
- two shalots
- two cloves
- a bay leaf
- thyme and basil
- some flour
- a glass of red wine
- a glass of water
- a spoonful of vinegar
- a little pepper and salt

## Directions

1. Put a bit of butter as big as an egg into a stewpan with two or three onions, carrots, and turnips cut in slices, a clove of garlic, two shalots, two cloves, a bay leaf, thyme and basil
2. Keep turning them in the pan until they get a colour
3. Shake in some flour
4. Add a glass of red wine, a glass of water, and a spoonful of vinegar, with a little pepper and salt
5. Boil half an hour
6. Skim and strain it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pone Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- meal
- water

## Directions

1. Make in the same way as the ash cake
2. Bake in a round kettle standing on legs, with a lid fitting down, so that you may put hot coals on the top as well as under
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Poor Clergyman's Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter of a pound of flour
- a quarter of a pound of suet
- a quarter of a pound of currants
- a quarter of a pound of raisins stoned
- a table-spoonful of moist sugar
- half a tea-spoonful of ginger
- a little salt

## Directions

1. Put the flour, suet, currants, raisins, sugar, ginger, and salt together
2. Put the mixture into a pudding-cloth
3. Boil for two hours, or a little more
4. Turn the pudding out of the cloth
5. Serve with sifted sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Poor Man's Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a handful of parsley leaves
- salt
- half a dozen young green onions
- three table-spoonfuls of oil
- five table-spoonfuls of vinegar
- black ground pepper

## Directions

1. Pick a handful of parsley leaves from the stalks, mince them very fine, strew over them a little salt
2. shred fine half a dozen young green onions, add them to the parsley
3. put them into a sauceboat, with three table-spoonfuls of oil and five of vinegar
4. add some black ground pepper and salt
5. stir together, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Portugal Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of flour
- one pound of white sugar
- one pound of fresh butter
- ten eggs
- half a pound of currants
- a little white wine

## Directions

1. Mix one pound of flour, one pound of white sugar, and one pound of fresh butter
2. Work it well up until it crumbles
3. Add ten eggs and half a pound of currants with a little white wine
4. Bake in tins only half filled
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Portuguese Roast Pork
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- loin of pork
- onions
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Cut the skin of the loin across at the distance of half an inch
2. Roast as usual
3. Cut two onions up small, and put them into the dripping-pan, with a pint of vinegar
4. Baste well with this, and serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pot au Feu, or Stock
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef shin
- beef leg bone
- beef neck
- carrots
- parsnips
- turnips
- leeks
- celery
- onion
- water

## Directions

1. Select the best pieces of beef: the shin, the leg bone, and the neck
2. Cut the meat in pieces and put in a digester with a sufficient quantity of water
3. Skim the meat sufficiently
4. Add carrots, parsnips, turnips, leeks, celery, and onion which have been browned in a pan
5. Simmer for four or five hours
6. Take out the meat and strain the stock for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pot-au-feu
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 pounds leg of beef
- 2 quarts water
- 1 fagot of herbs
- salt and pepper
- 2 onions
- 2 carrots
- 2 turnips
- 1 dozen peppercorns
- tape
- sippets of toast

## Directions

1. Tie the meat up into a nice shape with a piece of tape
2. Put it into cold water
3. Bring slowly to the boil, and very carefully remove the scum
4. Peel and slice up the vegetables, and put them in with the fagot of herbs and the peppercorns tied in a piece of muslin
5. Bring to simmering point, and keep it so for five hours
6. The liquor can then be served as a soup with part of the vegetables and some sippets of toast
7. Take the tapes off the meat, and serve with the rest of the vegetables round the dish as a border or garnish
8. The remains of the beef can be pressed between heavy weights till cold, or put into a brawn tin and served cold with a salad
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potage Maigre
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- vegetables
- a piece of butter
- the yolks of three eggs
- water

## Directions

1. Make a soup maigre by using vegetables boiled tender enough to be rubbed through a tamis
2. Add water to the soup before the eggs are put in
3. Add a piece of butter, and the yolks of three eggs well beaten
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Potato and Tomato Pie
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Tomatoes
- 6 Potatoes
- 1 lb. Short Pastry
- Dripping
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- 1½ teaspoonful Sweet Herbs
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Peal and slice up the potatoes and tomatoes; lay them alternately in a pie-dish and sprinkle over them some parsley, herbs, salt, and pepper.
2. When the dish is full, cover with a short pastry and bake for one hour; serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potato Balls
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Boiled Potatoes
- Bread Crumbs
- 2 Eggs
- 1 oz. Butter
- Hot Fat

## Directions

1. Rub the potatoes through a sieve or mash them smoothly.
2. Put the butter into a saucepan, and, when melted, season with pepper and salt; put in the potatoes and turn them about till hot through.
3. Drop in the egg and mix into a paste, turn on to a plate to cool, and roll into balls.
4. Beat up an egg and brush over the balls, cover well with crumbs, and fry in hot fat.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potato Beer
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes
- Eighty quarts of water per scheffel of potatoes
- Crushed malt, twelve scheffels
- Ninety gallons of cold water (English)
- Six barrels of cold water (English)
- Hops, due proportion

## Directions

1. Wash the potatoes well
2. Rub down the potatoes by a grating cylinder into a hopper
3. Crush and grind them into pulp
4. Put eighty quarts of water with each scheffel of potatoes into a copper and make to boil
5. Work twelve scheffels of crushed malt in the mash-tub with ninety gallons of cold water to a thick pap
6. Suddenly introduce six barrels of cold water with constant stirring and let stand an hour at rest
7. When the potatoes have been boiled to a fine starch paste, speedily ladle the whole malt mash, thin and thick, into the copper
8. Stir the mixture for an hour, keeping the temperature at from 144° to 156°F. all the time, so that the diastase of the malt may convert the starch into sugar and dextrine
9. When the white pasty liquid becomes transparent and thin, raise the fire to make the mash boil and keep it at its heat for ten minutes
10. Withdraw the fire and transfer the contents of the copper into the mash, work well, and let settle for half an hour
11. During this time, wash out the copper and quickly charge it once more with boiling water
12. Draw off the clear wort from the tun as usual and boil as soon as possible with the due proportion of hops
13. Add boiling water in any desired quantity to the drained mash for the second mashing
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Potato Chips
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes
- Salt
- Fat for frying

## Directions

1. Peel the potatoes and dry them in a cloth.
2. Cut into any shape — slices, strips, quarters, &c. — and dry again.
3. Have a good quantity of very hot fat ready, put the chips into a frying basket, and plunge into the fat.
4. Fry quickly, and directly they are brown enough they are done.
5. Throw them on to some kitchen paper to drain off the fat.
6. Pile high on a dish, sprinkle with salt, and serve very hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potato Fritters
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cold Potatoes
- 1 Egg
- 2 oz. Sugar
- Nutmeg or Lemon Peel
- Hot Fat

## Directions

1. Mash up the potatoes very smoothly, beat in the egg and a flavouring of nutmeg or grated lemon peel.
2. Beat up the egg and pour over the potatoes and mix into a paste.
3. Form into small round cakes.
4. Fry in very hot fat till brown.
5. Pile high on a dish, sprinkle with sugar, and serve. One Egg is sufficient for about 1 lb. potatoes.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potato Pie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- chops or slices of mutton
- gravy or thickening
- potatoes
- salt

## Directions

1. Put a few chops or slices of mutton at the bottom of a pie-dish
2. Add a little gravy or thickening on the meat
3. Fill up the dish with potatoes sliced green
4. Add salt
5. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Potato Polls
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of potatoes
- an ounce of butter
- milk
- half a pint of yeast
- a quarter of a pint of lukewarm water
- two pounds of flour

## Directions

1. Boil two pounds of potatoes, mash them, and work them with an ounce of butter and as much milk as will pass them through a colander
2. Mix with the potatoes half a pint of yeast, and a quarter of a pint of lukewarm water
3. Pour the whole on two pounds of flour, knead it well
4. Let it stand before the fire to rise, and make it into rolls
5. Toast and butter them after baking
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Potato Puff
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. Cold Potatoes
- 2 Eggs
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 gill Milk

## Directions

1. Mash the potatoes, beat the butter to a cream, then beat in the eggs, pepper, salt, and milk.
2. Stir up the potatoes, pour into a buttered pie-dish, and bake for about half an hour.
3. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potato Rolls
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of potatoes
- an ounce of butter
- milk
- half a pint of yeast
- a quarter of a pint of lukewarm water
- two pounds of flour

## Directions

1. Boil two pounds of potatoes
2. Mash them
3. Work them with an ounce of butter and as much milk as will pass them through a colander
4. Mix with the potatoes half a pint of yeast, and a quarter of a pint of lukewarm water
5. Pour the whole on two pounds of flour
6. Knead it well
7. Let it stand before the fire to rise
8. Make it into rolls
9. Toast and butter them after baking
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Potato Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- cold boiled potatoes
- salt
- pepper
- chopped parsley
- oil
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Slice up some cold boiled potatoes
2. Sprinkle with salt, pepper, and chopped parsley
3. Mix the oil and vinegar together in the proportion of two of oil to one of vinegar
4. Pour this over, let it stand for an hour, and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potato Sausages
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 Cold Potatoes
- 1 lb. Cold Meat
- Nutmeg, Pepper, and Salt
- 1 Egg
- Bread Crumbs
- Hot Fat

## Directions

1. Mash up the potatoes, and mince the meat.
2. Mix together, season nicely, and mix into a paste with half the egg.
3. Roll into sausages, egg and bread crumb, and fry in hot fat.
4. Dish in a pyramid, and garnish with fried parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potato Scones
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- potatoes
- milk
- egg
- flour

## Directions

1. Mash the potatoes with a little milk, keeping them as dry as you can
2. Add an egg well beaten
3. Add a sprinkling of flour
4. Fry them of a light brown
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Potatoes a la Maitre d'Hotel
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cold Potatoes
- 1/2 pint Maitre d'Hotel Sauce
- Lemon juice
- Pepper
- Salt

## Directions

1. Make the sauce by recipe given elsewhere, flavour nicely with lemon juice, pepper, and salt.
2. Slice up the potatoes, and put them into it; simmer for five minutes, dish, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potatoes in White Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Potatoes
- 1 pint White Sauce
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Peel and slice the potatoes, put them in water seasoned with salt, and boil for five minutes; strain off the water.
2. Make some white sauce by directions given elsewhere.
3. Lay in the potatoes and simmer gently till they are soft, but not broken.
4. Place them on a hot dish and pour the sauce over.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potatoes Saute
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cold potatoes
- Dripping
- 1 oz. Butter
- Lemon juice
- Parsley
- Pepper
- Salt

## Directions

1. Cut up any cold potatoes that may be left into strips, not too thin, put some dripping into a frying pan, and when very hot put in the potatoes and fry them a pale colour.
2. Place them on a hot dish; melt one ounce of butter in a saucepan.
3. Season with lemon juice, parsley, pepper, and salt.
4. Pour this over the potatoes, and serve very hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Potatoes Stuffed
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Large Potatoes
- 1/2 lb. Cold Meat
- 1/2 gill Gravy or Sauce
- Pepper, Salt, and Parsley

## Directions

1. Wash and scrub the potatoes, and bake them in the oven till quite done.
2. Cut them in half so that they will stand nicely.
3. Scoop out the inside, and mix the potato meal with some butter, pepper, and salt.
4. Make a little savoury meat by directions given for mince, and nearly fill the potato skins with this.
5. Put some of the potato on top, making it look as rough and rocky as possible.
6. Stand in the oven till quite hot, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Poulard a la Montmorency
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fowl
- lard
- liver
- bacon
- hard eggs
- pale stock
- flour
- butter
- onions
- carrots
- fine herbs

## Directions

1. Lard the fowl
2. Stuff it with the liver cut in pieces, some bacon, and hard eggs
3. Stew it in pale stock with flour browned in butter, some onions, carrots, and a few fine herbs
4. Reduce the sauce to a glaze
5. Serve with the fowl
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Poulard à la Montmorency
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fowl
- liver
- bacon
- hard eggs
- pale stock
- flour
- butter
- onions
- carrots
- fine herbs

## Directions

1. Lard the fowl, and stuff it with the liver, cut in pieces, some bacon, and hard eggs
2. Stew it in pale stock, with flour browned in butter, some onions, carrots, and a few fine herbs
3. Reduce the sauce to a glaze, and serve with the fowl
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Poulet à Moi
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fowl
- small onion
- chopped parsley
- two sticks of celery
- pound of sausages
- pepper
- salt
- cayenne
- water
- butter
- jelly

## Directions

1. Put a fowl, breast downwards, in an earthen pan with a cover
2. Shred over it a small onion, a table-spoonful of chopped parsley, and two sticks of celery
3. Fill in the hollow with a pound of sausages
4. Season with pepper, salt, and cayenne to taste
5. Cover it close
6. Put in the oven just after breakfast and forget it till dinner
7. If it should be forgotten till night, take off the cover before putting it away in the larder, and in the morning turn it out in a mould of jelly
8. If the celery is bought, i.e., long gathered, add a little water
9. If you have no sausages, add lumps of butter and more water
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Prawn Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Prawns
- 6 Tomatoes
- Mayonnaise or Salad Dressing

## Directions

1. Pick the prawns, leaving the skin on a few fine ones for a garnish.
2. Peel and slice up the tomatoes and arrange them on a dish; put over them the prawns, and pour over all some mayonnaise or salad dressing.
3. Place the other prawns round as a garnish with a few lettuce leaves broken up.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Prenesas
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of milk
- flour
- ten eggs
- clarified butter
- clarified sugar
- lemon essence

## Directions

1. Stir flour into one pint of milk as much as will bring it to the consistency of hasty pudding
2. Boil it till it becomes thick
3. Let it cool, and beat it up with ten eggs
4. When smooth, take a spoonful at a time, and drop it into a frying-pan in which there is a good quantity of boiling clarified butter
5. Fry of a light brown, and serve with clarified sugar flavoured with lemon essence
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Preserved Ginger
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- large pieces of common ginger
- white sugar (sugar-candy is best)

## Directions

1. Simmer ginger in water until tender
2. Put in cold water
3. Take it out and drain
4. To equal quantities in weight of ginger add white sugar
5. Let the sugar be clarified, and put to the ginger
6. Simmer constantly, until the sugar has penetrated right through the ginger
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pressed Beef
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 10 lbs. Thick Brisket of Beef, Corned or Fresh
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 1 stick Celery
- 1 Onion
- 2 Carrots
- 1 Turnip
- 40 Peppercorns

## Directions

1. Bind the beef with tapes to keep it a good shape
2. If it is corned, put it on in cold water; if fresh, in hot stock or water, and bring to the boil, then skim carefully and put in the vegetables and peppercorns
3. Simmer very gently indeed for four hours, then take it up
4. Take off the tapes, slip out the bones, and put it into a dish
5. Place a piece of board on the top and some heavy weights and leave till the next day, then turn out and serve with a salad
6. If fresh meat is used for this dish the liquor may be used for soup, or the bones may be put back when removed from the meat and boiled without the lid very quickly for an hour
7. Then strain off and stand away till the next day; it should then be in a strong jelly
8. This may be cut into blocks and put round the meat
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Prince of Wales Cakes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/4 lb. Flour
- 1/4 lb. Cornflour
- 1 gill Milk
- 1/4 teaspoonful Baking Powder
- 2 oz. Butter
- 1 Egg
- Flavouring
- 2 oz. Sugar

## Directions

1. Mix the flour, cornflour, and baking powder together
2. Beat the butter and sugar to a cream
3. Beat in the egg, flavouring, and milk
4. Beat in the flour, &c., and continue to beat for five minutes
5. Butter some small bun tins, half fill them with the mixture
6. Put into a moderate oven and bake for about twenty minutes
7. Stand on a sieve till cold
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Prussian Outlets
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal
- fat
- two spoonfuls of shalot
- one spoonful of salt
- half a spoonful of pepper
- nutmeg
- egg
- bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Chop up some veal, with a little fat
2. Add two spoonfuls of shalot, one of salt, half a one of pepper, a little nutmeg
3. Chop the whole, to give the shape of a cutlet
4. Egg well with bread crumbs
5. Fry it of a light brown, and serve with any sauce you please
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Puff Paste
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of butter
- one pound of flour
- half a salt-spoon of salt
- a little lemon-juice
- about a quarter of a pint of the coolest water
- the yolk of an egg

## Directions

1. Put the flour on the paste-board and make a hole in the centre
2. Put the yolk of an egg, lemon-juice, and salt in the hole
3. Mix the whole with cold water into a flexible paste with the right hand, handling it as little as possible
4. Squeeze all the buttermilk from the butter and wring it through a cloth
5. Roll out the paste and place the butter on this
6. Fold the edges of the paste over to hide the butter
7. Roll it out again to the thickness of a quarter of an inch
8. Fold over one-third, over which again pass the rolling-pin
9. Fold over the other third, thus forming a square
10. Place it with the ends, top, and bottom before you
11. Repeat the rolls and turns twice more
12. Flour a baking-sheet and put the paste on this
13. Let it remain in a cool place for half an hour
14. Roll twice more, turning it as before
15. Place it again in a cool place for a quarter of an hour
16. Give two more rolls, making seven in all
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Puffs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two table-spoonfuls of flour
- a quarter of a pint of cream
- two eggs
- some grated nutmeg
- four bitter almonds pounded
- two tea-spoonfuls of ratafia
- an ounce of butter

## Directions

1. Mix two table-spoonfuls of flour with a quarter of a pint of cream, two eggs well beaten, some grated nutmeg, four bitter almonds pounded, two tea-spoonfuls of ratafia, and an ounce of butter beaten to a cream
2. Bake these ingredients in small buttered cups for half an hour
3. Turn them out in a dish, and serve immediately with sweet sauce poured over them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pumpkin Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- part of a pumpkin
- some water
- about half a quarter of a pound of butter
- a little salt
- a quart of milk
- bread

## Directions

1. Take part of a pumpkin, pare off the skin and remove the seeds
2. Cut it into small pieces, and place them in a stewpan on the fire, with some water
3. When the pumpkin is pulped, put in about half a quarter of a pound of butter, and a little salt
4. Boil a quart of milk, and mix it with the pumpkin pulp
5. Put some bread, cut in thin slices, in the tureen, and pour the soup hot over it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Pumpkin Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 small Pumpkin
- 2 oz. Butter
- 1 pint of Milk
- 2 Onions
- 1 Carrot
- 2 quarts of Water
- pepper
- salt
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up the pumpkin, onions, and carrot
2. Put them into a saucepan with half the butter, and sweat the vegetables in it for five minutes
3. Pour over the boiling water and boil until the vegetables are very soft
4. Rub through a sieve and return to the saucepan with the milk and some pepper and salt
5. Stir until it boils up
6. Just before serving, stir in, in tiny pieces, the rest of the butter and a little lemon juice
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Puree of Green Peas
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two quarts shelled green peas
- parsley
- chives
- salt
- butter or gravy

## Directions

1. Put peas into boiling water
2. Let them boil with a little parsley, chives, and salt
3. Strain them and rub them through a sieve
4. Add a little butter or gravy
5. Warm them up again and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Quarter of Lamb Roasted, with Bread Crumbs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lamb
- bacon
- bread crumbs
- salt
- parsley
- sharp sauce

## Directions

1. Lard the lamb on the thin side with small pieces of bacon
2. Sprinkle the other part with bread crumbs
3. When almost done take it from the fire
4. Sprinkle it a second time with crumbs of bread, seasoned with salt and parsley
5. Put the joint down to brown
6. When done serve with sharp sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Queen Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- One pound of sugar
- One pound of butter
- Eight eggs
- One pound and a quarter of flour
- Two ounces of currants
- Half a nutmeg

## Directions

1. Mix the butter with sugar and spice
2. Add half the eggs, and beat for a few minutes
3. Add the rest of the eggs, and work for five minutes longer
4. Stir in the flour and currants
5. Bake in shapes
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Quick Beef Tea No. 2
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pound gravy beef
- 1 pint water
- Salt to taste

## Directions

1. Pass the meat twice through a sausage machine
2. Put it into a saucepan and pour over the cold water
3. Stand on the stove and stir constantly until it comes to boiling point but do not allow it to boil
4. As soon as it changes colour from red to brown strain through a colander
5. Add salt to taste
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Quick Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Egg
- 1 tablespoonful Flour
- 1 tablespoonful Jam
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar
- 1 teaspoonful Baking Powder

## Directions

1. Mix the flour and baking powder together, beat the egg till very light, whisk in the sugar, and stir in the flour.
2. Pour into a buttered tin, and bake five minutes.
3. Turn on to a sugared paper spread with jam.
4. Roll up and serve. Custard sauce is nice with this.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Quin Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two wine-glassfuls of port
- two wine-glassfuls of walnut pickle
- four wine-glassfuls of ketchup
- half a dozen pounded anchovies
- a few shalots sliced and pounded
- a table-spoonful of soy
- half a drachm of cayenne

## Directions

1. Let these ingredients simmer for ten minutes
2. Strain it into bottles for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Raised Pies
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound and a half of the best lard
- three quarters of a pint of new milk
- four pounds of flour
- pork or veal
- white pepper
- salt
- dried sage
- egg

## Directions

1. Boil the lard and milk together
2. Stir the boiling mixture into four pounds of flour
3. Knead the paste well
4. Set it down before the fire for twenty minutes before using
5. Make your paste warm, roll it out, and mould it in a mould
6. When cold take it off
7. Fill it up with pork or veal, which must be cut up small, seasoned with white pepper, salt, and dried sage
8. Put egg on the top if you like
9. Lay fancy leaves on the top to make it ornamental
10. Bake well
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Raisin Wine with Frontignac Flavour
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- twelve pounds of Malaga raisins
- twelve gallons of water
- twenty-four pounds of loaf sugar
- half a pint of yeast
- half a peck of elder-flowers

## Directions

1. Boil twelve pounds of Malaga raisins in twelve gallons of water till they are quite absorbed
2. Take them out, extract the stones, and return the pulp to the water
3. Pour on twenty-four pounds of loaf sugar, and ferment it with half a pint of yeast
4. When the fermentation begins to subside, tie up in a muslin bag half a peck of elder-flowers, and suspend the bag for some days in the liquor
5. Remove it, and when clear draw off and bottle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Raspberry and Currant Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three pints of fruit
- one quart of water
- a pound of the best sugar per quart
- brandy

## Directions

1. Bruise three pints of fruit, and add one quart of water
2. Let it stand, and after twenty-four hours strain the liquor, and put to every quart a pound of the best sugar
3. It will work of its own accord
4. Put a bottle of brandy to every five gallons
5. Keep it for a time and bottle it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Raspberry Jam
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- raspberries
- a pound of sugar for each pound of fruit

## Directions

1. Weigh your fruit, and put it in the preserving pan to simmer or boil
2. When the fruit has set, add a pound of sugar for each pound of fruit
3. Let it boil ten or twelve minutes
4. Skim occasionally
5. When it sticks to the spoon it is fit for the jar or pot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Raspberry Vinegar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of fruit
- one quart of the best white wine vinegar
- one pound of fresh raspberries

## Directions

1. Put one pound of fruit into a bowl
2. Pour upon it one quart of the best white wine vinegar
3. Next day strain the juice on one pound of fresh raspberries
4. The following day do the same
5. Bottle and cork well
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Raspberry Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- raspberries
- water
- loaf sugar
- brandy

## Directions

1. To every quart of raspberries put the same quantity of water
2. Bruise, and let them stand two days
3. Strain off the liquor, and to every gallon put three pounds of loaf sugar
4. Put the liquor in a cask
5. When ready to bottle, add a table-spoonful of good brandy to each bottle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ratafia
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of peach, apricot, or other kernels
- half a pound of white sugar-candy
- a cup of cold water
- brandy

## Directions

1. Blanch two ounces of peach, apricot, or other kernels, and put them in a bottle
2. Dissolve half a pound of white sugar-candy in a cup of cold water
3. Add to the brandy after it has stood a month on the kernels
4. Strain, and bottle for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Raw Beef Tea
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 pound gravy beef
- 1 gill of water

## Directions

1. Scrape the meat to a pulp with a sharp knife
2. Pour over it the water
3. Cover over and stand away for an hour
4. Strain off
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Recipe to Prepare Fat to Fry Fish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ten pounds of beef suet
- five pounds of fresh butter
- three pounds of hog's lard
- two turnips
- four carrots
- an onion stuck with cloves
- a bunch of parsley
- half a dozen shalots
- a small bit of thyme
- two bay-leaves
- a little sweet basil
- baysalt
- whole pepper

## Directions

1. Carefully pick ten pounds of beef suet
2. When ready put it into an iron pan to melt
3. As soon as melted add five pounds of fresh butter and three pounds of hog's lard
4. The moment the whole is in a boiling state, throw in two turnips, four carrots, an onion stuck with cloves, a bunch of parsley, half a dozen shalots, a small bit of thyme, two bay-leaves, and a little sweet basil
5. Let the herbs and roots fry until they are dried up
6. Strain the melted fat through a sieve
7. When nearly cold, stir in some baysalt and some whole pepper
8. Put in jars for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Red Currant Jelly
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- red currants
- table-spoonful of water
- a pound of sugar to every pint of juice

## Directions

1. Put the fruit on the fire or hot plate, with a table-spoonful of water at the bottom of the pan, and boil for twenty to twenty-five minutes
2. Pass the juice through a flannel jelly bag
3. To every pint of juice add a pound of sugar of the best kind
4. Return it to the pan, and boil quickly for a little better than ten minutes, or until the preserve sticks to the spoon
5. Run it into pots or jelly glasses
6. Cover with paper, steeped in spirits of any kind, and use bladder for top covering
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Regent's Punch
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three bottles of champagne
- one bottle of hock
- one bottle of curaçoa
- a quart of brandy
- a pint of rum
- two bottles of Madeira
- two bottles of seltzer-water
- four pounds of bloom raisins, stoned
- Seville oranges
- lemons
- white sugar-candy
- green tea

## Directions

1. Mix three bottles of champagne, one bottle of hock, one bottle of curaçoa, a quart of brandy, a pint of rum, two bottles of Madeira, and two bottles of seltzer-water together
2. Add four pounds of bloom raisins (stoned), Seville oranges, lemons, and white sugar-candy
3. Use green tea instead of water
4. Ice in summer and warm in winter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rhenish Cup No. 2
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of hock
- about half a pint of highly-flavoured strawberries
- powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Take to each bottle of hock about half a pint of highly-flavoured strawberries
2. Add powdered sugar
3. Take the fruit with the wine after having been in it about an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rhenish Cup No. 3
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of hock
- some thin slices of pineapple
- powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Take some thin slices of pineapple instead of the strawberries
2. Add powdered sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rhenish Cup No. 4
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bottle of hock
- two highly-flavoured peaches
- powdered sugar

## Directions

1. Take to each bottle of hock two highly-flavoured peaches peeled and cut in slices
2. Add powdered sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rhubarb Mould
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bundle Rhubarb
- 6 oz. Sugar
- 1/4 lb. Sago
- 1 pint Water

## Directions

1. Wipe and cut up the rhubarb and put it on to boil with one gill water
2. Boil for about ten minutes
3. Wash the sago and soak it in one gill warm water
4. Add to the rhubarb
5. Stir in also the sugar and boil for about ten minutes or longer, stirring constantly
6. Pour into a basin or mould which has been dipped in cold water
7. Stand away till cold and firm
8. Turn out and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Rice Blancmange
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 1½ oz. Ground Rice
- 1 oz. Sugar
- Flavouring

## Directions

1. Put the milk on to boil with a strip of lemon peel in it ; when nearly boiling mix the rice in a spoonful of cold milk and pour it in.
2. Stir till it boils, and let it boil two or three minutes ; pour into a mould which has been dipped in cold water, and stand away till firm.
3. Turn out when cold, and serve with jam, stewed fruit, or custard sauce.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Rice Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of ground rice
- half a pound of white sugar
- four eggs

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs separately
2. Beat the whole ingredients together for some time
3. Bake in a mould
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rice Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one half pound of rice
- one quarter of a pound of butter
- one quart of milk
- a little salt
- six eggs

## Directions

1. Put half a pound of rice to soak over night
2. Early in the morning boil it very soft
3. Drain it from the water
4. Mix with it a quarter of a pound of butter
5. Set it away to cool
6. When cold stir in one quart of milk
7. Add to it a little salt
8. Beat up six eggs and mix
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rice Cutlets
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Cold Meat
- 2 oz. Rice
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 Egg
- Bread Crumbs
- Hot Fat
- Nutmeg or Lemon Peel

## Directions

1. Mince the meat finely; if there is any cold rice in the larder it will do; if not, boil some.
2. Mix the rice and meat well together, season and flavour with a little nutmeg or lemon peel; if the meat is very lean add 2 oz. fat or beef suet.
3. Shape into cutlets, egg and bread crumb them, and fry in hot fat.
4. Dish in a circle and garnish with fried parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Rice Oakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of rice
- a quarter of a pound of butter
- a quart of milk
- a little salt
- six eggs
- half a pint of flour

## Directions

1. Put half a pound of rice to soak over night
2. Early in the morning boil it very soft
3. Drain it from the water
4. Mix with it a quarter of a pound of butter
5. Set it away to cool
6. When cold stir in a quart of milk, and add to it a little salt
7. Beat up six eggs and mix with half a pint of flour
8. Stir the eggs and flour into the rice and milk
9. Bake it on the girdle in cakes the size of a dessert-plate
10. Butter them, and send to table hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rice Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a little rice
- a pint of milk
- onion
- white pepper
- salt
- cream

## Directions

1. Boil a little rice in a pint of milk
2. add onion and white pepper and salt
3. Rub it through a tamis sieve, and, if necessary, thicken it with cream
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- 10 pounds of sirloin
- fat
- flour
- salt
- horseradish

## Directions

1. Put the joint at first at a distance from the fire, which must be brisk and clear
2. As the meat warms, bring it nearer
3. During the time it is roasting baste it well
4. Keep the fat paper-covered
5. When nearly done dredge it with flour
6. A few minutes before taking it up, sprinkle some salt on it
7. Put horseradish round the dish
8. Serve with its own gravy, quite devoid of fat
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Goose à la Francois
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- hundred chestnuts
- half a pound of sausage-meat
- liver
- butter
- parsley
- chives
- shalot
- clove of garlic
- glass of wine
- stock
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Take a hundred chestnuts, and put them on the fire in a poele with holes
2. After having taken off the first skin, put them again in poele till you remove the second skin
3. Chop half the chestnuts, and put them into a stewpan with half a pound of sausage-meat, the liver chopped, a little butter, some parsley, chives, shalot, and a clove of garlic, finely chopped
4. Put this on the fire for a quarter of an hour
5. Stuff the goose with it, and roast
6. Put the rest of the chestnuts into a stewpan, with a glass of wine, a little stock, pepper, and salt
7. When done serve round the goose
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Hare
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- hare
- milk or cream
- butter
- hare liver
- flour
- red currant jelly

## Directions

1. Put the hare down to roast, away from the fire at first, but afterwards draw nearer
2. Keep eternally basting with milk or cream, and subsequently with butter
3. Mince the liver
4. Flour the hare well when nearly done, so as to raise a froth on the joint
5. Serve hot with red currant jelly
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Kangaroo
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- hind quarters of a medium-sized brush kangaroo
- bacon
- good veal stuffing
- forcemeat balls
- good gravy
- currant jelly
- good dripping or milk

## Directions

1. Lard the hind quarters of a medium-sized brush kangaroo with bacon
2. Stuff with good veal stuffing
3. Put down to roast
4. Make some forcemeat balls and put in the dripping-pan to warm
5. Well baste the kangaroo during roasting with either good dripping or milk (the latter is best)
6. Serve with some good gravy, properly made, and currant jelly
7. This dish must be well but not over-done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Kid
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- kid quarter
- gravy
- slices of bacon
- veal stuffing balls

## Directions

1. Put a quarter down to roast
2. Let it be well browned
3. Serve with gravy, slices of bacon, and some veal stuffing balls
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Lamb
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lamb fore-quarter
- gravy
- mint sauce
- lemon
- salad

## Directions

1. Roast the fore-quarter before a clear fire
2. Bast from the time it is warmed until it is thoroughly done
3. Never place so near the fire as to scorch the fat
4. Serve with a little gravy made in the pan
5. Have mint sauce in a tureen, with a lemon cut ready for use
6. A salad is a favourite addition
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Leg of Mutton with Garlic
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- leg of mutton
- garlic
- salt

## Directions

1. Throw the garlic into five boiling waters, with a little salt
2. Boil five minutes in each water
3. Drain the garlic
4. Put it into the dripping-pan under the roasting mutton
5. Roast until done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Ox Tongue
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ox tongue
- cloves
- port wine
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Peel the outside skin off before putting the tongue down
2. Trim the root
3. Put before the fire in a cradle spit
4. Baste it well when being roasted
5. When done, stick cloves in the tongue, in the part uppermost in the dish
6. Make some gravy with port wine, rather thick
7. Add the juice of a lemon
8. Serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Pork
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- leg of pork
- sage
- onion
- bread crumbs
- pepper
- salt
- salad oil
- apple sauce

## Directions

1. Pork must invariably be scored
2. The leg must be stuffed with sage and onion, mixed fine, and bread crumbs, with pepper and salt
3. Rub a little salad oil on the skin to crisp the crackling
4. Do not put the meat too near the fire at first
5. Roast for full three hours
6. Serve with apple sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Sucking Pig
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sucking pig
- salad oil
- onion
- sage
- bread crumbs
- egg
- pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Rub the skin all over with salad oil
2. Stuff the pig with onion, sage, and bread crumbs, mixed together with egg, some pepper and salt
3. Baste the pig well, as everything depends on this attention
4. The pettitoes, liver, and brains are generally made into gravy, and served up in a sauce tureen with the pig
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roast Veal
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal
- stuffing
- force-meat
- melted butter
- bacon or ham
- lemon

## Directions

1. Roast the veal and continually bast it
2. The fillet will require stuffing and force-meat
3. Paper the kidney and fat, serve hot on toast for the veal gourmet
4. Roast the shoulder or breast
5. Parboil the sweetbread before roasting
6. Put good melted butter over it
7. Ensure it is well browned
8. Accompany with a little bacon or ham, either as a garnish or separate dish
9. A little lemon is an improvement
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roasted Haunch of Venison
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- haunch of venison
- salt
- water
- clean dripping or butter
- flour
- white paper
- plain gravy
- currant jelly

## Directions

1. Set the haunch before a slow fire, taking care to paper it to prevent its scorching.
2. Baste it well with salt and water at first, but afterwards use clean dripping or butter.
3. Dredge it with flour.
4. When it is done it will be frothed and browned on both sides.
5. Wrap a white paper ruffle round the knuckle-bone.
6. Send it to table with plain gravy and currant jelly.
7. If the venison has hung long, the outer skin had better be removed.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rock Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of butter
- half a pound of sugar
- one pound of flour
- three eggs
- half a pound of currants
- white of egg

## Directions

1. Beat the butter to a cream
2. beat up the eggs thoroughly, and mix in with the butter and sugar
3. stir in the flour very gradually
4. lastly stir in the currants
5. Keep a little of the white of egg out to froth the top with
6. Drop the buttons on buttered paper, and bake quickly
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rock Cakes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. Flour
- 2 oz. Dripping
- 1/4 lb. Sugar
- 2 oz. Currants
- 1 Egg
- 1 oz. Peel
- 1 teaspoonful Baking Powder

## Directions

1. Rub the dripping and flour together
2. Stir in the sugar, currants (well cleaned), the baking powder, and the peel
3. Beat up the egg and pour it in
4. Make into a very stiff dough
5. Take up in rough pieces and lay on a greased tin
6. Bake in rather a quick oven for fifteen minutes
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Roly Poly; or, Dog in a Blanket
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- light suet crust
- jam

## Directions

1. Make a light suet crust and roll it out thin
2. Spread the jam equally over it, leaving a small margin of paste without any where the pudding joins
3. Roll it up and fasten the ends securely
4. Tie it in a floured cloth and boil for two hours
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roman Ragout
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1.5 lbs. Gravy Beef
- 2 oz. Fat Bacon
- 2 oz. Onion
- 1 pint Milk
- 3 Tomatoes
- 1/2 pint Gravy
- 1.5 oz. Semolina
- 1 oz. Dry Cheese

## Directions

1. Mince the onion and bacon very fine indeed, put them into a saucepan and fry a good brown, then add half the gravy, and stir until a sort of half glaze.
2. Rub the tomatoes through a sieve and stir them in with the rest of the gravy, bring to the boil.
3. Cut the meat into strips and put it in with a little salt and pepper, and simmer very gently for about three hours.
4. While it is cooking put the milk on to boil, mix the semolina with a little cold milk, and stir it in.
5. Cook it until the spoon will come out quite clean, then turn it on to a dish till cold.
6. Cut it into squares and lay some in a deep dish, sprinkle with grated cheese, then more.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Rothsay Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Flour
- 1/2 lb. Bread Crumbs
- 1/2 lb. Suet
- 1 oz. Sugar
- 1 tablespoonful Vinegar
- 1 gill Milk
- 1 tablespoonful Raspberry Jam
- 1 Egg
- 1/2 teaspoonful Carbonate of Soda

## Directions

1. Mix the flour, crumbs, finely chopped suet, and sugar in a basin
2. Stir in the jam
3. Beat up the egg and milk and stir it in
4. Mix up the carbonate of soda and the vinegar together
5. Beat it in
6. When well mixed pour it into a buttered basin
7. Tie up carefully and boil for two hours
8. Turn out on to a hot dish and serve either with sifted sugar or custard sauce
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Roulades of Beef
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1/2 lb. Fillet of Beef
- 1/2 lb. Cold Boiled Bacon
- 1 Egg
- 1 1/2 oz. Dripping
- 1/2 pint Gravy
- Pepper and Mustard
- 2 oz. Crumbs

## Directions

1. Trim away the fat from the fillet and cut it into very small thin slices.
2. Cut the bacon also into thin slices, but smaller.
3. Spread the side of the beef with mustard and pepper.
4. Cover with the bacon, and roll up as lightly as possible.
5. When all are rolled beat up an egg, mix it with a spoonful of water.
6. Brush over the rolls.
7. Cover them with crumbs and thread on a small skewer.
8. Put the dripping into a frying-pan, and when quite hot lay in the rolls and fry until a good colour.
9. Place on a hot dish and slip out the skewers.
10. Make the gravy hot, season and flavour, and pour boiling round the roulades.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Round of Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- round of beef
- salt
- fat

## Directions

1. Well and properly salt the beef
2. Skewer it tightly, and as round and evenly as possible
3. Put more fat in the centre, if the meat is too lean
4. Use a roomy pot
5. Heat gradually and allow it to simmer until done
6. Take off the scum
7. When dished, see that the silver skewers hold the meat well together
8. Ladle a small quantity of the liquor over the meat
9. Garnish with sliced carrots
10. If to be eaten cold, let it remain in the water until it cools, and the meat will be more mellow
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rout Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- one pound of butter
- one pound of sugar
- one pound of currants
- three eggs beaten
- half a pint of milk
- two glasses of white wine
- one glass of brandy

## Directions

1. Mix two pounds of flour, one pound of butter, one pound of sugar, and one pound of currants
2. Wet them with three eggs beaten, half a pint of milk, two glasses of white wine, and one glass of brandy
3. Work them together
4. Bake in a tin plate
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Roux
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a piece of butter
- flour

## Directions

1. Melt a piece of butter in a saucepan
2. Add as much flour as will give the sauce proper consistency
3. When it becomes of a brown colour, add the other ingredients which your sauce is made from
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rum Shrub
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- juice of oranges, three pints
- a pound of white sugar
- a gallon of rum

## Directions

1. Put the juice of oranges, three pints, and a pound of white sugar to a gallon of rum
2. Let it remain in a cask for a few weeks, and it will be fit for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rump Steak Pie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of rump-steaks
- shalots
- half an ounce of pepper and salt mixed
- mushroom ketchup
- thickening
- hard-boiled eggs
- oysters

## Directions

1. Cut two pounds of rump-steaks into pieces
2. Chop very fine a few shalots, and mix them with half an ounce of pepper and salt mixed
3. Strew some of the mixture on each layer of steaks, and so on until the dish is full
4. Add a mushroom ketchup and thickening, with a few hard-boiled eggs
5. A few oysters is an improvement
6. The paste is elsewhere given
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Rusk Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 slice of Dry Bread
- 2 Eggs
- 1 oz. Sugar
- Half a Lemon
- 1 1/2 pints of Milk
- 1 tablespoonful Jam
- 1/2 tablespoonful Cornflour

## Directions

1. Take a piece of very stale bread and cut it into small squares
2. Bake it in the oven till a good colour
3. Break the eggs into a pie-dish
4. Beat in the sugar and grated rind of the lemon
5. Pour in one pint of milk, and mix well
6. Drop in the rusks and put into a cool oven and bake till firm
7. Spread on the top a layer of jam
8. Put half a pint of milk into a saucepan, and when it nearly boils, stir in the cornflour which has been mixed with a little lemon peel and sugar
9. Pour it on top of the pudding
10. Put it back in the oven for a few minutes
11. Stand away till cold
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Russian Charlotte
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ladies' finger cakes
- whipped cream

## Directions

1. Place in a mould, in a circle, some ladies' finger cakes, and let them lie closely together
2. line the sides of the mould with them by placing them upright
3. Pour into the middle a whipped cream
4. turn it upside down in the dish
5. If made in warm weather, it must be iced
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sack Posset
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sugar, four ounces
- sack, a pint
- nutmeg
- eggs, ten
- milk, a quart

## Directions

1. Heat sugar and sack over flaming coals until the sack dissolves the sugar
2. Over such another fire put ten eggs
3. Stir them with steady hand
4. Boil milk and when cold, put milk and sack to eggs
5. Unite them firmly
6. Put them together on the fire until Miss sing twice, 'You must not kiss and tell'
7. Each lad and lass take up a silver spoon and fall on fiercely
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sage and Onion Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- an ounce of onion
- half an ounce of green sage leaves
- water
- a tea-spoonful of pepper and salt
- an ounce of bread crumbs
- a pint of gravy or broth

## Directions

1. Chop fine an ounce of onion and half an ounce of green sage leaves
2. put them into a stewpan, with a little water
3. simmer for ten minutes
4. then put in a tea-spoonful of pepper and salt and an ounce of bread crumbs
5. mix together
6. then pour in a pint of gravy or broth, and simmer a little longer
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sago Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 oz. Sago
- 1 pint Milk
- 2 quarts Bone Stock
- 1 Leek
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Wash the sago in cold water
2. Boil the leek in the stock for ten minutes
3. Take it out and stir in the sago
4. Continue stirring until the sago is transparent and the stock quite thick
5. Pour in the milk and bring up to the boil
6. Season with salt and pepper, and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Salad of Cold Vegetables
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- cold vegetables such as potatoes, cauliflowers, peas, beans, haricots
- oil
- vinegar
- salt
- pepper
- hard boiled eggs

## Directions

1. Take any cold vegetables that there may be in the larder
2. Slice up the potatoes, branch the cauliflower, and mix in the peas and beans
3. Put all into a salad bowl
4. Take oil and vinegar in the proportion of one of oil to two of vinegar, blend them together and season with salt and pepper
5. Pour this over the vegetables
6. Slice up one or two hard boiled eggs into very thin slices, and lay round as a garnish
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Salad of Corned Beef
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Slices of Corned Beef
- 1 Lettuce
- 2 Eggs
- Mayonnaise or Salad Dressing

## Directions

1. Take some slices of cold corned beef, dip them in a salad dressing, and lay them in a dish with alternate layers of lettuce leaves.
2. Garnish with hard boiled eggs cut in slices.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Sally Lumns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of fine flour
- two table-spoons of yeast
- a little warm water
- two ounces of butter
- the yolk of an egg
- new milk

## Directions

1. Mix two pounds of fine flour and two table-spoons of yeast with a little warm water
2. Put to rise in half an hour
3. Put two ounces of butter and the yolk of an egg in as much new milk as will make it a proper stiffness
4. Mix all well up
5. When risen, bake in a quick oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Salmis of Game
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- under-done partridges
- butter
- ham
- shalots
- dried carrot
- mushrooms
- parsley
- thyme
- bay-leaf
- cloves
- peppercorns
- allspice
- sherry
- brown gravy
- mushroom ketchup
- pepper
- cayenne
- sugar
- fried bread sippets

## Directions

1. Take off the legs, wings, and breasts of as many under-done partridges as you intend to use
2. Remove the skin from them very carefully
3. Put into a stewpan, with a bit of butter, a few slices of ham, four or five shalots, a dried carrot, a few mushrooms, a little parsley, sprig of thyme, a bay-leaf, two cloves, three or four peppercorns, and some allspice
4. Fry the vegetables slightly
5. Add two wine-glassfuls of sherry, four or five spoonfuls of brown gravy, and a spoonful of mushroom ketchup
6. Add the parts of the bird not intended to be eaten, and let them simmer for a couple of hours
7. Season with pepper and cayenne
8. Skim off the fat and add a lump of sugar
9. When the sugar is dissolved, strain this gravy over the wings, legs, and the breast of the partridge
10. Put the partridge parts into a clean saucepan
11. Let it simmer by the fire until the meat is saturated with the sauce
12. Serve with fried bread sippets
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Salmis of Game
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- under-done partridges
- butter
- ham
- shalots
- dried carrot
- mushrooms
- parsley
- thyme
- bay-leaf
- cloves
- peppercorns
- allspice

## Directions

1. Take off the legs, wings, and breasts of as many under-done partridges as you intend to use
2. Remove the skin from them very carefully
3. Put into a stewpan with a bit of butter, a few slices of ham, four or five shalots, a dried carrot, a few mushrooms, a little parsley, sprig of thyme, a bay-leaf, two cloves, three or four peppercorns, and some allspice
4. Fry the vegetables
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Salmon Cutlets
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- salmon cutlets
- salad oil
- cayenne pepper
- salt
- paper
- fat

## Directions

1. Cut them in slices
2. Rub them with salad oil, and season with cayenne pepper and salt
3. Wrap them in paper, and fry in boiling fat
4. When nearly done, lay them in their paper shrouds on a gridiron, to dry; or place them in a hot oven for a similar purpose
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Salmon Cutlets
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- salmon cutlets
- salad oil
- cayenne pepper
- salt

## Directions

1. Cut them in slices
2. Rub them with salad oil, and season with cayenne pepper and salt
3. Wrap them in paper, and fry in boiling fat
4. When nearly done, lay them in their paper shrouds, on a gridiron
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Salmon Pie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of fine fresh salmon
- five or six anchovies
- small pottle of mushrooms
- handful of fresh parsley
- couple of shalots
- little green thyme
- three ounces of butter
- pepper
- salt
- nutmeg
- tarragon
- juice of a lemon
- half a pint of good brown gravy
- six eggs
- short paste

## Directions

1. Cut two pounds of fine fresh salmon in slices, about three-quarters of an inch thick, and set them aside on a dish
2. Clean and scrape five or six anchovies, and halve them
3. Chop a small pottle of mushrooms, a handful of fresh parsley, a couple of shalots, and a little green thyme
4. Put these together into a saucepan, with three ounces of butter, a little pepper, salt and nutmeg, and tarragon
5. Add the juice of a lemon, and half a pint of good brown gravy
6. Let the whole simmer, stirring it gently all the time
7. Slice six eggs boiled hard
8. Line a pie-dish with short paste
9. Fill it with alternate layers of the slices of salmon, hard eggs, and fillets of anchovies, spreading between each layer the herb sauce
10. Cover the dish with the paste, and bake in a moderately-heated oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Salt Fish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- salted fish
- cold water
- egg sauce
- boiled beet
- yolks of hard-boiled eggs

## Directions

1. Salted fish generally requires soaking, according to the time they are salted.
2. Put it into plenty of cold water, and let it simmer till done.
3. Serve with egg sauce and boiled beet as an accompaniment, with yolks of hard-boiled eggs.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Salt Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- salt fish
- cold water
- parsnips
- egg sauce
- parsley
- lemon

## Directions

1. To cook salt fish it should be soaked in cold water for twelve hours, then well washed in fresh water, scraped and cleaned.
2. Lay it in a fish-kettle, cover with cold water, then simmer very gently indeed from one hour to one hour and a half, according to the thickness of the fish.
3. It should be dished on a serviette, and garnished with sprigs of parsley and slices of lemon.
4. Send it to table with boiled parsnips and egg sauce.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Salt Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- salt fish
- cold water
- fresh water
- parsley
- lemon
- boiled parsnips
- egg sauce

## Directions

1. Soak salt fish in cold water for twelve hours
2. Well wash in fresh water, scrape and clean
3. Lay it in a fish-kettle and cover with cold water
4. Simmer very gently from one hour to one hour and a half, according to the thickness of the fish
5. Dish on a serviette and garnish with sprigs of parsley and slices of lemon
6. Send it to table with boiled parsnips and egg sauce
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Salt Mutton (French Fashion)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a leg of mutton
- oil
- vinegar
- salt
- pepper
- garlic
- onion
- thyme
- a bay-leaf
- parsley

## Directions

1. Bone the leg of mutton and put it into a pan
2. Pour over it a marinade made of oil, vinegar, salt, pepper, garlic, onion, thyme, a bay-leaf, and parsley
3. Let it marinade three days
4. Roast it or stew it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sardine Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 tin Sardines
- 2 Eggs
- 1 Lettuce
- Salad Dressing

## Directions

1. Split the sardines open and remove the bone.
2. Break some of the lettuce into a bowl, lay on this the sardines.
3. Chop up one of the eggs and sprinkle over them, pour on the dressing.
4. Cover with the rest of the lettuce, and garnish with the other egg cut in slices, and a little watercress or beetroot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Sauce a la Maitre d'Hotel
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a piece of butter
- a little flour
- parsley and chives
- half a glass of water
- a little salt and pepper
- the juice of a lemon

## Directions

1. Put a piece of butter, a little flour, parsley and chives chopped small into a saucepan
2. Add half a glass of water, a little salt and pepper
3. Just before serving, set on the fire
4. Keep turning it till all the ingredients are well incorporated
5. Add the juice of a lemon and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sauce a la Rimoulade
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a shalot
- chervil
- chives
- a head of garlic
- salt and pepper
- mustard
- oil
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Put into a sauceboat a shalot, chervil, chives, a head of garlic, all chopped very fine
2. Add salt and pepper
3. Beat it up with mustard, oil, and vinegar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sauce a la Tartare
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the yolk of an egg
- oil
- vinegar
- chopped parsley
- mustard
- pepper
- salt
- a spoonful of pate de diabley or French mustard

## Directions

1. Mix the yolk of an egg with oil, vinegar, chopped parsley, mustard, pepper, and salt
2. A spoonful of pate de diabley or French mustard renders the sauce more piquante
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sauce au Roi
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two quarts of vinegar
- a pint of soy
- a pint of walnut ketchup
- six cloves
- six shalots
- a quarter of an ounce of cayenne pepper

## Directions

1. Mix two quarts of vinegar, a pint of soy, and a pint of walnut ketchup, with six cloves, six shalots, and a quarter of an ounce of cayenne pepper
2. let it stand for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sauce Blonde
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of butter
- flour
- pale stock
- salt and pepper

## Directions

1. Roll about two ounces of butter in flour
2. Moisten it with pale stock
3. Season it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sauce for Fried Meat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fat
- a couple of table-spoonfuls
- half a pint of flour and water
- a table-spoonful of ketchup

## Directions

1. When meat of any kind has been fried in fat in a pan — whether chops, steaks, or cutlets — when they are done remove them
2. pour out of the pan all but a couple of table-spoonfuls
3. Mix up, say half a pint of flour and water, pour it into the pan, and let it simmer until done
4. add a table-spoonful of ketchup
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sauce for Roast Wild Ducks
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one salt-spoonful of salt
- one salt-spoonful of cayenne
- one dessert-spoonful of lemon-juice
- one dessert-spoonful of ketchup
- two dessert-spoonfuls of Harvey
- three dessert-spoonfuls of port wine
- three dessert-spoonfuls of claret

## Directions

1. Mix the ingredients
2. Warm them
3. Pour over before the dish comes on the table
4. Keep away all fat from the bird when done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sauce Of Turnips
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- turnips
- butter
- stock
- sugar
- salt

## Directions

1. Peel the turnips and cut them into pieces like the quarter of an orange
2. Put them into a small stewpan with the butter, sprinkle over them the sugar and salt, and stir about
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Sauce Robert
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- a couple of onions
- a table-spoonful of ketchup
- pepper
- salt
- a tea-spoonful of mustard
- the juice of half a lemon or a table-spoonful of vinegar
- half a pint of stock

## Directions

1. Put a quantity of flour into a dry pan, and brown it
2. When browned cut a couple of onions up and brown them
3. then add a table-spoonful of ketchup, a little pepper and salt, a tea-spoonful of mustard, the juice of half a lemon, or a table-spoonful of vinegar, and half a pint of stock
4. let it simmer for ten minutes
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sausages
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pork
- veal
- fat
- bread-crumbs
- thyme
- parsley
- sage
- leeks
- garlic or shalot
- pepper
- salt
- nutmeg
- egg

## Directions

1. To each pound of pork allow a pound of veal and one pound of fat
2. Mix them well
3. Allow a pound of bread-crumbs, thyme, parsley, an ounce of sage, two heads of leeks, a little garlic or a shalot, chopped fine, with pepper, salt, and nutmeg
4. To each pound allow one egg, the yolks and whites separate
5. Beat both well, mix in the yolks, and as much of the whites as necessary to moisten the bread
6. Add all the ingredients together
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Saute of Turnips
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Turnips
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 gill Stock
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar
- 1 teaspoonful Salt

## Directions

1. Peel the turnips and cut them into pieces like the quarter of an orange; put them into a small stewpan with the butter, sprinkle over them the sugar and salt, and stir about till quite brown.
2. Pour on the stock, bring it to the boil, and simmer till soft but not broken.
3. Dish the turnips, season the gravy with salt and a few drops of lemon juice, pour over, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Saveloys
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pork
- saltpetre
- common salt
- pepper
- sage
- grated bread
- eggs

## Directions

1. Pork, free from bone and skin, three pounds
2. Salt it with an ounce of saltpetre, and half a pound of common salt for about forty-eight hours
3. Mince it fine
4. Add three spoonfuls of pepper, a dozen leaves of sage, and a pound of grated bread
5. Mix well
6. Fill the skins, and boil or bake them slowly
7. Or the ingredients may be made flat and round, without being put into skins, and cooked in that manner, after being mixed with beaten eggs, in order to bind the meat together
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Savoury Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sweet herbs
- lemon peel
- bay leaf
- shalot

## Directions

1. Make in the same way as beef gravy
2. Add a larger proportion of sweet herbs and lemon peel, a bay leaf and shalot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Savoury Potatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 5 or 6 Large Potatoes
- 2 oz. Cheese
- 1 spoonful of Milk
- 1 Egg
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Scrub the potatoes and bake them in the oven.
2. Cut off the end, scoop out all the meal; grate up some dry pieces of cheese, beat it into the potatoes with the yolk of the egg, and some seasoning.
3. Whip the white till stiff and stir lightly in; fill the potatoes with this mixture.
4. Lay in a baking sheet and bake for about twenty minutes.
5. Garnish with parsley, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Savoury Sausages
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lean beef
- pork
- common salt
- saltpetre
- sugar
- black pepper
- allspice
- cayenne
- garlic
- shalot

## Directions

1. Salt a piece of lean beef and pork with common salt, a little saltpetre, sugar, black pepper, and allspice, for two days
2. Season with pepper, cayenne, garlic, and a shalot
3. Mince fine and put into clean gut
4. Smoke them
5. When wanted for use they may be boiled and eaten cold
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Savoy Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six eggs
- three ounces of white sugar
- lemon-peel
- four ounces of flour

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs first
2. Mix the other articles
3. Drop on paper
4. When they have been a short time in the oven, sprinkle a little sugar over them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scalloped Fish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fish
- beaten yolk of an egg
- pepper
- salt
- bread crumbs
- chopped parsley
- grated lemon-peel
- juice of a lemon
- warmed butter

## Directions

1. Break the fish into pieces
2. Pour over the beaten yolk of an egg
3. Sprinkle with pepper and salt
4. Strew with bread crumbs, chopped parsley, and grated lemon-peel
5. Squeeze in the juice of a lemon
6. Drop over a little warmed butter
7. Brown before the fire
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scalloped Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 0.5 lb. Cold Fish
- 2 oz. Bread Crumbs
- 1 gill Cold Fish Sauce
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 oz. Butter

## Directions

1. Flake up the fish, butter a small dish, and sprinkle well with bread crumbs.
2. Put in a layer of fish, a little sauce and seasoning, and some bread crumbs.
3. Continue this in layers until all the fish is used up.
4. Put plenty of crumbs on top and the rest of the butter in small pieces.
5. Bake in a moderate oven for 20 minutes.
6. Garnish with a sprig of parsley, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Scalloped Oysters
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bottle Oysters
- 3 oz. Bread Crumbs
- 2 oz. Butter
- Lemon Juice
- Pepper
- Salt

## Directions

1. Strain the liquor from the oysters, boil it up and pour over them, cover down for five minutes, and strain off again.
2. Melt the butter, season with lemon juice, pepper, and salt.
3. Butter a dish, put in a layer of crumbs, then one of oysters; moisten with the butter, then more crumbs, and continue in layers till the dish is full.
4. Pour over all the rest of the butter, and bake for a quarter of an hour.
5. Serve at once.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Scandinavian Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 bream
- 2 eggs
- 1 pint milk
- 1 lb. flour
- pepper
- salt
- 1 pint fish sauce
- butter
- melted butter sauce
- anchovy

## Directions

1. Fillet the fish, skin and chop very fine
2. Sift the flour into a basin
3. Drop in the eggs and make into a batter with the milk
4. Season with salt and pepper
5. Stir in the chopped fish
6. Butter a basin and pour in the mixture
7. Boil for one hour
8. Turn out of the basin and serve with melted butter sauce, flavoured with anchovy, or with any other fish-sauce that may be preferred
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Scones
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds flour
- quarter of an ounce bi-carbonate of soda
- quarter of an ounce salt
- one pint sour buttermilk

## Directions

1. Mix to the consistence of light dough
2. Roll out about half an inch thick, or less
3. Cut them in any shape you please
4. Bake them on a girdle, turning, to brown on each side
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scones No. 1
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 1 pint milk
- 1 oz. butter
- 2 teaspoonsful baking powder

## Directions

1. Rub the butter into the flour, stir in the baking powder, and make into a very light dough with the milk; turn on to a floured board, knead for a few minutes, roll out about half an inch thick.
2. Cut into shapes, put on to a floured tin, and bake in a quick oven for about ten minutes.
3. Serve either hot or cold.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Scones No. 2
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 1 pint sour milk
- 3 teaspoonsful baking powder
- 1 teaspoonful salt

## Directions

1. Mix the flour, baking powder, and salt together, mix into a very light dough with the milk, adding a little more milk if necessary; turn on to a floured board and knead till smooth, roll out half an inch thick.
2. Cut into small rounds and bake for about five minutes.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Scones No. 3
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 2 oz. dripping
- 1 oz. sugar
- 1 pint sour milk
- 1 teaspoonful cream of tartar
- 1 teaspoonful carbonate of soda

## Directions

1. Rub the dripping into the flour, stir in the sugar, cream of tartar, and soda.
2. Mix into a very light dough with the milk, turn on to a floured board; divide into two parts.
3. Flatten these out into two cakes, divide each one into four pieces, brush over with milk.
