4. Put on to a floured tin and bake in a hot oven from fifteen to twenty minutes.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Scotch Barley Broth
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a neck of mutton
- half a gallon of water
- three table-spoonfuls of Scotch barley
- some turnips
- onions

## Directions

1. Put a neck of mutton on the fire in a saucepan, with half a gallon of water, and three table-spoonfuls of Scotch barley, and let it simmer.
2. After it has been on some time, add some turnips, and onions cut into dice.
3. Skim well, and allow it to simmer about a couple of hours.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scotch Broth
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 quarts of the Liquor in which Mutton has been cooked
- Salt
- 1 oz. Rice
- 1 Carrot
- 1 Turnip
- Stalk of Celery

## Directions

1. Carefully remove all the fat from the liquor; put it into a saucepan.
2. Wash the rice and cut all the vegetables into dice; stir them in, and simmer by the side of the fire for an hour.
3. It must be cooked very slowly and without the lid.
4. Add salt to taste, and pour it into a tureen. Pearl barley may be used instead of rice.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Scotch Buns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of flour
- one pound of raisins, stoned and cut
- one pound of currants
- three ounces of orange-peel
- three ounces of citron
- three ounces of almonds blanched and cut
- mixed spice
- three-quarters of a pound of butter
- warm water
- a quarter of a pint of yeast
- half a gill of yeast

## Directions

1. Take two pounds of flour, one pound of raisins stoned and cut, and one pound of currants
2. Add three ounces of orange-peel, the same of citron, and of almonds blanched and cut
3. Mix together and add some mixed spice
4. Make a hole in the flour and break in three-quarters of a pound of butter
5. Pour warm water on the butter to soften it
6. Work the flour and butter together
7. Add a quarter of a pint of yeast and work it smooth
8. Cut a third part of the paste for sheets
9. Spread out the rest of the paste on the table and put the fruit in
10. Pour about half a gill of yeast over the fruit and paste and work together
11. Make it round and roll out the sheet in a circular form
12. Lay the bun in the middle and gather the sheet round it
13. Roll it out the desired thickness
14. Run a fork through in different parts down to the bottom and pinch it on the top
15. Flour double paper and put the bun on it and give a cut round the side
16. Bake moderately
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scotch Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound and a half of flour
- a pound and a half of butter
- a pound and a half of white sugar
- six ounces of blanched sweet almonds
- three-quarters of a pound of candied orange-peel
- half a pound of citron, cut small
- half a nutmeg grated
- a tea-spoonful of caraway-seeds
- twelve eggs
- a glass of brandy
- carraway comfits

## Directions

1. Beat the butter to a cream, add the sugar and eggs gradually
2. Mix the flour by degrees, and then the sweetmeats, almonds, and spice
3. Stir in a glass of brandy
4. Butter the hoop or tin, and pour in the cake so as to nearly fill it
5. Smooth the top, and strew over it carraway comfits
6. Bake in a moderate oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scotch Collops
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef
- flour
- stock
- onion
- ketchup
- pepper
- salt
- lemon acid

## Directions

1. Cut beef in pieces of about half an inch square
2. Flour them and fry until half done
3. Remove the collops and put them in a stewpan with a small quantity of stock
4. Add an onion cut up small, ketchup, pepper, and salt, with a dessert-spoonful of lemon acid
5. Let it simmer until done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scotch Collops
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Lean Steak
- 1 gill Stock
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 oz. Butter
- Quarter of an Onion
- Small Sippets of Toast

## Directions

1. Remove all the fat, and cut the meat into very thin and small dice.
2. Mince up the onion very finely.
3. Mix together, season with some pepper and salt, and put into a saucepan with the butter.
4. Stir it about for five minutes, then pour on the stock, bring to the boil, and simmer for one hour.
5. Arrange neatly on a hot dish, and put the sippets of toast round.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Scotch Fancy Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of butter
- a pound and a half of flour
- four eggs
- three quarters of a pound of sugar
- half a gill of cream

## Directions

1. Rub half a pound of butter into a pound and a half of flour
2. Break four eggs into an earthen pan with three quarters of a pound of sugar
3. Whisk them for five minutes
4. Make a print with the butter and flour
5. Pour in the eggs and sugar and add half a gill of cream
6. Mix all into a dough
7. Cut into pieces two inches across and an eighth of an inch in thickness
8. Cover tins with wafer paper
9. Lay cakes on it
10. Bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scotch Kail
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a cupful of pearl barley
- about three quarts of cold water
- about two pounds of neck of mutton
- two carrots
- two turnips
- a few small onions
- pepper
- salt
- a cabbage or a similar quantity of greens

## Directions

1. Put a cupful of pearl barley with about three quarts of cold water in a pot and allow it to simmer
2. Put into the pot about two pounds of neck of mutton and let it continue simmering for an hour, skimming occasionally
3. Add two carrots, two turnips, and a few small onions cut in pieces, with some pepper and salt
4. Add a cabbage cut into pieces or a similar quantity of greens
5. Simmer another hour and add a little more boiling water if necessary
6. Serve the meat in a separate dish garnished with carrots and turnips, and serve the broth in a tureen
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Scotch Shortbread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of butter
- twelve ounces of fine sugar
- two pounds of flour
- four eggs
- slices of candied lemon-peel
- carraway comfits

## Directions

1. Rub one pound of butter and twelve ounces of fine sugar into two pounds of flour, with the hand
2. Make it into a stiff paste with four eggs
3. Roll it out to double the thickness of a penny-piece
4. Cut it into cakes, pinch the edges
5. Stick slices of candied lemon-peel and carraway comfits on the top
6. Bake on plates in a moderate oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sea Pie
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- thick crust
- sliced onions
- salt beef
- potatoes
- pork
- pepper
- cloth
- flour

## Directions

1. Make a thick crust, as for a pudding
2. Line a dish with it
3. Put in a layer of sliced onions
4. Add a layer of salt beef cut in slices
5. Add a layer of potatoes sliced
6. Add a layer of pork
7. Add another layer of onions
8. Strew pepper over all
9. Cover with the crust
10. Tie with a cloth, well floured, so that it will not stick when done
11. Boil for a couple of hours
12. Serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Seed Buns
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- butter
- yeast
- half an ounce of carraway-seeds

## Directions

1. Make as plain buns
2. Add half an ounce of carraway-seeds instead of spice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Seed Case
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 6 oz. dripping
- 6 oz. sugar
- 1.5 teaspoonsful caraway seeds
- 1 egg
- 2 teaspoonsful baking powder
- 1 gill milk or water

## Directions

1. Sift the flour into a basin and rub in the dripping carefully, stir in the sugar, baking powder, and caraway seeds.
2. Beat up the egg and milk or water, and mix the dry ingredients into a dough; beat for two or three minutes.
3. Turn into a cake tin which has been well rubbed with dripping, stand on a baking sheet and place in a moderate oven.
4. Bake for one hour and a half or longer, test it by running a skewer right through the centre; if it comes out clean the cake is done.
5. Turn it out of the tin carefully and stand on a sieve till cold.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Semolina Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. Semolina
- 1 pint of Milk
- 3 pints Bone Stock
- Salt and Pepper
- onion
- carrot
- fagot of herbs
- dozen peppercorns

## Directions

1. If the stock has been made without vegetables, boil an onion, carrot, fagot of herbs, and a dozen peppercorns in it for half an hour
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Shalot Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- few drops of shalot vinegar
- pint of gravy

## Directions

1. Add a few drops of shalot vinegar to a pint of gravy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sharp Sauce for Venison, Hare, or Kangaroo
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of the best white wine vinegar
- a quarter of a pound of white sugar

## Directions

1. Put into a saucepan half a pint of the best white wine vinegar, and a quarter of a pound of white sugar
2. let it simmer, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sheep's Head and Fry
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sheep's head
- liver
- heart
- tongue
- stock
- gravy
- pepper
- salt
- lemon
- ketchup
- egg
- bread crumbs
- brains
- batter
- bacon

## Directions

1. Take the head, and after being well cleaned, parboil it
2. Egg and crumb it well, and put it before the fire to brown, or in an oven
3. Mince the liver, the heart and tongue, and add to it a small quantity of stock, or good gravy
4. Add pepper and salt, with the squeeze of a lemon, a little ketchup, and let it simmer
5. Fry the brains in thick batter
6. Put the mince in the dish, the head in the centre, and round the dish the brain fritters
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sheep's Head Broth
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a sheep's head
- a cupful of pearl barley
- two carrots
- two turnips
- an onion
- some parsley
- salt
- pepper
- parsley and butter
- parsley leaves or boiled beet

## Directions

1. Clean the head properly and put it on to simmer with a cupful of pearl barley
2. Skim it carefully, and when it has simmered an hour, add two carrots, two turnips, an onion cut into slices, and some parsley
3. Season with salt and pepper
4. When the head is sufficiently tender, serve
5. The head can be put on a separate dish and parsley and butter poured over it and garnished with parsley leaves or boiled beet
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sheep's Tongues in Tomato Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Tongues
- 1 pint Tomato Sauce
- 1 doz. Peppercorns
- 1 Onion
- 1 fagot Herbs
- 1 Carrot
- Salt

## Directions

1. Wash the tongues in cold water, put them into a saucepan, cover them with cold water or stock, and bring to the boil, then skim well.
2. Either corned or fresh tongues will do for this dish. If corned, no salt is required; but if fresh ones are used, put in a dessertspoonful of salt.
3. Put in the vegetables and peppercorns and simmer gently for two hours.
4. Take them up, plunge them into cold water and remove the skin.
5. Trim them off and cut in half.
6. Make some tomato sauce by recipe given elsewhere. The liquor in which the tongues were boiled may be used for this if it is not too salt.
7. Lay the tongues in and simmer for another hour.
8. Dish carefully, boil up the sauce and pour over.
9. Garnish with chopped parsley.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Sheep's Trotters en Marinade
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sheep's feet
- vinegar
- salt
- egg
- bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Clean the sheep's feet well, and boil them in water
2. Steep them a day in vinegar and salt
3. Drain them, and fry them with egg and bread crumbs, until of a good colour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sherry Gobbler
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a lump of ice
- two glasses of cool sherry
- a spoonful of white sugar
- a few small pieces of lemon

## Directions

1. Take a lump of ice and fix it at the edge of a board
2. rasp it with the proper tool
3. collect the fine raspings in a capacious tumbler
4. pour thereon two glasses of cool sherry, and a spoonful of white sugar, with a few small pieces of lemon
5. Stir with a macerator, and drink through a tube of macaroni
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Shin of Beef Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- shin of beef, about six pounds
- two carrots
- two turnips
- a head of celery
- a little allspice
- cloves
- savory
- thyme
- parsley
- pepper
- salt
- cold water

## Directions

1. Take a shin of beef, of about six pounds; chop the bone in three pieces
2. Put it into a soup-kettle to simmer and cover it well with cold water
3. After about an hour, add two carrots, two turnips, a head of celery, a little allspice, cloves, savory, thyme, and parsley
4. Let it continue simmering for two or three hours
5. Add pepper and salt
6. Strain the soup and serve the vegetables and soup served up thick, and the boulli and gristle in a separate dish with a small quantity of parsley and butter over it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Short Pastry No. 1
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 6 or 8 oz. dripping
- 1 gill water

## Directions

1. Sift the flour into a basin, rub in the dripping very lightly until it is quite fine, mix into a very stiff dough with the water, turn on to a floured board, and knead into a smooth paste.
2. Roll out to the required thickness, and it is ready at once.
3. The dripping should be clarified, directions for which are given elsewhere.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Short Pastry No. 2
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 8 oz. butter
- 1 gill water
- Juice of half a lemon

## Directions

1. Sift the flour into a basin, rub the butter lightly in, until it is as fine as bread crumbs; make a well in the centre, and strain the lemon juice.
2. Mix into a stiff paste with the water, knead for a few minutes until it is quite smooth, it is then ready for use.
3. A crust may be made with lard just in the same way; this is much lighter of digestion than a butter crust, and should always be given to anyone suffering from a weak digestion.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Shoulder of Veal la Bourgeoise
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- shoulder of veal
- salt
- pepper
- grated nutmeg
- a piece of butter
- a glass of water
- a bay-leaf
- broth

## Directions

1. Bone the shoulder, and season it with salt, pepper, and grated nutmeg
2. Roll it, and give it an oblong shape, then tie it up
3. Put it into an oval stewpan, with a piece of butter, and stew it over a slow fire
4. Let it take a fine light colour, then add a glass of water, and sprinkle it with a little salt, adding a bay-leaf
5. Place fire above and below till a glaze is formed
6. Dress the shoulder on the dish, and drain the butter, detaching what remains with a spoonful of broth and pour the gravy on the veal
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Shrewsbury Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- One pound of white sugar
- One pound of fresh butter
- A pound and a half of flour
- Half a gill of milk
- One egg beaten

## Directions

1. Rub together one pound of white sugar, one pound of fresh butter, and a pound and a half of flour
2. Mix it into a paste, with half a gill of milk and one egg beaten
3. Let it lie half an hour
4. Roll it out thin, cut it into cakes
5. Bake them in a moderate oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Siesta Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of butter
- a little milk
- one pound of flour
- six eggs
- a quarter of a pound of sweet almonds
- two table-spoonfuls of yeast
- pounded almonds
- sugar
- citron
- cinnamon
- clarified sugar

## Directions

1. Take one pound of butter, warm it over the fire with a little milk.
2. Pour it into a pan with a pound of flour, six eggs, a quarter of a pound of sweet almonds, finely powdered, and two table-spoonfuls of yeast.
3. Beat these ingredients well together into a light paste.
4. Set it before the fire to rise.
5. Butter the inside of a pan, and fill it with alternate layers of the paste, and of pounded almonds, sugar, citron, and cinnamon.
6. Bake.
7. When baked and while hot, make holes through the siesta with a small silver skewer, taking care not to break it.
8. Pour over clarified sugar until it is perfectly soaked through.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Siftings Beer
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- bran
- sugar
- yeast

## Directions

1. Pour boiling water on bran
2. add sugar, and yeast to ferment
3. before it has quite done working, bottle it for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Simple Dish of Mutton
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mutton steaks
- quarter of a pound of maccaroni
- water
- pepper
- salt
- cayenne
- chutney
- table-spoonful of vinegar or juice of half a lemon

## Directions

1. Put a few steaks of mutton on to simmer for an hour, with a quarter of a pound of maccaroni, and a little water
2. Season with pepper, salt, cayenne, and chutney, and a table-spoonful of vinegar, or the juice of half a lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Slippery Bob
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- kangaroo brains
- flour
- water
- pepper
- salt
- emeu fat

## Directions

1. Take kangaroo brains, and mix with flour and water, and make into batter.
2. Well season with pepper, salt, &c.
3. Pour a table-spoonful at a time into an iron pot containing emeu fat.
4. Take them out when done.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Small Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- small fish
- butter
- onion or shallot
- parsley
- pepper
- salt
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Wash and dry them; well butter a sheet of stiff writing paper, lay the fish in, sprinkle them with a little very finely chopped onion or shallot, parsley, pepper, and salt.
2. Squeeze over a few drops of lemon juice, and put a few little pieces of butter about them; wrap them up in the paper and bake for twenty minutes.
3. Serve in the paper in which they were cooked.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Small Fish, Fried
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- small fish
- dripping or lard

## Directions

1. Clean them well, and let them dry.
2. Fry them plain or egg them.
3. Put plenty of dripping or lard into the pan, and as soon as the fat boils put them in and brown them.
4. They will hardly take a couple of minutes.
5. Drain them in a hair sieve, and serve.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Snow Cream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ounces of ground rice
- two ounces of sugar
- a few drops of essence of lemon
- two ounces of butter
- a quart of milk

## Directions

1. Put into a stewpan four ounces of ground rice, two ounces of sugar, a few drops of essence of lemon, with two ounces of butter
2. Add a quart of milk
3. Boil for about twenty minutes, until it is smooth, but not too thick
4. Pour it into a mould, or basin, which has been rinsed with butter, to let it set and cool
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Snow Trifle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the whites of eight eggs
- a pint and a half of milk
- a bit of lemon-peel
- sugar
- the yolks of eight eggs
- a table-spoonful of orange-flower water

## Directions

1. Whip the whites of eight eggs until they form a very thick froth
2. Put a pint and a half of milk on to boil, and when it boils place on its surface as many table-spoonfuls of the whipped whites of eggs as will stand on it without touching each other
3. As each spoonful becomes cooked and assumes the appearance of snow, take it off and put on another, until all the whipped eggs are done
4. As you take off the snow from the milk, put it on a hair sieve to drain
5. Add to the milk a bit of lemon-peel, and sugar enough to sweeten it well
6. As soon as it has acquired the flavour of the lemon-peel, stir into it the yolks of eight eggs beaten up with a table-spoonful of orange-flower water
7. When of proper consistency, but not so thick as cream, pour it into a cream-dish
8. After it is cold, put the snow on the surface to make the sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Soda Cake
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Flour
- 1/2 lb. Currants
- 1/4 lb. Sugar
- 6 oz. Dripping
- 1 1/2 teaspoonsful Carbonate of Soda
- 1/2 pint Milk

## Directions

1. Rub the dripping and the flour together
2. Clean and stir in the currants and sugar
3. Stir in the soda and mix into a dough with the milk
4. Beat for a few minutes
5. Pour into a tin which has been well rubbed with dripping
6. Bake in a moderate oven for two hours
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Soda Oakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of butter
- one pound of flour
- half a pound of sugar
- half a pound of currants
- three-quarters of an ounce of carraway-seeds
- a tea-spoonful of soda
- a little warm water
- new milk

## Directions

1. Rub six ounces of butter into one pound of flour
2. Mix in half a pound of sugar, half a pound of currants, and three-quarters of an ounce of carraway-seeds
3. Dissolve a tea-spoonful of soda in a little warm water, and mix with new milk to make a pint
4. Combine all ingredients
5. Bake an hour, putting it in the oven as soon as possible after mixing
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sopa d'Oro; or, Golden Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of sugar
- a quarter of a pint of water
- a quarter of a pint of orange-flower water
- a thin slice of toasted bread
- an ounce of sweet almonds pounded very finely
- the beaten yolks of four eggs
- powdered cinnamon

## Directions

1. Clarify the pound of sugar in a quarter of a pint of water and the same quantity of orange-flower water
2. Cut into pieces the size of dice a thin slice of toasted bread, or cut it into shapes with a paste-cutter
3. Throw it, while hot, into the sugar, with an ounce of sweet almonds pounded very finely
4. Take the beaten yolks of four eggs, pour over the sugar and bread
5. Stir gently, and let it simmer a few minutes
6. Serve in a deep glass dish, sprinkled over with pounded cinnamon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sopa d'Oro; or, Golden Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of sugar
- a quarter of a pint of water
- a quarter of a pint of orange-flower water
- a thin slice of toasted bread

## Directions

1. Clarify a pound of sugar in a quarter of a pint of water, and the same quantity of orange-flower water
2. Cut into pieces the size of dice, a thin slice of toasted bread
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Soubise Cutlets
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Neck Chops
- 1.5 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Flour
- 3 Onions
- 1 gill Milk
- Pepper, Salt, and Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. Trim off the cutlets, lay them in a tin dish, cover with buttered paper, and bake in the oven from fifteen to twenty minutes, according to thickness, turning once while cooking.
2. Peel the onions, put them into cold water, bring to the boil, throw away the water.
3. Put them on again in cold water and boil until rather soft, then strain all the water away, put in the butter, let it get quite hot, then cover down and finish cooking the onions in this, but do not brown them.
4. Stir in the flour and pour over the milk, stir until it boils, let it boil two or three minutes, then rub through a sieve.
5. Season with salt, pepper, and lemon juice.
6. Dish the cutlets in a circle, pour away some of the fat, and rinse the tin with a spoonful of gravy.
7. Pour this round the dish and put the soubise sauce in the centre.
8. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Souffle de Pomme de Terre
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of cream
- a quarter of a pound of sugar
- six spoonfuls of potato flour
- four yolks of eggs
- a piece of butter the size of an egg
- a little lemon-peel
- six yolks of eggs
- four eggs

## Directions

1. Dilute the potato flour with the four egg yolks and cream.
2. Add a piece of butter the size of an egg and a little lemon-peel.
3. Put this mixture on the fire and let it boil a few minutes, stirring it.
4. Allow it to cool.
5. Add six yolks of eggs and beat all together.
6. Whisk the whites of four eggs and mix them quickly and lightly with the rest.
7. Put all in a pan and bake.
8. Serve quickly.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Soup ala Mousquetaire
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of stock
- a pint of green peas
- a handful of sorrel
- a quart of water
- a few pounds of mutton

## Directions

1. Boil in a pint of stock a pint of green peas and a handful of sorrel
2. Add a quart of water, and boil a few pounds of mutton in it
3. When done, glaze as africandeau, and serve together
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Soup in an Hour
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of beef
- a carrot
- a turnip
- an onion
- some celery
- pepper
- salt
- water
- fine herbs or parsley and butter

## Directions

1. Chop two pounds of beef into pieces
2. Add a carrot, turnip, an onion, and some celery, all cut up
3. Put with the meat into a saucepan
4. Season it with pepper and salt and add a quantity of water
5. Let it boil an hour
6. Strain it through a tamis sieve
7. You can serve the meat with gravy, adding a few fine herbs, or parsley and butter
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Soup Julienne
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- carrots
- turnips
- celery heads
- onions
- leeks
- a little butter
- lettuces
- sorrel
- celery
- sufficient stock
- bread

## Directions

1. Cut up small, carrots, turnips, and celery heads, onions, and leeks, and put them into a stewpan, with a little butter
2. Then add to them lettuces, sorrel, and celery, cut in pieces
3. To these vegetables put sufficient stock, and simmer until the vegetables are well done
4. Serve with or without bread
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Soup Maigre
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter of a pound of butter
- half a dozen sliced onions
- a few heads of celery
- two handfuls of spinach
- two cabbage lettuces
- parsley
- two quarts of water
- crusts of bread
- a tea-spoonful of pepper
- a few blades of mace
- a handful of white beet leaves
- the yolks of two eggs
- a table-spoonful of vinegar

## Directions

1. Melt a quarter of a pound of butter in a stewpan
2. Shake it well, and throw in half a dozen sliced onions
3. Shake the pan for a few minutes, then put in a few heads of celery, two handfuls of spinach, two cabbage lettuces, cut small, and some parsley
4. Shake the pan well for five minutes
5. Put in two quarts of water, some crusts of bread, a tea-spoonful of pepper, and a few blades of mace, also a handful of white beet leaves, cut small
6. Let it simmer for an hour
7. Before serving, beat up and add the yolks of two eggs, and a table-spoonful of vinegar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Soup Maigre
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Rice
- 2 oz. Butter
- 1 gill Milk
- Salt
- 2 Eggs
- 1 Carrot
- 1 Onion

## Directions

1. Wash the rice well in two waters, put into a saucepan with 2½ pints of cold water and the onion and carrot whole.
2. As the rice begins to swell add some more boiling water, until it is about the right consistency.
3. Take out the onion and carrot and stir in the butter, a small piece at a time.
4. Beat the yolks of the eggs in a basin, stir them quickly in, and bring again to boiling point, but do not let it boil.
5. Season with salt, and serve at once, with tiny rusks of bread.
6. Make these by cutting up a dry crust into small pieces, dipping them in water, and baking until crisp in a moderate oven.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Sour-krout
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- seven or eight pounds of beef (brisket or fillet off the shoulder)
- water
- sour-krout

## Directions

1. Boil about seven or eight pounds of beef, either brisket or a fillet off the shoulder, in enough water to cover it
2. When it has boiled for an hour, add as much sour-krout as may be approved
3. It should then stew gently for four hours
4. Be served in a deep dish
5. Remove the fat for a more delicate dish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spanish Dream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of water
- an ounce of isinglass
- a quart of cream or good milk
- the beaten yolks of six eggs
- a glass of white wine
- loaf sugar
- lemon peel

## Directions

1. Boil in half a pint of water an ounce of isinglass till dissolved
2. Strain, and mix with it a quart of cream or good milk (if cream, do not use so much isinglass)
3. Stir it over the fire till it comes to a boil
4. When a little cooled, add gradually the beaten yolks of six eggs and a glass of white wine
5. Pour it into a deep dish
6. Sweeten with loaf sugar
7. Stir it till cold
8. Put it into a shape
9. It may be flavoured with the peel of a lemon instead of the wine
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spanish Flummery
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quart of cream
- cinnamon or mace
- half a pound of rice flour
- sugar

## Directions

1. Scald a quart of cream with a little cinnamon or mace
2. Mix this gradually into half a pound of rice flour
3. Stir it over a gentle fire until it is of the thickness of jelly
4. Sweeten it to taste
5. Pour it into cups or shapes
6. Turn it out when cold, and serve up
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spanish Gaspacho
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two onions
- tomatoes
- a handful of green pimento
- a cucumber
- a clove of garlic
- parsley
- chervil
- crumbed bread
- salt
- pepper
- oil
- vinegar
- water

## Directions

1. Take two onions, some tomatoes, a handful of green pimento, a cucumber, a clove of garlic, parsley, and chervil
2. Cut the whole into small pieces, and put them into a salad bowl
3. Add as much crumbed bread as will form double the quantity which the dish already contains
4. Season with salt, pepper, oil, and vinegar, like a salad
5. Complete the gaspacho with a pint of water to make the bouillon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spanish Puffs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of water
- a quarter of a pound of butter
- four table-spoonfuls of flour
- six yolks and four whites of eggs
- boiling clarified dripping or lard

## Directions

1. Put into a saucepan half a pint of water and a quarter of a pound of butter
2. Stir it until it boils, and mix with it four table-spoonfuls of flour
3. Stir it together, and add six yolks and four whites of eggs, two at a time
4. Let it cool, and, with a dessert-spoon, drop it into boiling clarified dripping or lard
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spanish Salad
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lettuce or other salad
- vinegar
- water
- tea-spoonful of pepper and salt
- oil
- fine herbs, especially tarragon and chervil

## Directions

1. Take the lettuce, or any other salad that is to be procured, and tear the leaves off from the stem
2. Put into a bowl equal quantities of vinegar and water, a tea-spoonful of pepper and salt, and four times as much oil as vinegar and water
3. Mix well together
4. Chop small on a plate any fine herbs that can be got, especially tarragon and chervil
5. Immediately before eating pour the sauce over the salad and herbs
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spanish Salad
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lettuce or any other salad
- vinegar
- water
- pepper and salt
- oil

## Directions

1. Take the lettuce, or any other salad that is to be procured, and tear the leaves off from the stem
2. Put into a bowl equal quantities of vinegar and water, a tea-spoonful of pepper and salt, and four times as much oil as vinegar and water
3. Mix well together
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spiced Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fine thick piece of brisket of beef
- pickle
- ground allspice
- black pepper
- cloves
- ginger
- nutmegs
- brown sugar
- dry sweet herbs
- water

## Directions

1. Let a fine thick piece of brisket of beef, not fat, lay three days in pickle
2. Take it out and rub in a mixture of spices consisting of equal quantities of ground allspice, black pepper, cloves, ginger, and nutmegs, and a little brown sugar
3. Repeat this daily for a week
4. Cover it with pounded dry sweet herbs
5. Roll or tie it tightly
6. Put it into a pan with a little water
7. Bake slowly for eight hours
8. Take it out and untie it
9. Put a heavy weight upon it
10. This is a fine relish when eaten cold
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spinach
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- spinach
- salt

## Directions

1. Well clean the vegetable
2. Boil quick
3. Put a little salt in the water
4. When well done, strain it well from the water
5. Serve with poached eggs and fried bread as a garnish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sponge Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- whites and yolks of eight eggs
- a pound of finely-pounded sugar
- a pound of fine flour
- rind of two lemons

## Directions

1. Beat the whites and yolks of eggs well
2. Add to finely-pounded sugar
3. Add a pound of fine flour and the rind of two lemons grated
4. Put it into shapes
5. Sift some white sugar over them
6. Bake in tin moulds for some time
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sponge Roll
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 tablespoonsful flour
- 3 tablespoonsful sugar
- 3 eggs
- 2 teaspoonsful baking powder
- 3 teaspoonsful jam

## Directions

1. Beat the eggs and sugar together for five minutes, mix the flour and baking powder together and stir them lightly in.
2. Pour into a well-buttered tin and bake in a quick oven for eight or ten minutes.
3. Turn on to a damp cloth and roll up directly; warm the jam in a saucepan while the roll is cooking, and if it is very stiff mix in a spoonful of water.
4. Take the roll out of the cloth and lay flat on a piece of sugared paper, spread the jam on quickly and roll up again; place on a sieve till cold.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Spring Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three dozen sticks of rhubarb
- a little lemon
- cinnamon
- moist sugar
- paste

## Directions

1. Peel and wash three dozen sticks of rhubarb, put into a stewpan with a little lemon, cinnamon, and moist sugar to sweeten
2. set it over the fire to marmalade
3. when done put paste round a dish, insert the rhubarb, and bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Spruce Beer
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four gallons of cold water
- four gallons of boiling water
- six pounds of molasses
- eight table-spoonfuls of the essence of spruce
- about half a pint of yeast

## Directions

1. Put four gallons of cold water into a nine-gallon keg
2. Add four more gallons of boiling water
3. Add six pounds of molasses and eight table-spoonfuls of the essence of spruce
4. When cool, put to it about half a pint of yeast
5. Let it work
6. Just before it has done working, bottle in stone bottles
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Staffin
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fruit prepared as in Lamplich recipe
- rich paste
- clarified sugar

## Directions

1. Line the dish with the paste.
2. Lay the fruit with alternate layers of paste till the dish is filled.
3. The paste must form the top layer.
4. Pour clarified sugar over before it is put in the oven.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stanley Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 2 oz. Flour
- 1 oz. Sugar
- 2 Eggs
- 1 spoonful Jam

## Directions

1. Put the milk into a saucepan, mix the flour with a little cold milk.
2. When the milk in the saucepan is nearly boiling, stir it in, and let it boil up.
3. Pour into a basin and beat in the yolks of the eggs and the sugar.
4. Turn into a pie-dish and bake till firm.
5. Spread a spoonful of jam on the top.
6. Whip the whites of the eggs to a stiff froth and spread them over, sprinkle with sugar, and put back in the oven to set.
7. Serve cold.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Steak and Kidney Pie
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Steak
- 2 Kidneys
- 1 lb. Flour
- 1/2 lb. Dripping
- 1 gill Water
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Mix a teaspoonful of flour in a plate with some pepper and salt
2. Slice up the meat into pieces about three inches long by two broad
3. Dip each piece lightly in the flour
4. Skin and slice up the kidneys, and cut the fat into small pieces
5. Roll a piece of kidney and a piece of fat alternately in the slices of meat
6. Pile high in a dish, and pour in a gill of water or stock
7. Make a short crust by directions given for short pastry
8. Wet the edge of the dish and line it with a strip of the paste
9. Wet this strip again with water and cover the dish with paste
10. Trim off the edge, cut a small piece out of the centre of the pie, and ornament it with a few leaves cut out of the paste trimmings
11. Brush over with water and bake in a moderate oven for one hour and a half
12. As soon as the crust has acquired some colour, cover with a piece of paper well rubbed with dripping
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Steak and Kidney Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Steak
- 2 Kidneys
- 1 lb. Flour
- 1/2 lb. Suet
- 1/2 pint Water
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Make a dry crust by directions given elsewhere, of the flour, suet, and butter
2. Rub the pudding basin well with dripping
3. Roll out the crust, take two-thirds and line the basin, well pressing the crust in
4. Slice up the meat and kidney, season with pepper and salt
5. Pile lightly in the basin, pour in half a gill of water
6. Wet the edge of the crust
7. Roll out the piece left, and cover the pudding securely
8. Dip a cloth in boiling water, put it over the top, tie it round with string, and pin or tie the ends of the cloth over the top
9. Plunge into plenty of boiling water, and boil for three hours
10. Take it up, take off the cloth, turn it out of the basin on to a hot dish, and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Steak and Macaroni
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Steak
- 2 oz. Macaroni
- 1 oz. Dripping
- 1/2 pint Stock
- 1 Onion
- 1 doz. Peppercorns
- Salt

## Directions

1. Cut the steak into neat pieces
2. Put the butter or dripping into a saucepan and fry the steak quickly
3. Take it out, shred the onion and put it in with the peppercorns, and let it get quite brown
4. Pour over the stock and stir until it boils
5. Put back the steak and let it simmer very gently for three hours
6. While it is cooking, boil the macaroni in weak stock or water for twenty-five minutes
7. If it is ready before it is wanted keep it in hot water
8. When the steak is done, dish it neatly, flavour the gravy, boil it up and pour over
9. Cut the macaroni into short pieces and place it round the dish as a garnish
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Steak Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one ounce of black pepper
- half an ounce of allspice
- one ounce of salt
- one ounce of grated horseradish and shalots
- one pint of mushroom ketchup

## Directions

1. Pound the black pepper, allspice, salt, and grated horseradish and shalots
2. Put these articles to a pint of mushroom ketchup
3. Let them infuse for a month
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Steak à la Jardinière
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Steak
- 1 gill Green Peas
- 1 gill French Beans
- 1 oz. Flour
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 Carrot
- 1 Turnip
- 1 pint Gravy
- Salt

## Directions

1. Cut the steak into neat pieces and fry very quickly in the butter.
2. Take it out, put in the flour, and when quite smooth pour on the gravy and stir until it boils.
3. Put back the steak, and simmer very gently for three hours.
4. Cut the carrot and turnip up into thin strips, and put them in when the steak has been cooking for two hours.
5. Boil the peas and beans separately, and add them to the stew five minutes before serving.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three or four pounds beef (rump or any part)
- sweet herbs chopped fine
- a little shalot
- spice whole
- a little bacon cut in slips
- flour
- a small bit of butter

## Directions

1. Take three or four pounds and trim it
2. Add sweet herbs chopped fine, a little shalot, and spice whole
3. Add a little bacon cut in slips
4. Lard the beef and rub it over with the herbs and spices, flour, and a small bit of butter
5. The beef can either be stewed or baked in an oven
6. Serve hot or cold, garnished with pickles
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stewed Beetroot and Mashed Potatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bunch Beetroot
- 2 Onions
- 1 oz. Flour
- Mashed Potatoes
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 1/2 oz. Butter
- 1/2 pint Milk
- 1 dessertspoonful Vinegar

## Directions

1. Peel and cut the onions into dice, put them into a frying-pan with the butter, and fry, but do not let them brown.
2. Sprinkle in the flour, pour in the milk, and stir until it boils.
3. Season with salt, pepper, and vinegar.
4. Boil the beetroot carefully, and when cold, peel and slice up.
5. Put it into the sauce and simmer for half an hour.
6. Make the mashed potatoes into a border on a hot dish, and put the beetroot in the centre; boil up the sauce, pour it over, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Cabbage
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- cabbage
- salt
- pepper
- butter
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Boil the cabbage as directed, and squeeze very dry
2. Melt the butter in a saucepan, season with pepper, salt, and a drop or two of lemon juice
3. Put in the cabbage and cook in the butter for ten minutes, stirring frequently
4. Arrange neatly in a hot dish, and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Celery
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- celery
- water
- slice of lemon
- mace
- butter
- flour
- egg or two
- nutmeg
- table-spoonful of cream
- half a cupful of gravy or stock

## Directions

1. Take off the outside and the green ends of the celery, boil them in water till tender
2. put in a slice of lemon, a little mace, thicken with butter and flour
3. Beat up an egg or two, grate in some nutmeg, mix with a table-spoonful of cream, put to it half a cupful of gravy or stock
4. let it simmer until sufficiently thick
5. Serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stewed Celery
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- celery
- water
- lemon
- mace
- butter
- flour
- egg
- nutmeg
- cream
- gravy or stock

## Directions

1. Take off the outside and the green ends of the celery
2. Boil them in water till tender
3. Put in a slice of lemon and a little mace
4. Thicken with butter and flour
5. Beat up an egg or two and grate in some nutmeg
6. Mix with a table-spoonful of cream
7. Put to it half a cupful of gravy or stock
8. Let it simmer until sufficiently thick
9. Serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stewed Celery
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 heads of Celery
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 gill Milk
- 1 oz. Flour
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Take only the white and best part of celery for this dish, pull it to pieces, wash well in salt and water, and tie in a bundle.
2. Put it into boiling water seasoned with salt, and boil for about half an hour, or until the fork will go through easily.
3. Take half a pint of the water in which it was boiled and mix it with the milk; make a sauce with this and the butter and flour by directions given for sauces.
4. Dish the celery and pour the sauce over.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Chops
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Chops
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1/2 oz. Flour
- 1 gill Gravy
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Trim some of the fat from the chops, put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is melted stir in the flour.
2. Mix well, and pour in the gravy; stir until it boils.
3. Lay in the chops, and simmer very gently for one hour.
4. Dish the chops in a circle, boil up and season the gravy, and pour over the stew.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Duck with Olives
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- duck
- lemon
- butter
- stock
- olives

## Directions

1. Give the duck a round form
2. Rub it with lemon and brown it in a pan with butter
3. When it has taken a good colour add some stock
4. When nearly done scald some olives and stone them without breaking
5. Add the stoned olives into the sauce in which the duck was cooked
6. Serve the duck with olives round it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stewed Fish
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 fish
- 1 pint stock
- 1 blade of mace
- 2 cloves
- 1 oz. flour
- 2 tablespoonsful ketchup
- 1 onion
- 1 egg
- bread crumbs
- 1 doz. peppercorns

## Directions

1. Fillet the fish and fry them in egg and bread crumbs
2. Slice and fry the onion
3. Lay the onion and the fish in a tin dish
4. Cover with stock
5. Put in the cloves, peppercorns, and mace
6. Cover over and put into a moderate oven for an hour
7. Mix the flour and ketchup together and stir it in
8. Put back into the oven for ten minutes
9. Dish the fish and strain the sauce over it
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Giblets
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one or more sets of giblets
- veal or chicken
- good gravy
- pepper
- salt
- browning
- mushroom powder
- lemon-juice
- one glass of white wine
- forcemeat balls
- hard-boiled eggs

## Directions

1. Scald one or more sets of giblets
2. Set them on the fire with a little veal or chicken, or both, in a good gravy
3. Season to taste
4. Thicken the gravy, and colour it with browning
5. Flavour with mushroom powder and lemon-juice, and one glass of white wine
6. Add forcemeat balls a few moments before serving
7. Garnish with thin slices of hard-boiled eggs
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stewed Kidneys and Macaroni
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Kidneys
- 1 gill Stock
- 1 oz. Butter
- 2 oz. Macaroni
- Parsley, Pepper, and Salt

## Directions

1. Put the macaroni into boiling water seasoned with salt, and boil for about twenty minutes, or until quite soft, but not broken
2. When it is boiling, skin and cut the kidneys in half
3. Put them into a frying-pan with the butter, and toss them over the fire for two or three minutes
4. Sprinkle with parsley, pepper, and salt
5. Pour over the stock or water
6. Bring it to the boil, then cover down by the side of the fire for five minutes
7. Place carefully in the centre of a hot dish
8. Boil up the gravy and pour over
9. Arrange the macaroni round the dish as a border, and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Leeks
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 bunch Leeks
- 1/2 pint Stock
- Pepper and Salt
- 1/2 oz. Butter
- 1/2 oz. Flour
- Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. Cut off the roots and green tops of the leeks and wash well.
2. Put them into a saucepan with the stock and stew very gently till soft; take them up and put on to a hot dish.
3. Put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is dissolved stir in the flour, mix well, and strain in the stock.
4. Stir until it boils.
5. Season with some pepper, salt, and a few drops of lemon juice.
6. Pour over the leeks, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Ox Cheek
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the cheek
- two onions
- two cloves
- three turnips
- three carrots
- two bay leaves
- a couple of heads of celery
- allspice
- pepper
- salt
- sweet herbs
- an ounce of butter
- a half-pint of the soup
- flour
- a table spoonful of ketchup
- tarragon vinegar
- a wineglass of port wine
- cayenne

## Directions

1. Put the cheek into a stewpan, with two onions, two cloves, three turnips, three carrots cut up, two bay leaves, a couple of heads of celery, and a little allspice, pepper, and salt, with a few sweet herbs
2. Let it simmer for three hours, and skim it frequently
3. Take out the cheek, and melt an ounce of butter in a stewpan, add to it a half-pint of the soup, and thicken it with some flour and a table spoonful of ketchup, tarragon vinegar, and a wineglass of port wine, with a little cayenne, and let it come to near a boil
4. Serve with the head on a dish, divided, and the soup in a tureen
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stewed Pigeons
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- pigeons
- brown sauce
- potato-onions

## Directions

1. Stew pigeons in brown sauce with whole potato-onions
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stewed Rump-steaks
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two pounds of steaks
- water
- six large onions
- pepper
- salt
- a carrot
- two table-spoonfuls of flour
- ketchup
- a tea-spoonful of mustard

## Directions

1. Take two pounds of steaks, and put them into a stewpan with just sufficient water to cover them, and let them simmer for half an hour
2. then add six large onions whole, with a little pepper and salt, a carrot cut up, and some thickening, consisting of two table-spoonfuls of flour and some ketchup, and a tea-spoonful of mustard
3. Let it simmer for an hour longer, and the steaks will be done
4. They must be sufficiently cooked, and at the same time not done to rags, or they are spoilt
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stewed Steak and Walnuts
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Steak
- 1 pint Water or Stock
- 3 Pickled Walnuts
- 1 teaspoonful Vinegar
- 1 teaspoonful Cornflour
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Cut the steak into neat pieces
2. Put it into hot water and bring to the boil
3. Keep it below boiling point, but simmering very gently for two hours and a half
4. Mix the cornflour with a tablespoonful of the vinegar from the walnuts and stir it in
5. Add salt to taste and a small pinch of pepper
6. Cut up three walnuts and put them in
7. Bring to simmering point again, and cook for at least another half-hour
8. Dish neatly
9. Boil up the gravy and pour over it
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Steak with Vegetables
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 gill French Beans
- 1/2 oz. Flour
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 Carrot
- 1 Turnip
- 1/2 pint Gravy
- Salt

## Directions

1. Cut the steak into neat pieces and fry very quickly in the butter
2. Take it out, put in the flour, and when quite smooth pour on the gravy and stir until it boils
3. Put back the steak, and simmer very gently for three hours
4. Cut the carrot and turnip up into thin strips, and put them in when the steak has been cooking for two hours
5. Boil the peas and beans separately, and add them to the stew five minutes before serving
6. Arrange the steak on a hot dish, put the vegetables round, and pour over the gravy
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stewed Sweetbreads
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pair Sweetbreads
- 1 pint Gravy
- Salt and Pepper
- 1/2 Onion
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1/2 oz. Flour
- 1 Carrot

## Directions

1. Put the sweetbreads in cold water, bring to the boil.
2. Strain away the water, scrape and clean them and remove the pieces of skin.
3. Put the butter into a stewpan and flour the sweetbreads.
4. Dry very lightly and quickly, take them out.
5. Slice up and fry the onion and carrot.
6. Stir in the flour and gravy, and bring to the boil.
7. Lay in the sweetbreads and simmer very gently for one hour.
8. Take them up on a hot dish, season and flavour the gravy.
9. Remove the fat, boil up and pour round them.
10. Serve hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stock From Bones (Fresh Bones)
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Beef bones
- Two teaspoonsful of salt
- One onion
- One carrot
- Half a turnip
- A little piece of the outside stalk of celery
- 1 doz. peppercorns
- Cold water

## Directions

1. Break beef bones up very small with a chopper
2. Put them into a large saucepan and cover well with cold water
3. Add two teaspoonsful of salt
4. When it boils up remove the scum carefully
5. Put in one onion, one carrot, half a turnip, a little piece of the outside stalk of celery, and 1 doz. peppercorns
6. Boil steadily for six hours, or longer
7. Strain off through a colander or sieve
8. Stand in a cool place till the next day
9. Carefully remove the fat and it is ready for use
10. In very hot weather omit all the vegetables
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stock From Bones No. 2
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Bones from joints of meat, whether roasted or boiled
- Beef bones
- Mutton and veal bones
- Bones and trimmings of poultry, game, and rabbits
- One teaspoonful of salt
- Cold water

## Directions

1. Remove all pieces that may be burnt
2. Chop the bones very small
3. Well cover with cold water
4. When the pot boils put in a teaspoonful of salt and skim well
5. Boil steadily for six hours or longer
6. Strain off and remove the fat
7. Let it stand till the next day before converting it into soup or gravy
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stuffed Conger Eel
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Eel
- 3 oz. Veal Seasoning
- 1 1/2 oz. Flour
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 1/2 oz. Butter
- Water

## Directions

1. Make the veal seasoning by recipe given elsewhere
2. Sew it into the eel and put it into a deep dish
3. Just cover it with water, and bake it in a good oven for about one hour
4. Take it up and keep hot, strain the liquor in which it has been cooked
5. Take about one pint and make into a brown sauce with the butter and flour
6. Colour it with a few drops of caramel, let it boil for a few minutes, season with salt, pepper, and lemon juice
7. Pour over the fish, and serve very hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stuffed Flathead
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Flathead
- 2 oz. Forcemeat
- 1 gill Gravy
- 1 oz. Dripping

## Directions

1. Take a little veal forcemeat and season nicely.
2. Sew this into the flathead and truss it into the shape of the letter S.
3. Rub some dripping on to a baking sheet, which should only be just large enough to take the fish.
4. Put some dripping on the top, and bake in a moderate oven for half-an-hour, or longer if large.
5. Slip it on to a hot dish, draw out the trussing string carefully, flavour and boil up the gravy and pour round it.
6. Serve very hot.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Stuffing for Hare or Kangaroo
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of beef suet, chopped fine
- three ounces of bread crumbs
- some parsley
- shalot
- marjoram
- thyme
- nutmeg
- pepper
- salt
- a little cayenne
- the yolk and white of an egg

## Directions

1. Mix two ounces of beef suet, chopped fine, three ounces of bread crumbs, some parsley, shalot, marjoram, thyme, nutmeg, pepper, salt, a little cayenne thick with the yolk and white of an egg
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stuffing for Veal, Turkey, Fowl, &c.
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter of a pound of beef stuffing in marrow
- the same weight of bread crumbs
- two drachms of parsley
- an onion
- sweet marjoram
- grated nutmeg
- pepper and salt
- the yolks and whites of two eggs

## Directions

1. Mix a quarter of a pound of beef stuffing in marrow and the same weight of bread crumbs
2. Add two drachms of parsley, an onion, sweet marjoram, grated nutmeg, pepper and salt
3. Pound them with the yolks and whites of two eggs
4. They can be made into balls or sausages, and floured when so required
5. They may be fried or boiled
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stuffing-balls for Hashes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three-quarters of a pound of bread crumbs
- half a pound of beef suet, finely chopped
- a little lemon
- thyme
- parsley
- marjoram
- savory
- nutmeg
- mace
- pepper
- cayenne
- two well-beaten eggs

## Directions

1. Add lemon, thyme, parsley, marjoram, and savory, all chopped, nutmeg, mace, pepper, and cayenne to the bread crumbs and suet
2. Mix with two well-beaten eggs
3. Divide into small balls
4. Fry them dry
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Stuffing-balls for Hashes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three-quarters of a pound of bread crumbs
- half a pound of beef suet
- a little lemon
- thyme
- parsley
- marjoram
- savory
- nutmeg
- mace
- pepper
- cayenne
- two well-beaten eggs

## Directions

1. Take three-quarters of a pound of bread crumbs and half a pound of beef suet, finely chopped
2. Add a little lemon, thyme, parsley, marjoram, and savory, also chopped, nutmeg, mace, pepper, and cayenne
3. Mix with two well-beaten eggs
4. Divide into balls
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Substitute for Cream in Tea or Coffee
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- white of an egg
- a small piece of butter
- tea or coffee

## Directions

1. Beat the white of an egg to a froth
2. Put to it a small piece of butter, and mix well
3. Turn the tea or coffee to it gradually, so that it may not curdle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Suedoise of Peaches
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ripe peaches
- a third of a pint of water
- three ounces of white sugar
- a couple of pounds of ripe peaches
- a blanched peach kernel
- macaroons
- candied citron
- lemon-juice

## Directions

1. Pare and divide four ripe peaches
2. Let them simmer for about five minutes in a syrup made with the third of a pint of water and three ounces of white sugar
3. Lift them out carefully into a deep dish, and pour half the syrup over them
4. Into the remaining half put a couple of pounds of ripe peaches, and boil them to marmalade
5. Lift the other peaches from the syrup, and reduce it by boiling more than half
6. Spread a deep layer of marmalade in a dish, and arrange the peaches round it, and fill the places with the marmalade
7. Place the half of a blanched peach kernel on each
8. Pour the reduced syrup equally over the surface
9. Form a border round the dish with macaroons or, instead, candied citron, sliced thin, and cut into leaves with a paste-cutter
10. Add a little lemon-juice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Suet Crust, for Pies or Puddings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of beef suet
- one pound of flour
- half a pint of water

## Directions

1. Chop the suet fine
2. Rub it well into the flour
3. Work the whole to a paste with the water
4. Roll it out
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Suet Pastry No. 1
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 10 oz. beef suet
- 0.5 pint water

## Directions

1. Sift the flour into a basin, and make it into a firm paste with the water.
2. Free the suet from skin, and put it twice through a sausage machine.
3. Roll the paste out, and put half over it in very tiny pieces; sprinkle with flour and fold into three.
4. Double the ends over till they meet, roll out again, and put on the rest of the suet and proceed as before.
5. It is then ready for use, but is much improved by standing for an hour in a cold place.
6. This is a very wholesome pastry, and particularly nice for meat pies.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Suet Pastry No. 2
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 8 oz. suet
- 0.5 pint water
- Pinch of salt

## Directions

1. Sift the flour into a basin; prepare the suet by cutting it into very thin slices and then shredding it up very fine indeed; mix it in with the flour.
2. Stir in the water until it is of a firm consistency, but do not use too much water, or the paste will be tough.
3. Turn it on to a floured board and knead for a few minutes.
4. It is then ready for use; this crust is suitable for all kinds of boiled puddings, such as meat, apple, jam, &c.
5. These puddings require to be boiled for a very long time and must always be plunged into boiling water, and kept boiling and covered with water all the time they are cooking.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Suet Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. flour
- 8 or 10 oz. suet
- 0.5 teaspoonful salt
- 0.5 pint water
- 2 cold potatoes

## Directions

1. Sift the flour and salt into a basin, mash the potatoes or rub them through a sieve, and stir them in.
2. Shred the suet finely and mix in thoroughly with a knife; make into rather a stiff paste with the water.
3. Dip a pudding cloth into boiling water.
4. Put the pudding into the centre, and tie up tightly.
5. Plunge into boiling water and boil steadily for two hours; turn out of the cloth carefully into a hot dish, and serve.
6. This pudding is delicious with roast meat, or it may be served as a sweet; jam sauce is nice poured round it.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Suet Pudding or Dumplings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six ounces of suet
- six ounces of flour
- two ounces of bread crumbs
- a tea-spoonful of salt
- two eggs
- six table-spoons of milk

## Directions

1. Chop six ounces of suet fine, put it into a basin, with six ounces of flour, two ounces of bread crumbs, and a tea-spoonful of salt
2. stir well together, beat up two eggs, and add to them six table-spoons of milk
3. put by degrees into the basin, and stir well together
4. Divide into dumplings, or boil in a cloth floured
5. Boil an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sugar Beer
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eight pounds of brown sugar
- a pound and three-quarters of hops
- six gallons of water
- half a cup of yeast

## Directions

1. Pour boiling water on the sugar
2. boil the hops
3. when cool, add half a cup of yeast
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sugar Beer without Yeast
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter to half a pound of hops
- two and a half gallons of water
- six pounds and a quarter of brown sparkling sugar
- a quarter of a pound of ginger
- two and a half gallons of cold water

## Directions

1. Boil from a quarter to half a pound of hops, according to taste, in two and a half gallons of water
2. Strain the water from the hops on six pounds and a quarter of brown sparkling sugar to dissolve it
3. Bruise and boil a quarter of a pound of ginger
4. add to it two and a half gallons of cold water, and fill up a five-gallon keg
5. Put in the tap, and bung it up tight
6. in a week the beer will be fit to drink
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sugar Beer without Yeast
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- A quarter to half a pound of hops, according to taste
- Two and a half gallons of water
- Six pounds and a quarter of brown sparkling sugar
- A quarter of a pound of ginger
- Two and a half gallons of cold water

## Directions

1. Boil the hops in two and a half gallons of water
2. Strain the water from the hops on six pounds and a quarter of brown sparkling sugar to dissolve it
3. Bruise and boil a quarter of a pound of ginger, add to it two and a half gallons of cold water, and fill up a five-gallon keg
4. Put in the tap, and bung it up tight; in a week the beer will be ready
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sugar, Cider, Fruit, and Beer Vinegars
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound and a quarter of sugar
- every gallon of water
- a quarter of a pint of good yeast
- one ounce of cream of tartar
- one ounce of crushed raisins
- juices of currants, gooseberries, and other indigenous fruit

## Directions

1. Add to a syrup consisting of one pound and a quarter of sugar for every gallon of water, a quarter of a pint of good yeast
2. Maintain the liquor at a heat of from 75° to 80° F.
3. Allow acidification to proceed for two or three days
4. Rack off from the sediment into the ripening cask
5. Mix with one ounce of cream of tartar and one ounce of crushed raisins
6. When completely freed from the sweet taste, draw off into bottles and closely cork up
7. The juices of currants, gooseberries, and many other indigenous fruit may be acetified either alone or in combination with syrup
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Superior Oakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Jones's patent flour
- a little butter
- new milk

## Directions

1. Mix with Jones's patent flour, a little butter, and moisten with new milk
2. Bake on the girdle, and butter hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Superior Recipe for Almond Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- yolks of ten eggs
- whites of seven eggs
- half a pound of sweet almonds
- half a pound of white sugar
- half an ounce of bitter almonds
- a table-spoonful of orange-flower water
- powdered sugar
- clarified sugar

## Directions

1. Beat up the yolks of ten eggs, and the whites of seven
2. Add half a pound of sweet almonds pounded finely, half a pound of white sugar, half an ounce of bitter almonds, and a table-spoonful of orange-flower water
3. When thoroughly mixed, grease a dish
4. Put in the pudding, and bake in a brisk oven
5. When done, strew powdered sugar over the top, or pour over clarified sugar with orange-flower water
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Supreme Nectar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six pounds of moist sugar
- five ounces of bruised ginger
- four ounces of cream of tartar
- four lemons
- seven gallons of boiling water
- half a pint of yeast
- a pint of brandy

## Directions

1. Put into a nine-gallon cask six pounds of moist sugar, five ounces of bruised ginger, four ounces of cream of tartar, four lemons, and seven gallons of boiling water
2. When cool, add half a pint of yeast
3. Let it work a couple of days
4. then strain, and add a pint of brandy
5. In a few days bottle and wire down
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sussex Dumplings
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of flour
- a little salt
- water

## Directions

1. Mix a pound of flour, a little salt, and water to a paste
2. Form into small round dumplings
3. Drop them into boiling water and boil for half an hour
4. Serve with roast or boiled meat, or if with the latter, boiled in the same water
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sweet Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eight eggs
- a pound of white sugar
- the peel of a grated lemon
- a pound of flour
- a tea-spoonful of rose-water

## Directions

1. Beat eggs to a froth
2. Add white sugar and grated lemon peel
3. Whisk the whole well together
4. Add flour and rose-water
5. Divide it into biscuits
6. Sugar them over
7. Bake in tins
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sweet Omelet
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 Eggs
- ½ oz. Butter
- 1 teaspoonful Jam
- Sugar

## Directions

1. Put the yolks of the eggs into a basin and beat in half the sugar, put the whites on to a plate with a little sugar, and whip till stiff; mix with the yolks.
2. Put the butter into a small frying-pan, and when it is dissolved pour in the mixture.
3. Leave over the fire for about three minutes.
4. Hold the pan in front of the fire for a minute or two to brown the top.
5. Put the jam on to a hot plate, slip the omelet on the top.
6. Serve at once.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Sweet Omelette
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the yolks and whites of three eggs
- a little ginger
- salt
- a little cream or new milk

## Directions

1. Beat the yolks and whites of eggs separately
2. Grate on them a little ginger with salt
3. Add a little cream or new milk
4. Fry the omelette
5. Serve with white sugar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Sweet Omelette
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three eggs, well beaten
- a tablespoonful of milk
- a spoonful of sugar
- a little salt
- two ounces of butter

## Directions

1. Mix the well beaten eggs with milk, sugar, and salt
2. Put two ounces of butter into the frying-pan
3. When it boils pour in the batter and fry
4. Fry of a light brown
5. Serve with sifted sugar over them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Swiss Dream
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of cream
- lemon-peel
- six ounces of white sugar
- two tea-spoonfuls of flour
- the juice of two lemons
- a quarter of a pound of macaroons
- a glass of white wine
- sliced citron or iced lemon-peel

## Directions

1. Flavour with lemon-peel a pint of cream
2. Add six ounces of white sugar
3. Put the cream and sugar into a saucepan
4. When simmering, add, by degrees, two tea-spoonfuls of flour
5. Let it simmer for five minutes, stirring it all the time
6. Pour it out, and when cold mix with it, by degrees, the juice of two lemons
7. Take a quarter of a pound of macaroons, put them in a dish, and pour over them a glass of white wine
8. Add part of the cream, then the macaroons, and cream again
9. Ornament the dish with sliced citron or iced lemon-peel
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Swiss Pates
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cold Roast Veal, Fowl, or Lamb
- Half a Stale Loaf
- Sweet Herbs or Parsley
- 1 Egg
- 1 gill Melted Butter
- Pepper and Salt
- Hot Fat

## Directions

1. Mince the meat very finely, season with any forcemeat that may be left, or else some grated lemon peel, parsley and sweet herbs, pepper and salt.
2. Make one gill of melted butter by recipe given elsewhere, stir in the meat and let it simmer for a few minutes.
3. Cut some slices of bread about an inch and a half thick, stamp them out with a round cutter about two inches across.
4. Remove the centre for about half way through with a smaller cutter, brush them over with a raw egg beaten up, and cover them with fine crumbs.
5. Fry in hot fat till a good colour, drain away the fat from them on kitchen paper.
6. Fill these with the mince, garnish with sprigs of parsley, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Sydney Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- ½ doz. Tomatoes
- 1 Carrot
- 2 Small Onions
- 12 Peppercorns
- 1 fagot Herbs
- ½ teaspoonful Salt
- 2 quarts Stock
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Cornflour
- 1 oz. Tapioca
- 1 cup of Green Peas
- Curry Powder
- ½ teaspoonful of Sugar

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a saucepan, slice up the onions and carrot and fry them in it with the herbs, peppercorns, and a good pinch of curry powder.
2. Mix the cornflour with a little stock and pour it over.
3. Slice up the tomatoes and add them with the boiling stock; stir until it boils, and then simmer slowly for an hour.
4. Rub through a sieve and return to the saucepan.
5. Add the salt, sugar, and the tapioca; stir until this becomes transparent and thickens the soup.
6. Put in a cupful of cold boiled peas; boil up and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Syllabub
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cream
- wine
- sugar
- lemon juice
- lemon peel
- cinnamon

## Directions

1. Make a strong whip as for trifle
2. Mix a pint of cream with half a pint of wine
3. Add sugar and flavour with the juice and grated peel of a lemon, and a little cinnamon
4. Stir briskly
5. Fill the glasses sufficiently to put some of the whip on the top of each glass
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Syrup of Absinthe (Wormwood)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two ounces of the tops of wormwood
- four quarts of boiling water
- the white of an egg
- two pounds of sugar

## Directions

1. Put in a basin two ounces of the tops of wormwood, and pour over it four quarts of boiling water
2. Let it steep four hours on a hot hearth
3. Strain the decoction through linen
4. Beat the white of an egg, melt two pounds of sugar, and simmer until done
5. Bottle for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tamarind Drink
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one ounce and a half of tamarinds
- three ounces of currants
- two ounces of stoned raisins
- three pints of water
- lemon-peel

## Directions

1. Boil tamarinds, currants, and stoned raisins in three pints of water
2. Strain
3. Add a little lemon-peel
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tamarind Drinks
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- an ounce and a half of tamarinds
- three ounces of currants
- two ounces of stoned raisins
- three pints of water
- a little lemon-peel

## Directions

1. boil tamarinds, currants, and stoned raisins in three pints of water
2. Strain, and add a little lemon-peel
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tapioca Meringue
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 1½ oz. Tapioca
- 1 oz. Sugar
- Whites of 2 Eggs
- Flavouring
- 1 oz. Beef Suet

## Directions

1. Wash the tapioca well in cold water, strain off the water, and put it into a pie dish.
2. Chop the suet very finely and mix it in with the sugar.
3. Flavour with grated lemon peel or nutmeg, pour over the milk and mix well.
4. Stand in a very cool oven for two hours.
5. Whip the whites of the eggs to a very stiff froth, flavour the same as the pudding, spread these on top, sprinkle with sugar, and stand in the oven till set.
6. Serve cold. This meringue is very much improved if a few macaroons are broken up and laid on the top before the eggs are put on, or if a spoonful of raspberry jam is spread over.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tapioca Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. Tapioca
- 1 Onion
- 1 Carrot
- 3 quarts Bone Stock
- Salt
- Lemon juice

## Directions

1. Boil the onion and carrot in the stock for twenty minutes
2. If the stock is not a good colour put in half a teaspoonful of burnt sugar
3. Strain out the vegetables
4. Wash the tapioca in cold water and stir it in
5. Continue stirring until the tapioca is quite clear
6. Flavour with salt and lemon juice, and serve very hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tart de Moy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three-quarters of a pound of savoury biscuits
- a quart of milk
- six ounces of fresh butter
- four eggs
- one ounce of candied orange-peel
- one ounce of lemon-peel
- one ounce of citron
- white sugar

## Directions

1. Soak three-quarters of a pound of savoury biscuits in a quart of milk
2. Add six ounces of fresh butter, four eggs, one ounce of candied orange-peel, the same quantity of lemon-peel, and one ounce of citron
3. Mix all well together
4. Sweeten with white sugar, and bake in a quick oven
5. When nearly done, spread over the top the whites of the eggs well whisked
6. Return it to the oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tasmanette
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mulberry jam
- Aldridge plum jam
- morella jam
- quince jam
- sugar
- a tablespoon of vinegar per pint

## Directions

1. In making jam — mulberry, Aldridge plum, morella, and quince — after the sugar has been added, skim off and strain the surplus syrup
2. Keep in a dry place till all are ready
3. Add a tablespoon of vinegar to every pint
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tea Cake
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Flour
- ½ pint Milk
- 2 oz. Butter
- 1 Egg
- 2 teaspoonsful Baking Powder
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar

## Directions

1. Rub the butter into the flour, stir in the sugar and baking powder.
2. Beat up the egg and milk, and mix the dry ingredients into a dough with them ; divide into two pieces and form each into a flat cake.
3. Cut lightly across into four with a knife, put on to a buttered tin, and bake twenty minutes.
4. Cut open, butter, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tears of the Widow of Malabar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ten pounds of spirit (pale brandy)
- four pounds of white sugar
- four pints of water
- four drachms of powdered cinnamon
- forty-eight grains of cloves
- forty-eight grains of mace
- caramel

## Directions

1. To ten pounds of spirit add four pounds of white sugar and four pints of water
2. Add four drachms of powdered cinnamon, forty-eight grains of cloves, and the same quantity of mace
3. Colour with caramel
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tears of the Widow of Malabar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ten pounds of spirit, pale brandy
- four pounds of white sugar
- four pints of water
- four drachms of powdered cinnamon
- forty-eight grains of cloves

## Directions

1. To ten pounds of spirit add four pounds of white sugar, and four pints of water
2. Add four drachms of powdered cinnamon and forty-eight grains of cloves
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tewahdiddle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of small beer
- a table-spoon of brandy
- a tea-spoonful of brown sugar
- a little grated nutmeg
- a roll of thin lemon-peel

## Directions

1. Combine a pint of small beer, a table-spoon of brandy, a tea-spoonful of brown sugar, a little grated nutmeg, and a roll of thin lemon-peel
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tewahdidle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pint of small beer
- a tablespoon of brandy
- a teaspoonful of brown sugar
- a little grated nutmeg
- a roll of thin lemon-peel

## Directions

1. Combine a pint of small beer, a tablespoon of brandy, and a teaspoonful of brown sugar with a little grated nutmeg and a roll of thin lemon-peel
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: The Abingdon Pudding
