author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pound of raisins, nicely stoned
- one pound of currants washed and picked
- one pound of beef suet, freed from skin and finely chopped
- eight ounces of bread crumbs, sifted through a colander
- two ounces of flour
- half a pound of moist sugar, good brown
- six eggs, well whisked
- half an ounce of candied lemon
- half an ounce of candied orange
- the peel of one lemon, chopped fine
- the juice of half a lemon
- a nutmeg, grated
- a thimbleful of salt
- a wine-glass of brandy

## Directions

1. Mix the whole thoroughly together
2. Have ready a well-buttered mould or basin
3. Pack the mixture in
4. Place over it a dusting of flour
5. Tie down with a buttered paper and strong cloth
6. Boil gently for six hours
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: The Bakewell Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- thin puff paste
- any kind of jam
- eight egg yolks
- ten egg whites
- half a pound of sugar
- half a pound of butter melted
- almond flavour to taste

## Directions

1. Cover a dish with thin puff paste
2. put a layer of any kind of jam, about half an inch thick
3. take the yolks of eight eggs and ten whites, half a pound of sugar, half a pound of butter melted, and almond flavour to taste, beat all well together
4. pour the mixture into a dish, an inch thick
5. bake an hour in a moderate oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: The Best Way to Cook a Ham
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ham
- cold water

## Directions

1. Put the ham in cold water to soak for twenty-four hours before boiling
2. Scrape it well
3. Place in your copper, well covered with cold water
4. Let it come to boil slowly
5. After it has boiled for twenty minutes take all the fire from under the copper
6. Let it remain until the water is cool, or until it is convenient to remove it out of the water
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: The Empress of Russia's Brine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Six pounds of common salt
- Two pounds of powdered loaf sugar
- Three ounces of saltpetre
- Three gallons of water

## Directions

1. Boil together over a slow fire
2. Skim it while boiling
3. When cold, pour it over the meat, ensuring every part is covered with the brine
4. Small pork will be sufficiently cured in four or five days, and hams intended for drying in two weeks
5. This pickle may be used frequently if boiled up, skimmed, and more ingredients used
6. Before putting the meat into the water, wash it well and dry it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: The Loving Cup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a tea-cupful of capillaire
- sugar
- a few drops of orange-flower water
- a pint of brown sherry
- one bottle of ale
- a bottle of soda-water
- grated nutmeg
- a piece of toast

## Directions

1. Combine half a tea-cupful of capillaire, sugar, a few drops of orange-flower water, a pint of brown sherry, and one bottle of ale
2. Just before placing it on the table, add a bottle of soda-water
3. Sprinkle some grated nutmeg on the top of the liquor and put a piece of toast in it
4. Place a napkin on the handle of the cup
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: The Sighs of Love
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- spirit
- water
- sugar
- otto of roses
- cochineal

## Directions

1. Use spirit, water, and sugar as in Tears of the Widow of Malabar
2. Perfume with otto of roses
3. Slightly colour with cochineal
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: The Whig
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter of a pound of fresh butter
- two pounds of flour
- half a pint of warm cream
- a quarter of a pint of good yeast
- grated nutmeg
- mace
- cloves
- a quarter of an ounce of caraway-seeds
- a quarter of a pound of fine sugar

## Directions

1. Rub a quarter of a pound of fresh butter into two pounds of flour till none of it is visible
2. Add half a pint of warm cream and a quarter of a pint of good yeast
3. Mix the whole into a light paste, and set it to rise
4. When the dough has risen, work in the grated nutmeg, mace, cloves, caraway-seeds, and fine sugar
5. Roll the paste out thin, and divide into cakes
6. Put the cakes on tins to rise, and then bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Boil Cabbage
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Cabbage
- Salt
- Water
- Carbonate of Soda

## Directions

1. Remove the outer leaves of the cabbage, then cut it into quarters and cut out the stalk.
2. Wash it well in salt and water, and leave in the water for half-an-hour.
3. Put it into a colander and shake all the water from it.
4. Place on the fire a large saucepan of water, and when it boils, put in two teaspoonsful of salt and a quarter of a teaspoonful of carbonate of soda.
5. Put in the cabbage and cover down till it boils up; then remove the lid and boil very quickly, pressing it down into the water from time to time.
6. Boil for from fifteen to twenty minutes; try it with a fork, and if soft turn into a colander, and very carefully press all the water from it.
7. Slip into a vegetable dish and cut into neat pieces.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: To Boil Cauliflowers
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- cauliflowers
- salt
- water

## Directions

1. Soak the cauliflowers in plenty of salt and water, with the flower downwards
2. Cook in plenty of boiling water seasoned with salt, putting the flower to the bottom of the saucepan
3. Keep uncovered all the time of cooking
4. Take up with a slice and strain in a colander
5. Turn carefully into a vegetable dish, and serve with or without sauce
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: To Boil French Beans
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- beans
- boiling water
- salt
- carbonate of soda

## Directions

1. Slice up the beans and wash in cold water
2. Put them into plenty of boiling water, seasoned with salt and a quarter of a teaspoonful of carbonate of soda
3. Boil quickly without the lid for about ten minutes or a quarter of an hour
4. When soft, strain off and shake the water out thoroughly
5. Put into a hot dish, and serve plainly or with melted butter
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: To Boil Green Peas
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- peas
- cold water
- salt
- sugar
- mint

## Directions

1. Shell the peas and wash them well
2. Just cover them with cold water, season it with a little salt, sugar, and mint
3. Bring quickly to the boil and cook for about twenty minutes
4. When soft, but not broken, strain off the water and put them into a vegetable dish
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: To Boil New Potatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- New potatoes
- Salt
- Water
- Mint

## Directions

1. New potatoes may be either scraped while raw, or peeled after boiling; they are a better flavour if cooked in their skins.
2. Well wash in cold water, plunge into boiling water seasoned with salt and a sprig of mint.
3. Boil quickly until a fork will go through easily; then strain off the water, dry, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: To Boil Potatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Potatoes
- Salt
- Water

## Directions

1. Peel potatoes thinly, and well wash in cold water, but not soak; put them into a saucepan kept for this vegetable only.
2. Just cover them with cold water seasoned with salt, and bring to a boil.
3. Simmer very gently for about twenty minutes; test them with a fork, and if soft, strain off the water and toss them in a saucepan over the fire until they are dry.
4. If potatoes begin to break soon after they boil up, pour off nearly all the water, leaving only one inch at the bottom of the saucepan, and cook the potatoes slowly in this, then strain off and dry.
5. Potatoes that are very troublesome to boil often steam well; allow from an hour to one hour and a half, according to the quantity of water over which they are cooking.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: To Boil Rice
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Rice
- Salt
- Water

## Directions

1. Wash the rice well in two or three waters
2. Have a large saucepan on the fire full of boiling water seasoned with salt
3. Throw in the rice and boil very quickly for five or six minutes
4. Take up a grain, and if it feels quite soft it is done; if not, boil another minute
5. Strain off the water and pour over it some clean hot water to separate the grains
6. If required immediately, put it back in the saucepan and toss over the fire till dry
7. If not, spread it on a sieve or dish and dry on the stove, covered with a cloth, or in the oven with the door open
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: To Boil Turbot
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- turbot
- salt
- cold water
- red spawn of the lobster
- parsley
- lemon
- horseradish
- fried smelts
- lobster or crayfish sauce

## Directions

1. Soak the fish in cold water a couple of hours before you dress it, with some salt in it
2. Score the skin across the back
3. Put a handful of salt into a fish-kettle with cold water
4. Lay your fish on a strainer
5. When coming to the boil, skim it well
6. Set the kettle on the side of the fire to simmer for about fifteen minutes
7. Rub some of the red spawn of the lobster through a sieve, and sprinkle over
8. Garnish with parsley, lemon, and horseradish; or it may be served with fried smelts
9. Serve with lobster or crayfish sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Clarify Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- stock
- egg

## Directions

1. Put the stock in a stewpan
2. Break an egg and use the white and the shell
3. Beat them together and put them into the stock
4. Stir it with a whisk
5. When it has simmered for a short time, strain through a tamis
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Cook Kangaroo
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- hind-quarters of kangaroo
- tail
- legs
- small onion
- peppercorns
- bay leaves
- grated bread
- shred suet
- thyme
- parsley
- onion
- sausage-meat or ham or bacon
- pepper
- salt
- eggs
- caul of sweet mutton suet
- mushroom ketchup
- flour
- butter
- currant jelly
- ham or bacon

## Directions

1. Take the hind-quarters, above the kidney.
2. Cut off the tail, also the legs, at the first joint from the back.
3. Discard the feet and cut up the remainder of the legs with the tail to stew for gravy.
4. Add to the gravy meat a small onion sliced, a few peppercorns, and one or two bay leaves.
5. Let it stew till all goodness is out of the meat, then strain.
6. Make a good stuffing as for turkey, with a large cupful of grated bread, half as much shred suet, some thyme, parsley, and a small portion of onion, chopped very fine.
7. Add either half a pound of sausage-meat, or a little finely-shred ham or bacon.
8. Season with pepper and salt, and mix well with one or two eggs.
9. Put as much stuffing as it will hold into the aperture of the body and make the rest into balls about the size of pullets' eggs.
10. You may lard the kangaroo, but it is very good without, if covered with a caul of sweet mutton suet while baking.
11. Bake with long cooking, letting it stay in the oven a full half hour after you think it done.
12. Serve with some of the gravy having added to it some mushroom ketchup, and a little flour and butter.
13. Have currant jelly to eat with it or some persons prefer ham or bacon instead.
14. Hashed with the remaining gravy, the kangaroo is often nicer than it was the first day.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Cure a Sound of Beef in Hot Weather, or if Needed for Immediate Use
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sound of beef
- fine Liverpool salt
- saltpetre
- cold brine
- sugar

## Directions

1. Rub in thoroughly a rather large quantity of fine Liverpool salt, with a little saltpetre
2. Place it over a tub or pan of water on two sticks
3. Place a good quantity of salt on the upper side
4. Turn it once after twelve hours, laying additional salt on the turned-up side
5. It will be cured in twenty-four hours
6. Should it be required at once, with a small squirt or syringe, if cold brine be at hand, it might be squirted into all the crevices, and then it could be cooked in twelve hours
7. If beef is not required in a hurry, a little sugar and less salt very much improves the flavour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Dress a Ham for Table
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ham
- parsley
- nutmeg

## Directions

1. When skinned, chop parsley (free from grit, but not wetted or washed) impalpably fine
2. Scatter it over the ham
3. Grate a whole nutmeg over it
4. This will keep it almost any length of time, and adds a nice flavour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Dress Macaroni
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- macaroni
- milk or water
- salt
- cheese, grated or cut into small pieces
- butter
- bread-crumbs
- pepper or cayenne

## Directions

1. Put the quantity required on the fire to boil, either in milk or water, with a little salt
2. When done, take the macaroni out of the liquid, and put into a tin dish that will fit into a toaster or salamander
3. Have ready cheese grated, or cut up into suitable small pieces
4. Place cheese on the macaroni, with a small quantity of butter and a few bread-crumbs
5. Put a little salt in, with grated cheese on the top
6. Brown before the fire, and serve hot
7. Let the consumer use either pepper or cayenne, according to fancy; put none in the dish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Fry Fish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fish
- bread crumbs or oatmeal
- egg
- clarified fat or salad oil

## Directions

1. Wash the fish thoroughly, and dry them well.
2. Prepare some bread crumbs, by rubbing stale bread through a colander; or oatmeal may be used instead.
3. Beat an egg up, and use it on the fish on both sides.
4. Put clarified fat or salad oil into the pan, and then put in the fish, which must literally boil in the fat.
5. When done, take them off and dry them; serve with a napkin laid on the dish, or without a napkin.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Fry Parsley
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Parsley
- Fat

## Directions

1. Pick the top or flower of parsley carefully and rub well in a damp cloth, and then in a dry cloth
2. Put into a frying basket and plunge into the fat when the fish, or whatever it is to be served with, has been fried
3. Leave it in not more than one minute
4. Turn it on to some kitchen paper and stand for a minute on the stove to dry
5. It is then ready
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: To Hash Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- meat
- broth or gravy
- fried onion
- pepper
- salt
- ketchup or other sauce
- flour
- sippets of toast
- port wine
- saffron
- chorissa

## Directions

1. Put meat on the fire in a little broth or gravy with a little fried onion, pepper, salt, and a spoonful of ketchup or any other sauce at hand
2. Let it simmer for about ten minutes
3. Mix in a cup a little flour with a little of the gravy
4. Pour it into the stewpan to thicken the rest
5. Serve sippets of toast with hashes
6. A little port wine, a pinch of saffron, or a piece of chorissa may be considered great improvements
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Keep Gooseberries and Damsons
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- gooseberries or damsons
- rosin

## Directions

1. Pick the fruit, not too ripe, and put them into wide-mouthed bottles
2. Cork them gently
3. Put them in the oven — not over-warm — until they have shrunk a third of their weight
4. Take them out of the oven, tighten the corks, and rosin them down close
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Make Bread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one bushel of flour
- one pint of yeast
- milk-warm water
- six ounces of salt
- milk-warm water or milk

## Directions

1. Put a bushel of flour into a kneading trough
2. Mix a pint of yeast thoroughly with as much milk-warm water
3. Make a deep hole in the middle of the flour, and pour the yeast and water into it
4. Take a spoon and work it round the edges of this body of moisture, so as to bring into it by degrees flour enough to make a thin batter
5. Stir well for a minute or two
6. Throw a handful of flour over the surface of this batter, and cover the whole with a cloth thickly folded to keep it warm
7. Set it by the fire, regulating the distance by the state of the weather and the season of the year
8. When the batter has risen enough to make cracks in the flour, form the whole mass into dough by strewing six ounces of salt over the heap
9. Beginning round the hole containing the batter, work the flour into it, pouring in milk-warm water or milk as it is wanted
10. When the whole mass is moistened, knead it well
11. Mould the loaves, let them rise for twenty minutes
12. Put them into an oven that has been previously heated
13. Bake for about an hour for a four pound loaf, fifty minutes for a three pound, and so on in proportion
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Make Strong Beer or Ale
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Twelve bushels of malt to the hogshead for beer, and eight for ale
- Three-quarters of a pound of hops to a bushel for strong beer, or half a pound for ale
- Two quarts of yeast
- A pound and a half of hops dried before the fire

## Directions

1. Pour the whole quantity hot, but not boiling, on at once, and let it infuse three hours, being covered
2. Mash it in the first half-hour, and let it stand the remainder of the time
3. Run it on the hops, previously infused in water
4. Boil them with the wort two hours from the time it begins to boil
5. Cool a pailful to add two quarts of yeast to, which will prepare it for putting to the rest when ready next day; but if possible, put them together the same night
6. Tun as usual
7. Cover the bung-hole with paper when the beer has done working
8. When it is to be stopped, have ready a pound and a half of hops dried before the fire, put them into the bung-hole, and fasten it up
9. After the beer or ale has run from the grains, you can make table-beer of a similar quantity
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Mull Wine
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- water, one and a half wine-glassfuls
- spice (cinnamon, bruised ginger, and cloves), quarter of an ounce
- fine sugar, three ounces
- port wine or claret, one pint
- orange-rind (optional)
- sherry, fine raisin, or ginger wine
- yolks of four fresh eggs

## Directions

1. Boil in a wine-glass and a half of water a quarter of an ounce of spice (cinnamon, bruised ginger, and cloves) with three ounces of fine sugar, until they form a thick syrup, which must not on any account be allowed to burn
2. Pour in a pint of port wine, and stir it gently until it is on the point of boiling only
3. Serve immediately
4. If using orange-rind, boil it with the spice
5. Sherry, fine raisin, or ginger wine may be stirred hot to the yolks of four fresh eggs for an excellent variation
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Pot Fish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fish
- butter
- cayenne
- pepper
- salt
- mace
- oil
- vinegar
- clarified butter

## Directions

1. Pull the fish to pieces, and pick out every bone
2. Mix with a little butter, cayenne, pepper, salt, mace, oil, and vinegar
3. Place the fish in pots, and pour clarified butter over it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Preserve Apples, Pears, and Quinces
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- apples, pears, or quinces
- lukewarm water

## Directions

1. Peel and core them, and cut them in thin slices
2. Dry them in the sun
3. When used in pies or puddings, soak them sufficiently in lukewarm water, and the flavour of the apple will be found excellent
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Preserve Gooseberries Whole
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- full grown but not ripe gooseberries
- new corks
- resin

## Directions

1. Top and tail the gooseberries
2. Put the fruit into wide-mouthed bottles
3. Gently cork them with new corks
4. Put them into a pan of boiling water
5. Let them remain in the water until they have shrunk to a fourth
6. Beat the corks in tight, and resin the tops
7. Set the bottles in a dry place
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Remove the Muddy Taste from Fish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fish
- fresh water

## Directions

1. Put them alive into a tub, and pour fresh water over them
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Stew Fish
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fish
- half a pint of claret or port wine
- a quart of stock or gravy
- a large onion
- a few cloves
- a little mace
- flour
- pepper
- salt
- anchovy
- mushroom ketchup
- chilli vinegar

## Directions

1. When the fish have been well washed, lay them in a stewpan, with half a pint of claret or port wine, and a quart of stock or gravy, a large onion sliced, and a few cloves and a little mace.
2. Cover the stewpan, and let it simmer for about twenty minutes, according to the size of the fish.
3. Take the fish out, and lay it on a hot hearth in a dish.
4. Thicken the liquor that it was stewed in with a little flour, and season with pepper, salt, anchovy, and mushroom ketchup, and chilli vinegar.
5. When it has boiled about ten minutes, either pour it over the fish or return the fish into the stewpan to warm.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Stew Lobsters the Irish Way
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a boiled lobster
- mustard
- vinegar
- cayenne
- butter
- a glass of sherry or madeira
- sliced lemon

## Directions

1. Cut and break the boiled lobster, but not into small pieces
2. Prepare a mixture of mustard and vinegar, seasoned with cayenne
3. Put the mixture with the lobster and a good-sized piece of butter into a stewpan
4. Keep the cover close for about five minutes
5. Put in a glass of sherry or madeira, and let it boil for about five minutes more
6. Serve it up, garnished with sliced lemon
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: To Stew Spanish Beans and Peas
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beans
- cold water
- two tablespoonfuls of oil
- pepper
- salt
- white sugar

## Directions

1. Soak the beans over night in cold water
2. Stew them in only sufficient water to cover them, with two tablespoonfuls of oil, a little pepper and salt, and white sugar
3. When done they should be perfectly soft and tender
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Toasted Cheese
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mild cheese
- butter
- ale

## Directions

1. fit six or eight movable small tin dishes into the toaster
2. put the least bit of butter in the tin at the bottom, with the cheese, or a little ale
3. expose to the fire until done
4. Serve hot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tomato Pie
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 or 4 Tomatoes
- 1 lb. Chops
- 1 oz. Butter
- 4 Cold Potatoes
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 tablespoonful Milk

## Directions

1. Skin and slice up the tomatoes, put a layer at the bottom of a pie-dish, then lay in the chops.
2. Season with pepper and salt, and cover with the rest of the tomatoes.
3. Mash up the potatoes until very smooth.
4. Warm the butter and milk, and pour it over them and make into a paste.
5. Cover the dish with this crust, brush the top over with a little milk, put into the oven and bake for about one hour and a quarter.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tomato Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- fifteen ripe tomatoes
- a capsicum
- a few spoonfuls of gravy
- pepper
- salt
- onion
- a shalot

## Directions

1. Take fifteen ripe tomatoes, cut them in half, and squeeze them
2. put them into a stewpan, with a capsicum and a few spoonfuls of gravy
3. simmer for an hour
4. run them through a sieve
5. add pepper, salt, and onion cut small, or a shalot
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tomato Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Tomatoes
- 1 oz. Butter
- 1.5 oz. Flour
- Sugar
- Salt

## Directions

1. If the tomatoes are ripe they need not be cooked; but if at all hard, boil them for five minutes
2. Slice up and rub through a sieve
3. Put the butter into a small saucepan, and when it is dissolved stir in the flour and sugar
4. Pour in the tomato juice and stir until it boils
5. Season with salt to taste
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tomato Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 doz. Tomatoes
- 1 oz. Butter
- 2 Onions
- 1 Carrot
- 2 oz. Flour
- Salt
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar
- 2 doz. Peppercorns
- 3 quarts Bone Stock

## Directions

1. Slice up the onions and carrot, and fry them in the butter with the peppercorns and sugar.
2. Sprinkle over the flour and mix well together.
3. Cut up the tomatoes and put them in, then pour over the boiling stock and stir until it boils.
4. Simmer slowly for an hour.
5. Rub through a sieve, return to the saucepan and make thoroughly hot, pour into a warm tureen, and serve with fried bread.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tomatoes and Eggs
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 Eggs
- 1 pint Tomato Sauce
- Fried Bread
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley

## Directions

1. Take some thick tomato sauce and pour it on to a hot dish
2. Poach the eggs carefully and lay them on the sauce
3. Garnish with parsley and fried bread, and serve hot
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tomatoes and Mince
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 8 Tomatoes
- 8 pieces Toast
- 1/2 lb. Minced Meat
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Take any remains of cold mince or hash, add more flavouring if necessary, and make it hot in the saucepan.
2. Wipe the tomatoes and scrape out the centre, fill it up with the mince, and stand in the oven for ten minutes.
3. Have ready some rounds of toast about the same size as the tomatoes.
4. When the tomatoes are cooked enough, stand them on the toast, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tomatoes Stuffed
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Tomatoes
- 1 lb. Veal Forcemeat
- 1 oz. Cheese
- 6 pieces Fried Bread

## Directions

1. Choose tomatoes of a good colour, and all about the same size; scoop out the centre.
2. Grate up the cheese and mix it with the forcemeat, put this into the tomatoes.
3. Place on a buttered tin, and bake in the oven for ten minutes.
4. Put each tomato on to a round of fried bread, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tourtes a la Creme
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a pound of flour
- a quarter of a pint of thick cream
- a pinch of salt
- half a pound of butter
- yolk of egg

## Directions

1. Put a pound of flour on a paste-board, make a hole in the middle, and put in a quarter of a pint of thick cream and a pinch of salt
2. Mix it lightly, and let it stand half an hour
3. Put in it half a pound of butter, at five times, letting it stand a quarter of an hour twice
4. Make it into several cakes
5. Feather with yolk of egg, and bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Treacle Parkins
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one quart of oatmeal
- three pounds of treacle
- three-quarters of a pound of sugar
- a little butter and flour
- sweetmeats or carraways ad libitum

## Directions

1. Mix all together
2. Roll it out thin, and cut into round cakes
3. Bake in a tin
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Trench Hung Beef
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef (bones removed)
- a pound of salt
- two ounces of saltpetre
- an ounce of sal prunella
- half a pound of coarse brown sugar
- parsley
- shalots
- thyme
- bay-leaves
- marjoram
- sweet basil
- winter savory
- coriander-seed
- a tablespoonful of juniper berries
- three cloves of garlic
- vinegar
- a couple of onions
- a few cloves
- nutmeg
- herbs

## Directions

1. Remove the bones from the meat and put it in a pan
2. Rub well with a pickle composed of a pound of salt, two ounces of saltpetre, an ounce of sal prunella, and half a pound of coarse brown sugar, well mixed together
3. Cover with parsley, shalots, thyme, bay-leaves, marjoram, sweet basil, winter savory, a little coriander-seed, a tablespoonful of juniper berries, and three cloves of garlic
4. Turn every day for ten days in the brine, and put the herbs on the top
5. Hang up and smoke
6. When boiling, put a little vinegar in the water, a couple of onions cut, a few cloves, some nutmeg, and herbs
7. When done, let it remain in the same liquor until cold
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Trifle
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four sponge biscuits
- a quarter of a pound of macaroons and ratafias
- a quarter of a pint of sherry
- a pint of custard
- a layer of raspberry jam
- whipped cream

## Directions

1. Put in the trifle-dish four sponge biscuits and a quarter of a pound of macaroons and ratafias
2. Cover the dish with these, and soak into them a quarter of a pint of sherry
3. When soaked sufficiently pour in a pint of custard, then a layer of raspberry jam, and cover with whipped cream well frothed
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Tripe and Tomatoes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Tripe
- 1 doz. Tomatoes
- ½ pint Water or Stock
- 1 oz. Cornflour
- ½ Onion
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Cut the tripe into neat pieces, put it on in cold water and bring to the boil.
2. Let it boil for five minutes, put it into cold water, and wash and scrape it well.
3. Slice up the tomatoes and rub them through a sieve.
4. Mix them with the stock or water, and season with pepper and salt.
5. Pour this into a saucepan, slice in the onion, put in the tripe, and let it boil up.
6. Simmer gently for four hours.
7. Mix the cornflour smoothly with a little water or stock, and pour it in.
8. Stir until it boils, dish the tripe carefully, season and flavour the sauce to taste, and pour it over.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tripe in Milk
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Tripe
- 1 pint Milk
- Pepper and Salt
- 2 Onions
- 1 oz. Flour
- ½ pint Water

## Directions

1. Prepare the tripe as in the preceding recipe.
2. Mix the milk and water together, pour it into a saucepan.
3. Lay in the tripe.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Tripe Stewed
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- tripe
- onions
- sweet herbs
- pint of milk or stock
- ounce of butter
- flour
- white pepper
- salt
- spice

## Directions

1. Put the tripe into a stewpan, with onions whole (at least a dozen), and some sweet herbs
2. Add a pint of milk or stock, and let it simmer for a time
3. Add an ounce of butter mixed with flour, white pepper, salt, and a little spice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Turkey Braised
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- turkey
- slices of bacon
- chopped carrots
- onions
- sweet herbs
- salt
- pepper
- allspice
- mace

## Directions

1. Truss and stuff the turkey in the usual manner
2. Cover the bottom of a stewpan with slices of bacon, chopped carrots, onions, sweet herbs, salt, pepper, allspice, and mace
3. Lay the turkey on these ingredients with its breast downwards
4. Place a layer of the same materials over the bird
5. Cover the top close and put the pan in an oven or on a hot-plate for three or four hours until done
6. Let the dish gently simmer in its own steam until mellow and tender
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Turkish Kebobs
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- lean meat
- curry stuff
- half a shalot between each piece
- yolk of an egg
- bread crumbs
- mixed parsley
- sweet herbs
- salt
- cayenne
- a little salad oil

## Directions

1. Take lean meat, cut it into pieces about an inch square, and a quarter of an inch thick
2. Rub these pieces with a little curry stuff, and put them upon skewers, with half a shalot between each
3. Dip the skewered meat into the yolk of an egg, and then into bread crumbs, mixed parsley, sweet herbs, salt, and cayenne
4. Dip them again in the egg, and a second time cover them with bread crumbs
5. Put these sticks of meat on a spit, and roast them, basting them with a little salad oil
6. Serve them up brown, with or without sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Turkish Pilau
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- mutton, fowls, or pigeons
- butter
- rice
- broth
- pepper
- salt
- saffron
- pomegranate juice

## Directions

1. According to the number of guests, take mutton, fowls, or pigeons, boil them until half done, then put the meat and broth into a basin
2. Cut the half-cooked meat into pieces, and put them into a pot, with some butter, and fry them of a light brown colour
3. Wash the rice well, then place it over the meat in the pot, and pour the broth over the rice till it is covered to a full finger's depth
4. Cover the pot, and keep a clear fire under it, until the rice is done
5. Cover the top of the pot with a cloth folded, and melt some butter and pour in
6. Cover it again, and let it simmer
7. Pepper and salt to taste
8. Serve in dishes, with the meat arranged at the top. One dish may be of its natural colour, white; another made yellow with saffron; and a third red, with pomegranate juice
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Turnip and Rice Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 Turnips
- 1/2 lb. Rice
- 2 quarts Water
- 1 pint Milk
- Onion and Salt
- 1 doz. white peppercorns

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up the turnips
2. Wash the rice and put into a saucepan with the onion and 1 doz. white peppercorns
3. Pour over the water and boil for an hour
4. Rub through a sieve and return to the saucepan
5. Add the milk and a seasoning of salt and pepper
6. Stir until it boils
7. Pour into a warm tureen and sprinkle some chopped parsley on top
8. This soup is much improved by putting one ounce of butter into the water in which the rice and turnips are boiled
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Turnip Salad
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 4 Young Turnips
- 2 Spring Onions
- 2 Boiled Potatoes
- Half a Lettuce
- Salad Dressing

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up the turnips and boil them for twenty minutes, or until soft.
2. Let them get quite cold.
3. Shred up very small the onions.
4. Slice up the potatoes.
5. Break up half a lettuce.
6. Arrange these neatly in a bowl.
7. Pour over a simple salad dressing or remoulade sauce.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Turtle Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of beef gravy thickened
- a wine-glassful of sherry
- the juice and peel of a lemon
- a few leaves of basil
- a shalot
- a few grains of cayenne
- a little essence of anchovy

## Directions

1. Put into a pan a pint of beef gravy thickened
2. Add a wine-glassful of sherry, the juice and peel of a lemon, a few leaves of basil, a shalot divided, a few grains of cayenne, and a little essence of anchovy
3. Let them simmer for five minutes
4. Strain through a tammy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Turtle Soup
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- turtle
- stock
- shalots
- thyme
- marjoram
- spice
- butter

## Directions

1. Hang up the turtle the night before it is to be dressed
2. Cut off its head, or a weight may be placed on its back to make it extend itself
3. When dead, cut the belly part clear off
4. Sever the fins at the point and take away the white meat, and put it into water
5. Draw, cleanse, and wash the entrails
6. Scald the fins, the head, and the belly shells
7. Saw the shell about two inches deep all round, scald and cut it into pieces
8. Put the head, shell, and fins in a pan and cover them with stock
9. Add shalots, thyme, marjoram, and spice, chopped
10. Stew it till tender
11. Take out the meat and strain the liquor through a sieve
12. Cut the fins in three pieces and take out all the brown from the bones, and cut it in square pieces
13. Melt some butter in a stewpan and put the white meat to it
14. Simmer it till nearly done
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Twelfth Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- Two pounds of flour
- Two pounds of white sugar
- Two pounds of butter
- Eighteen eggs
- Four pounds of currants, washed and cleaned
- Half a pound of almonds, blanched and cut into pieces
- One pound of candied orange and lemon-peel
- Half a pound of citron
- Half a nutmeg, grated
- Half an ounce of mixed spice
- A gill of brandy

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a stewpan, and work it smooth with a wooden spatula, and mix it with the sugar and spice in a pan for a time
2. Break in the eggs by degrees, and beat the mixture
3. Stir in the brandy, and then the flour, and work it a little
4. Add the fruit, sweetmeats, and almonds, and mix all lightly together
5. Put it in a hoop, and bake on a baking-tin for four hours
6. When nearly cold, ice it
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Twice-laid, for Fast-days
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- any kind of fish, either fresh or salt
- mashed potatoes
- a little butter

## Directions

1. Mix fish with mashed potatoes, with a little butter, in the proportion of about one-fourth fish to three-fourths potatoes.
2. Brown before the fire.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Ude's Royal Paste, au Choux
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pint of water
- a quarter of a pound of fresh butter
- two ounces of sugar
- a little salt
- the peel of a lemon
- very dry flour
- eggs

## Directions

1. Take a stewpan large enough to hold four quarts of water
2. Pour half a pint of water in it, with a quarter of a pound of fresh butter, two ounces of sugar, a little salt, and the peel of a lemon
3. Let them boil until the whole is properly melted
4. Take some very dry flour and pass through a sieve
5. Take the lemon-peel out with a ladle
6. Throw a handful of flour into the preparation while boiling, taking care not to put more flour than the liquor can soak up
7. Stir with a wooden spoon until the paste can be easily detached from the stewpan
8. Take it off the fire
9. Break an egg into this paste and mix it well
10. Break a second egg and mix it in
11. Do not put more eggs than the paste can absorb
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Unfermented Bread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- white meal, three pounds avordupois
- bi-carbonate of soda, four and a half ounces troy
- hydrochloric acid, five drachms
- water, thirty fluid ounces
- salt, two-thirds of an ounce troy

## Directions

1. Combine white meal, bi-carbonate of soda, hydrochloric acid, water, and salt to form bread
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Unfermented Bread
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- flour, three pounds
- bi-carbonate of soda, four drachms
- hydrochloric acid, five drachms
- salt, one drachm
- water, twenty-four ounces

## Directions

1. Mix the ingredients together
2. Immediately put into the oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Universal Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of broth
- a glass of white wine
- salt
- pepper
- grated lemon
- a bay leaf
- a spoonful of lemon juice

## Directions

1. Into a pint of broth put a glass of white wine, salt, pepper, grated lemon, a bay leaf, and a spoonful of lemon juice
2. Let them macerate by the side of the fire for ten hours
3. When served, pour over either game, fish, or vegetables, or send to table in a sauceboat
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Vanilla Ice (German)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- half a pound of sugar
- one quart of good cream
- half a drachm of bruised vanilla
- four or five yolks of eggs

## Directions

1. Boil half a pound of sugar into a syrup
2. add one quart of good cream
3. having tied up half a drachm of bruised vanilla in a muslin slip, boil the whole together
4. having beaten up four or five yolks of eggs, add them to the cream
5. press the vanilla, and take the slip out, and place it in a refrigerator to cool
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal Cutlets en Papillote
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal cutlets
- egg
- bread crumbs
- bacon
- parsley
- chives
- mushrooms
- salt
- pepper
- butter
- white paper

## Directions

1. Cover the cutlets on both sides with an egg
2. Add a forcemeat of bread crumbs, bacon, parsley, chives, and mushrooms, all chopped fine
3. Add salt and pepper
4. Lay thin slices of bacon over this seasoning
5. Envelope the whole carefully in white paper, well buttered
6. Let them fry gently over a slow fire
7. Serve them in the paper
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal Cutlets Plain
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal cutlets
- salt
- pepper
- parsley
- shalots
- dripping
- water
- flour
- broiled ham or bacon

## Directions

1. Put the cutlets in the frying-pan, with salt and pepper, parsley, shalots, chopped fine
2. Fry in the best dripping, over a quick fire
3. When the cutlets are done on one side, turn them, and brown them
4. Take out the cutlets and pour all the fat out of the pan but a couple of spoonfuls
5. Add half a pint of water, thickened with flour
6. Let this gravy brown, and pour over the cutlets
7. Garnish the dish with broiled ham, or bacon toasted
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal Forcemeat
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- four ounces of lean veal
- two ounces of bread crumbs
- two ounces of suet
- some parsley and sweet herbs
- an onion
- spice
- the yolk of an egg
- pepper and salt
- a little ham, bacon, or salt tongue

## Directions

1. Pound in a mortar four ounces of lean veal, two ounces of bread crumbs, two ounces of suet, and some parsley and sweet herbs, an onion, and spice
2. Break into it the yolk of an egg
3. Mix all together
4. Season with pepper and salt
5. A little ham, bacon, or salt tongue would make it more savoury
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal Forcemeat
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 oz. Suet (Beef)
- 3 oz. Bread Crumbs
- Pepper and Salt
- 1 Egg
- 1 teaspoonful Parsley
- 0.5 teaspoonful Sweet Herbs
- Half a Lemon

## Directions

1. Shred the suet and mix it with the bread crumbs
2. Chop the parsley and sweet herbs very finely and stir them in
3. Stir in the grated rind of half a lemon, and the pepper and salt
4. Drop in the egg and bind into a paste
5. It is ready to use
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Veal Fry
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal liver
- veal heart
- bacon
- sharp sauce

## Directions

1. Cut the liver and heart into slices
2. Fry with bacon
3. Serve with sharp sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal Gravy
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three pounds of veal
- small quantity of ham or bacon

## Directions

1. Cut veal into slices with a small quantity of ham or bacon
2. Simmer in the same manner as beef gravy
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal in White Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Neck of Veal
- 2 oz. Butter
- 1 oz. Flour
- Salt and Pepper
- 1 Egg
- 1 pint Milk
- 1 Onion
- 1 fagot of Herbs
- 1 dozen Peppercorns
- Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a saucepan, and when it is melted stir in the flour and cook well, but do not brown.
2. Boil the onion, herbs, and peppercorns in the milk, strain them out, pour the milk over the butter and flour, and stir till it boils.
3. Cut the meat into cutlets, lay them in and simmer very gently till the meat is tender, then take it up and arrange nicely on a dish.
4. Beat up an egg with a drop or two of lemon juice and a spoonful of gravy or milk.
5. Pour into the sauce in which the meat was cooked, and stir briskly over the fire until it thickens; strain over the meat, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Veal Kissoles
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal
- cooked ham
- Bechamel sauce
- pastry

## Directions

1. Mince and pound veal fine
2. Grate into it some remains of cooked ham
3. Mix them together with Bechamel sauce
4. Form into balls
5. Inclose each in pastry
6. Fry them of a nice brown
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal Olives
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal slices
- egg yolk
- bread-crumbs
- chopped parsley
- lemon-peel grated
- pepper
- salt
- nutmeg
- a thin slice of bacon for each piece
- a pint of good gravy
- flour
- ketchup
- pickled mushrooms

## Directions

1. Cut them not too thick, and rub over them some egg yolk
2. Strew on the bread-crumbs, mixed with chopped parsley, lemon-peel grated, pepper, salt, and nutmeg
3. Lay on each piece a thin slice of bacon, roll them tightly, skewer them
4. Rub the outside with egg rolled in bread-crumbs, and put them in a Dutch oven to do
5. Take a pint of good gravy, thicken it with flour, add ketchup, pickled mushrooms, boil this up a few minutes
6. Pour the sauce into the dish
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal Sausages
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- veal
- bacon

## Directions

1. Veal sausages are made like Oxford sausages, except bacon is used instead of sage
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Veal Shape
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Neck of Veal
- 1 Lemon
- Pepper and Salt
- 1/2 lb. Ham or Bacon
- 3 Eggs
- 1/2 pint Stock

## Directions

1. Put the meat into a saucepan with the rind of the lemon cut very thinly.
2. Pour in the stock and simmer very gently for three hours.
3. If the bacon is not cooked put it in and stew it for the last half-hour.
4. Take up the meat and ham, cut it off the bones.
5. Put these back in the saucepan and let them boil quickly without the lid.
6. Boil the eggs hard and cut them in slices and arrange in a plain mould or dish.
7. Lay in the veal and ham, and season with pepper
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Veal Stock
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- Knuckle of veal
- Peppercorns and vegetables
- Cold water
- Dessertspoonful of salt
- 2 dozen white peppercorns
- One onion stuck with six cloves
- A fagot of herbs made with sprigs of parsley, marjoram, and thyme, tied up with a bay or peach leaf

## Directions

1. Have the butcher chop the bones very small
2. Cut the meat across in several places and lay it in a very clean stock pot
3. Cover well with cold water and bring to the boil slowly
4. Put in a dessertspoonful of salt and skim very carefully
5. Draw away from the fire and place it where it will boil steadily
6. Put in 2 dozen white peppercorns, one onion stuck with six cloves, and a fagot of herbs
7. Boil steadily for six hours and strain off
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Vegetable Essences and Tinctures
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cayenne
- lemon-peel
- celery
- ginger
- allspice
- nutmeg
- cloves
- mace
- cinnamon
- marjoram
- herbs of any kind
- rectified spirits or brandy

## Directions

1. Infuse the different ingredients in rectified spirits or brandy
2. Allow them to steep for a month or more
3. Strain the liquor for use
4. The various articles must be good of their kind, and not adulterated
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Vegetable Marrow
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- marrow
- white sauce
- salt

## Directions

1. Peel the marrow, take out the seeds, and cut it into small pieces
2. Put into boiling water nicely seasoned with salt, and boil gently for about fifteen minutes
3. Take up with a slice and strain in a colander
4. Place in a hot dish, and pour over the sauce
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Vegetable Marrow and Tomato Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 doz. Tomatoes
- 1 Vegetable Marrow
- 9 Onions
- 1 oz. Butter
- 2 doz. Peppercorns
- 1 teaspoonful Sugar
- 3 pints Stock
- Salt

## Directions

1. Peel the vegetable marrow, slice it up, and take out the seeds.
2. Slice up the tomatoes and put them, with the marrow, into the saucepan with the butter, sugar, salt, and peppercorns.
3. Sweat them for five minutes.
4. Pour over the boiling water or stock, and simmer for one hour.
5. Rub through a sieve and return to the saucepan.
6. Add more salt, if necessary, bring it to the boil, pour into a tureen, and serve.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Vegetable Marrow Stuffed
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 Vegetable Marrow
- 1/4 lb. Veal Forcemeat
- 1/4 pint Melted Butter Sauce
- Salt
- Tape

## Directions

1. Peel a marrow and cut it in half length-ways.
2. Prepare some veal forcemeat by recipe given elsewhere, and make it hot in a saucepan.
3. Remove the seeds from the marrow and put in their place the forcemeat; put the pieces together and bind round with tape.
4. Have ready a fish kettle full of boiling water seasoned with salt; lay the marrow on the drainer and plunge into the water.
5. Boil gently for about twenty-five minutes.
6. Slip the marrow carefully into a dish and pour over some melted butter sauce.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Vegetable Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 lbs. Mixed Vegetables
- 2 oz. Butter
- ½ lb. Haricot Beans
- Peppercorns
- Salt
- Sugar
- 4 quarts of Water
- carrots
- turnips
- leeks
- onions
- celery

## Directions

1. Take any vegetables that may be in season, such as carrots, turnips, leeks, onions, and celery, and slice them up
2. Put them into a saucepan with the haricot beans and the butter, and turn them all about till the butter is all absorbed
3. Sprinkle over them a teaspoonful each of salt and sugar, add the peppercorns and the water
4. Boil until the vegetables are very soft
5. Rub them through a sieve, return to the saucepan and make thoroughly hot
6. It is ready to serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Venetian Rice
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Rice
- 1 lb. Cheese
- 1 pint Stock
- 1 oz. Butter
- Pepper and Salt

## Directions

1. Boil some rice, or take any cold rice that may be left, put it into a saucepan with the stock, and simmer till the stock is absorbed
2. Grate up some dry, hard pieces of cheese, stir them in with the butter, pepper, and salt
3. Cover down by the side of the fire for about half an hour
4. Pile on a dish, and serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Venison or Kangaroo Hashed
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- meat
- stock gravy
- gill of port wine
- table-spoonful of ketchup
- lemon
- salt
- cayenne
- currant jelly sauce

## Directions

1. Cut the meat in slices
2. Put it in a stewpan, with a small quantity of stock gravy, a gill of port wine, a table-spoonful of ketchup, some lemon, with salt and cayenne in moderation
3. Let it simmer
4. Serve in a hot dish
5. Serve with currant jelly sauce
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Venison Pasty
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- shoulder of venison, boned
- fine well-hung loin of mutton
- rape
- vinegar
- port
- sugar
- two gills of port wine
- one gill of claret
- shalots
- pepper
- salt
- mace
- allspice
- stock
- paste
- lemon juice
- thickening

## Directions

1. Beat and season the boned shoulder.
2. Supply the want of fat with that of a fine well-hung loin of mutton, steeped twenty-four hours in equal parts of rape, vinegar, and port.
3. Rub the shoulder well with sugar for two or three days.
4. Wipe it extremely clean from the sugar and the wine.
5. Cut the meat into pieces two inches square.
6. Put them into a stewpan with two gills of port wine, one of claret, a few shalots, some pepper, salt, mace, and allspice, and enough stock to cover it.
7. Let it simmer until three parts done.
8. Take out the meat you intend for the pasty, and put into a deep dish with a part of the liquor.
9. Cover the pasty an inch thick with paste.
10. Bake for two hours.
11. Before it is sent to table pour in a sauce made from the liquor the venison was stewed in, free from fat, with added pepper, salt, the juice of half a lemon, and half a gill of port wine or claret, and a little thickening.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Vermicelli or Macaroni Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- sufficient vermicelli or macaroni to fill three-fourths of pie-dish
- a little sugar
- butter
- spice
- a custard

## Directions

1. Boil sufficient vermicelli or macaroni to fill three-fourths of your pie-dish, with a little sugar, butter, and spice, on the top of which put a custard
2. bake
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Vermicelli Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 ounce vermicelli
- 2 quarts bone stock
- 3 or 4 threads of saffron
- 1 onion or leek
- 6 cloves
- 1 dozen white peppercorns
- salt

## Directions

1. Put the stock into a saucepan with three or four threads of saffron, an onion or leek stuck with six cloves, 1 dozen white peppercorns and some salt, and boil all together for half an hour
2. Strain out the vegetables and put it back into the saucepan
3. It should be of a bright straw colour; if it is not, a thread more saffron may be added before straining
4. Put in the vermicelli broken small, and simmer for twenty minutes
5. It is then ready to serve
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Victoria Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- The yolks of twelve eggs, leaving out the whites of six
- One pound of white sugar
- The juice of one lemon
- The peel of two lemons cut very fine
- Twelve ounces of flour

## Directions

1. Whisk these ingredients together for half an hour
2. Add twelve ounces of flour
3. Put the cake in the oven and bake an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Victoria Cake
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- yolks of twelve eggs
- whites of six eggs
- one pound of white sugar
- juice of one lemon
- peel of two lemons
- twelve ounces of flour

## Directions

1. Whisk the yolks of twelve eggs, leaving out the whites of six, one pound of white sugar, the juice of one lemon, and the peel of two cut very fine together for half an hour
2. Add twelve ounces of flour
3. Put the cake in the oven and bake an hour
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Vinegar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- eleven gallons of water
- fourteen pounds of sugar
- yeast

## Directions

1. Boil eleven gallons of water and fourteen pounds of sugar for twenty minutes
2. Pour it into a vessel to cool
3. When milk-warm, add yeast, and let it ferment
4. Place it in a warm place, and in a few months you will have good vinegar
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Vinegar for Family Use
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- two quarts of good vinegar
- light French wine

## Directions

1. Have a barrel to hold from thirty to forty quarts
2. Boil two quarts of good vinegar, pour it into the barrel, stop the bung-hole, and roll it about well
3. Half fill the barrel with light French wine, and leave it for a time in a warm place
4. At the end of a week boil two more quarts of vinegar, pour it into the barrel, and fill it up with wine and set it by
5. The vinegar may be used in a month, and it will acquire strength by keeping
6. If the vinegar should at any time be getting weak, add two quarts of vinegar to the cask, after boiling
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Vinegar in Twenty-four Hours
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- cider, beer, or wine
- beech shavings
- vinegar
- cotton wicking

## Directions

1. Take a clean flour barrel, and bore auger holes all around the sides and in the bottom
2. Set it over a tub or open cask, and fill it lightly with beech shavings which have been soaked in vinegar
3. On the top of this barrel, which is open, lay two strips of wood, and rest on these a pail filled with cider, beer, or the like
4. Procure fifteen lengths of cotton wicking, about thirty inches long, which, after dipping in the liquid, arrange round the side of the pail at regular intervals, so that one end of each will be hanging in the cider and the other hanging down outside, and below the bottom of the pail
5. By means of these wicks the pail will be gradually emptied of its contents, which, trickling over the shavings, will be exposed to the air, absorb oxygen, and finally be received in the tub beneath
6. By returning the liquor into the pail above, and suffering this trickling process to be repeated two or three times, a splendid vinegar will be obtained
7. Perform the process in a room heated to 80° or higher
8. Cover the whole apparatus with a tight hogshead, without a head, turned upside down
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Waders
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a cup and a half of thick yeast
- a little warm milk
- two pounds of flour
- one pound of fresh butter
- ten eggs
- a grated nutmeg
- a quarter of a pint of orange-flower water
- a little powdered cinnamon
- three pints of warm milk

## Directions

1. Mix a cup and a half of thick yeast with a little warm milk, and set it, with two pounds of flour, before the fire to rise
2. Mix with them one pound of fresh butter, ten eggs, a grated nutmeg, a quarter of a pint of orange-flower water, a little powdered cinnamon, and three pints of warm milk
3. When the batter is perfectly smooth, butter the irons
4. Fill them with it, close them down tightly
5. Put them between the bars of a bright clear fire
6. When sufficiently done, they will slip easily out of the irons
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Water Souchet
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 6 Small Fish
- Vegetables
- Salt and Pepper
- Lemon Juice
- 1 doz. peppercorns
- 1 carrot
- 1 small turnip
- 1 onion
- A small piece of celery
- A fagot of herbs

## Directions

1. Choose small fish of different kinds and fillet them
2. Wash the bones in cold water and remove the black substance from them
3. Put them into two quarts of cold water with a teaspoonful of salt
4. When it boils remove the scum and add 1 doz. peppercorns, one carrot, one small turnip, one onion, a small piece of celery, and a fagot of herbs
5. Put the vegetables in whole
6. Boil this together for one hour
7. Strain off through a hair sieve and return to the saucepan
8. Wash the vegetables that have been boiled in it, slice them up and put them into the liquor
9. Cut the fillets of fish into small pieces and put them in
10. Simmer for half an hour
11. Put in a little lemon juice
12. Pour into a tureen, and sprinkle a little chopped parsley on the top
13. Send brown bread and butter to table with it and a lemon
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Welsh Nectar
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- the peel of four lemons
- two gallons of boiling water
- the strained juice of the lemons
- a pound of raisins, stoned and chopped fine
- brandy

## Directions

1. Cut the peel of four lemons thin, pour on it two gallons of boiling water
2. when cold, add the strained juice of the lemons, and a pound of raisins, stoned and chopped fine
3. Let it stand a few days, and then strain it and bottle for use
4. A small addition of brandy is an improvement
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Welsh Rabbit
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a slice of bread
- butter
- a slice of mellow cheese
- mustard

## Directions

1. Toast a slice of bread on both sides, and butter it
2. Toast a slice of mellow cheese on one side, and lay that next the bread
3. Toast the other side with a salamander
4. Rub mustard over
5. Serve hot and covered
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: West India Punch
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- boiling water
- sugar
- bottle of rum
- limes
- guava jelly
- green tea, two ounces
- six glasses of cognac
- two glasses of Madeira
- nutmeg

## Directions

1. Brew about a pint of green tea (two ounces)
2. Dissolve two-thirds of the sugar in the tea infusion
3. Throw aside the tea-leaves
4. Rub the remainder of the sugar on the rind of the limes
5. Add the delicious acid of the fruit to the already impregnated sugar
6. Dissolve the guava jelly in a pint or so of boiling water
7. Commingle the tea, the sweets, and acids
8. Taste the foundation or sherbet
9. Add six glasses of cognac, two of Madeira, and the bottle of old rum
10. Add about a quart more of boiling water
11. Add the slightest possible sprinkling of nutmeg
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Wexford Biscuits
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- ten ounces of butter
- four ounces of flour
- three ounces of white sugar
- yolks of two eggs
- one table-spoonful of brandy
- white of the eggs
- white sugar

## Directions

1. Rub butter into flour and sugar
2. Mix in the yolks of eggs and brandy
3. Roll the paste out thin
4. Cut with a cutter into round shapes
5. Egg over the tops of each with the white of the eggs
6. Sift on white sugar
7. Bake in a quick oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: White Champagne
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three quarts of water
- one gallon of fruit
- gooseberries
- four pounds of loaf sugar or best crystallised sugar per gallon of liquor
- brandy

## Directions

1. Pour three quarts of water to one gallon of fruit, and let the mixture stand a week, after the gooseberries have been well macerated
2. Strain through a sieve, and add to every gallon of liquor four pounds of loaf sugar, or the best crystallised sugar
3. Let the fermentation go on for several days
4. Pass the liquor through a jelly-bag into the cask, leaving it open until the fermentation has nearly subsided, but not quite
5. When the fermentation is in the latter state, bung the cask up, having previously inserted a small quantity of brandy
6. Let it remain twelve months, and then bottle
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: White Champagne
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- three quarts of water
- one gallon of gooseberries
- four pounds of loaf sugar or crystallised sugar per gallon

## Directions

1. Pour three quarts of water to one gallon of fruit, and let the mixture stand a week, after the gooseberries have been well macerated
2. Strain through a sieve, and add to every gallon of liquor four pounds of loaf sugar, or the best crystallised sugar
3. Let the fermentation go on for several days, then pass the liquor through a jelly-bag into the cask, leaving it open until the fermentation has nearly subsided, but not quite
4. When the fermentation is in the latter state, bung
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: White Macaroni Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1½ oz. Macaroni
- 1 pint Milk
- 1 oz. Butter
- 3 pints Bone Stock
- Vegetables
- Flour
- 1 tablespoonful Cornflour
- Salt
- White Pepper
- Lemon Juice

## Directions

1. The stock made from veal or mutton bones is the best for this soup, as it must be white.
2. If not plenty of vegetables have been boiled in it, boil an onion or leek, a slice of turnip, and a small piece of celery stalk in the stock for twenty minutes, and strain them out.
3. Put the butter into a stewpan, and when it is melted stir in a tablespoonful of cornflour, pour over the milk and stock, and stir until it boils.
4. Boil the macaroni in salt and water for twenty minutes, strain off the water, and cut it into pieces about 1 inch long; put these into the soup, and simmer for ten minutes.
5. Just before serving, flavour with salt, a dust of white pepper, and a few drops of lemon juice.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: White Onion Soup
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint of Milk
- 1 oz. Butter
- 4 Onions
- Salt and Pepper
- 1 pint White Bone Stock
- Dry Crusts

## Directions

1. Peel and slice up the onions and put them into a saucepan with the butter; make them very hot, and then cover them down and leave them to cook by the side of the fire for an hour, but they must not get any colour.
2. Break in some dry, hard pieces of bread; it should be crust only for this soup.
3. Boil the milk and stock together, pour it over the onions and bread, and let it simmer very slowly, closely covered, for an hour.
4. Rub through a sieve, season with salt and pepper and a few drops of lemon juice.
5. Boil up and serve with fried bread.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: White Sauce
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pint Milk
- 1 oz. Butter
- 0.5 oz. Flour
- Salt and Pepper

## Directions

1. Put the butter into a small saucepan, and when it is dissolved put in the flour
2. Mix well and pour on the cold milk and stir till it boils
3. Let it boil for two minutes and it is ready
4. It may be served either as a sweet or savoury sauce, putting either sugar or pepper and salt, as required
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Whitebait
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- whitebait
- flour
- hot lard
- lemon-juice
- cayenne pepper

## Directions

1. The fish should be dressed within an hour after being caught, or they are apt to cling together.
2. They are kept in water, from which, they are taken by a skimmer as required.
3. They are then thrown upon a layer of flour contained in a large napkin, in which they are shaken until completely enveloped in flour.
4. They are then put into a colander, and all the superfluous flour is removed by sifting.
5. The fish are next thrown into hot lard, contained in a copper cauldron, or stewpan, placed over a charcoal fire.
6. In about two minutes they are removed by a tin skimmer, thrown into a colander to drain, and served up instantly, by placing them on a fish-drainer, in a dish.
7. At table, lemon-juice is squeezed on them, and they are seasoned with cayenne pepper.
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Wild Ducks and Teal
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- wild ducks
- port wine sauce
- lemon
- cayenne pepper

## Directions

1. Keep them sufficiently
2. Roast them rather under-done, not forgetting to baste well
3. Serve with lemon and port wine sauce, and cayenne pepper
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Wine Sauce for Venison, Hare, or Kangaroo
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- a quarter of a pint of port wine or claret, or dark Sydney wine
- the same quantity of good gravy
- a table-spoonful of currant jelly

## Directions

1. warm up, and serve
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Worcester Sausages
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- beef
- suet
- allspice
- herbs

## Directions

1. Made of beef, suet, and allspice, with other herbs
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Wou-Wou Sauce
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- parsley leaves
- two or three pickled walnuts or cucumbers
- butter
- a table-spoonful of flour
- about half a pint of broth
- a table-spoonful of vinegar
- a table-spoonful of ketchup or port wine
- a tea-spoonful of mustard

## Directions

1. Chop some parsley leaves fine, and add two or three pickled walnuts or cucumbers
2. Put into a pan a bit of butter
3. when it has melted stir in a table-spoonful of flour, and about half a pint of broth, a table-spoonful of vinegar, the same quantity of ketchup, or port wine, and a tea-spoonful of mustard
4. Let it simmer till thick, and then add the parsley leaves and pickles
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Yankee Pudding
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 egg and its weight in flour
- Sugar
- Bread crumbs
- 1 tablespoonful marmalade
- 1/2 teaspoonful carbonate of soda
- 1 gill milk

## Directions

1. Mix the flour, sugar, and bread crumbs together; stir in the marmalade.
2. Make the milk just warm, dissolve in it the soda.
3. Beat up the egg and mix together, pour this over the dry ingredients, beat for a few minutes; turn into a buttered basin.
4. Tie over it a cloth, plunge into boiling water, and boil one hour.
5. Serve either hot or cold. A spoonful of marmalade placed on the top of this pudding just before serving is an improvement.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia

title: Yeast (Cornish Recipe)
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one handful of hops
- three quarts of water
- a pint of flour
- a tea-cupful of moist sugar
- a tea-cupful of yeast

## Directions

1. Put one handful of hops to three quarts of water, and let it boil two hours
2. Strain the hops away, mixing a pint of flour with the liquor
3. While hot add a tea-cupful of moist sugar
4. Let it stand and get luke-warm
5. Work it with a tea-cupful of yeast, stirring it often
6. Let it stand one day, and then put it into jars for use
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Yorkshire Cakes
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- one pint of milk
- four ounces of butter
- two pounds of flour
- three table-spoonfuls of yeast
- two eggs well beaten

## Directions

1. Melt butter in a pint of milk
2. Add flour, yeast, and eggs
3. Let rise
4. Knead them lightly, and make into cakes
5. Let them rise on the tins
6. Bake in a moderately heated oven
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Yorkshire Pudding
author: Edward Abbott
body:
## Ingredients

- six tablespoonfuls of flour
- salt
- a pint of milk
- three eggs

## Directions

1. Put six tablespoonfuls of flour into a basin, with a little salt
2. Stir in gradually a pint of milk to make a thin batter, and make sure it is not lumpy
3. When it is well mixed, put in three eggs, well beaten up, and a little more milk
4. Place it under the meat, so that the dripping may fall on it
5. Serve as free from fat as possible
source_title: The English and Australian Cookery Book

title: Yorkshire Tea Cakes
author: Philip E. Muskett
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 lb. Flour
- 1 Egg
- 1½ gills Milk
- 1 tablespoonful Yeast
- ½ tablespoonful Sugar
- 1 oz. Butter

## Directions

1. Rub the butter and flour together, make a well in the centre, sprinkle in the sugar, and drop in the egg.
2. Mix the yeast and sugar in a basin, make the milk just tepid, and pour it over the yeast.
3. Strain into the flour and egg and work into a light dough, divide into two parts.
4. Rub a little butter over two small tins, and put one cake in each tin.
5. Cover with thin paper, and stand the tins near the stove for an hour, or until they have risen to at least three times their original size ; then bake in a quick oven for fifteen minutes.
6. Serve either plain, or toasted and buttered.
source_title: The Art of Living in Australia
